topics.title: Traditional Computing - Limitations. Overview of Computing Paradigms - Grid Computing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT525 - CLOUD COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Traditional Computing - Limitations. Overview of Computing Paradigms - Grid Computing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Traditional Computing — The Classical Model

**Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
Traditional Computing refers to the **standalone, isolated computing paradigm** in which a single desktop computer or workstation operates independently using its own local processor, memory, storage, and peripheral devices to execute applications and process data. Resources are **dedicated, non-shared, and tightly coupled** to a single user or task at a time.

In the KTU 2024 scheme context, "Traditional Computing" is studied specifically as the **baseline model** against which distributed paradigms (Grid, Cluster, Cloud) are compared. It embodies the pre-distributed era of computing where vertical scaling (bigger machines) was the only path to performance.

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Traditional Computing is **NOT** asked as a standalone definition question. It is always framed comparatively — *"Compare Traditional Computing with Grid/Cloud Computing"* (a 14-mark favorite). Master the **limitations table** thoroughly.

---

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Single-Room Office"

Imagine a small business operating from **one isolated office room** with:
- One desk (CPU)
- One filing cabinet (Hard Disk)
- One telephone (Network card)
- One employee (User)

When work piles up, the only solution is to **buy a bigger desk, more filing cabinets, or hire one more person into the same room**. You **cannot borrow a desk from the office next door** because there is no connection. If the room floods (hardware failure), **all work halts**.

**This is Traditional Computing in a nutshell:**
| Office Element | Computing Equivalent |
|---|---|
| One isolated room | One standalone PC/workstation |
| Bigger desk = more work | Vertical scaling (upgrading CPU/RAM) |
| Flooded room = stoppage | Single point of failure |
| No phone line to neighbors | No network resource sharing |
| One employee's workload | One user's tasks |

---

### 1.3 Core Limitations of Traditional Computing

> [!NOTE]  
> These limitations are the **primary motivation** for the evolution toward distributed paradigms. Expect KTU questions to ask: *"Discuss the limitations of traditional computing that led to distributed systems."* (Module 1, CO1 mapped — Cognitive Level: Understand/Analyze)

1. **Resource Underutilization** — Idle CPU cycles and unused RAM on idle desktops are wasted (average desktop utilization is only **5–15%**).
2. **Single Point of Failure (SPOF)** — Hardware crash = complete work stoppage; no redundancy built-in.
3. **Limited Scalability** — Scaling is **vertical only** (buy bigger machine); horizontal scaling (more machines) is not possible.
4. **High Capital Expenditure (CAPEX)** — Heavy upfront investment in high-end hardware that may sit underused.
5. **Data Silos** — Data trapped on local disks; no easy sharing across departments/geographies.
6. **Maintenance Overhead** — OS patches, hardware upgrades, antivirus, backups — all manual and per-machine.
7. **No Collaboration Support** — No native mechanism for cross-organizational resource pooling.
8. **Geographic Restrictions** — Resources accessible only physically near the machine.

---

### 1.4 Computing Paradigms — The Evolutionary Timeline

A **Computing Paradigm** is a fundamental model/conceptual framework that defines **how computing resources (compute, storage, network) are organized, accessed, and managed** to solve problems.

The evolution from Traditional → Distributed → Parallel → Grid → Cluster → Cloud:

| Era | Paradigm | Core Idea |
|---|---|---|
| 1970s–80s | **Traditional Computing** | Single isolated machine |
| 1980s–90s | **Parallel Computing** | Multiple processors inside one machine (tightly coupled) |
| 1990s–2000s | **Distributed Computing** | Independent computers connected via network, working together |
| 1990s–2000s | **Network Computing** | Thin clients accessing remote resources |
| Late 1990s–2000s | **Grid Computing** | Coordinated sharing of **geographically distributed**, **heterogeneous** resources across **virtual organizations** |
| 2000s | **Cluster Computing** | Homogeneous nodes in a **single location**, tightly integrated |
| 2000s–2010s | **Utility Computing** | Pay-per-use like electricity |
| 2008–present | **Cloud Computing** | On-demand, elastic, virtualized resources as a service over the internet |

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **KTU Distinction to Memorize:**  
> - **Cluster** = many machines, **same location**, **same hardware/OS** (homogeneous)  
> - **Grid** = many machines, **different locations**, **different hardware/OS** (heterogeneous)  
> - **Cloud** = massively scalable virtualized resources delivered **as a service** over the internet

---

### 1.5 Grid Computing — Formal Definition

**Definition (Foster & Kesselman, 1998 — the canonical reference):**
> *"Grid Computing is a type of **distributed computing** paradigm that coordinates **disparate, geographically dispersed computing resources** (computers, storage, instruments, data) to deliver consistent, pervasive, and inexpensive access to high-end computational capabilities, coordinated through **open standards and protocols** to support **virtual organizations**."*

**Simplified for KTU exam answers:**
Grid Computing is a distributed computing model that enables the **coordinated sharing of heterogeneous, geographically distributed resources** across multiple administrative domains to form a single virtual computational entity called a **Virtual Organization (VO)**.

> [!NOTE]  
> **The Three Cardinal Characteristics of a Grid (MUST memorize for KTU):**
> 1. **Coordinates resources that are not subject to centralized control** (no single owner)
> 2. **Uses standard, open, general-purpose protocols and interfaces** (not application-specific)
> 3. **Delivers nontrivial qualities of service** (high throughput, low latency, security, etc.)

These are called the **Foster's Triad** — coined by Ian Foster, often regarded as the "Father of the Grid."

---

### 1.6 Intuitive Analogy — The "National Power Grid"

| National Power Grid Concept | Grid Computing Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Electricity generation plants across the country | Geographically distributed compute servers |
| Transmission lines & substations | High-speed network (LAN/MAN/WAN) |
| Unified socket in every home | Standardized middleware interfaces (OGSI, WSRF) |
| Pay only for units consumed | Metering/usage accounting |
| Power from anywhere, on demand | Compute from anywhere, on demand |
| Multiple utility companies cooperating | Virtual Organization (VO) |

Just as a national power grid pools electricity from many plants to serve any home, a **computational grid pools compute cycles** from many machines to serve any user task.

---

### 1.7 Why Grid Computing Matters — Real-World Motivations

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **KTU 2024 CO1 Mapping:** This section maps to **CO1: Understand the evolution and architecture of distributed computing paradigms including Grid and Cloud.**

- **Scientific Problems** — Problems like protein folding, climate modeling, and particle physics (LHC at CERN) require **PetaFLOPS** of compute — impossible on a single machine.
- **Resource Sharing Across Institutions** — Universities, research labs, and hospitals need to share expensive instruments (telescopes, MRI scanners) and datasets.
- **Cost Reduction** — Better utilization of existing machines rather than buying one supercomputer.
- **Collaboration** — Enables cross-organizational teamwork (e.g., the human genome project).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Detailed Limitations of Traditional Computing — Analytical Breakdown

| # | Limitation | Root Cause | Engineering Impact |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Resource Underutilization** | One user cannot fully load modern CPUs | Wasted CAPEX; average server utilization in data centers ~ **10–15%** |
| 2 | **Vertical Scaling Bottleneck** | Moore's Law hitting physical limits | Cost grows **super-linearly** with performance |
| 3 | **Single Point of Failure (SPOF)** | No redundancy built-in | MTBF (Mean Time Between Failures) of system = MTBF of single component |
| 4 | **Geographic Lock-in** | Resources tethered to physical location | Cannot serve global users efficiently |
| 5 | **Heterogeneity Incompatibility** | No standard for cross-platform cooperation | Windows, Linux, macOS machines cannot pool resources natively |
| 6 | **High TCO** | Hardware + Software + Power + Cooling + Admin | TCO over 5 years ≈ **3× the purchase price** |
| 7 | **Static Provisioning** | Resources sized for peak load | Over-provisioned 90% of the time |
| 8 | **No QoS Guarantees** | No SLA (Service Level Agreement) | No business-grade reliability |

> [!NOTE]  
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Always frame limitations as the **triggering motivation** for distributed paradigms. Examiners reward answers that **connect** limitations to features of Grid/Cloud.

---

### 2.2 Grid Computing — Theoretical Architecture Layers

The **OGSA (Open Grid Services Architecture)** defines a layered model:

**Layer 1 — Fabric Layer (Physical Resources)**
- Actual physical/logical resources: compute hosts, storage systems, networks, sensors, instruments.
- Provides the raw capability that the grid exposes upward.

**Layer 2 — Connectivity Layer (Communication & Security)**
- Defines core communication and authentication protocols.
- Sub-components:
  - **Communication protocols** — Internet protocols (TCP/IP, HTTP, IIOP)
  - **Security protocols** — GSI (Grid Security Infrastructure), PKI, X.509 certificates
  - **Authentication & Authorization** — Single Sign-On (SSO)

**Layer 3 — Resource Layer (Resource Management)**
- Manages individual resources: discovery, negotiation, monitoring, accounting.
- Examples: GRAM (Grid Resource Allocation Manager), GridFTP
- Functions: reservation, allocation, monitoring

**Layer 4 — Collective Layer (Coordinated Multiple Resources)**
- Coordinates multiple resources: directory services (MDS — Monitoring and Discovery Service), brokering, replication, workload management.
- This is where **virtualization** of the resources happens for the application.

**Layer 5 — Application Layer (User Applications)**
- The actual scientific/engineering applications that consume grid services.
- Applications are written using grid APIs (e.g., Globus Toolkit).

---

### 2.3 Grid Architecture — The Five-Level Conceptual Stack (Foster's Stack)

Ian Foster's classical Grid Architecture describes **five layers**:

| Layer | Name | Protocols/Services |
|---|---|---|
| L0 | **Fabric** | Physical devices, sensors, compute nodes |
| L1 | **Connectivity** | Internet Protocol, DNS, Authentication, Authorization |
| L2 | **Resource** | Single resource management (allocation, monitoring) |
| L3 | **Collective** | Multi-resource coordination (directory, scheduling) |
| L4 | **Application** | User-level scientific/engineering apps |

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **KTU Favorite Question:** *"Explain the architecture of Grid Computing with a neat diagram."* — Memorize this stack and draw it in the exam.

---

### 2.4 Types of Grid Computing

**1. Compute Grid**
- Focuses on pooling **CPU processing power** from many machines.
- Used for compute-intensive, embarrassingly parallel problems (e.g., Monte Carlo simulations, drug discovery).

**2. Data Grid**
- Focuses on pooling **storage and data**.
- Provides unified access to huge datasets distributed across the world (e.g., CERN's LHC Data Grid handling **petabytes/year**).

**3. Service Grid** *(most common in modern Grids)*
- Provides services (workflow, scheduling, brokering) on top of compute/data resources.
- Foundation for what evolved into **Cloud Computing** — many see Cloud as "the next generation Grid."

---

### 2.5 KTU Formula / Metrics Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]  
> Grid Computing is conceptual, not heavily formula-driven. However, certain metrics appear in KTU problems.

| Metric | Formula | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **System Throughput** | $T = \frac{N_{\text{completed\_jobs}}}{T_{\text{total\_time}}}$ | Jobs finished per unit time |
| **Speedup (Amdahl's Law)** | $S(N) = \frac{1}{(1-p) + \frac{p}{N}}$ | Where $p$ = parallelizable fraction, $N$ = processors |
| **Efficiency** | $E = \frac{S(N)}{N}$ | How well $N$ processors are utilized |
| **Grid Utilization** | $U = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} U_i}{n}$ | Average CPU utilization across $n$ grid nodes |
| **Availability** | $A = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$ | Mean Time Between Failures & Mean Time To Repair |
| **Cost per Job** | $C_{\text{job}} = \frac{C_{\text{hardware}} + C_{\text{ops}}}{N_{\text{jobs}}}$ | Total Cost of Ownership amortization |

**Boundary Conditions & Constraints:**
- $0 \le p \le 1$ (parallelizable fraction)
- $N \ge 1$ (number of nodes)
- For $N \to \infty$: $S_{\max} = \frac{1}{1-p}$ — the **hard ceiling** of parallelization
- If $p = 0.95$: $S_{\max} = 20$ (even infinite nodes give only 20× speedup)
- If $p = 0.50$: $S_{\max} = 2$ (parallelization is largely useless)

---

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility of Grids

- **CERN LHC Computing Grid (LCG)** — **10,000+ nodes**, processes **petabytes of particle collision data** annually.
- **SETI@home** — Distributed volunteer computing, analyzed radio telescope data using idle home PCs (peak **3 million participants**).
- **Folding@home** — Stanford's protein-folding simulation grid (peak **exaflops** of volunteer compute).
- **Earth System Grid (ESG)** — Climate modeling for IPCC reports.
- **TeraGrid** (now XSEDE) — US NSF-funded academic grid linking **10+ supercomputing centers**.
- **BOINC** — Berkeley Open Infrastructure for Network Computing, an open-source grid middleware.

---

### 2.7 Grid vs. Cluster vs. Cloud — The Holy Trinity Comparison

| Parameter | **Cluster** | **Grid** | **Cloud** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Geography** | Single location | Geographically distributed | Geographically distributed (data centers worldwide) |
| **Hardware** | Homogeneous (same) | Heterogeneous (different) | Virtualized, abstract |
| **Ownership** | Single admin domain | Multiple admin domains | Single provider |
| **Coupling** | Tightly coupled via LAN | Loosely coupled via WAN | Loosely coupled via Internet |
| **Purpose** | High-performance computing | Resource sharing across orgs | On-demand service delivery |
| **Access** | Dedicated internal users | Virtual Organization members | Public (pay-per-use) |
| **Middleware** | OS-level (e.g., MPI) | Globus Toolkit, gLite | Hypervisor (VMware, KVM) |
| **Scalability** | Limited to LAN | Moderate (cross-org) | Massive (elastic) |
| **Failure handling** | Redundant nodes | VO-level fault tolerance | Provider-managed SLAs |
| **Examples** | Google internal clusters | SETI@home, LCG | AWS EC2, Azure, GCP |

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **KTU 2024 Exam Pattern:** This comparison table is the **#1 most asked 14-mark question** in Module 1. Draw the table in your answer booklet — examiners reward visual organization.

---

### 2.8 Grid Middleware — The Globus Toolkit

The most influential Grid middleware is the **Globus Toolkit** (originally from Argonne National Lab / University of Chicago).

Key Globus Services:
- **GSI** — Grid Security Infrastructure (X.509 certs, single sign-on)
- **GRAM** — Grid Resource Allocation Manager (job submission)
- **GridFTP** — High-performance secure data transfer protocol
- **MDS** — Monitoring and Discovery Service (resource info directory)
- **GSI-OpenSSH** — Secure shell for grid

> [!NOTE]  
> **Other Grid Middleware** (for breadth): gLite (used in European EGEE), UNICORE, BOINC, Condor, Legion.

---

### 2.9 The Virtual Organization (VO) — Heart of Grid Computing

A **Virtual Organization** is a collection of individuals, institutions, and resources brought together to share resources based on a common set of rules. Examples:
- A **physics collaboration** across 50 universities sharing telescope time and compute.
- A **biomedical consortium** pooling MRI data and analysis pipelines.

VOs enable dynamic, secure, policy-driven resource sharing without requiring physical co-location or unified ownership.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Analytical Problem 1 — Amdahl's Law Applied to Grid Scaling

**Problem Statement (KTU-style):**
A scientific application takes **100 seconds** to run on a single machine. **90%** of the program is parallelizable, and 10% is inherently sequential. Calculate the **speedup** and **efficiency** when running on a grid of **8 nodes**.

---

**Step 1 — Identify given values**
- $T_{\text{serial}} = 100 \, \text{seconds}$ (total execution time on 1 node)
- $p = 0.90$ (parallelizable fraction)
- $(1 - p) = 0.10$ (sequential fraction)
- $N = 8$ (number of grid nodes)

**Step 2 — Apply Amdahl's Law formula**

$$
S(N) = \frac{1}{(1 - p) + \frac{p}{N}}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute values**

$$
S(8) = \frac{1}{(1 - 0.90) + \frac{0.90}{8}}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute denominator numerically**

$$
(1 - 0.90) = 0.10
$$

$$
\frac{0.90}{8} = 0.1125
$$

$$
\text{Denominator} = 0.10 + 0.1125 = 0.2125
$$

**Step 5 — Compute speedup**

$$
S(8) = \frac{1}{0.2125} = 4.7058 \approx 4.71
$$

**Step 6 — Interpret the result**
Running on 8 nodes gives only a **4.71× speedup** — because 10% sequential code becomes the bottleneck.

**Step 7 — Calculate efficiency**

$$
E = \frac{S(N)}{N} = \frac{4.7058}{8} = 0.5882 \approx 58.82\%
$$

**Step 8 — Interpretation**
Efficiency drops as $N$ increases. For large $N$, most grid nodes sit idle waiting for the sequential part.

---

### 3.2 Analytical Problem 2 — Maximum Theoretical Speedup

**Problem:** What is the **maximum speedup** achievable even if we add infinite grid nodes, when only 95% of the code is parallelizable?

**Step 1 — Take the limit $N \to \infty$**

$$
\lim_{N \to \infty} S(N) = \lim_{N \to \infty} \frac{1}{(1 - p) + \frac{p}{N}}
$$

**Step 2 — As $N \to \infty$, $\frac{p}{N} \to 0$**

$$
S_{\max} = \frac{1}{(1 - p)} = \frac{1}{0.05} = 20
$$

**Step 3 — Conclusion**
**No matter how many grid nodes** are added, the speedup can never exceed **20×**. This is the **fundamental ceiling** of parallelization — the **sequential bottleneck**.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Problem 3 — Grid Resource Utilization Calculation

**Problem:** A grid has **5 clusters** with the following CPU utilizations: $U_1 = 65\%$, $U_2 = 80\%$, $U_3 = 45\%$, $U_4 = 90\%$, $U_5 = 70\%$. Calculate the **average grid utilization** and identify which cluster should receive new load.

**Step 1 — Sum all utilizations**

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{5} U_i = 65 + 80 + 45 + 90 + 70 = 350\%
$$

**Step 2 — Divide by number of clusters**

$$
\bar{U} = \frac{\sum U_i}{n} = \frac{350}{5} = 70\%
$$

**Step 3 — Identify underutilized cluster**
Cluster 3 with **45%** utilization has the most spare capacity → should receive new workload.

**Step 4 — Engineer insight**
Average grid utilization of **70%** is excellent compared to traditional standalone utilization (~10–15%). This is the **economic justification** for Grid Computing.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Problem 4 — Availability Calculation for a Redundant Grid

**Problem:** A traditional standalone server has MTBF = **5000 hours** and MTTR = **25 hours**. A grid replaces it with 3 redundant nodes (each with MTBF = 5000 hours, MTTR = 25 hours) in parallel. Compare availability.

**Step 1 — Traditional availability**

$$
A_{\text{traditional}} = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}} = \frac{5000}{5000 + 25} = \frac{5000}{5025}
$$

$$
A_{\text{traditional}} = 0.9950 = 99.50\%
$$

**Step 2 — Failure rate per node**

$$
\lambda = \frac{1}{\text{MTBF}} = \frac{1}{5000} = 0.0002 \, \text{failures/hour}
$$

**Step 3 — Probability of one node being UP**

$$
P_{\text{up}} = A = 0.9950
$$

**Step 4 — Probability of system failure (all 3 nodes down)**

System fails **only if all 3 nodes fail simultaneously**:

$$
P_{\text{all\_down}} = (1 - P_{\text{up}})^3 = (0.0050)^3 = 1.25 \times 10^{-7}
$$

**Step 5 — System availability**

$$
A_{\text{grid}} = 1 - P_{\text{all\_down}} = 1 - 1.25 \times 10^{-7} \approx 0.999999875
$$

$$
A_{\text{grid}} \approx 99.9999875\% \text{ (six nines)}
$$

**Step 6 — Compare**

$$
\text{Improvement Factor} = \frac{A_{\text{grid}}}{A_{\text{traditional}}} = \frac{0.999999875}{0.995} \approx 1.005
$$

In absolute terms, downtime drops from:
- Traditional: ~ **43.8 hours/year** 
- Grid: ~ **0.01 hours/year (38 seconds/year)**

---

### 3.5 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Problem | Concept Tested | Formula Used | KTU Bloom Level |
|---|---|---|---|
| 3.1 | Speedup & Efficiency | $S = \frac{1}{(1-p) + p/N}$ | Apply |
| 3.2 | Max Speedup Limit | $S_{\max} = \frac{1}{1-p}$ | Analyze |
| 3.3 | Grid Utilization | $\bar{U} = \frac{\sum U_i}{n}$ | Apply |
| 3.4 | Availability of Redundant Grid | $A = 1 - (1-A_{\text{node}})^n$ | Analyze |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Evolution of Computing Paradigms — Timeline Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Traditional Computing\n1970s - 80s\nStandalone PC] --> B[Parallel Computing\n1980s - 90s\nMultiple CPUs in one machine]
    B --> C[Distributed Computing\n1990s\nNetworked independent computers]
    C --> D[Network Computing\n1990s\nThin clients + servers]
    D --> E[Grid Computing\nLate 1990s - 2000s\nCross-organization VO]
    E --> F[Cluster Computing\n2000s\nHomogeneous LAN nodes]
    F --> G[Utility Computing\n2000s\nPay per use model]
    G --> H[Cloud Computing\n2008 - Present\nOn-demand elastic services]
```

---

```mermaid
    end

```

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question 1:** Define Grid Computing. List any two characteristics.  
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper 1]**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):**
Grid Computing is a distributed computing paradigm that enables the **coordinated, secure, and policy-driven sharing of heterogeneous, geographically dispersed resources** (compute, storage, data, instruments) across multiple administrative domains, organized into a **Virtual Organization (VO)**, to deliver high-end computational capability as if they were a single unified resource.

**Two Characteristics (1 mark):**
1. **Resource Sharing Across Organizational Boundaries** — Resources owned by different institutions can be pooled.
2. **Heterogeneity** — Grid integrates diverse hardware, OS, and network resources seamlessly.

---

#### **Question 2:** Mention any three limitations of Traditional Computing.  
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Model Answer (3 × 1 = 3 marks):**
1. **Limited Scalability** — Only vertical scaling possible (upgrading a single machine); cost grows super-linearly.
2. **Single Point of Failure** — Hardware crash leads to complete downtime; no built-in redundancy.
3. **Resource Underutilization** — Average CPU utilization of a standalone PC is only **5–15%**, wasting expensive resources.

*(Alternative accepted limitations: high CAPEX, data silos, no collaboration, geographic lock-in, high TCO.)*

---

### 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — Traditional Computing + Grid

> **(a)** Discuss the major limitations of Traditional Computing that motivated the shift to distributed paradigms like Grid and Cloud. **(7 marks)**  
> **(b)** With a neat diagram, explain the architecture of Grid Computing. Differentiate Grid from Cluster Computing. **(7 marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 1]**

---

#### **Part (a) Model Solution — Limitations of Traditional Computing (7 marks)**

Traditional Computing, where a single standalone machine handles all tasks independently, suffers from several fundamental limitations that prevent it from meeting modern computational demands:

**1. Single Point of Failure (SPOF) — 1 mark**
In a standalone setup, if the CPU, motherboard, or hard disk fails, the entire workflow halts. There is no built-in redundancy. The system's availability is entirely dependent on a single machine's MTBF.

**2. Limited Scalability — 1 mark**
Traditional computing supports only **vertical scaling** — replacing components with higher-capacity ones. This approach has physical and economic limits. Horizontal scaling (adding more machines) is impossible without a distributed architecture.

**3. Resource Underutilization — 1 mark**
Studies indicate that typical desktops and servers operate at only **10–15%** of their CPU capacity on average, with peak loads being rare. This represents massive wastage of expensive hardware investments.

**4. High Capital and Operational Expenditure — 1 mark**
Purchasing high-end workstations, software licenses, cooling, and dedicated IT staff results in a high Total Cost of Ownership (TCO). Organizations often over-provision for peak loads, inflating CAPEX.

**5. Data Silos and Lack of Collaboration — 1 mark**
Data and applications reside on isolated machines, making cross-departmental or cross-organizational collaboration difficult. There is no native mechanism for secure, policy-driven sharing.

**6. Geographic Restrictions — 1 mark**
Resources are accessible only by users physically near the machine, with no native mechanism for remote, distributed access across geographies.

**7. Heterogeneity Incompatibility — 1 mark**
Different OS, hardware, and software stacks across machines prevent seamless resource pooling. Traditional systems cannot natively bridge Windows, Linux, and macOS environments for compute sharing.

---

#### **Part (b) Model Solution — Grid Architecture + Grid vs Cluster (7 marks)**

**Grid Architecture (5 marks):**

The canonical Grid architecture, as defined by Ian Foster, consists of **five layers**:

| Layer | Name | Functions |
|---|---|---|
| **L0 — Fabric** | Physical Resources | Actual compute nodes, storage devices, sensors, instruments |
| **L1 — Connectivity** | Communication & Security | TCP/IP for transport, GSI for authentication, PKI for encryption |
| **L2 — Resource** | Single Resource Management | Job submission (GRAM), monitoring, allocation, accounting |
| **L3 — Collective** | Multi-Resource Coordination | Directory services (MDS), scheduling, brokering, replication |
| **L4 — Application** | User Applications | Scientific/engineering software using grid APIs (e.g., Globus Toolkit) |

*(Insert a clear diagram of the 5-layer stack in your exam booklet.)*

**Grid vs Cluster (2 marks):**

| Parameter | Grid | Cluster |
|---|---|---|
| **Geography** | Geographically distributed | Single location |
| **Hardware** | Heterogeneous (different) | Homogeneous (same) |
| **Ownership** | Multiple admin domains | Single admin domain |
| **Coupling** | Loosely coupled (WAN) | Tightly coupled (LAN) |

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Computing Paradigms + Grid Details

> **(a)** Explain the evolution of computing paradigms from Traditional Computing to Cloud Computing, highlighting the key driver at each stage. **(7 marks)**  
> **(b)** Describe the components and working of the Globus Toolkit. Explain the concept of Virtual Organization with an example. **(7 marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 1]**

---

#### **Part (a) Model Solution — Evolution of Computing Paradigms (7 marks)**

The evolution of computing paradigms is driven by the need to **overcome the limitations of the previous paradigm** and to meet **growing computational demands**.

**1. Traditional Computing (1970s–80s) — 1 mark**
The earliest paradigm where a single standalone machine handled all processing. *Limitation:* No sharing, no scalability, single point of failure.

**2. Parallel Computing (1980s–90s) — 1 mark**
Multiple processors within a single machine worked simultaneously on a problem. *Driver:* Need for higher performance beyond what single CPUs could deliver (SIMD, MIMD architectures).

**3. Distributed Computing (1990s) — 1 mark**
Multiple independent computers connected via a network collaborated on tasks. *Driver:* Need to share resources across geographic distances and combine idle machines.

**4. Network Computing (mid-1990s) — 1 mark**
Thin clients accessed compute resources from powerful central servers. *Driver:* Cost reduction by using cheap terminals and centralizing management.

**5. Grid Computing (Late 1990s–2000s) — 1 mark**
Coordinated sharing of heterogeneous, geographically distributed resources across Virtual Organizations. *Driver:* Scientific problems requiring petaflop-scale compute (e.g., CERN's LHC).

**6. Cluster Computing (2000s) — 0.5 mark**
Homogeneous, tightly-coupled machines in a single location. *Driver:* Need for cost-effective HPC using commodity hardware (e.g., Beowulf clusters).

**7. Utility Computing (Mid-2000s) — 0.5 mark**
Pay-per-use resource consumption model. *Driver:* Need for metered, on-demand access mimicking electricity utilities.

**8. Cloud Computing (2008–Present) — 1 mark**
Massively scalable, virtualized resources delivered as a service over the internet. *Driver:* Need for elastic, on-demand, self-service provisioning with pay-as-you-go pricing (AWS, Azure, GCP).

---

#### **Part (b) Model Solution — Globus Toolkit & Virtual Organization (7 marks)**

**Globus Toolkit Components (4 marks):**
The Globus Toolkit, developed by Argonne National Laboratory, is the most widely used open-source Grid middleware. Its key components are:

1. **GSI (Grid Security Infrastructure)** — Provides authentication via X.509 certificates and single sign-on (SSO) using PKI.
2. **GRAM (Grid Resource Allocation Manager)** — Handles remote job submission, allocation, and monitoring on grid resources.
3. **GridFTP** — A high-performance, secure, parallel data transfer protocol built on FTP, used for moving large datasets.
4. **MDS (Monitoring and Discovery Service)** — A directory service (LDAP-based) that publishes information about available grid resources.

**Working:** A user authenticates via GSI → discovers available resources via MDS → submits a job via GRAM → transfers input/output data via GridFTP → the job executes on a remote cluster.

**Virtual Organization (3 marks):**
A **Virtual Organization (VO)** is a dynamic collection of individuals, institutions, and resources united by a common set of sharing rules. VOs enable **cross-organizational collaboration** without requiring physical merger or unified ownership.

**Example:**
The **LHC Computing Grid (LCG)** is a global VO comprising over **170 institutions across 40+ countries**. Physicists from CERN, MIT, TIFR, and other labs share compute clusters, storage systems, and datasets to analyze particle collision data from the Large Hadron Collider. Members agree on a common access policy, security framework, and resource-sharing model, all coordinated through Globus middleware.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]  
> **Where Students Lose Marks — Common Pitfalls:**

1. **Writing only Grid definition in "Limitations of Traditional" question** — Examiners specifically want the *limitations* discussed in depth (each 1 mark), not just a list. Add engineering impact.

2. **Confusing Grid with Cluster** — A **#1 mistake**. Always remember: Cluster = **same location + same hardware**, Grid = **different locations + different hardware**. Examiners deduct 2 marks instantly for this confusion.

3. **Forgetting Foster's Triad** — When asked to "explain characteristics of Grid," always mention the **3 conditions by Ian Foster**: (i) not centrally controlled, (ii) open standards, (iii) nontrivial QoS. This is a 3-mark easy win.

4. **Skipping the diagram** — For "Explain Grid architecture," a **diagram is COMPULSORY**. Examiners allocate 1–2 marks specifically for the diagram. A text-only answer caps at 80%.

5. **Not mentioning Virtual Organization (VO)** — VO is the **defining concept** of Grid Computing. Any Grid answer without VO is incomplete and will lose at least 2 marks.

6. **Mixing up middleware names** — GRAM is for **job submission**, GridFTP is for **data transfer**, MDS is for **discovery**. Don't interchange them.

7. **Amdahl's Law calculation errors** — When solving numericals, students often forget the $(1-p)$ sequential component. Always show the full denominator explicitly.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Read this before every Module 1 exam**

✅ **Traditional Computing = single isolated machine, dedicated resources, no sharing.**

✅ **Key limitations:** SPOF, vertical scaling only, low utilization (~10%), high TCO, data silos, geographic lock-in, heterogeneity issues.

✅ **Grid Computing = distributed paradigm sharing heterogeneous, geographically dispersed resources across Virtual Organizations using open standards.**

✅ **Foster's Triad (3 conditions):** Not centrally controlled + Open standard protocols + Nontrivial QoS.

✅ **5-Layer Grid Architecture:** Fabric → Connectivity → Resource → Collective → Application.

✅ **Types of Grid:** Compute Grid (CPU), Data Grid (storage), Service Grid (services).

✅ **Grid Middleware = Globus Toolkit.** Components: GSI, GRAM, GridFTP, MDS.

✅ **Virtual Organization (VO) = heart of Grid Computing.** Example: CERN's LHC Computing Grid (LCG).

✅ **Amdahl's Law:** $S(N) = \frac{1}{(1-p) + p/N}$, $S_{\max} = \frac{1}{1-p}$.

✅ **Grid vs Cluster:** Grid = distributed + heterogeneous + multi-org. Cluster = co-located + homogeneous + single org.

✅ **Real-world examples:** SETI@home, Folding@home, CERN LCG, BOINC, TeraGrid/XSEDE.

✅ **Evolution path:** Traditional → Parallel → Distributed → Network → Grid → Cluster → Utility → Cloud.

✅ **Always draw a diagram** for architecture questions — it is mandatory for full marks.

✅ **KTU 2024 CO Mapping:** This topic maps to **CO1** (Understand distributed computing paradigms) and **Bloom Levels L1–L3** (Remember, Understand, Apply).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cluster Computing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECPT525 - CLOUD COMPUTING > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Cluster Computing # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Cluster Computing** is a tightly-coupled distributed system comprising a collection of interconnected stand-alone computers (called **nodes**) that work cooperatively as a **single, unified computing resource**. The nodes are connected through high-speed local area networks (LANs) and coordinated by specialized **cluster middleware** to present a single system image (SSI) to the user. In the context of the KTU PECPT525 syllabus, Cluster Computing is introduced as the **precursor and building block** of modern Cloud Computing — since cloud data centers internally rely on massive physical clusters of commodity hardware to deliver elastic, on-demand services. > [!NOTE] > **Key Term — Single System Image (SSI):** > Despite being physically multiple machines, the cluster appears to the outside world (and to the user) as ONE computer. This illusion of unity is the defining characteristic of a cluster. --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Office Team" Intuition Imagine a busy accounting firm. Instead of hiring one super-genius accountant who can finish 1000 tax files in a month (expensive, single point of failure), the firm hires **20 average accountants** sitting in the same office, sharing a common filing cabinet system, and managed by one **team lead** who distributes files. | Real-World Analogy | Cluster Computing Equivalent | |---|---| | 20 Accountants | 20 Compute Nodes | | Common office / shared cabinets | High-speed LAN / shared storage (SAN/NAS) | | Team Lead (distributes work) | Cluster Middleware / Job Scheduler | | Clients see "one firm" | Single System Image (SSI) | | If one accountant is sick, others cover | **High Availability (HA)** via Failover | | Splitting 1000 files evenly | **Load Balancing** | > [!TIP] > **Memory Hook:** *"Many cheap machines pretending to be one expensive one."* — This is the entire philosophy of cluster computing. --- ## 1.3 Core Metrics and Standards (Highlighted) - **Node:** A single physical or virtual machine that is a member of the cluster. Often commodity x86/x64 hardware. - **Throughput (T):** Measured in **transactions per second (TPS)** or **FLOPS (Floating Point Operations Per Second)**. - **Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF):** The average operational time between two consecutive system failures. For a cluster of *N* identical nodes, effective MTBF ≈ MTBF_node / N. - **High Availability (HA):** Quantified as a percentage — e.g., **99.999% ("Five Nines")** ≈ 5.26 minutes of downtime per year. - **Beowulf Cluster:** A classic class of Linux-based commodity cluster pioneered at NASA (1994). --- ## 1.4 Classification of Clusters (KTU Module 1 Syllabus Highlight) The KTU syllabus specifically identifies **three primary cluster types**, and examiners love asking this: > [!IMPORTANT] > **Three Pillars of Cluster Computing:** > 1. **High-Performance Computing (HPC) Cluster** — optimized for *raw compute speed* (scientific simulations, weather modeling). > 2. **Load-Balancing Cluster** — distributes incoming requests evenly across nodes to maximize *throughput*. > 3. **High-Availability (HA) Cluster** — designed to provide *uninterrupted service* through redundancy and failover. A fourth modern class, **Storage Cluster**, is also often referenced for distributed file systems (e.g., HDFS). # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Architecture — Layer-by-Layer Breakdown A cluster is **not** just a bunch of computers plugged into a switch. The KTU 2024 syllabus expects students to know its layered architecture. From top (user) to bottom (hardware): ### Layer 1: Application / Job Layer - The user submits a job, a web request, or a scientific computation. - Job is broken down into **tasks** (parallelism granularity). ### Layer 2: Cluster Middleware (The Brain) This is the **most important layer** for examination purposes. It is the software "glue" that turns many machines into one. Sub-components: - **Job Scheduler / Resource Manager:** Decides *which node runs which task*. Examples: SLURM, Torque, PBS, Kubernetes Scheduler. - **Load Balancer:** Distributes incoming traffic to avoid hot-spots. Examples: HAProxy, NGINX, LVS (Linux Virtual Server). - **Failover Manager:** Detects node failure and reassigns its workload. Heartbeat signals are exchanged between nodes. Examples: Pacemaker, Corosync, Keepalived. - **Distributed Lock Manager (DLM):** Ensures two nodes don't write to the same data simultaneously. ### Layer 3: Operating System & Network Layer - Usually a **lightweight Linux distribution** (CentOS, Rocky Linux, Ubuntu Server) for HPC. - **Private high-speed interconnect**: Gigabit Ethernet, 10 GbE, InfiniBand, or Omni-Path. ### Layer 4: Hardware Layer - Homogeneous **commodity nodes** (HPC) or heterogeneous (load-balancing). - Shared storage: **SAN (Storage Area Network)**, **NAS (Network Attached Storage)**, or distributed file systems (Lustre, GlusterFS). --- ## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Clustering — Design Drivers | Design Driver | Explanation | Example Use-Case | |---|---|---| | **Scalability (Horizontal)** | Add more nodes → linear performance gain | Adding 10 web servers behind a load balancer | | **High Availability** | No single point of failure; redundant nodes | Banking systems, telecom switches | | **Cost Efficiency** | Commodity hardware >> proprietary mainframes | Google's original cheap-PC datacenters | | **Throughput Maximization** | Parallel execution of independent tasks | Batch image processing | | **Manageability** | Centralized administration of many machines | University research labs | --- ## 2.3 The "How" — Critical Operational Mechanisms ### A. Failover Mechanism (HA) - A **heartbeat** packet is sent every Δt seconds between paired nodes. - If **K consecutive heartbeats are missed**, the standby node declares the primary dead and takes over the **Virtual IP (VIP)**. - Typical K = 3, Δt = 1s → failure detection time ≈ 3 seconds. ### B. Load Balancing Distribution Policies 1. **Round-Robin (RR):** Request 1 → Node A, Request 2 → Node B, ... cyclic. 2. **Weighted Round-Robin (WRR):** Powerful nodes get higher weight. 3. **Least-Connections:** New request goes to the node currently handling the fewest active connections. 4. **IP-Hash:** Same client IP always maps to the same node (used for session persistence). ### C. Job Scheduling (HPC) - **FCFS (First-Come, First-Served):** Simple but causes head-of-line blocking. - **Backfilling:** Small jobs can jump ahead of large waiting jobs IF they don't delay the large job's estimated start time. Boosts utilization by up to **20–30%**. --- ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Yield Equations for Cluster Computing Problems** | Concept | Formula / Definition | Variables | Unit | |---|---|---|---| | Cluster Speedup (Amdahl's Law) | $S = \dfrac{1}{(1 - p) + \dfrac{p}{N}}$ | $p$ = parallelizable fraction, $N$ = number of nodes | dimensionless | | Cluster Efficiency | $E = \dfrac{S}{N} = \dfrac{1}{N(1-p) + p}$ | $S$ = speedup, $N$ = nodes | dimensionless (0 to 1) | | Effective MTBF (independent failures) | $\text{MTBF}_{\text{cluster}} = \dfrac{\text{MTBF}_{\text{node}}}{N}$ | $N$ = number of nodes | hours | | Availability | $A = \dfrac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$ | MTTR = Mean Time To Repair | dimensionless (e.g., 0.99999) | | Downtime per Year | $D = (1 - A) \times 365 \times 24 \times 60$ | $A$ = availability fraction | minutes/year | | Heartbeat Failure Detection | $T_{\text{detect}} = K \times \Delta t$ | $K$ = missed-heartbeat threshold, $\Delta t$ = heartbeat interval | seconds | | Aggregate FLOPS | $F_{\text{agg}} = N \times F_{\text{node}} \times \eta$ | $\eta$ = parallel efficiency (0 to 1) | FLOPS | | Round-Robin Index | $i_{k} = (i_{k-1} + 1) \bmod N$ | $i$ = node index, $N$ = node count | integer index | > [!NOTE] > The pipe symbol restriction: Use $\vert$ or $\mid$ in equations. Example: $N \mid 1$ is written as `N \mid 1` in LaTeX, never with vertical bars inside markdown tables. --- ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Weather Forecasting (e.g., IBM GRAF):** Runs on HPC clusters ingesting terabytes of satellite data every hour. - **CERN Large Hadron Collider:** Generates ~1 PB/second; filtered by worldwide compute clusters (the Grid). - **E-commerce (Amazon, Flipkart):** Load-balancing clusters handle 50,000+ requests/second during festive sales. - **Telecom 5G Core:** HA clusters ensure < 5ms latency for millions of concurrent users. - **Genomic Sequencing:** HPC clusters reduce DNA alignment time from years to hours (BWA, BLAST tools). # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation 1 — Amdahl's Law for Cluster Speedup Amdahl's Law is the **single most important equation** in parallel/cluster computing. It quantifies the *theoretical maximum speedup* achievable by parallelizing a task across *N* nodes. ### Step-by-Step Derivation Let the total execution time of a serial program on one node be $T_1$. Suppose a fraction $p$ of this program is **parallelizable** and the remaining fraction $(1 - p)$ is strictly **serial** (e.g., input/output, sequential setup). **Step 1:** Decompose total time on a single node. The total time on a single node is the sum of the parallel portion and the serial portion. $$ T_1 = T_{\text{serial}} + T_{\text{parallel}} $$ Where: - $T_{\text{serial}} = (1 - p) \cdot T_1$ - $T_{\text{parallel}} = p \cdot T_1$ **Step 2:** Normalize by setting $T_1 = 1$ (i.e., measure time in units of the original execution time). This is a standard simplification that makes the math cleaner without losing generality. $$ T_1 = 1 $$ So: - Serial portion time = $(1 - p)$ - Parallel portion time = $p$ **Step 3:** Apply $N$ nodes to the parallel portion only. The serial portion CANNOT be parallelized (this is the key insight). The parallel portion gets divided among $N$ nodes. $$ T_N = (1 - p) + \dfrac{p}{N} $$ **Conversion logic:** We keep the serial fraction unchanged because no number of additional nodes can make inherently sequential code run faster. We divide the parallel fraction by $N$ because that work is now shared equally across $N$ workers (assuming perfect load balance and no communication overhead). **Step 4:** Compute speedup as the ratio of original time to parallel time. $$ S(N) = \dfrac{T_1}{T_N} = \dfrac{1}{(1 - p) + \dfrac{p}{N}} $$ **Step 5:** Compute efficiency — the fraction of theoretical node capacity actually utilized. $$ E(N) = \dfrac{S(N)}{N} = \dfrac{1}{N(1 - p) + p} $$ **Step 6:** Take the asymptotic limit as $N \to \infty$. As the number of nodes grows very large, the parallel term $\frac{p}{N} \to 0$. The speedup is then **bounded** by the serial bottleneck: $$ \lim_{N \to \infty} S(N) = \dfrac{1}{1 - p} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Interpretation:** Even with INFINITE nodes, the maximum speedup is capped at $\frac{1}{1-p}$. If 5% of your code is serial, the best you can ever achieve is a **20× speedup**, no matter how many nodes you buy. This is why KTU examiners love asking: *"Why can't clusters achieve linear speedup?"* --- ### Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style Problem) > **Problem:** A weather simulation has 80% parallelizable code ($p = 0.8$) and 20% serial code. The cluster has 16 nodes. Find: (i) Speedup, (ii) Efficiency, (iii) Maximum theoretical speedup with infinite nodes. **Solution (i) — Speedup:** $$ S(16) = \dfrac{1}{(1 - 0.8) + \dfrac{0.8}{16}} = \dfrac{1}{0.2 + 0.05} = \dfrac{1}{0.25} = 4 $$ **Solution (ii) — Efficiency:** $$ E(16) = \dfrac{4}{16} = 0.25 = 25\% $$ **Solution (iii) — Infinite-node limit:** $$ S_{\max} = \dfrac{1}{1 - 0.8} = \dfrac{1}{0.2} = 5 $$ > [!NOTE] > Notice that with 16 nodes, we get only 4× speedup (vs. the ideal 16×). Increasing to 1,000,000 nodes would only push speedup from 4 to 5 — a brutal illustration of Amdahl's Law. --- ## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation 2 — Availability & Downtime ### Step-by-Step Derivation **Step 1:** Define the operational cycle. Every system alternates between two states: **UP** (working) and **DOWN** (failed/repairing). The average durations are: - $\text{MTBF}$ = Mean Time Between Failures (average UP time) - $\text{MTTR}$ = Mean Time To Repair (average DOWN time) **Step 2:** The total cycle time of one failure-recovery round is the sum. $$ T_{\text{cycle}} = \text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR} $$ **Step 3:** Availability is the fraction of time the system is UP. $$ A = \dfrac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}} $$ **Step 4:** Rearrange to express the unavailability (downtime fraction). $$ 1 - A = \dfrac{\text{MTTR}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}} \approx \dfrac{\text{MTTR}}{\text{MTBF}} \;\; \text{(since MTBF >> MTTR)} $$ **Step 5:** Convert to minutes per year. Total minutes in a year = $365 \times 24 \times 60 = 525{,}600$ minutes. $$ D_{\text{min/year}} = (1 - A) \times 525{,}600 $$ **Conversion logic:** Multiply the downtime *fraction* by the total available *minutes* in a year to obtain the actual *absolute* downtime. ### Worked Example > **Problem:** A cluster node has $\text{MTBF} = 5000$ hours and $\text{MTTR} = 1$ hour. Find: (i) Availability, (ii) Downtime per year in minutes. **Solution (i):** $$ A = \dfrac{5000}{5000 + 1} = \dfrac{5000}{5001} \approx 0.99980 = 99.980\% $$ **Solution (ii):** $$ D = (1 - 0.99980) \times 525{,}600 = 0.00020 \times 525{,}600 \approx 105.1 \text{ minutes/year} $$ > [!TIP] > **The "Five Nines" benchmark:** $A = 0.99999$ implies $D \approx 5.26$ minutes/year. Achieving this requires redundant failover clusters (HA), since a single node cannot realistically reach this level. --- ## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Cluster vs. Grid vs. Cloud | Parameter | Cluster Computing | Grid Computing | Cloud Computing | |---|---|---|---| | **Coupling** | Tight (LAN) | Loose (WAN/Internet) | Virtualized, elastic | | **Node Ownership** | Single organization | Multiple federated orgs | Public/Private provider | | **Location** | Same room/building | Geographically distributed | Datacenters worldwide | | **Middleware** | Cluster middleware (e.g., SLURM) | Globus Toolkit, gLite | Hypervisor + Orchestrator (K8s) | | **Resource Type** | Homogeneous | Heterogeneous | Virtualized, abstracted | | **Primary Goal** | High performance / HA | Resource sharing across orgs | On-demand, pay-per-use elasticity | | **Example** | Beowulf, IBM Spectrum | SETI@home, LHC Grid | AWS EC2, Azure VMs | | **Network** | High-speed LAN (1–100 Gbps) | Public Internet (variable) | SDN-managed datacenter fabric | > [!WARNING] > **Common Student Mistake:** Students frequently write *"Grid and Cloud are the same."* They are NOT. A grid federates *existing* resources from multiple organizations (no virtualization central to its mission), whereas a cloud *creates virtual* resources on demand via virtualization. Clusters are the **physical substrate** on which both grids and clouds are often built. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 High-Level Cluster Architecture (Mermaid) ```mermaid flowchart TB client[Client Users] lb[Load Balancer
HAProxy LVS] master[Master Node
Job Scheduler SLURM] node1[Compute Node 1] node2[Compute Node 2] node3[Compute Node 3] node4[Compute Node N] san[Shared Storage
SAN or NAS] mon[Monitoring
Ganglia Nagios] client --> lb lb --> master master --> node1 master --> node2 master --> node3 master --> node4 node1 -.shared data.-> san node2 -.shared data.-> san node3 -.shared data.-> san node4 -.shared data.-> san master -.heartbeat.-> mon node1 -.heartbeat.-> mon node2 -.heartbeat.-> mon node3 -.heartbeat.-> mon node4 -.heartbeat.-> mon ``` --- ## 4.2 High-Availability Failover Sequence (Mermaid) ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant Client participant VIP as Virtual IP participant Pri as Primary Node participant Sto as Standby Node Note over Pri,Sto: Heartbeats every 1 second Pri->>Sto: HB_OK Pri->>Sto: HB_OK Pri->>Sto: HB_OK Note over Pri: PRIMARY CRASHES Sto-->>Sto: 3 missed HBs detected Sto->>VIP: Acquire Virtual IP Sto->>Sto: Promote to PRIMARY Client->>VIP: Send Request VIP->>Sto: Forward to new Primary Sto-->>Client: Service Response ``` --- ## 4.3 Cluster Classification Tree (Mermaid) ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Cluster Computing] A --> B[High Performance HPC] A --> C[Load Balancing LB] A --> D[High Availability HA] A --> E[Storage] B --> B1[Scientific Research] B --> B2[Weather Modeling] B --> B3[Genomic Analysis] C --> C1[Web Server Farms] C --> C2[API Gateways] C --> C3[CDN Edge Nodes] D --> D1[Database Replication] D --> D2[Telecom Core] D --> D3[Banking Systems] E --> E1[HDFS Hadoop] E --> E2[Lustre FS] E --> E3[Ceph Object Store] ``` --- ## 4.4 Functional Request Flow — Load Balancer Dispatch (Mermaid) ```mermaid flowchart LR req1[Request 1] --> alg{Round Robin Algorithm} req2[Request 2] --> alg req3[Request 3] --> alg req4[Request 4] --> alg alg --> n1[Node A] alg --> n2[Node B] alg --> n3[Node C] alg --> n4[Node D] n1 --> resp1[Response] n2 --> resp2[Response] n3 --> resp3[Response] n4 --> resp4[Response] ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** **Differentiate between tightly-coupled and loosely-coupled distributed systems. Give one example of each.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Parameter | Tightly-Coupled (Cluster) | Loosely-Coupled (Grid) | |---|---|---| | Network | High-speed LAN (1–100 Gbps) | Public Internet / WAN | | Latency | Very low (microseconds) | High (milliseconds to seconds) | | Bandwidth | Dedicated, high | Shared, variable | | Coupling | Strong (shared memory or fast interconnect) | Weak (message passing over IP) | | Example | Beowulf cluster, IBM HPC | SETI@home, CERN Worldwide LHC Grid | *(Award 1.5 marks for the difference table, 1 mark for one example, 0.5 mark for naming the system class clearly.)* --- ### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — June 2024]** **List any three advantages of cluster computing over a single high-end server.** **Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark per advantage):** 1. **Scalability:** A cluster can scale horizontally by simply adding more nodes, while a single server has a hard physical and financial ceiling. 2. **Cost-effectiveness:** Commodity hardware (e.g., off-the-shelf x86 servers) is significantly cheaper per unit of compute than a high-end mainframe or SMP server. 3. **High Availability:** Redundant nodes provide failover capability, ensuring that failure of one node does not crash the entire service — a single server is a single point of failure. 4. *(Bonus 4th point: Higher aggregate throughput via parallel processing of independent tasks.)* --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice) > As per the **KTU 2024 ESE pattern**, Part B Module 1 questions carry 14 marks and present an **internal choice**. Below are two alternatives. --- ### ❓ Question A (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Module 1, Choice-1]* **(a)** Explain the architecture of a cluster computing system with a neat diagram. Discuss the role of cluster middleware in detail. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A parallel application has 90% parallelizable code and 10% serial code. It is executed on a cluster of 8 nodes. Calculate the speedup, efficiency, and the maximum theoretical speedup with infinite nodes using Amdahl's Law. Comment on the result. **(7 marks)** #### ✅ Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks] **Architecture (3 marks):** A cluster consists of the following layers (top to bottom): 1. **Client / User Layer** — submits jobs or requests. 2. **Cluster Middleware Layer** — the brain. Contains the **Job Scheduler**, **Load Balancer**, **Failover Manager**, and **Distributed Lock Manager**. 3. **Operating System Layer** — typically Linux, optimized for parallel I/O. 4. **Interconnect Layer** — high-speed LAN (Gigabit Ethernet, 10 GbE, InfiniBand). 5. **Hardware / Node Layer** — commodity x86 nodes, often with shared storage (SAN/NAS). **Role of Cluster Middleware (4 marks — 1 per sub-point):** - **Job Scheduling:** Assigns incoming tasks to nodes optimally (FCFS, backfilling). - **Load Balancing:** Distributes requests evenly so no single node becomes a bottleneck. - **Failover Management:** Monitors heartbeats; on failure, reassigns workload to a healthy node, presenting a **Virtual IP (VIP)** that migrates automatically. - **Resource Management:** Maintains a global view of node availability, memory, and CPU load via distributed state. *(Award 1 mark for the diagram, 2 marks for the 4-layer description, 4 marks for the 4 middleware roles.)* #### ✅ Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks] **Given:** $p = 0.9$, $N = 8$. **Step 1 — Apply Amdahl's Speedup Formula:** $$ S(8) = \dfrac{1}{(1 - 0.9) + \dfrac{0.9}{8}} = \dfrac{1}{0.1 + 0.1125} = \dfrac{1}{0.2125} \approx 4.71 $$ **Step 2 — Compute Efficiency:** $$ E(8) = \dfrac{S}{N} = \dfrac{4.71}{8} \approx 0.589 = 58.9\% $$ **Step 3 — Maximum Theoretical Speedup (N → ∞):** $$ S_{\max} = \dfrac{1}{1 - 0.9} = \dfrac{1}{0.1} = 10 $$ **Comment (1 mark):** Even with 8 nodes, we achieve only ~4.7× speedup (vs. ideal 8×), and efficiency drops to ~59%. The serial 10% bottleneck caps the maximum achievable speedup at 10× — adding more nodes beyond a point yields diminishing returns. This justifies the KTU Module 1 emphasis on *parallel code optimization*, not just throwing more hardware at the problem. --- ### ❓ Question B (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Module 1, Choice-2]* **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the three main types of clusters: HPC, Load-Balancing, and High-Availability. State one real-world use case for each. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A cluster node has an MTBF of 8000 hours and MTTR of 2 hours. Calculate its availability and the expected downtime per year. If we deploy 10 such nodes independently, what is the cluster's effective MTBF, and what inference can be drawn about cluster reliability? **(7 marks)** #### ✅ Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks] **Three Cluster Types (2 marks each, 1 mark for diagram):** 1. **High-Performance Computing (HPC) Cluster:** - Goal: maximize raw compute throughput for a single large job. - Architecture: tightly-coupled nodes with low-latency interconnect (InfiniBand) and parallel file system (Lustre). - Use case: **Weather forecasting (e.g., IMD, IBM GRAF)** running WRF or COSMO models. 2. **Load-Balancing Cluster:** - Goal: distribute many independent client requests evenly. - Architecture: front-end load balancer (HAProxy/LVS) routes traffic to a pool of identical backend nodes. - Use case: **Amazon.com web servers** handling 50,000+ requests/second. 3. **High-Availability (HA) Cluster:** - Goal: ensure zero or near-zero downtime via redundancy. - Architecture: primary + standby node pair, sharing a **Virtual IP**, with heartbeat monitoring and automatic failover. - Use case: **Banking core systems (e.g., TCS BaNCS)** needing 99.999% uptime. #### ✅ Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks] **Step 1 — Availability of a single node:** $$ A = \dfrac{8000}{8000 + 2} = \dfrac{8000}{8002} \approx 0.99975 = 99.975\% $$ **Step 2 — Downtime per year:** $$ D = (1 - 0.99975) \times 525{,}600 = 0.00025 \times 525{,}600 = 131.4 \text{ minutes/year} \approx 2.19 \text{ hours/year} $$ **Step 3 — Effective MTBF for 10 independent nodes:** $$ \text{MTBF}_{\text{cluster}} = \dfrac{8000}{10} = 800 \text{ hours} \approx 33.3 \text{ days} $$ **Inference (1 mark):** Paradoxically, simply adding more nodes **decreases** the cluster's MTBF because the probability of *at least one* node failing rises with N. This is why pure HA clusters use **redundancy (N+1, 2N configurations) and shared-nothing designs** — more nodes alone do not make the system more reliable; only *redundancy* and *failover* do. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Confusing Cluster with Grid with Cloud** (loses 2–3 marks instantly). Memorize the comparison table. > 2. **Forgetting to draw the diagram in long answers.** A neat block diagram is worth 1–2 marks by itself. > 3. **In Amdahl's Law problems, NOT converting to the right unit.** Always confirm whether the problem asks for speedup (dimensionless) or time (seconds/hours). > 4. **Ignoring the "comment" / "inference" part.** KTU awards marks for analytical reasoning, not just arithmetic. > 5. **Stating "Cloud is the same as Cluster"** — this is a classic wrong answer that examiners flag. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] > **Last-Minute Revision Checklist for Cluster Computing** - ✅ **Definition:** Collection of interconnected stand-alone computers working as a **single unified resource** with a **Single System Image (SSI)**. - ✅ **Three types:** **HPC** (speed), **Load-Balancing** (throughput), **HA** (uptime). - ✅ **Beowulf Cluster** = classic Linux-based HPC cluster (NASA, 1994). - ✅ **Cluster Middleware** = Job Scheduler + Load Balancer + Failover Manager + DLM. - ✅ **Heartbeat mechanism** = primary–standby health check; 3 missed HBs (typical) trigger failover. - ✅ **Amdahl's Law:** $S = \dfrac{1}{(1-p) + \dfrac{p}{N}}$ and $S_{\max} = \dfrac{1}{1-p}$ as $N \to \infty$. - ✅ **Efficiency:** $E = \dfrac{S}{N}$; drops as $N$ grows unless $p \to 1$. - ✅ **Availability:** $A = \dfrac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$; "Five Nines" = 99.999% ≈ 5.26 min downtime/year. - ✅ **Cluster MTBF paradox:** $\text{MTBF}_{\text{cluster}} = \dfrac{\text{MTBF}_{\text{node}}}{N}$ — more nodes = lower MTBF; hence **redundancy** is mandatory for reliability. - ✅ **Differentiate** Cluster (LAN, tight) vs Grid (WAN, federated) vs Cloud (virtualized, elastic). - ✅ **Load-balancing algorithms:** Round-Robin, Weighted-RR, Least-Connections, IP-Hash. - ✅ **Key examples to memorize:** SLURM, HAProxy, Pacemaker, Beowulf, NAS, SAN, InfiniBand, Lustre. - ✅ **Real-world anchors:** Google search (cluster), SETI@home (grid), AWS EC2 (cloud). - ✅ **Connection to Cloud Computing:** Cloud data centers are essentially **virtualized, geographically-distributed mega-clusters** — the reason Module 1 covers this before introducing IaaS/PaaS/SaaS.

topics.title: Distributed Computing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT525 - CLOUD COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Distributed Computing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Distributed Computing** is a computing paradigm in which a system is composed of multiple autonomous computers (nodes) that communicate and coordinate their actions by passing messages over a network to achieve a common goal. The components interact with one another in order to achieve a unified, coherent computation, while appearing to the end user as a single, integrated computing facility.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Distributed computing refers to a system whose components are located on different networked computers, which communicate and coordinate their actions by passing messages to one another. The interconnected computers share resources and work collaboratively toward a common objective, forming the foundational layer upon which **Cloud Computing** architectures are built.

**Key Term Highlights (KTU High-Yield Vocabulary):**
- **Node (Host):** An individual autonomous computer participating in the distributed system.
- **Middleware:** The software layer that sits between the operating system and the application, providing abstraction and supporting heterogeneity, transparency, and communication.
- **Transparency:** The property by which the distributed system presents itself to users and applications as a single, coherent entity rather than as a collection of independent components.
- **Concurrency:** Multiple computations executing simultaneously across different nodes.
- **Fault Tolerance:** The ability of the system to continue functioning in the event of partial hardware or software failures.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Kitchen Brigade" Model**
> Imagine a large restaurant kitchen preparing a complex multi-course meal for hundreds of guests:
> - Each **chef** is a *node* (autonomous processor).
> - The **pass (counter)** is the *network* through which tickets (messages) are exchanged.
> - The **Head Chef (Expediter)** acts as the *coordinator/middleware*, ensuring every station works in sync.
> - The **guest** sees only the finished meal — they don't know, nor care, that 8 stations worked in parallel to make it. This is **transparency**.
> - If one chef falls ill, the system still produces food (reassigned tasks). This is **fault tolerance**.

Just as a well-run kitchen divides labor to serve hundreds efficiently, a distributed system divides computation across many machines to deliver scalable, reliable service.

### 1.3 Why Distributed Computing Matters (KTU Board Perspective)

> [!NOTE]
> Distributed Computing is the **theoretical foundation** of Cloud Computing. The KTU 2024 syllabus deliberately places this topic first in Module 1 to ensure students understand *why* cloud platforms (AWS, Azure, GCP) exist — they are essentially **commercial, large-scale distributed systems** with virtualized resources offered as on-demand services.

**Core Motivations (Examinable in 3-mark and 14-mark questions):**
1. **Scalability** — Easily add more nodes to handle increasing load.
2. **Reliability/Availability** — Failure of one node does not halt the entire system.
3. **Resource Sharing** — Expensive peripherals (printers, storage, databases) can be shared.
4. **Performance** — Parallelism across nodes yields faster execution.
5. **Openness & Extensibility** — New components can be added without disrupting existing services.
6. **Economics** — Clusters of commodity hardware are cheaper than a single supercomputer.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Characteristics of a Distributed System (Tanenbaum Model)

The KTU 2024 syllabus adopts the **Andrew Tanenbaum (Maarten van Steen)** definition. A true distributed system exhibits the following key characteristics:

1. **Concurrency of Components** — Multiple operations execute at the same time across different nodes.
2. **No Global Clock** — There is no single, perfectly synchronized clock; nodes coordinate using logical timestamps (Lamport clocks).
3. **Independent Failures** — Each node can fail independently without immediately affecting the others.
4. **Heterogeneity** — Nodes may run different hardware, OS, programming languages, and network protocols.
5. **Openness** — Systems are built using standardized protocols and interfaces (e.g., TCP/IP, HTTP, gRPC).
6. **Transparency** — Hides the distributed nature from users (covered in detail below).

### 2.2 The Eight Forms of Transparency (KTU Frequently Tested)

| # | Transparency | Meaning | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Access** | Hide differences in data representation and resource access. | Same API across Windows & Linux servers. |
| 2 | **Location** | Hide where a resource physically resides. | URL works regardless of server geography. |
| 3 | **Migration** | Hide that a resource may move to another location. | VM live-migration in VMware. |
| 4 | **Relocation** | Hide that a resource may be moved while in use. | Mobile cloud apps moving between data centers. |
| 5 | **Replication** | Hide that a resource is replicated across nodes. | Dropbox syncs files transparently. |
| 6 | **Concurrency** | Hide that a resource is shared by multiple users. | Google Docs concurrent editing. |
| 7 | **Failure** | Hide the failure and recovery of a resource. | DNS failover, redundant web servers. |
| 8 | **Persistence** | Hide whether a resource is in memory or on disk. | Object storage abstraction (S3). |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** The examiner frequently asks: *"Differentiate between Location Transparency and Migration Transparency."* Memorize the table above — it is **direct 7-mark material** in Part B.

### 2.3 Architectural Models of Distributed Computing

There are **four classical architectural models** that the KTU 2024 syllabus emphasizes:

#### A. Client–Server Model
- **Nodes** are classified as **Clients** (request services) or **Servers** (provide services).
- Server is centralized; clients are distributed.
- Examples: Web servers, FTP, Email.

#### B. Peer-to-Peer (P2P) Model
- All nodes are **equal peers**; each can act as both client and server.
- Highly scalable, fault-tolerant, no single point of failure.
- Examples: BitTorrent, blockchain, Skype (older versions).

#### C. Three-Tier / Multi-Tier Model
- Layers: **Presentation Tier** (UI) → **Application/Logic Tier** → **Data Tier** (Database).
- Most web-based distributed systems follow this.

#### D. Service-Oriented Architecture (SOA) / Microservices
- Application is decomposed into **loosely coupled services** that communicate over a network (often via REST/SOAP/gRPC).
- Foundation of modern **cloud-native** applications.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Expression | Description | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Speedup (Amdahl's Law)** | $S = \dfrac{1}{(1 - P) + \dfrac{P}{N}}$ | Maximum speedup with parallel fraction $P$ and $N$ processors. | Dimensionless ratio |
| **Efficiency** | $E = \dfrac{S}{N}$ | Fraction of time processors are doing useful work. | Dimensionless (0–1) |
| **Scalability (ideal)** | $S = N$ | Linear speedup with N processors. | Dimensionless |
| **Communication Latency** | $L = T_{prop} + T_{trans} + T_{queue}$ | Total time to send a message. | Seconds (s) |
| **Throughput** | $X = \dfrac{N_{req}}{T_{total}}$ | Number of requests completed per unit time. | Requests/sec |
| **Availability** | $A = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$ | Uptime ratio; MTBF = Mean Time Between Failures, MTTR = Mean Time To Repair. | Dimensionless (0–1) |
| **MIPS Aggregate** | $MIPS_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} MIPS_i$ | Total processing power of N nodes. | Million Instructions/sec |
| **Bandwidth–Delay Product** | $BDP = B \times RTT$ | Maximum data in-flight on a network link of bandwidth $B$ and round-trip time $RTT$. | Bits |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examination Focus:** Amdahl's Law and the Availability formula are the **two most-tested equations** for distributed computing problems. Always substitute values into $S$ and $E$ and explicitly compute.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **High-Performance Computing (HPC):** Weather modeling, molecular dynamics.
- **Big Data Processing:** Hadoop, Apache Spark — distributed MapReduce across clusters.
- **Telecommunications:** 5G core networks, VoIP.
- **Banking & Financial Systems:** Distributed ledgers, transaction processing.
- **E-commerce:** Amazon's distributed order processing across multiple data centers.
- **Healthcare:** Distributed PACS (Picture Archiving and Communication Systems) for radiology.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Amdahl's Law (Gustafson's Insight)

**Amdahl's Law** is a foundational formula in distributed computing that quantifies the theoretical maximum speedup achievable by parallelizing a computation.

**Statement:** The speedup of a program using multiple processors in parallel computing is limited by the sequential fraction of the program.

**Step-by-Step Derivation:**

Let:
- $T$ = Total execution time of the program on a single processor.
- $P$ = Proportion of the program that **can be parallelized** (decimal, e.g., 0.8 for 80%).
- $(1 - P)$ = Proportion that **must remain sequential**.

On a **single processor**, the execution time is $T$.

When we use $N$ processors in parallel, the parallel portion runs in time $\frac{P \cdot T}{N}$, while the sequential portion remains $(1 - P) \cdot T$.

**Step 1: Express the new total execution time $T_N$ with $N$ processors:**

$$
T_N = (1 - P) \cdot T + \dfrac{P \cdot T}{N}
$$

**Step 2: Factor out $T$:**

$$
T_N = T \left[ (1 - P) + \dfrac{P}{N} \right]
$$

**Step 3: Speedup $S$ is defined as the ratio of original time to new time:**

$$
S = \dfrac{T}{T_N}
$$

**Step 4: Substitute $T_N$:**

$$
S = \dfrac{T}{T \left[ (1 - P) + \dfrac{P}{N} \right]}
$$

**Step 5: Simplify (the $T$ cancels out):**

$$
S = \dfrac{1}{(1 - P) + \dfrac{P}{N}}
$$

This is the **final form of Amdahl's Law**.

**Step 6: Limiting Case Analysis (High-Yield for KTU):**

- As $N \to \infty$, the term $\frac{P}{N} \to 0$, so:

$$
\lim_{N \to \infty} S = \dfrac{1}{1 - P}
$$

- This means even with **infinite processors**, the speedup is bounded by the sequential fraction.
- Example: If $P = 0.9$ (90% parallel), maximum speedup = $\frac{1}{0.1} = 10\times$.

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Problem (KTU 14-Mark Style)

**Problem:** A distributed image-processing workload has 85% parallelizable code. The system uses 16 processors. Calculate: (i) Speedup, (ii) Efficiency, (iii) Maximum possible speedup with infinite processors.

**Given:**
- $P = 0.85$
- $N = 16$

**Solution:**

**(i) Speedup using Amdahl's Law:**

$$
S = \dfrac{1}{(1 - 0.85) + \dfrac{0.85}{16}}
$$

$$
S = \dfrac{1}{0.15 + 0.053125}
$$

$$
S = \dfrac{1}{0.203125}
$$

$$
S \approx 4.923
$$

**(ii) Efficiency:**

$$
E = \dfrac{S}{N} = \dfrac{4.923}{16} \approx 0.3077
$$

$$
E \approx 30.77\%
$$

**(iii) Maximum Speedup ($N \to \infty$):**

$$
S_{max} = \dfrac{1}{1 - 0.85} = \dfrac{1}{0.15} \approx 6.667
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** Even adding 1,000 more processors beyond $N=16$ will *not* exceed $6.667\times$ speedup. The **sequential 15% bottleneck** dominates performance. This is why distributed system designers obsess over minimizing sequential code paths.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Pros vs. Cons of Distributed Computing

| Aspect | Advantages | Disadvantages / Challenges |
|---|---|---|
| **Performance** | Parallelism → faster execution. | Communication overhead can dominate. |
| **Reliability** | High availability via replication. | Complex consensus algorithms (Paxos, Raft) needed. |
| **Scalability** | Add nodes to scale horizontally. | Bottlenecks in shared resources (DB, network). |
| **Cost** | Commodity hardware is affordable. | Maintenance and skilled workforce cost. |
| **Sharing** | Resource pooling across users. | Security & privacy of shared resources. |
| **Flexibility** | Heterogeneous systems interoperate. | Heterogeneity increases complexity. |
| **Openness** | Standardized interfaces. | Standardization is difficult to enforce. |
| **Concurrency** | Multiple users served simultaneously. | Race conditions, deadlocks, consistency issues. |

### 3.4 CAP Theorem (KTU Module 1 High-Priority)

The **CAP Theorem**, formulated by **Eric Brewer (2000)**, states that a distributed data store can simultaneously guarantee **only two** of the following three properties:

- **C — Consistency:** Every read receives the most recent write or an error.
- **A — Availability:** Every request receives a response (non-error), without guarantee of most recent data.
- **P — Partition Tolerance:** The system continues to operate despite arbitrary message loss between nodes.

$$
\text{CAP} \Rightarrow \text{Choose at most 2 of \{C, A, P\}}
$$

| System Type | Chooses | Examples |
|---|---|---|
| **CA (no partition tolerance)** | Consistency + Availability | Traditional RDBMS (single-node) |
| **CP (no availability)** | Consistency + Partition tolerance | HBase, MongoDB (with strong config), Redis |
| **AP (no consistency)** | Availability + Partition tolerance | Cassandra, DynamoDB, CouchDB |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 High-Level Architecture of a Distributed System

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph UserLayer["USER LAYER - Clients and Devices"]
        client1["Client Node 1"]
        client2["Client Node 2"]
        client3["Client Node 3"]
        mobileApp["Mobile Client"]
    end

    subgraph NetworkLayer["NETWORK LAYER - TCP/IP Internet"]
        router1["Router Gateway"]
        loadbal["Load Balancer"]
    end

    subgraph MiddlewareLayer["MIDDLEWARE LAYER - Coordination"]
        coord["Coordinator / Name Service"]
        sec["Security and Auth Module"]
    end

    subgraph ServerLayer["SERVER LAYER - Compute Nodes"]
        nodeA["Server Node A - CPU 1"]
        nodeB["Server Node B - CPU 2"]
        nodeC["Server Node C - CPU 3"]
        nodeD["Server Node D - CPU 4"]
    end

    subgraph StorageLayer["STORAGE LAYER - Shared Resources"]
        db1["Database 1 - Replicated"]
        db2["Database 2 - Replicated"]
        cache["Distributed Cache"]
    end

    client1 --> router1
    client2 --> router1
    client3 --> router1
    mobileApp --> router1
    router1 --> loadbal
    loadbal --> coord
    coord --> sec
    sec --> nodeA
    sec --> nodeB
    sec --> nodeC
    sec --> nodeD
    nodeA <--> nodeB
    nodeB <--> nodeC
    nodeC <--> nodeD
    nodeA --> db1
    nodeB --> db1
    nodeC --> db2
    nodeD --> db2
    nodeA --> cache
    nodeB --> cache
    nodeC --> cache
    nodeD --> cache
```

### 4.2 Comparison: Centralized vs. Distributed System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Centralized["CENTRALIZED SYSTEM"]
        c1["Client 1"] -->|Request| server["Single Server"]
        c2["Client 2"] -->|Request| server
        c3["Client 3"] -->|Request| server
        server -->|Response| c1
        server -->|Response| c2
        server -->|Response| c3
    end
```

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Distributed["DISTRIBUTED SYSTEM"]
        d1["Client 1"] -->|Request| lb["Load Balancer"]
        d2["Client 2"] -->|Request| lb
        d3["Client 3"] -->|Request| lb
        lb --> n1["Node 1"]
        lb --> n2["Node 2"]
        lb --> n3["Node 3"]
        n1 <-->|Message| n2
        n2 <-->|Message| n3
        n1 <-->|Sync| db[("Shared DB")]
        n2 <-->|Sync| db
        n3 <-->|Sync| db
    end
```

### 4.3 Architectural Models — Comparison Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    start["Distributed Computing Architecture Models"]
    
    start --> cs["Client Server Model"]
    start --> p2p["Peer to Peer Model"]
    start --> tier["Three Tier N Tier Model"]
    start --> soa["Service Oriented / Microservices"]
    
    cs --> cs1["Centralized control"]
    cs --> cs2["Examples: Web Email FTP"]
    
    p2p --> p2p1["Decentralized equal peers"]
    p2p --> p2p2["Examples: BitTorrent Blockchain"]
    
    tier --> t1["Presentation Layer"]
    tier --> t2["Application Logic Layer"]
    tier --> t3["Data Storage Layer"]
    
    soa --> s1["Loosely coupled services"]
    soa --> s2["REST gRPC SOAP APIs"]
    soa --> s3["Cloud Native Apps"]
```

### 4.4 Amdahl's Law Visualization

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inp["Input Parameters P and N"] --> calc["Apply Amdahl Formula"]
    calc["S = 1 divided by 1 minus P plus P over N"] --> out["Output Speedup S"]
    out --> check["If N approaches infinity"]
    check --> limit["S bound = 1 divided by 1 minus P"]
    out --> eff["Efficiency E = S divided by N"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Define distributed computing. List any four characteristics of a distributed system.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1.5 Marks):** Distributed computing is a model in which components of a software system are shared among multiple computers networked together, communicating and coordinating their actions via message passing to appear as a single coherent system to the end user.

**Four Characteristics (1.5 Marks — 0.375 each):**
1. **Concurrency** — Multiple processes execute simultaneously across nodes.
2. **No Global Clock** — Coordination achieved through logical timestamps.
3. **Independent Failures** — Each node can fail without halting the entire system.
4. **Heterogeneity** — Supports diverse hardware, OS, and protocols.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
**Explain any three forms of transparency in distributed systems with examples.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

1. **Location Transparency (1 Mark):** Users access resources without needing to know their physical location. *Example:* A URL `www.example.com/page` works regardless of which data center the server resides in.

2. **Replication Transparency (1 Mark):** Users are unaware that multiple copies of a resource exist on different nodes. *Example:* Dropbox files appear the same across global data centers.

3. **Failure Transparency (1 Mark):** The system masks partial failures and recovers automatically. *Example:* A web server farm uses redundant servers; if one fails, another takes over transparently.

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks**
