<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define Hybrid Cloud. List any four characteristics.**

**Model Answer (verbatim, 3 marks):**
A hybrid cloud is a cloud deployment model that **combines a private cloud with one or more public cloud services**, bound together by enabling technology that permits data and application portability between them, while keeping the constituent clouds as unique entities.

**Four characteristics:**
1. **Workload Portability** — VMs, containers, and data move seamlessly across tiers.
2. **Elastic Bursting** — on-demand scaling into the public cloud during peak load.
3. **Unified Orchestration** — single management plane governs both environments.
4. **Regulatory Compliance** — sensitive workloads remain on private infrastructure.

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between Public, Private, and Hybrid Cloud deployment models.**

**Model Answer (tabular, 3 marks):**

| Parameter | Public Cloud | Private Cloud | Hybrid Cloud |
|---|---|---|---|
| Ownership | Third-party provider | Single organization | Mix of both |
| Location | Off-premise (internet) | On-premise / hosted | Both |
| Cost Model | Pay-per-use (OpEx) | CapEx + OpEx | Mixed |
| Scalability | Virtually unlimited | Limited by on-prem capacity | High (bursts to public) |
| Security | Provider-shared | Full organizational control | Tiered (sensitive on private) |
| Use Case | Startups, web apps | Banks, government | Enterprises with regulated + elastic workloads |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A: `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 1]`
**(a)** Explain the **architecture of a Hybrid Cloud** with a neat block diagram. Describe the role of the **Cloud Service Broker** in workload distribution. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A company maintains a private cloud of **80 VMs** at ₹12,000/VM/year capex. It bursts to public cloud (**150 VMs × ₹45/hr × 8 hrs × 10 events/year**) plus **600 GB egress × ₹5/GB × 10 events**. Compute the **total annual hybrid TCO** and the **percentage cost saved versus running everything on public cloud (8760 hrs/year)**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A(a) — 7 Marks

1. **Definition (1 mark):** Hybrid cloud = private + public cloud bound by orchestration technology.
2. **Architecture Block Diagram (3 marks):** Draw four tiers — Client → Orchestration → Connectivity → Cloud Tiers (Private + Public). Label all arrows with protocols (HTTPS, VPN, REST API).
3. **Cloud Service Broker Role (3 marks):**
   - Acts as an **intermediary** between the cloud consumer and providers.
   - Performs **service aggregation, arbitrage, and intermediation**.
   - Decides **placement policy**: which workload → which tier based on cost, latency, compliance.
   - Provides **SLA monitoring and unified billing**.

---

#### Model Solution for Question A(b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Private cloud annual cost**

$$C_{private} = 80 \times 12{,}000 = \text{₹}960{,}000$$

**Step 2 — Per-burst public cost**

$$C_{burst\_one} = 150 \times 45 \times 8 = \text{₹}54{,}000$$

**Step 3 — Annual burst cost**

$$C_{public} = 54{,}000 \times 10 = \text{₹}540{,}000$$

**Step 4 — Annual egress cost**

$$C_{egress} = 600 \times 5 \times 10 = \text{₹}30{,}000$$

**Step 5 — Total Hybrid TCO**

$$C_{hybrid} = 960{,}000 + 540{,}000 + 30{,}000 = \text{₹}1{,}530{,}000$$

**Step 6 — Pure Public Cost (80 + 150 = 230 VMs × 8760 hrs × ₹45)**

$$C_{pure} = 230 \times 45 \times 8760 = \text{₹}90{,}702{,}000$$

**Step 7 — Savings Percentage**

$$\text{Savings} = \frac{C_{pure} - C_{hybrid}}{C_{pure}} \times 100$$

$$\text{Savings} = \frac{90{,}702{,}000 - 1{,}530{,}000}{90{,}702{,}000} \times 100$$

$$\boxed{\text{Savings} \approx 98.31\%}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> 1. **Unit errors** — students frequently forget to multiply burst cost by the *number of events* (K). Always re-read the problem statement for "× N times/year".
> 2. **Egress omitted** — examiners *deliberately* leave egress as a trap; failing to add it costs 1 mark.
> 3. **Comparison baseline** — for the "savings %" sub-part, you **must** state the assumption that pure-public means *24×7×365* (8760 hours), not just during peak.
> 4. **Decimal precision** — keep at least 2 decimal places in percentage answers.

---

### Question B (Alternative Choice): `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 1]`
**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain **cloud bursting** as a use case of hybrid cloud. List **four benefits** and **three challenges** of hybrid cloud. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A hybrid cloud SLA guarantees **99.95%** annual availability. Compute the **permitted downtime per year in hours, minutes, and seconds**, and state whether it satisfies a contractual limit of **4 hours 20 minutes/year**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B(a) — 7 Marks

1. **Cloud Bursting Definition (1 mark):** The automatic, on-demand shifting of workloads from a private cloud to a public cloud when private capacity is exhausted.
2. **Diagram (2 marks):** Show a private cloud cluster with an overflow arrow to a public cloud, gated by a load monitor + policy engine.
3. **Four Benefits (2 marks):**
   - Cost efficiency (pay only for burst hours)
   - Elastic scalability
   - Business continuity & DR
   - Compliance + flexibility coexistence
4. **Three Challenges (2 marks):**
   - **Network latency** across hybrid boundary
   - **Data egress costs** and bandwidth bottlenecks
   - **Integration complexity** (multi-vendor orchestration)

---

#### Model Solution for Question B(b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Total minutes in a year**

$$T_{year} = 60 \times 24 \times 365 = 525{,}600 \text{ min}$$

**Step 2 — Permitted downtime in minutes**

$$D = 525{,}600 \times (1 - 0.9995) = 525{,}600 \times 0.0005 = 262.8 \text{ min}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to h : m : s**

$$\text{Hours} = \lfloor 262.8 / 60 \rfloor = 4 \text{ hours}$$

$$\text{Remaining} = 262.8 - 240 = 22.8 \text{ min}$$

$$\text{Seconds} = 0.8 \times 60 = 48 \text{ sec}$$

$$\boxed{D = 4 \text{ hours } 22 \text{ minutes } 48 \text{ seconds}}$$

**Step 4 — SLA Compliance Check**

$4\text{h }22\text{m }48\text{s} \le 4\text{h }20\text{m}$ ?

$4\text{h }22\text{m }48\text{s} > 4\text{h }20\text{m}$ — **SLA VIOLATED** by **2 min 48 sec**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Traps in Question B(b):**
> 1. **Conversion mistake** — students often compute $D = 525{,}600 \times 0.0005$ correctly as 262.8 min but then **incorrectly convert** to "4 hours 22 minutes" without the seconds component. Always show the seconds.
> 2. **Direction of comparison** — when asked "does it satisfy", explicitly state **YES or NO with margin** (e.g., "Violated by 168 seconds"). Examiners award 1 mark for this conclusion.
> 3. **Misreading the SLA limit** — "4 hours 20 minutes" = 260 minutes, *not* 420 minutes. A classic trap.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mandatory Revision Points for KTU ESE — Module 1, Hybrid Cloud:**

- ✅ **Definition (NIST-aligned):** Composition of two or more distinct cloud infrastructures bound by enabling technology.
- ✅ **Two mandatory tiers:** Private + Public (community cloud may also participate).
- ✅ **Three architectural planes:** Infrastructure, Connectivity, Orchestration.
- ✅ **Cloud Service Broker roles:** aggregation, arbitrage, intermediation.
- ✅ **Cloud Bursting:** elastic offload of peak workload to public tier.
- ✅ **Key protocols/tech:** IPsec VPN, Direct Connect, REST APIs, Kubernetes Federation.
- ✅ **Master the "nines" table** — exam questions appear almost every cycle.
- ✅ **Cost formulas to memorize:** $C_{hybrid} = C_{private} + C_{public} + C_{egress}$; Savings % = $(C_{pure} - C_{hybrid})/C_{pure} \times 100$.
- ✅ **Use cases:** DR, burst scaling, regulatory compliance, AI/ML training offload.
- ✅ **Challenges:** latency, egress cost, integration complexity, security boundary management.
- ✅ **Indian regulatory context:** DPDPA 2023, RBI data localization, MeitY empanelment — hybrid is the legally preferred model.
- ✅ **Differentiate from Multi-Cloud:** Multi-cloud = multiple *public* providers, *no* private tier; Hybrid = private + public federation.
- ✅ **Always state units** in numerical answers (₹, minutes, GB) — KTU examiners deduct 0.5 marks for missing units.
- ✅ **Show all intermediate steps** in cost/numerical problems — partial marking is generous when steps are visible.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Para-virtualization, VMware: Full Virtualization
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT525 - CLOUD COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Para-virtualization, VMware: Full Virtualization

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 2 — PARA-VIRTUALIZATION & VMWARE FULL VIRTUALIZATION

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Para-Virtualization — Formal Definition

**Para-virtualization** is a virtualization technique in which the guest Operating System (OS) is *aware* that it is running inside a virtualized environment and is **deliberately modified** (ported) so that its non-virtualizable privileged instructions are replaced with safe **hypercalls** that communicate directly with the Virtual Machine Monitor (VMM/Hypervisor).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PECPT525 – Module 2):**
> Para-virtualization is classified under **Type-1 (Bare-Metal) Hypervisor architecture**. The host OS kernel is NOT involved in instruction translation — the modified guest itself co-operates with the hypervisor.

| Term | Meaning |
|------|---------|
| **Hypercall** | A software trap from guest to hypervisor (analogous to a `syscall` but crossing the VM boundary) |
| **Ported Kernel** | A guest OS whose source code is modified before deployment |
| **VMM / Hypervisor** | The control program that owns physical hardware and arbitrates requests |
| **Privileged Instruction** | An instruction that must run in Ring 0; in para-virtualization these are *removed* from guest |

### 1.2 VMware Full Virtualization — Formal Definition

**Full Virtualization** is a technique in which the **guest OS is completely unaware** of virtualization. The VMM intercepts and **binary-translates** every privileged/sensitive instruction at runtime, presenting each VM with the *illusion* of owning dedicated hardware. VMware was the first commercial product (1999) to make full virtualization practical on x86 despite the lack of hardware-assisted virtualization (pre-Intel VT-x / AMD-V era).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Full Virtualization = Guest OS *untouched*. Para-Virtualization = Guest OS *co-operatively modified*.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition)

Think of a **shared office building** where multiple companies share one physical building.

* **Para-Virtualization** is like a company that *knows* it is renting a floor. It openly walks to the landlord's reception desk (hypervisor) and says, "Please switch on the AC for me." The tenant (guest OS) co-operates because it was briefed at move-in.
* **VMware Full Virtualization** is like a company that *believes* it owns the entire building. Whenever it flips a wall switch (executes a privileged instruction), the building's smart system silently intercepts it, translates it to a real electrical signal, and dispatches maintenance staff. The company never realizes it is being served.

In both cases, **one physical building (hardware) hosts many tenants (VMs)**, but the trust and modification contract differ drastically.

### 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Performance Metric — Virtualization Overhead:**
>
> $$\text{Overhead}_{\%} = \left(\frac{T_{\text{VM}} - T_{\text{Native}}}{T_{\text{Native}}}\right) \times 100$$
>
> where $T_{\text{Native}}$ is execution time on bare metal and $T_{\text{VM}}$ is execution time inside the VM. **Industry-acceptable overhead for full virtualization is ≤ 10%**, while para-virtualization typically achieves **2%–5%**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Para-Virtualization — Operational Mechanics

### 2.1.1 Why Para-Virtualization Exists

The x86 architecture (pre-2005) defined **17 privileged instructions** that could not be safely trapped by classic traps-and-emulates. VMware solved this with **binary translation**; the open-source community (Xen project at Cambridge, 2003) solved it with **para-virtualization** instead — by *removing* those instructions from the guest kernel.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why modify the guest?** Because changing the guest is *cheaper* than building a complex dynamic translator. Para-virtualization gains **near-native speed** at the cost of OS portability.

### 2.1.2 The Hypercall Mechanism (Step-by-Step Logic)

1. **Guest boots** in Ring 1 (user-equivalent privilege).
2. **Guest kernel** needs to update the page table → instead of executing `MOV CR3` (privileged), it issues a hypercall.
3. **Hypercall** = `int 0x82` (Xen) or `VMCALL` (Hyper-V) — a software interrupt.
4. **Hypervisor** validates the request against its policy database.
5. **Hypervisor** performs the privileged action on behalf of the guest.
6. **Control returns** to the guest at the next instruction.

### 2.1.3 Advantages & Disadvantages (Board-Exam Favorite)

| Parameter | Para-Virtualization |
|-----------|---------------------|
| **Performance** | Near native (≈ 95–98% of bare-metal) |
| **Guest Modification** | **Required** — open-source OS only (Linux, FreeBSD, NetBSD) |
| **Portability** | Limited — Windows cannot be para-virtualized |
| **Hypercall Overhead** | Each hypercall ≈ 1 syscall equivalent (~hundreds of ns) |
| **Isolation** | Strong (Type-1 hypervisor) |
| **Examples** | Xen, IBM POWER Hypervisor, VMware Tools (PV drivers) |

## 2.2 VMware Full Virtualization — Operational Mechanics

### 2.2.1 The Three Pillars of VMware's Approach

VMware introduced **three foundational techniques** that made full virtualization commercially viable on x86:

1. **Direct Execution** — User-level (Ring 3) instructions run *directly* on the CPU.
2. **Binary Translation (BT)** — Privileged/sensitive Ring-0 instructions are rewritten into safe sequences at runtime.
3. **Shadow Page Tables / Memory Mapping** — Guest page tables are kept consistent with actual hardware TLB entries.

### 2.2.2 Trap-and-Emulate with Binary Translation

For every privileged instruction, the VMM:

1. Allows guest to attempt execution.
2. CPU traps into VMM (Ring 0 → Ring -1 transition in modern VT-x).
3. VMM **decodes** the original instruction bytes.
4. VMM **rewrites** (translates) into an equivalent safe sequence.
5. VMM **emulates** the side-effect (register update, page-table change).
6. VMM **resumes** guest.

### 2.2.3 VMware Product Family Mapping (KTU Industry Context)

| VMware Product | Hypervisor Type | Virtualization Mode |
|----------------|-----------------|---------------------|
| **VMware Workstation** | Type-2 (Hosted) | Full Virtualization |
| **VMware Fusion** | Type-2 (macOS) | Full Virtualization |
| **VMware ESXi** | Type-1 (Bare-Metal) | Full + PV drivers via VMware Tools |
| **VMware vSphere** | Cloud/Enterprise Suite | Hybrid (Full + PV + Hardware-assisted) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Trap:** Even ESXi (a Type-1 hypervisor) *uses* para-virtualization drivers (vmxnet3, paravirt-scsi) installed **inside** the guest via VMware Tools. So in modern cloud, **full + para + hardware-assisted** all coexist.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table consolidates every formula you may need for numerical/analytical questions on this topic.

| # | Concept | Equation | Variables / Units | Boundary / Typical Value |
|---|---------|----------|-------------------|---------------------------|
| 1 | Virtualization Overhead | $O_{\%} = \frac{T_{\text{VM}} - T_{\text{Native}}}{T_{\text{Native}}} \times 100$ | $T$ in seconds | $O_{\%} \leq 10\%$ (industry norm) |
| 2 | Effective VM Throughput | $Th_{\text{eff}} = \frac{Th_{\text{Native}}}{1 + O_{\%}/100}$ | Throughput in ops/s | Approximates native for para-virt |
| 3 | Memory Ballooning (VMware) | $M_{\text{reclaim}} = M_{\text{config}} - M_{\text{active}}$ | $M$ in MB/GB | $M_{\text{reclaim}} \geq 0$ |
| 4 | Hypercall Latency vs. Trap-and-Emulate | $L_{\text{HC}} \ll L_{\text{BT}}$ | Latency in $\mu s$ | $L_{\text{HC}} \approx 0.5\,\mu s$, $L_{\text{BT}} \approx 2$–$5\,\mu s$ |
| 5 | VM Density on Host | $\rho_{\text{VM}} = \frac{N_{\text{VM}}}{N_{\text{pCore}}}$ | VMs per physical core | $\rho_{\text{VM}} \approx 4$–$12$ for production |
| 6 | Total Memory Capacity | $M_{\text{total}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} M_{\text{VM}_i} \leq M_{\text{phys}}$ | GB | Cannot be exceeded |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

* **Public Cloud (AWS EC2)** — Originally used Xen para-virtualization (PV) and later Hardware-assisted VMX (HVM); now AWS Nitro offloads virtualization to dedicated hardware.
* **Enterprise Data-Centers** — VMware vSphere dominates due to full virtualization allowing any legacy OS.
* **Research Cloud (OpenStack)** — Combines KVM (full + hardware-assisted) and Xen (para-virtualization).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Derivations

## 3.1 Derivation: Virtualization Overhead for Full vs. Para-Virtualization

**Problem Statement:**
A benchmark application takes $T_{\text{Native}} = 8.0 \text{ seconds}$ on bare metal.
* Under **full virtualization** it takes $T_{\text{FV}} = 8.8 \text{ s}$.
* Under **para-virtualization** it takes $T_{\text{PV}} = 8.2 \text{ s}$.

Compute the overhead in each case and determine the absolute speed-up of PV over FV.

### Step 1 — Define the Overhead Formula

$$
O_{\%} = \left(\frac{T_{\text{VM}} - T_{\text{Native}}}{T_{\text{Native}}}\right) \times 100
$$

This expresses the **percentage time penalty** introduced by the virtualization layer.

### Step 2 — Compute Overhead for Full Virtualization

$$
O_{\text{FV}} = \left(\frac{8.8 - 8.0}{8.0}\right) \times 100
$$

$$
O_{\text{FV}} = \left(\frac{0.8}{8.0}\right) \times 100
$$

$$
O_{\text{FV}} = 0.10 \times 100 = \mathbf{10.0\%}
$$

This is at the *upper industry-acceptable limit*, indicating heavy binary-translation activity.

### Step 3 — Compute Overhead for Para-Virtualization

$$
O_{\text{PV}} = \left(\frac{8.2 - 8.0}{8.0}\right) \times 100
$$

$$
O_{\text{PV}} = \left(\frac{0.2}{8.0}\right) \times 100
$$

$$
O_{\text{PV}} = 0.025 \times 100 = \mathbf{2.5\%}
$$

This is well within the *near-native* envelope.

### Step 4 — Compute Absolute Speed-Up of PV over FV

$$
\text{Speedup} = \frac{T_{\text{FV}}}{T_{\text{PV}}} = \frac{8.8}{8.2}
$$

$$
\text{Speedup} \approx \mathbf{1.0732}
$$

That is, **para-virtualization is ~7.3% faster** than full virtualization on this workload.

### Step 5 — Effective Throughput Analysis

Using $Th_{\text{eff}} = \dfrac{Th_{\text{Native}}}{1 + O_{\%}/100}$, with $Th_{\text{Native}} = 100 \text{ ops/s}$:

* FV: $Th_{\text{eff}} = 100 / 1.10 \approx 90.91 \text{ ops/s}$
* PV: $Th_{\text{eff}} = 100 / 1.025 \approx 97.56 \text{ ops/s}$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** Para-virtualization preserves ~**97.6%** of native throughput vs. only **90.9%** for full virtualization — this is why hyperscalers historically preferred Xen PV for compute-bound workloads.

## 3.2 Derivation: VM Memory Over-Commitment via Ballooning

**Problem Statement:**
A VMware ESXi host has $M_{\text{phys}} = 64 \text{ GB}$ RAM. Three VMs are configured with $M_{\text{VM}} = \{24, 24, 24\} \text{ GB}$.
Total configured = $24 \times 3 = 72 \text{ GB}$.
How can this run on 64 GB? Compute the **ballooned reclamation** if each VM actually uses $M_{\text{active}} = \{18, 16, 20\} \text{ GB}$.

### Step 1 — Identify Over-Commitment Ratio

$$
R_{\text{over}} = \frac{\sum M_{\text{VM}_i}}{M_{\text{phys}}} = \frac{72}{64} = 1.125
$$

Over-committed by **12.5%** — common in production clouds.

### Step 2 — Compute Idle Memory per VM

$$
M_{\text{idle},i} = M_{\text{config},i} - M_{\text{active},i}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{\text{idle,1}} &= 24 - 18 = 6 \text{ GB} \\
M_{\text{idle,2}} &= 24 - 16 = 8 \text{ GB} \\
M_{\text{idle,3}} &= 24 - 20 = 4 \text{ GB}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Total Reclaimable Memory

$$
M_{\text{reclaim}} = \sum_{i=1}^{3} M_{\text{idle},i} = 6 + 8 + 4 = 18 \text{ GB}
$$

### Step 4 — Verify Sufficiency

$$
M_{\text{active,total}} = 18 + 16 + 20 = 54 \text{ GB}
$$

Since $54 \text{ GB} \leq 64 \text{ GB}$, the configuration is **feasible** thanks to ballooning (vmmemctl driver inside guest).

> [!NOTE]
> This is **para-virtualization in action at the memory layer** — VMware Tools balloon driver cooperates with the ESXi hypervisor to *borrow* unused pages.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Architecture Comparison — Full vs. Para-Virtualization

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph FV["FULL VIRTUALIZATION - VMWARE"]
        direction TB
        APP_FV["Guest Application Ring 3"]
        GUEST_FV["Guest OS UNMODIFIED Ring 0 attempted"]
        BT["VMM Binary Translator + Shadow Page Tables"]
        HV_FV["VMware Hypervisor Ring -1"]
        HW_FV["Physical CPU RAM Disk NIC"]

        APP_FV --> GUEST_FV
        GUEST_FV -- privileged instruction trapped --> BT
        BT --> HV_FV
        HV_FV --> HW_FV
    end
```

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph PV["PARA-VIRTUALIZATION - XEN"]
        direction TB
        APP_PV["Guest Application Ring 3"]
        GUEST_PV["MODIFIED Guest OS Ring 1 with Hypercalls"]
        HC["Hypercall Interface int 0x82 or VMCALL"]
        HV_PV["Xen Hypervisor Ring 0"]
        HW_PV["Physical CPU RAM Disk NIC"]

        APP_PV --> GUEST_PV
        GUEST_PV -- hypercall --> HC
        HC --> HV_PV
        HV_PV --> HW_PV
    end
```

## 4.2 Instruction-Handling Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Instruction Fetched in Guest VM"] --> B{"Privilege Level?"}
    B -- "User Mode Ring 3" --> C["Execute Directly on CPU"]
    B -- "Privileged Ring 0" --> D{"Virtualization Mode?"}
    D -- "Full Virt VMware" --> E["CPU traps to VMM"]
    E --> F["Binary Translation Decode Rewrite"]
    F --> G["Emulate Side Effect"]
    G --> H["Resume Guest"]
    D -- "Para Virt Xen" --> I["Hypercall issued by modified guest"]
    I --> J["Hypervisor executes natively"]
    J --> K["Return to Guest"]
    H --> L["End"]
    C --> L
    K --> L
```

## 4.3 VMware Stack — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph USR["USER SPACE"]
        direction LR
        U1["vSphere Client"]
        U2["VM Console"]
        U3["REST API"]
    end

    subgraph MGMT["MANAGEMENT PLANE"]
        M1["vCenter Server"]
        M2["Resource Scheduler DRS"]
        M3["HA Failover Engine"]
    end

    subgraph CTRL["CONTROL PLANE per Host"]
        C1["vmkernel"]
        C2["VMX Process per VM"]
        C3["VMware Tools in Guest"]
    end

    subgraph HARD["PHYSICAL HARDWARE"]
        H1["CPU with VT-x or AMD-V"]
        H2["MMU with EPT or NPT"]
        H3["NIC with SR-IOV"]
        H4["NVMe Storage"]
    end

    USR --> MGMT
    MGMT --> CTRL
    CTRL --> HARD
    C2 -. binary translation .-> C1
    C3 -. hypercalls para drivers .-> C1
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank

## PART A — 3 Mark Questions

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Q: Differentiate between full virtualization and para-virtualization.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Full Virtualization | Para-Virtualization |
|-----------|---------------------|---------------------|
| Guest OS modification | **Not required** | **Required** |
| Guest awareness of VM | Unaware | Aware |
| Technique used | Binary translation & direct execution | Hypercalls |
| Performance | ~90% of native | ~95–98% of native |
| Example | VMware Workstation | Xen, IBM POWER |

*(3 marks — table comparison covers all CO mapping)*

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

**Q: What is a hypercall? How does it differ from a system call?**

**Model Answer:**

A **hypercall** is a software trap issued by a *para-virtualized guest* to request services from the hypervisor. It crosses the **VM-to-hypervisor** boundary, analogous to how a **system call** crosses the **user-to-kernel** boundary.

* System call → User mode to kernel mode (same OS)
* Hypercall → Guest VM to VMM (different OS instance)

Implementation: typically `int 0x82` (Xen), `VMCALL` (Hyper-V).

*(3 marks)*

---

## PART B — 14 Mark Questions

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Explain the architecture of VMware full virtualization with a neat diagram. Discuss binary translation in detail.**

**Model Solution:**

**Architecture Layers (top-down):**

1. **Guest Application Layer** — User processes running in Ring 3.
2. **Guest OS Layer** — Unmodified Windows/Linux kernel attempting Ring-0 access.
3. **VMware VMM Layer** — Core component; performs binary translation and direct execution.
4. **Host Hardware Layer** — Physical CPU, RAM, Disk, NIC.

**Binary Translation Mechanism:**

The VMM maintains a **Translation Cache** containing rewritten instruction blocks.

* **Step 1:** Guest attempts `OUT 0x80, AL` (privileged I/O port).
* **Step 2:** CPU traps (general-protection fault or I/O privilege trap).
* **Step 3:** VMM decoder reads the original instruction bytes.
* **Step 4:** VMM translator replaces the original block with an **equivalent safe sequence** stored in the translation cache.
* **Step 5:** VMM executes the rewritten block, updating VMCS/equivalent state.
* **Step 6:** VMM resumes guest via `VMRESUME`.

**Key Performance Trick:** User-level (Ring 3) instructions are **executed directly** on hardware — only the ~5–10% privileged instructions incur translation overhead. This is why overhead is bounded near 10%.

**Diagram:** *(Refer to Section 4.1)*

*(7 marks — 2 for architecture, 3 for BT steps, 1 for performance rationale, 1 for diagram)*

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Describe memory virtualization techniques in VMware — shadow paging, EPT, and ballooning.**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Shadow Page Tables (legacy):**
* Guest maintains *virtual* page tables (PTs).
* VMM maintains *shadow* PTs synchronized with actual MMU.
* On `MOV CR3`, VMM intercepts, marks shadow dirty, walks guest PT.
* **Drawback:** Heavy overhead on memory-intensive workloads.

**2. Extended Page Tables (EPT) — Intel VT-x / AMD NPT:**
* Hardware maintains **two-level** translation: Guest-Virtual → Guest-Physical → Host-Physical.
* VMM only loads the **EPT base pointer** into hardware; MMU does the rest.
* **Benefit:** Near-native memory performance.

**3. Ballooning (para-virtual memory):**
* A balloon driver (vmmemctl) loads **inside** the guest via VMware Tools.
* VMM instructs balloon to **inflate** → guest allocates pinned memory.
* Guest OS then frees equivalent pages to its own page cache.
* VMM can reclaim those physical pages for other VMs.

**Formula:**
$$
M_{\text{reclaim}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (M_{\text{config},i} - M_{\text{active},i})
$$

*(7 marks — 2 each for shadow/EPT/balloon + 1 synthesis)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Explain para-virtualization in detail with Xen architecture. List its advantages over full virtualization.**

**Model Solution:**

**Xen Architecture Components:**

1. **Xen Hypervisor** — Thin control layer at Ring 0.
2. **Dom0 (Control Domain)** — Privileged VM with management tools.
3. **DomU (Unprivileged Domains)** — Guest VMs (para-virtualized Linux, BSD).
4. **Hypercall Table** — Pre-defined service entries (e.g., `HYPERVISOR_console_io`).

**Operational Flow:**
* Guest kernel modified at compile-time (`CONFIG_XEN`).
* Privileged ops replaced with `hypercall_page[op]`.
* On boot, guest registers with Xen via `HYPERVISOR_start_info`.
* I/O via split-driver model: backend in Dom0, frontend in DomU (using shared memory rings + event channels).

**Hypercalls commonly used:**
* `HYPERVISOR_memory_op` — page allocation
* `HYPERVISOR_sched_op` — yield/block/wakeup
* `HYPERVISOR_console_io` — debug output

**Advantages over Full Virtualization:**

| # | Advantage |
|---|-----------|
| 1 | **Higher performance** — hypercall ≈ 1 syscall cost vs. BT emulation cost |
| 2 | **Simpler VMM** — no need for complex binary translator (~10,000 LOC saved) |
| 3 | **Better scalability** for CPU-bound workloads |
| 4 | **Predictable latency** — no dynamic translation cache misses |
| 5 | **Lower memory footprint** of the hypervisor itself |

**Drawback:** Cannot run closed-source OS like Windows without hardware assistance.

*(7 marks — 3 for architecture, 2 for hypercalls/IO, 2 for advantages table)*

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Compare Type-1 and Type-2 hypervisors with examples. Show how VMware ESXi and VMware Workstation differ.**

**Model Solution:**

| Parameter | Type-1 (Bare-Metal) | Type-2 (Hosted) |
|-----------|---------------------|-----------------|
| Installation | Directly on hardware | On top of host OS |
| Performance | Higher (no host OS overhead) | Lower (double scheduling) |
| Use Case | Enterprise / cloud | Development / testing |
| Driver Model | Native hardware drivers | Uses host OS drivers |
| Example | VMware ESXi, Xen, Hyper-V Server | VMware Workstation, VirtualBox |

**VMware ESXi:**
* Installs on bare metal.
* ~150 MB footprint (vSphere 7).
* Manages CPU/memory/storage directly via vmkernel.
* Supports DRS, HA, vMotion for live migration.
* Used in data-centers.

**VMware Workstation:**
* Installs on Windows/Linux host.
* Relies on host OS for I/O drivers.
* Suitable for desktop VMs, snapshots, cloning.
* Cannot perform live migration across hosts.

**Formula for VM Density (Type-1 vs Type-2):**
$$
\rho_{\text{T1}} \approx 1.4 \times \rho_{\text{T2}}
$$
because Type-1 eliminates host-OS scheduling jitter.

*(7 marks — 3 for comparison table, 2 each for ESXi & Workstation specifics)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing "para-virtualization" with "partial virtualization"** — these are *not* the same. Partial = address-space only; Para = modified guest + hypercalls.
> 2. **Forgetting to mention VMware Tools** — it is the *para-virtualization driver bundle* installed inside the guest for better I/O; examiners love this linkage.
> 3. **Skipping the diagram** — for any 7-mark sub-question in Part B, *always* include at least one labeled diagram (block/flow). It fetches at least 1.5 marks as "presentation credit".
> 4. **Mixing up Type-1 / Type-2 hypervisors with full / para virtualization** — they are orthogonal classification axes (deployment model vs. technique).
> 5. **Writing hypercalls as "interrupts" only** — they are a *software trap mechanism* (synchronous), not asynchronous interrupts.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Full Virtualization** = Guest OS unmodified; uses **binary translation**; example: VMware Workstation / ESXi.
- **Para-Virtualization** = Guest OS modified; uses **hypercalls**; example: Xen.
- VMware is the *pioneer* of practical full virtualization on x86 (1999, founded by Mendel Rosenblum, Ed Bugnion — derived from Stanford Disco project).
- Para-virtualization achieves ~**2–5%** overhead; full virtualization ~**5–10%**.
- **Hypercall** = software trap from guest to hypervisor (`int 0x82` / `VMCALL`).
- VMware modern products are **hybrid** — full virt + para-virt drivers (vmxnet3, pvscsi) + hardware-assisted (Intel VT-x / AMD-V / EPT).
- **Binary Translation** rewrites privileged Ring-0 instructions into safe Ring-3 sequences at runtime.
- **EPT / NPT** hardware feature eliminates shadow-page-table overhead.
- **Ballooning** = para-virt memory reclamation technique via VMware Tools balloon driver.
- **Type-1 (bare-metal)** vs **Type-2 (hosted)** is *separate* from full vs para classification.
- **Over-committed memory** is feasible via ballooning and transparent page sharing.
- KTU favorites: comparison tables, binary translation steps, hypercall flow, overhead calculations.
- Remember to draw a labeled block diagram for any 7-mark answer.
- Industry trend: **hardware-assisted virtualization (VT-x, AMD-V)** has made both techniques converge in performance; modern cloud uses hybrid approaches (AWS Nitro, KVM, Hyper-V).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Broadband Networks and Internet Architecture - Internet Service Providers (ISPs)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT525 - CLOUD COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Broadband Networks and Internet Architecture - Internet Service Providers (ISPs)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

An **Internet Service Provider (ISP)** is an organization that provides access to the Internet and related services, including web hosting, domain name registration, email, and connectivity to backbone networks. From a KTU 2024 cloud computing perspective, an ISP is the **physical and logical network gateway** through which cloud tenants, applications, and end-users reach hyperscale data centers, regional Point-of-Presence (PoP) nodes, and ultimately the public Internet.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Syllabus-aligned):**
> An ISP is a telecommunications-layer entity that owns, leases, or operates the **last-mile access infrastructure** (DSL, fiber, cable, wireless) and **backbone transport links** (high-capacity optical circuits) required to deliver IP packet forwarding services to customer-premises equipment (CPE) such as routers, firewalls, and cloud on-ramps.

### 1.1.1 Classification of ISPs by Access Technology

| Access Class | Physical Medium | Typical Downlink | Typical Uplink | KTU Use-Case |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| **DSL** | Copper twisted pair | 1–100 Mbps | 0.5–20 Mbps | Legacy SMB connectivity |
| **Cable (HFC)** | Coaxial + fibre hybrid | 10–1000 Mbps | 5–50 Mbps | Residential broadband |
| **FTTH/FTTP** | Optical fibre | 100–10000 Mbps | 100–10000 Mbps | Cloud data-centre uplinks |
| **Fixed Wireless (5G/LTE)** | Radio spectrum | 50–1000 Mbps | 10–100 Mbps | Edge cloud access |
| **Satellite (LEO/GEO)** | RF satellite link | 12–500 Mbps | 3–50 Mbps | Rural cloud edge |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Highway System"

Think of the Internet as a **national highway network**:
- **Tier-1 ISPs** are the **interstate freeways** (e.g., Lumen, NTT, Telia) — they own the longest, highest-capacity roads and do not pay tolls to anyone.
- **Tier-2 ISPs** are the **state highways** (e.g., regional carriers) — they connect to the freeways via tolled on-ramps (transit agreements).
- **Tier-3 ISPs** are the **local roads** (your neighbourhood cable/DSL provider) — they purchase wholesale access from Tier-2s and deliver packets to your door.

When you, the cloud user, request a webpage from a data centre in another country, your packet travels: **Home → Local Road (Tier-3) → State Highway (Tier-2) → Interstate Freeway (Tier-1) → Cloud Data Centre Peering Exchange → Server**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in Cloud Computing:**
> Cloud latency, throughput, and Service-Level Agreements (SLAs) are bounded by the **ISP's hop count, peering efficiency, and last-mile contention ratio**. A poorly chosen ISP can convert a 10 ms cloud response into a 300 ms one — even when the cloud itself is healthy.

## 1.3 Key Terminology Box

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Must-Know Terms (appear frequently in exam questions):**
> - **POP (Point of Presence):** A physical facility housing routers/switches where customers connect.
> - **NOC (Network Operations Centre):** 24×7 monitoring facility managing ISP infrastructure.
> - **ASN (Autonomous System Number):** A globally unique 16/32-bit identifier assigned to each ISP network.
> - **IXP (Internet Exchange Point):** A physical infrastructure where multiple ISPs peer directly.
> - **Peering vs Transit:** Settlement-free mutual exchange (peering) vs paid upstream connectivity (transit).
> - **BGP (Border Gateway Protocol):** The path-vector routing protocol used between ISP autonomous systems.
> - **SLA (Service-Level Agreement):** Contractual guarantees on uptime (e.g., 99.9%), latency (e.g., <20 ms), and packet loss (e.g., <0.1%).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three-Tier ISP Hierarchy

The KTU 2024 syllabus emphasises the **hierarchical interconnection model** of the Internet. This model, originally formalised in RFC 1932 (Guiding Principles for Architecture of the Internet) and updated in modern peering forums, classifies ISPs into three functional tiers:

### Tier 1 — The Global Backbone Carriers

- **Definition:** ISPs that operate **transoceanic, transcontinental, or trans-national backbone networks** and reach the **entire Internet routing table** without paying any other provider for transit.
- **Operational Why:** Tier-1 ISPs maintain settlement-free peering agreements with every other Tier-1, forming a "peering mesh."
- **Number Worldwide:** Approximately **15–18** (Lumen, NTT, Telia, GTT, Zayo, etc.).
- **KPI Targets:** Backbone capacity measured in **multi-terabits per second (Tbps)**; latency across the backbone typically **<30 ms** between major PoPs.
- **Cloud Relevance:** Hyperscale cloud providers (AWS, Azure, GCP) interconnect directly with Tier-1s at **Dedicated Interconnect / ExpressRoute / Cloud Interconnect** facilities.

### Tier 2 — The Regional Carriers

- **Definition:** ISPs that **purchase transit** from Tier-1 providers but also engage in **selective paid peering** to reduce transit costs.
- **Operational Why:** Tier-2s have regional reach but lack sufficient geographic footprint to peer settlement-free globally.
- **KPI Targets:** Backbone capacity **100 Gbps–1 Tbps** per long-haul circuit.
- **Cloud Relevance:** Common partners for **Direct Connect** and **Cloud OnRamp** enterprise services.

### Tier 3 — The Access / Local ISPs

- **Definition:** ISPs that **only purchase transit** (from Tier-1 or Tier-2) and deliver "last-mile" service to end users.
- **Operational Why:** Tier-3s focus on customer acquisition and local loop deployment.
- **KPI Targets:** Aggregate uplink **1–100 Gbps**; per-user **CIR (Committed Information Rate)** depends on plan.
- **Cloud Relevance:** These are the ISPs whose **contention ratio** (oversubscription) most directly impacts user experience of cloud services.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The **contention ratio** (CR) is a Tier-3 metric defined as the ratio of peak aggregate user demand to provisioned uplink capacity. A CR of **50:1** is common in residential broadband; a CR of **4:1** is typical in business-grade fibre.

## 2.2 Internet Exchange Points (IXPs) and Peering

### 2.2.1 Why Peering Exists — Economic Logic

Without peering, every packet would traverse **transit** paths, incurring per-megabit-mile fees. To bypass these fees, ISPs co-locate routers in shared facilities called IXPs (e.g., **DE-CIX Frankfurt, LINX London, AMS-IX Amsterdam, Equinix IX**).

**Peering settlement model:**

$$
\text{Cost}_{\text{sender}} = 
\begin{cases}
0 & \text{if peering} \\
T \times D & \text{if transit}
\end{cases}
$$

where $T$ is the transit tariff in \$/Mbps/month and $D$ is the bandwidth-months consumed.

### 2.2.2 Multi-Lateral Peering (MLP)

Modern IXPs (e.g., **Equinix Fabric, DE-CIX**) use a route-server that distributes the IXP's aggregated routing table to all participants. This enables **any-to-any** peering with a single BGP session, eliminating the $O(n^2)$ bilateral peering problem.

## 2.3 Autonomous System Numbers and BGP Routing

Each ISP is assigned a globally unique **Autonomous System Number (ASN)**, either:
- **2-byte ASN:** 1–65,535 (legacy pool)
- **4-byte ASN:** 65,536–4,294,967,295 (modern pool, RFC 6793)

BGP attributes that influence ISP path selection:

| Attribute | Meaning | Cloud Computing Impact |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **NEXT_HOP** | IP of next router | Affects ECMP load distribution |
| **AS_PATH** | Sequence of traversed ASNs | Longer path = higher cost, lower preference |
| **LOCAL_PREF** | Local preference (higher = preferred) | Used to prefer transit over peering or vice versa |
| **MED (MULTI_EXIT_DISC)** | Hint to neighbouring AS | Used at multi-homed peering points |
| **COMMUNITY** | Tag for policy signalling | Triggers blackholing, prepends, etc. |

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following equations appear in 80% of KTU 2024 numerical/analytical questions on ISP networks. Memorise the symbols and units — they are universally applicable to throughput, latency, and capacity problems.

| # | Formula | Symbol Glossary | Real-World Use |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | $\text{BDP} = B \times \text{RTT}$ | B = bandwidth (bps), RTT = round-trip time (s) | TCP window sizing to avoid bottleneck |
| 2 | $\text{Throughput} \le \dfrac{W_{\max}}{\text{RTT}}$ | $W_{\max}$ = max TCP window (bits) | Caps achievable bandwidth-delay product |
| 3 | $L = \dfrac{d_{\text{prop}} + d_{\text{trans}} + d_{\text{queue}} + d_{\text{proc}}}{1}$ | Total nodal delay components | ISP SLA latency design |
| 4 | $d_{\text{trans}} = \dfrac{L}{R}$ | L = packet length (bits), R = link rate (bps) | Determines serialization delay |
| 5 | $d_{\text{prop}} = \dfrac{d}{s}$ | d = distance (m), s = signal speed (~2×10⁸ m/s in fibre) | Speed-of-light bound for latency |
| 6 | $\text{Utilisation} = \dfrac{\text{Throughput}_{\text{actual}}}{\text{Bandwidth}_{\text{link}}}$ | Dimensionless (0–1) | Network capacity health check |
| 7 | $\text{Contention Ratio} = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{N} \text{PeakRate}_i}{C_{\text{uplink}}}$ | N = users, $C_{\text{uplink}}$ = provisioned uplink | Tier-3 oversubscription design |
| 8 | $\text{Packet Loss Ratio} = 1 - e^{-\lambda \cdot T_q}$ | $\lambda$ = arrival rate, $T_q$ = queue wait | M/M/1 queueing approximation |
| 9 | $\text{Jitter} = \sigma(\Delta t_i) = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(\Delta t_i - \overline{\Delta t})^2}$ | $\Delta t_i$ = per-packet delay variation | Critical for VoIP / video cloud traffic |
| 10 | $\text{Availability} = \dfrac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$ | MTBF = mean time between failures | ISP SLA availability calculation |
| 11 | $C_{95} = $ the rate below which 95% of measurements fall | Statistical | Cloud traffic-engineering SLAs |
| 12 | $\text{Redundancy Factor} = 1 + \dfrac{N_{\text{backup}}}{N_{\text{primary}}}$ | N = link/circuit count | ISP resilience design |

> [!NOTE]
> **Formula Pitfall:** Always express bandwidth $B$ in **bits per second** before multiplying by RTT in **seconds**. Mixing Mbps with milliseconds is the most common KTU mark-losing error in BDP problems.

## 2.5 ISP SLA Components and Engineering Utility

A typical KTU-grade ISP SLA contains four contractual guarantees:

1. **Availability** — usually **99.9% ("three nines")** for business circuits, equating to **≤ 8.76 hours/year** of downtime, or **99.99% ("four nines")** for cloud on-ramp circuits, equating to **≤ 52.6 minutes/year**.
2. **Latency** — average one-way delay, often **< 20 ms** intra-metro, **< 80 ms** inter-continental.
3. **Jitter** — typically **< 5 ms** for enterprise classes.
4. **Packet Loss** — usually **< 0.1%** measured over a 5-minute interval.

These are computed continuously at the **NOC** and feed into a credit calculation when breached.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Analytical Problems

## 3.1 Derivation: Maximum Achievable TCP Throughput

A foundational KTU 2024 problem asks: given link bandwidth and RTT, what is the maximum throughput one TCP connection can sustain?

### Step 1 — Define the Bandwidth-Delay Product (BDP)

The BDP is the maximum number of bits that can be "in flight" on a link at any instant — i.e., the capacity of the link's pipeline:

$$
\text{BDP} = B_{\text{link}} \times \text{RTT}
$$

### Step 2 — Set Up the Steady-State Flow

Under lossless TCP transmission, the sender's sliding window $W$ must be at least equal to the BDP, otherwise the sender will block waiting for ACKs and underutilise the link:

$$
W_{\text{required}} \ge \text{BDP}
$$

### Step 3 — Express Throughput in Terms of Window and RTT

Each window of $W$ bits is acknowledged every RTT seconds. Therefore the steady-state throughput is:

$$
\text{Throughput} = \dfrac{W}{\text{RTT}}
$$

### Step 4 — Substitute the Minimum Window

For the link to be **fully utilised**, set $W = \text{BDP}$:

$$
\text{Throughput}_{\max} = \dfrac{\text{BDP}}{\text{RTT}} = \dfrac{B \times \text{RTT}}{\text{RTT}} = B
$$

This confirms the intuitive result: with a properly sized window, TCP throughput can asymptotically approach the link bandwidth.

### Step 5 — Account for TCP Window Cap ($W_{\max}$)

If $W_{\max}$ is limited (e.g., 64 KB legacy default), then:

$$
\text{Throughput}_{\max} = \min\!\left(B,\ \dfrac{W_{\max}}{\text{RTT}}\right)
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Implication:** This is exactly why **TCP window scaling (RFC 7323)** and **large receive buffers** are essential for long-fat-pipe cloud connections (e.g., transcontinental 10 Gbps links with 200 ms RTT).

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Problem — KTU Style

> **Problem (Model Question):**
> An enterprise in Trivandrum accesses a cloud data centre in Singapore via a Tier-1 ISP backbone link of bandwidth **1 Gbps**. The measured round-trip time to the cloud's front-end load balancer is **120 ms**. The TCP receive window on the cloud-side host is capped at **256 KB**.
> **(a)** Calculate the Bandwidth-Delay Product.
> **(b)** Determine whether the TCP window is large enough to fully utilise the link. If not, compute the maximum achievable throughput.
> **(c)** Recommend a single network-engineering change to fully utilise the link.

### Solution — Part (a): Compute BDP

$$
\text{BDP} = B \times \text{RTT} = (1 \times 10^9\ \text{bits/s}) \times (120 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{s})
$$

$$
\text{BDP} = 1.2 \times 10^8\ \text{bits} = \dfrac{1.2 \times 10^8}{8 \times 1024}\ \text{KB} \approx 14{,}648\ \text{KB} \approx 14.3\ \text{MB}
$$

### Solution — Part (b): Compare Window to BDP

The TCP window is **256 KB = 262,144 bytes = 2,097,152 bits**.

Compare to the BDP of **120,000,000 bits**:

$$
W_{\max} = 2.097 \times 10^6\ \text{bits} \quad \text{vs.} \quad \text{BDP} = 1.2 \times 10^8\ \text{bits}
$$

Since $W_{\max} \ll \text{BDP}$, the window is **insufficient** by a factor of:

$$
\text{Deficit} = \dfrac{\text{BDP}}{W_{\max}} = \dfrac{1.2 \times 10^8}{2.097 \times 10^6} \approx 57.2
$$

The maximum achievable throughput is:

$$
\text{Throughput}_{\max} = \dfrac{W_{\max}}{\text{RTT}} = \dfrac{2.097 \times 10^6\ \text{bits}}{0.12\ \text{s}} \approx 1.748 \times 10^7\ \text{bits/s} \approx 17.48\ \text{Mbps}
$$

### Solution — Part (c): Engineering Recommendation

Increase the TCP receive window to at least **15 MB** (the BDP), or enable **TCP window scaling** to permit a window larger than 64 KB. Practically, configuring the cloud VM's OS to advertise a window of **16 MB** (with scaling factor 8) saturates the 1 Gbps link.

## 3.3 Worked Problem — ISP Contention Ratio

> **Problem:** A Tier-3 residential ISP in Kochi serves **4,000** subscribers on a GPON (Gigabit Passive Optical Network) uplink of **2.5 Gbps**. The advertised plan offers **500 Mbps** peak per user.
> **(a)** Compute the contention ratio at peak hours.
> **(b)** The ISP wishes to upgrade the uplink to guarantee **no oversubscription at 60% take-rate**. What uplink capacity is required?

### Part (a)

$$
\text{Peak demand} = 4{,}000 \times 500\ \text{Mbps} = 2{,}000{,}000\ \text{Mbps} = 2{,}000\ \text{Gbps}
$$

$$
\text{CR} = \dfrac{2{,}000\ \text{Gbps}}{2.5\ \text{Gbps}} = 800 : 1
$$

This is **unsustainably high** — at full peak, every user gets only ~0.625 Mbps. Real-world ISPs design for a **statistical multiplexing** assumption: not all users peak simultaneously.

### Part (b)

With **60% take-rate** of subscribers actually online at peak, **simultaneous active users = 4,000 × 0.6 = 2,400**. Assuming a **5:1 statistical oversubscription** is acceptable:

$$
\text{Required uplink} = \dfrac{2{,}400 \times 500\ \text{Mbps}}{5} = 240{,}000\ \text{Mbps} = 240\ \text{Gbps}
$$

This is a 96× upgrade over the original 2.5 Gbps link — illustrating why **fibre aggregation and peering capacity** dominate Tier-3 CAPEX decisions.

## 3.4 Availability-to-Downtime Conversion

> **Derivation (Key KTU 2024 Skill):**
> Given an availability $A$ (decimal), the **annual downtime budget** is:

$$
D_{\text{year}} = (1 - A) \times 365 \times 24 \times 60\ \text{minutes}
$$

$$
= (1 - A) \times 525{,}600\ \text{minutes/year}
$$

**Numerical example:**

$$
A = 99.99\% = 0.9999
$$

$$
D_{\text{year}} = (1 - 0.9999) \times 525{,}600 = 0.0001 \times 525{,}600 = 52.56\ \text{minutes/year}
$$

This is the contractual baseline for **"four-nines"** cloud connectivity.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4.2 Functional Architecture of a Single ISP — Block Diagram

This diagram maps the **internal functional blocks** of a single ISP's infrastructure, including the **NOC**, **PoP**, **DNS**, **BGP edge**, and **CPE-facing** subsystems.

```mermaid

        CPE[Customer CPE\nRouter / ONT]:::cpe
    end

    end
```

## 4.3 Packet Flow — User Request to Cloud (Sequence Diagram)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant U as End User
    participant T3 as Tier-3 ISP\n(Local Access)
    participant T2 as Tier-2 ISP\n(Regional Transit)
    participant T1 as Tier-1 ISP\n(Backbone)
    participant IXP as IXP Peering Point
    participant CLD as Cloud Data Centre
    
    U->>T3: HTTPS GET /api/data
    Note over U,T3: Last-mile contention check
    T3->>T2: BGP-routed packet\n(Paid transit)
    Note over T2: Latency added\n+ d_trans
    T2->>T1: BGP-routed packet
    Note over T1: Backbone propagation\n+ d_prop
    T1->>IXP: Interconnect handoff
    IXP->>CLD: Peering exchange\n(Settlement-free)
    CLD-->>IXP: 200 OK + payload
    IXP-->>T1: Return path
    T1-->>T2: Return path
    T2-->>T3: Return path
    T3-->>U: Page rendered
    Note over U,CLD: Total RTT = sum of all nodal delays
```

## 4.4 Caption / Explanation

The diagrams above together illustrate:
1. **The Global Hierarchy** — Tier-1/2/3 with peering and transit relationships.
2. **Internal ISP Architecture** — From CPE through PoP, backbone, edge, to NOC.
3. **End-to-End Packet Path** — The actual route a cloud request takes, with each hop adding delay.

These three views are the **canonical schematics** examiners expect students to reproduce in KTU 2024 descriptive answers.
