<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Style]**
> **Q1. Define the term Internet Service Provider (ISP) and list the three tiers of ISPs in the modern Internet hierarchy. [3 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**
An ISP is an organization that provides individuals and enterprises with access to the Internet, along with related services such as email, web hosting, and domain registration. The three tiers are:
- **Tier 1:** Global backbone carriers that reach the entire Internet routing table settlement-free (e.g., Lumen, NTT).
- **Tier 2:** Regional carriers that purchase transit from Tier 1 but engage in paid peering to reduce cost.
- **Tier 3:** Local access ISPs that deliver last-mile connectivity and purchase transit from Tier 1 or Tier 2.

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Style]**
> **Q2. Differentiate between Peering and Transit arrangements between ISPs. Why do ISPs prefer peering? [3 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**
| Aspect | Peering | Transit |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **Cost** | Settlement-free (no money exchanged, usually) | Paid per Mbps |
| **Scope** | Bilateral or multilateral between two parties | Provider-to-customer |
| **Traffic** | Customer networks of each peer | Full Internet routing table |
| **BGP** | One session per peer | One session per transit provider |

ISPs prefer peering because it **eliminates recurring transit costs**, reduces **latency** (shorter AS_PATH), and provides **redundancy** through multiple interconnect points. However, peering requires **traffic-volume balance** to be sustainable — the canonical rule of thumb is that peering is profitable when traffic between the two networks is **roughly symmetric**.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Descriptive Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

> **ESE Module 3 Choice Question — Part B Pattern**
> *Answer ONE full question. Each question has sub-parts (a) for 7 marks and (b) for 7 marks.*

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Style]**

**Q (a). With a neat block diagram, explain the three-tier hierarchy of Internet Service Providers. Describe the role of Internet Exchange Points (IXPs) in interconnecting ISPs. [7 Marks]**

**Model Solution — Q (a):**

**1. Three-Tier Hierarchy (4 marks):**

The modern Internet is organized into three functional tiers:

- **Tier-1 (Global Backbone):** Owns transcontinental fibre and IP backbone. ~15–18 exist worldwide. They interconnect via **settlement-free peering** and form the Internet's core.
- **Tier-2 (Regional Carriers):** Operate within continents or large countries. They **purchase transit** from one or more Tier-1s and engage in **paid peering** with other Tier-2s.
- **Tier-3 (Access / Local ISPs):** Provide the **last-mile connection** to end users. They **only purchase transit** and have no peering.

**2. Role of IXPs (3 marks):**

An Internet Exchange Point (IXP) is a **physical infrastructure** (typically a layer-2 Ethernet fabric) where multiple ISPs co-locate their BGP edge routers to **peer directly**. Functions:
- **Reduce transit cost** by enabling settlement-free exchange.
- **Lower latency** by shortening the AS_PATH (fewer hops).
- **Increase resilience** through geographic and topological diversity.
- **Examples:** DE-CIX Frankfurt (world's largest, ~10 Tbps peak), AMS-IX Amsterdam, LINX London, NIXI (India).

**Block Diagram to be drawn by the student:** Three stacked layers — Tier 1 at top, Tier 2 in middle, Tier 3 at bottom — with arrows for "transit (paid)" flowing downward and "peering" being lateral.

---

**Q (b). An enterprise connects to a cloud data centre via a 500 Mbps link with a measured RTT of 80 ms. The TCP receive window is limited to 64 KB.
(i) Compute the Bandwidth-Delay Product.
(ii) Determine the maximum achievable throughput and identify the bottleneck.
(iii) Propose two engineering solutions. [7 Marks]**

**Model Solution — Q (b):**

**(i) Bandwidth-Delay Product (2 marks):**

$$
\text{BDP} = B \times \text{RTT} = (500 \times 10^6\ \text{bits/s}) \times (80 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{s})
$$

$$
\text{BDP} = 4 \times 10^7\ \text{bits} = \dfrac{4 \times 10^7}{8 \times 1024} = 4{,}882.8\ \text{KB} \approx 4.77\ \text{MB}
$$

**(ii) Maximum Throughput (3 marks):**

Window in bits: $W_{\max} = 64\ \text{KB} \times 8 \times 1024 = 524{,}288\ \text{bits} = 5.24 \times 10^5\ \text{bits}$

$$
\text{Throughput}_{\max} = \dfrac{W_{\max}}{\text{RTT}} = \dfrac{5.24 \times 10^5}{0.08} = 6.55 \times 10^6\ \text{bits/s} \approx 6.55\ \text{Mbps}
$$

**Bottleneck:** The TCP receive window (64 KB) is **far smaller** than the required 4.77 MB, capping the throughput at only **~6.55 Mbps** out of an available 500 Mbps. The link is **utilised at only**:

$$
\text{Utilisation} = \dfrac{6.55}{500} = 1.31\%
$$

**(iii) Engineering Solutions (2 marks):**
1. Enable **TCP window scaling (RFC 7323)** to permit windows up to 1 GB; configure both endpoints to advertise a window of at least 5 MB.
2. Use **multiple parallel TCP connections** (e.g., HTTP/2 or HTTP/3 multiplexing, or application-level striping) to aggregate throughput.
3. (Bonus) Use **QUIC (HTTP/3)** which has native stream multiplexing and better loss recovery.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Style]**

**Q (a). Explain in detail the components and functions of a typical Internet Service Provider's internal architecture. Include POP, NOC, backbone, and edge in your answer with a neat block diagram. [7 Marks]**

**Model Solution — Q (a):**

**1. Point of Presence (POP) — 2 marks:**
A POP is a **physical access node** where customers' last-mile circuits terminate. It houses **DSLAMs / OLTs** (for DSL / fibre aggregation), **BRAS / BNG** (Broadband Remote Access Server / Broadband Network Gateway) for IP session management, **DHCP / RADIUS** servers, and **aggregation switches** that funnel traffic into the core.

**2. Backbone / Core — 2 marks:**
The backbone is the **high-capacity transport layer** of the ISP, typically using **DWDM** (Dense Wavelength Division Multiplexing) over single-mode fibre, with **MPLS** or **Segment Routing (SR-MPLS / SRv6)** for traffic engineering. Modern cores run at **100 Gbps, 400 Gbps, or 800 Gbps per wavelength**.

**3. Edge / Peering — 1.5 marks:**
The **BGP edge** connects the ISP to other networks. Edge routers maintain **eBGP sessions** with transit providers and **settlement-free BGP sessions** at IXPs. They implement **route filtering, prefix-limits, and RPKI validation** to prevent hijacks.

**4. NOC — 1.5 marks:**
The Network Operations Centre is a **24×7 facility** that monitors the entire ISP network using **SNMP, streaming telemetry (gNMI/gRPC), syslog, and SIEM** platforms. It enforces SLAs, runs incident response, and coordinates with upstream providers during outages.

**Block Diagram to be drawn:** Refer to Section 4.2 of these notes.

---

**Q (b). An ISP provides a contractual SLA of 99.95% availability. Calculate:
(i) The maximum allowable annual downtime in minutes.
(ii) The MTBF given an MTTR of 2 hours.
(iii) Comment on the engineering measures the ISP must implement to honour this SLA. [7 Marks]**

**Model Solution — Q (b):**

**(i) Annual Downtime (2 marks):**

$$
D_{\text{year}} = (1 - A) \times 525{,}600\ \text{minutes} = (1 - 0.9995) \times 525{,}600
$$

$$
D_{\text{year}} = 0.0005 \times 525{,}600 = 262.8\ \text{minutes/year} \approx 4.38\ \text{hours/year}
$$

**(ii) MTBF Calculation (2 marks):**

$$
A = \dfrac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}
$$

$$
0.9995 = \dfrac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + 2}
$$

$$
0.9995\,(\text{MTBF} + 2) = \text{MTBF}
$$

$$
0.9995 \cdot \text{MTBF} + 1.999 = \text{MTBF}
$$

$$
1.999 = \text{MTBF} - 0.9995 \cdot \text{MTBF} = 0.0005 \cdot \text{MTBF}
$$

$$
\text{MTBF} = \dfrac{1.999}{0.0005} = 3{,}998\ \text{hours} \approx 166.6\ \text{days}
$$

**(iii) Engineering Measures (3 marks):**

To deliver 99.95% ("three-and-a-half nines") availability, the ISP must implement:
1. **Geographic redundancy** — duplicate PoPs in different physical locations; dual-homed connections to upstream providers.
2. **Hardware redundancy** — power supplies, fans, line cards, and core routers in failover pairs; UPS + diesel generator backup at every PoP.
3. **Protocol-level fast convergence** — BFD (Bidirectional Forwarding Detection) at sub-50 ms, BGP Graceful Restart, MPLS Fast Reroute (FRR), or TI-LFA.
4. **Operational discipline** — scheduled maintenance windows outside business hours, pre-tested change-management procedures, and 24×7 NOC escalation paths.
5. **Continuous monitoring** — synthetic transactions, SLA probes, and real-time customer-impact dashboards.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit Mismatch:** Always convert bandwidth to **bits per second** and RTT to **seconds** before computing BDP. Mixing **Mbps** with **ms** is the #1 cause of lost marks in throughput problems.
> 2. **Confusing Peering with Transit:** Peering is **settlement-free (or paid but reciprocal)**; transit is **paid, hierarchical**. Do not interchange the terms.
> 3. **Forgetting the BDP Units:** BDP yields **bits** (not bytes). Convert using $\div 8$ before expressing in KB/MB.
> 4. **SLA Downtime in Years:** When asked for "annual downtime," use **525,600 minutes/year** (= 365 × 24 × 60). Examiners mark this strictly.
> 5. **Skipping the Diagram:** KTU 2024 descriptive answers to "explain with block diagram" questions receive **at least 2–3 marks** solely for a clear, correctly-labelled diagram. Always include one.
> 6. **IPv4 vs IPv6 ASN Distinction:** Don't get confused — ASN pool is independent of IP version. Both IPv4 and IPv6 networks use the same ASN space.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 3: ISPs**

- **Definition:** An ISP is a network operator that provides Internet access and IP services to customers.

- **Three Tiers:**
  - **Tier 1** — global backbone, settlement-free peering, ~15–18 worldwide.
  - **Tier 2** — regional carrier, pays for transit, selective paid peering.
  - **Tier 3** — local access, only buys transit, owns last mile.

- **Interconnection Models:**
  - **Transit (paid):** Customer → Provider — full routing table.
  - **Peering (settlement-free):** Equal partners, mutual traffic.
  - **Paid Peering:** Asymmetric traffic → monetary settlement.

- **Key Infrastructure Components:**
  - **POP** — last-mile termination.
  - **Backbone / Core** — DWDM + MPLS / SR.
  - **Edge / BGP router** — peering & transit interconnects.
  - **NOC** — 24×7 monitoring, SLA enforcement.
  - **IXP** — neutral fabric for multi-lateral peering.

- **BGP Essentials:**
  - **ASN** uniquely identifies an ISP.
  - **AS_PATH** length is the primary path-selection metric.
  - **LOCAL_PREF** governs egress policy.
  - **MED** governs inter-AS ingress preference.

- **Critical Formulas to Memorise:**
  - $\text{BDP} = B \times \text{RTT}$
  - $\text{Throughput} = W / \text{RTT}$
  - $d_{\text{trans}} = L / R$
  - $d_{\text{prop}} = d / s$
  - $A = \text{MTBF} / (\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR})$
  - $D_{\text{year}} = (1 - A) \times 525{,}600$ minutes.

- **Cloud Computing Linkage:**
  - Cloud SLAs (latency, throughput, availability) are **bounded** by the underlying ISP infrastructure.
  - Multi-cloud / hybrid cloud designs require **multi-ISP diversity** for resilience.
  - Hyperscalers (AWS, Azure, GCP) deploy **Direct Connect / ExpressRoute / Cloud Interconnect** services to bypass public Internet unpredictability.

- **Common KTU 2024 Exam Triggers:**
  - Numerical BDP + throughput calculation.
  - SLA → downtime conversion.
  - Peering vs Transit comparison.
  - Block diagram of ISP hierarchy.
  - Contention ratio for a Tier-3 access network.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Data Centre Technology
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT525 - CLOUD COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Data Centre Technology

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Data Centre (DC):** A dedicated, secured physical facility used to house **computer systems, servers, networking equipment, storage systems, and associated components** (such as telecommunications and redundant power supplies, cooling systems, and fire suppression). It is the centralized repository — physical or virtual — for the storage, management, processing, and dissemination of data and applications critical to an organization's IT operations.

A modern data centre is the **backbone of cloud computing**, because every SaaS, PaaS, and IaaS service is ultimately provisioned, executed, and stored within geographically distributed data centres owned by hyperscale cloud providers such as AWS, Microsoft Azure, Google Cloud, and Meta.

### Key Constituents of a Data Centre (from KTU 2024 syllabus perspective)

1. **IT Infrastructure** — Servers (rack/blade), Storage arrays (SAN/NAS), Network switches & routers.
2. **Power Infrastructure** — UPS, PDUs, generators, battery banks.
3. **Cooling Infrastructure** — CRAC units, chilled water systems, free cooling.
4. **Physical Infrastructure** — Racks, raised flooring, cabling, fire suppression.
5. **Security Infrastructure** — Biometric access, CCTV, mantraps.
6. **Management Layer** — DCIM (Data Centre Infrastructure Management) software.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Hotel of Computers"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition Box:** Imagine a data centre as a giant **5-star hotel for computers**.
> - **Servers** = the **rooms** where guests (data/applications) stay.
> - **Power systems** = the **electricity** that keeps the lights on 24×7.
> - **Cooling systems** = the **air conditioning** that keeps the hotel comfortable.
> - **Networking gear** = the **lifts, corridors, and reception desk** that move data in and out.
> - **Security** = the **CCTV and door locks** that keep unwanted guests out.
> - **Tier rating** = the **star rating** of the hotel — the more stars, the more reliable and redundant the facility.

A 1-star hotel (Tier I) may have a single generator that breaks down — your data goes offline. A 4-star hotel (Tier IV) has **2N+1 redundancy** — even if multiple systems fail simultaneously, your data keeps running. Cloud providers need 4-star hotels, which is why their data centres cost billions of dollars.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics You MUST Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Highlighted Core Standards:**
> - **PUE** = **Power Usage Effectiveness** (Global benchmark for DC efficiency, defined by *The Green Grid*).
> - **DCiE** = **Data Centre Infrastructure Efficiency** (Reciprocal of PUE).
> - **Tier Standards** = ANSI/TIA-942 and **Uptime Institute Tier I–IV** classifications.
> - **Floor Loading** = typically **7.2 kN/m² to 12 kN/m²** for raised floors.
> - **Raised Floor Height** = **600 mm to 1200 mm** standard.

---

## 1.4 Why Data Centres Matter in Cloud Computing

| Cloud Service Model | Data Centre Role |
|---|---|
| **SaaS** (e.g., Gmail) | Application hosted on DC servers |
| **PaaS** (e.g., Heroku) | Runtime/DB engines run on DC |
| **IaaS** (e.g., EC2) | Raw VMs, storage, network abstracted from DC hardware |

> Every "cloud" is, in reality, just **someone else's data centre** running your workload.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Data Centre Architecture — The Layered Stack

A data centre is engineered as a **multi-layered system** for fault tolerance, scalability, and high availability.

### Layer 1 — Physical Site Layer
- Location, building construction, seismic zone, flood plain avoidance.
- Perimeter security, vehicle barriers, mantrap entries.
- **Load-bearing capacity of the raised floor must support the weight of fully loaded server racks (typically 1–2 tons per rack).**

### Layer 2 — Power Infrastructure
- **Utility Power → Transformer → Generators (Diesel) → UPS (batteries) → PDU → Servers.**
- Redundancy levels: N, N+1, 2N, 2N+1.

### Layer 3 — Cooling & Environmental
- **CRAC (Computer Room Air Conditioning)** units.
- **Hot aisle / Cold aisle** containment.
- **Free cooling / economizers** for energy efficiency.

### Layer 4 — IT Equipment
- Servers (1U, 2U, blade).
- Storage (DAS, NAS, SAN).
- Network (Top-of-Rack, End-of-Row, Spine-Leaf).

### Layer 5 — Management & Orchestration
- **DCIM** (Data Centre Infrastructure Management).
- Virtualization hypervisors (VMware ESXi, KVM, Hyper-V).
- Monitoring: SNMP, IPMI, BMC.

## 2.2 The Uptime Institute Tier Classification System

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **single most important topic** for KTU 14-mark questions on Data Centre Technology.

| Tier | Availability | Redundancy | Downtime/Year | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Tier I — Basic** | 99.671% | N (single path) | **~28.8 hours** | Small business |
| **Tier II — Redundant** | 99.741% | N+1 (single path + spare) | **~22 hours** | Mid-size enterprise |
| **Tier III — Concurrently Maintainable** | 99.982% | N+1 (dual path) | **~1.6 hours** | Cloud providers, large enterprise |
| **Tier IV — Fault Tolerant** | 99.995% | 2N+1 (dual path + full redundancy) | **~26.3 minutes** | Mission-critical (banks, gov) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Availability Formula:**
> $$\text{Availability} = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$$
> where **MTBF** = Mean Time Between Failures, **MTTR** = Mean Time To Repair.

**Redundancy Architectures Explained:**
- **N** → Capacity required = N. **Zero** redundancy. Failure = downtime.
- **N+1** → N active + 1 spare. If one component fails, the spare takes over.
- **2N** → Two independent full-capacity systems. If one path fails entirely, the other handles 100% load.
- **2N+1** → Two full-capacity systems + 1 spare. Used in **Tier IV** for fault tolerance against simultaneous failures.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulae below are exam-grade and must be memorized.

| # | Metric / Concept | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **PUE** | $\text{PUE} = \dfrac{\text{Total Facility Power}}{\text{IT Equipment Power}}$ | Ideal = 1.0; Industry avg = 1.5–1.8 |
| 2 | **DCiE** | $\text{DCiE} = \dfrac{1}{\text{PUE}} \times 100\%$ | Expressed as percentage |
| 3 | **Availability** | $\text{Availability} = \dfrac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$ | Result is a ratio (×100% for %) |
| 4 | **Annual Downtime** | $\text{Downtime} = (1 - A) \times 525{,}600 \text{ min/year}$ | 525,600 = minutes in a year |
| 5 | **Data Centre Floor Area (approx.)** | $A = N \times (Rack\_Area + Aisle\_Area)$ | sq. meters |
| 6 | **Heat Dissipation (Watts)** | $Q = P \times 3.412$ | BTU/hr; 1 W ≈ 3.412 BTU/hr |
| 7 | **Cooling Load** | $C = m \times c_p \times \Delta T$ | $c_p$ of air = 1.005 kJ/kg·K |
| 8 | **Cabling Length (Optical)** | Margin = 10–15% slack | Standard practice |

> **Engineering Utility:** PUE is used by Google, Microsoft, and Facebook to publish sustainability reports. Modern hyperscale DCs target PUE < 1.2.

## 2.4 Real-World Significance

- **Power** accounts for ~40% of DC operating cost → drives PUE optimization.
- **Cooling** accounts for ~35–40% of facility power → hot/cold aisle containment is critical.
- **IT load** itself is only ~50–60% of total facility power in traditional DCs (rest = overhead).
- Cloud economics depend on **PUE < 1.5**; higher PUE = higher cost → lower cloud margins.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — PUE & DCiE Computation

> **Problem:** A data centre consumes a **total facility power of 12 MW**. The IT equipment (servers + storage + network) draws **8 MW**. Calculate:
> (a) PUE
> (b) DCiE
> (c) Power lost in overhead (cooling, lighting, UPS loss)

### Step 1 — Identify Inputs
- Total Facility Power ($P_{total}$) = 12 MW
- IT Equipment Power ($P_{IT}$) = 8 MW
- Overhead Power ($P_{oh}$) = $P_{total} - P_{IT}$

### Step 2 — Calculate Overhead Power

$$
P_{oh} = P_{total} - P_{IT}
$$

$$
P_{oh} = 12 \text{ MW} - 8 \text{ MW} = 4 \text{ MW}
$$

So **4 MW** is consumed by cooling, lighting, UPS, and PDU losses.

### Step 3 — Calculate PUE

$$
\text{PUE} = \frac{P_{total}}{P_{IT}}
$$

$$
\text{PUE} = \frac{12 \text{ MW}}{8 \text{ MW}} = 1.5
$$

> This data centre has a **PUE of 1.5**, which is the **global industry average** (The Green Grid, 2023).

### Step 4 — Calculate DCiE

$$
\text{DCiE} = \frac{1}{\text{PUE}} \times 100\%
$$

$$
\text{DCiE} = \frac{1}{1.5} \times 100\% = 66.67\%
$$

> This means **66.67%** of the facility power actually reaches IT equipment. The rest (33.33%) is overhead.

### Step 5 — Interpretation
A PUE of 1.5 means for every **1 unit** of useful compute power, **0.5 units** are wasted on infrastructure overhead.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Availability and Annual Downtime

> **Problem:** A cloud provider's data centre has **MTBF = 5000 hours** and **MTTR = 1 hour**. Calculate:
> (a) Availability ratio
> (b) Annual downtime in minutes
> (c) Identify the equivalent Tier level.

### Step 1 — Apply the Availability Formula

$$
A = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}
$$

$$
A = \frac{5000}{5000 + 1} = \frac{5000}{5001}
$$

$$
A \approx 0.99980 = 99.980\%
$$

### Step 2 — Compute Annual Downtime

First, convert to fraction:

$$
A_{frac} = 1 - 0.99980 = 0.0002
$$

Annual downtime in minutes:

$$
D = 0.0002 \times 525{,}600 \text{ min}
$$

$$
D = 105.12 \text{ minutes/year} \approx 1 \text{ hour } 45 \text{ min}
$$

### Step 3 — Tier Identification
Compare with the Tier table:
- Tier III: 99.982% → ~1.6 hours/year ≈ 96 min.
- Tier IV: 99.995% → ~26.3 min/year.

Our DC at **99.980% with 105 min downtime** falls just under Tier III. By improving MTTR to 0.5 hours:

$$
A = \frac{5000}{5000.5} = 99.990\% \quad \Rightarrow \quad 52.5 \text{ min/year}
$$

This places it between **Tier III and Tier IV**.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Cooling Load Calculation

> **Problem:** A server rack draws **5 kW** of power. Compute the heat it dumps into the room (in BTU/hr).

### Step 1 — Understand the 1:1 Conversion
**All electrical power consumed by IT equipment is eventually converted to heat** (100% conversion by the First Law of Thermodynamics, ignoring negligible light/sound).

### Step 2 — Apply Conversion Factor

$$
Q = P \times 3.412 \text{ BTU/hr per Watt}
$$

$$
Q = 5000 \text{ W} \times 3.412 = 17{,}060 \text{ BTU/hr}
$$

### Step 3 — Realistic Sizing
A single 5 kW rack requires a **CRAC unit sized to remove at least 17,060 BTU/hr** to maintain ambient temperature (typically 20–24 °C).

---

## 3.4 Analytical Summary Table — Key Trade-offs

| Design Choice | Pros | Cons | KTU Viewpoint |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Tier I** | Lowest cost | High downtime risk | Rarely used in cloud |
| **Tier IV** | < 30 min/year downtime | ~2.5× cost of Tier III | Used by AWS GovCloud, financial DCs |
| **Hot/Cold Aisle Containment** | PUE reduction by 20–30% | Higher initial CapEx | Strongly recommended |
| **Free Cooling** | Huge energy savings | Climate-dependent | Used in Nordic DCs (Google, Facebook) |
| **Blade Servers** | High density | More heat, more power | Cloud's preferred choice |
| **Hyperconverged Infrastructure (HCI)** | Simplified management | Vendor lock-in | Modern cloud trend |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Data Centre End-to-End Architecture (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Utility Power Grid 11kV HV] --> B[Step Down Transformer]
    B --> C[Generator Diesel Backup]
    B --> D[UPS System with Battery Bank]
    D --> E[PDU Power Distribution Unit]
    E --> F[Server Racks IT Load]
    F --> G[CRAC Cooling Units]
    G --> H[Chiller Plant or Free Cooling]
    F --> I[Top of Rack Switch TOR]
    I --> J[End of Row Switch EOR]
    J --> K[Core Spine Switch]
    K --> L[Edge Router to Internet]
    M[DCIM Management Software] -. monitors .-> F
    M -. monitors .-> D
    M -. monitors .-> G
    N[Security Biometric CCTV Mantrap] -. protects .-> F
```

> [!NOTE]
> This block diagram is a **safe fallback** for the prompt's "complex physical drawing" rule. It shows every major subsystem and the monitoring plane.

## 4.2 Hot Aisle / Cold Aisle Containment Layout

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Server Rack Front Cold Air Intake] --> B[Cold Aisle Sealed]
    B --> C[Perforated Floor Tiles]
    C --> D[Server Rack Rear Hot Air Exhaust]
    D --> E[Hot Aisle Sealed]
    E --> F[CRAC Return Air]
    F --> G[CRAC Unit Cools Air]
    G --> H[CRAC Supply Plenum]
    H --> I[Raised Floor Plenum]
    I --> B
```

> This is the **standard thermal management topology** in modern data centres. It separates intake and exhaust airflows to eliminate hot-cold mixing, reducing cooling energy by up to 30%.

## 4.3 Tier Classification Decision Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Data Centre Design] --> B{Number of Power Paths?}
    B -->|Single Path| C[Tier I Basic]
    B -->|Single Path + Spare| D[Tier II Redundant]
    B -->|Dual Path Concurrently Maintainable| E[Tier III]
    B -->|Dual Path Full Fault Tolerant| F[Tier IV]
    C --> G[99.671% Availability]
    D --> H[99.741% Availability]
    E --> I[99.982% Availability]
    F --> J[99.995% Availability]
    G --> K[28.8 hrs downtime per year]
    H --> L[22 hrs downtime per year]
    I --> M[1.6 hrs downtime per year]
    J --> N[26.3 minutes downtime per year]
```

## 4.4 Data Centre Network Topology — Spine-Leaf

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SpineLayer[Spine Layer Core Switches]
        S1[Spine 1]
        S2[Spine 2]
    end
    subgraph LeafLayer[Leaf Layer ToR Switches]
        L1[Leaf 1 Rack 1]
        L2[Leaf 2 Rack 2]
        L3[Leaf 3 Rack 3]
        L4[Leaf 4 Rack 4]
    end
    S1 --- L1
    S1 --- L2
    S1 --- L3
    S1 --- L4
    S2 --- L1
    S2 --- L2
    S2 --- L3
    S2 --- L4
    L1 --> R1[Server Rack 1]
    L2 --> R2[Server Rack 2]
    L3 --> R3[Server Rack 3]
    L4 --> R4[Server Rack 4]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Spine-Leaf topology** is the de-facto standard for modern cloud data centres (used in AWS, Azure, Facebook). It guarantees predictable latency and full bisectional bandwidth.

## 4.5 Virtualization Stack inside the Data Centre

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Bare Metal Server Hardware] --> B[Hypervisor Layer VMware ESXi KVM Hyper V]
    B --> C[Virtual Machine 1]
    B --> D[Virtual Machine 2]
    B --> E[Virtual Machine 3]
    F[Virtual Switch vSwitch] -. connects .-> C
    F -. connects .-> D
    F -. connects .-> E
    G[Shared Storage SAN NAS] -. backs .-> C
    G -. backs .-> D
    G -. backs .-> E
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## **Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)**

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Define Data Centre. List any four key components of a data centre.**

**Model Answer:**
A data centre is a dedicated physical facility that houses computer systems, servers, networking equipment, storage devices, and supporting infrastructure such as power backup, cooling, and security systems for centralized data processing, storage, and management.

**Four key components:**
1. **IT Equipment** — servers, storage, switches.
2. **Power Infrastructure** — UPS, generators, PDUs.
3. **Cooling Infrastructure** — CRAC, chillers, free cooling.
4. **Security & Management** — biometric access, DCIM software, fire suppression.

*(Examiner's note: Any 4 valid components accepted. Avoid vague terms like "computers only".)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**What is PUE? State its ideal value and the industry average.**

**Model Answer:**
**PUE (Power Usage Effectiveness)** is the ratio of total facility power consumed by a data centre to the power consumed by IT equipment.

$$
\text{PUE} = \frac{\text{Total Facility Power}}{\text{IT Equipment Power}}
$$

- **Ideal value** = **1.0** (100% of power reaches IT).
- **Global industry average** ≈ **1.5 – 1.8** (as per The Green Grid).
- Hyperscale cloud providers target **PUE < 1.2**.

---

---

## **Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)**
### *(Internal Choice Pattern — As per KTU 2024 ESE Module Layout)*

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024]**

**Q. (a)** Explain the **Uptime Institute Tier Classification System** for data centres in detail with availability and redundancy levels. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A data centre has **Total Facility Power = 18 MW** and **IT Load = 12 MW**. Compute **PUE, DCiE, and Overhead Power Loss**. Comment on the efficiency. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]**

The **Uptime Institute** defines **four tiers** for data centre infrastructure based on redundancy and availability:

**Tier I — Basic Site Infrastructure (99.671% availability)**
- Single path for power and cooling (N configuration).
- No redundant components.
- Annual downtime ≈ **28.8 hours**.
- Suitable for small businesses with non-critical workloads.

**Tier II — Redundant Site Infrastructure (99.741% availability)**
- N+1 redundancy: single active path + one redundant component.
- Slightly improved power and cooling redundancy.
- Annual downtime ≈ **22 hours**.

**Tier III — Concurrently Maintainable Site Infrastructure (99.982% availability)**
- Dual power and cooling paths (N+1 on each).
- Allows **planned maintenance without shutting down** IT operations.
- Annual downtime ≈ **1.6 hours (~96 minutes)**.
- Most cloud providers (AWS, Azure) operate at Tier III baseline.

**Tier IV — Fault Tolerant Site Infrastructure (99.995% availability)**
- **2N+1 redundancy**: two fully independent systems + additional spare.
- Tolerates **simultaneous failure of multiple components** during planned or unplanned events.
- Annual downtime ≈ **26.3 minutes**.
- Used by financial institutions, government, military clouds.

> **Key takeaway:** Higher tier = greater availability = exponentially higher cost and complexity.

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]**

**Given:**
- $P_{total}$ = 18 MW
- $P_{IT}$ = 12 MW

**Step 1 — Overhead Power:**

$$
P_{oh} = 18 - 12 = 6 \text{ MW}
$$

**Step 2 — PUE:**

$$
\text{PUE} = \frac{18}{12} = 1.5
$$

**Step 3 — DCiE:**

$$
\text{DCiE} = \frac{1}{1.5} \times 100\% = 66.67\%
$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation:**
- **66.67%** of total facility power reaches IT equipment.
- **33.33% (6 MW)** is lost to cooling, lighting, UPS conversion losses, and PDU distribution.
- This is exactly the **global industry average** for a well-managed data centre.
- A PUE of 1.5 is **acceptable** but not world-class — hyperscale DCs aim for 1.1–1.2.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

**Q. (a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **architecture of a modern data centre** covering all major subsystems. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **green data centre initiatives** and how virtualization improves data centre efficiency. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]**

A modern data centre architecture consists of **five integrated layers**:

1. **Site & Physical Layer** — Secure building, raised floor (600–1200 mm), seismic-rated construction, perimeter fencing, mantraps.
2. **Power Layer** — Utility → Transformer → Diesel Generator (N+1 or 2N) → UPS (battery) → PDU → IT load. Includes automatic transfer switches (ATS).
3. **Cooling Layer** — CRAC/CRAH units with hot/cold aisle containment, chilled water plant, free cooling economizers for outside air usage.
4. **IT Layer** — Rack servers, blade servers, SAN/NAS storage, top-of-rack switches, spine-leaf network fabric.
5. **Management Layer** — DCIM software, virtualization hypervisors (VMware ESXi, KVM), monitoring tools (SNMP, IPMI, Prometheus).

> **Refer to Section 4.1 Mermaid diagram for the schematic representation.**

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]**

**Green Data Centre Initiatives:**

1. **Renewable Energy** — Solar, wind, hydro powered DCs (Google's carbon-neutral DCs since 2007).
2. **Free Cooling / Economizers** — Use cold outside air to chill servers (used in Nordic regions).
3. **Hot/Cold Aisle Containment** — Reduces cooling energy by 20–30%.
4. **High-Efficiency UPS** — Modern UPS reach 96–98% efficiency vs old 85%.
5. **Server Consolidation via Virtualization** — Run multiple VMs on one physical server, increasing utilization from 10–15% to 70–80%.
6. **Liquid Cooling** — Direct-to-chip or immersion cooling for HPC and AI workloads.
7. **Waste Heat Reuse** — Channel exhaust heat to nearby buildings/greenhouses.

**Role of Virtualization:**
- Decouples OS and applications from physical hardware.
- Enables **server consolidation** (10:1 ratio typical).
- Reduces power, cooling, and physical space.
- Enables **rapid cloud provisioning** (the foundation of IaaS).
- Improves **PUE indirectly** by reducing the number of physical machines.

---

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing Tier II and Tier III** — Tier II is N+1 with a SINGLE active path. Tier III is DUAL path (active/active). Students often mix this up.
> 2. **PUE = 1.0 is IDEAL, not actual** — Never claim any real DC has PUE = 1.0 except theoretical. Google DCs come close (1.10).
> 3. **Forgetting units** in calculations — Always write MW, BTU/hr, or minutes. No units = 0.5 mark penalty.
> 4. **Drawing physical building sketches** — Examiners expect **block diagrams**, not architectural drawings.
> 5. **Skipping the 525,600 conversion** for annual downtime — Common 1-mark loss.
> 6. **Writing "cloud is virtual"** in green DC questions — Always tie it back to **physical hardware consolidation**.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Data Centre** = centralized facility for compute, storage, networking.
- ✅ **PUE** = Total Facility Power / IT Power. **Ideal = 1.0**, industry avg ≈ 1.5.
- ✅ **DCiE** = 1/PUE × 100%. Complement of PUE.
- ✅ **Availability = MTBF / (MTBF + MTTR)**.
- ✅ **Annual minutes = 525,600**.
- ✅ **Tier I**: 99.671% — 28.8 hr/yr downtime — N config.
- ✅ **Tier II**: 99.741% — 22 hr/yr — N+1 single path.
- ✅ **Tier III**: 99.982% — 1.6 hr/yr — N+1 **dual path**, concurrently maintainable.
- ✅ **Tier IV**: 99.995% — 26.3 min/yr — **2N+1**, fault tolerant.
- ✅ **5 layers**: Site, Power, Cooling, IT, Management.
- ✅ **Hot/Cold aisle containment** reduces cooling load by 20–30%.
- ✅ **Spine-Leaf topology** is the modern DC network standard.
- ✅ **Virtualization** = enabler of cloud computing inside DCs.
- ✅ **Green DC techniques**: free cooling, renewable energy, server consolidation, liquid cooling.
- ✅ **Heat conversion**: 1 W = 3.412 BTU/hr (must know for numericals).
- ✅ **Cloud = someone else's data centre** — every PaaS, SaaS, IaaS runs on a physical DC.
- ✅ **DCIM** = Data Centre Infrastructure Management software.
- ✅ **CRAC** = Computer Room Air Conditioning unit.
- ✅ **Raised floor height** standard = 600–1200 mm.
- ✅ **Green Grid** = industry consortium that defined PUE.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Web Technology
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT525 - CLOUD COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Web Technology

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Web Technology (KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition):** Web Technology refers to the integrated set of protocols, markup languages, programming frameworks, client-server communication mechanisms, and service-oriented architectures that collectively enable the creation, deployment, management, and consumption of distributed hypertext applications over the Internet or intranets. In the context of Cloud Computing, Web Technology forms the foundational *delivery layer* through which Software-as-a-Service (SaaS), Platform-as-a-Service (PaaS), and Infrastructure-as-a-Service (IaaS) resources are exposed to end-users via standard web browsers and HTTP-based APIs.

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme** Outcome-Based framework for *PECPT525 Cloud Computing*, "Web Technology" within Module 3 is treated as the **primary user-facing surface** of every cloud deployment. It is not merely about "building websites" — it covers the entire stack from how a browser resolves a domain name, to how a REST API authenticates a user, to how a CDN accelerates content delivery.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Smart Restaurant" Model

Imagine Web Technology as a **highly automated, multi-floor restaurant** in a metropolitan city:

| Restaurant Component | Web Technology Equivalent | Role |
|---|---|---|
| **Dining Hall & Menu Card** | Web Browser + HTML/CSS UI | Customer-facing interface |
| **Reception Desk** | DNS + Load Balancer | Routes incoming requests |
| **Waiter** | Web Server (Apache/Nginx) | Takes orders, serves food |
| **Kitchen** | Application Server (Node.js/Java/Python) | Processes business logic |
| **Cold Storage** | Database (MySQL/MongoDB) | Stores persistent data |
| **Recipe Book (Standardized)** | REST / SOAP API Contract | Communication protocol |
| **Quality Inspector** | TLS/SSL + Authentication | Security layer |
| **Food Delivery Van** | CDN (Content Delivery Network) | Caches content near users |

When you (the customer) sit in the dining hall and order a pizza (HTTP request), the menu card (HTML) shows options, the waiter (web server) takes it to the kitchen (app server), the cold storage (database) provides ingredients, the recipe book (API rules) ensures consistency, the inspector (TLS) checks authenticity, and the delivery van (CDN) ensures the pizza reaches you hot and fast.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for Cloud Computing:** In a pure cloud model, the "restaurant" itself is *elastic* — if 10,000 customers suddenly order, the kitchen magically expands more chefs (auto-scaling), and the dining hall adds more tables (horizontal scaling). This is **the fundamental link** between Web Technology and Cloud Computing.

---

## 1.3 Foundational Standards and Metrics

The following are the **canonical standards** (memorize these for KTU exams) that govern Web Technology:

- **HTTP/1.1** (RFC 2616) — *Stateless request-response protocol*
- **HTTP/2** (RFC 7540) — *Binary framing, multiplexed, HPACK header compression*
- **HTTP/3** (RFC 9114) — *Built on QUIC (UDP-based), 0-RTT handshake*
- **HTTPS** — *HTTP over TLS 1.3 (RFC 8446), default port 443*
- **HTML5** (W3C Recommendation, 2014) — *Markup language for structured documents*
- **CSS3** — *Style sheet language, currently modular*
- **JavaScript (ECMAScript 2024 / ES14)** — *Client & server scripting*
- **REST** — *Representational State Transfer (Roy Fielding, 2000 dissertation)*
- **SOAP** — *Simple Object Access Protocol (W3C, XML-based)*
- **JSON** (RFC 8259) — *JavaScript Object Notation, language-independent data format*
- **OAuth 2.0** (RFC 6749) — *Authorization framework*
- **JWT** (RFC 7519) — *JSON Web Token for stateless authentication*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Performance Metric to Memorize:** **Time To First Byte (TTFB)** — the duration from the client making an HTTP request to receiving the very first byte of the response. A good web stack should have TTFB **< 200 ms**, while a poor one may exceed **2,000 ms**. Google Core Web Vitals target *Largest Contentful Paint (LCP)* **< 2.5 seconds**.

---

## 1.4 KTU 2024 Course Outcome Alignment

| CO ID | Course Outcome (Module 3 mapping) | Bloom's Level |
|---|---|---|
| **CO3** | Explain web architectures, protocols, and service models used in cloud platforms | Understand (L2) |
| **CO4** | Design and deploy web services over cloud infrastructure | Apply (L3) |
| **CO5** | Analyze web security, scalability, and performance trade-offs | Analyze (L4) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Web Technology Stack — Layered Decomposition

Web Technology in Cloud Computing is conventionally broken into **five logical layers**. Each layer abstracts the complexity of the layer beneath it.

### Layer 1 — Presentation Layer (Client-Side)
This is what the user *sees and interacts with* in a browser. Built using:
- **HTML5** — Provides document structure (`<header>`, `<article>`, `<nav>`)
- **CSS3** — Provides styling, animations, responsive design (Flexbox, Grid)
- **JavaScript (ES14)** — Provides dynamic behavior, DOM manipulation
- **Frontend Frameworks** — React (Meta), Angular (Google), Vue.js
- **Build Tools** — Webpack, Vite, esbuild

### Layer 2 — Communication Layer (Transport Protocols)
- **HTTP/HTTPS** — The lingua franca of the web
- **WebSockets** — Full-duplex persistent connection (RFC 6455)
- **Server-Sent Events (SSE)** — One-way server-to-client streaming
- **gRPC** — High-performance RPC framework using HTTP/2 + Protocol Buffers

### Layer 3 — Application/Logic Layer (Server-Side)
- **Languages:** Python, Java, Node.js, Go, PHP, Ruby, C#
- **Frameworks:** Django, Spring Boot, Express.js, Flask, FastAPI, Laravel
- **Web Servers:** Nginx, Apache HTTP Server, Microsoft IIS, Caddy
- **Application Servers:** Tomcat, JBoss/WildFly, WebLogic, Gunicorn

### Layer 4 — Data Layer (Persistence)
- **Relational (SQL):** PostgreSQL, MySQL, Oracle, SQL Server
- **Document (NoSQL):** MongoDB, CouchDB
- **Key-Value:** Redis, Memcached, DynamoDB
- **Wide-Column:** Cassandra, HBase
- **Graph:** Neo4j

### Layer 5 — Infrastructure Layer (Cloud)
- **Compute:** EC2, Azure VMs, GCP Compute Engine, Kubernetes Pods
- **Storage:** S3, Azure Blob, GCP Cloud Storage
- **Network:** VPCs, Subnets, Load Balancers, CDN edges
- **Orchestration:** Kubernetes, Docker Swarm, Nomad

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic for KTU exams:** *"Pretty Cats Always Deliver Infrastructure"* → Presentation, Communication, Application, Data, Infrastructure.

---

## 2.2 HTTP Request–Response Cycle — The Heartbeat of the Web

Every web interaction, from loading a webpage to calling a cloud API, follows the **HTTP transaction model**.

### Step-by-Step Logical Flow

1. **URL Parsing** — Browser parses `https://api.example.com/users/42`
2. **DNS Resolution** — `api.example.com` → IP address (e.g., `203.0.113.42`) via recursive resolver
3. **TCP Three-Way Handshake** — `SYN` → `SYN-ACK` → `ACK`
4. **TLS Handshake** (for HTTPS) — Negotiates cipher suite, validates certificate, derives session keys
5. **HTTP Request Sent** — Contains method, path, headers, optional body
6. **Server Processing** — Web server → application server → database → response generation
7. **HTTP Response Returned** — Contains status code, headers, body (HTML/JSON/XML)
8. **Connection Management** — `keep-alive` reuses the TCP connection for subsequent requests

### HTTP Methods — The 9-Verb Vocabulary

| Method | Idempotent? | Safe? | Purpose | Example |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `GET` | Yes | Yes | Retrieve a resource | `GET /users/42` |
| `POST` | No | No | Create a new resource | `POST /users` |
| `PUT` | Yes | No | Replace a resource entirely | `PUT /users/42` |
| `PATCH` | No | No | Partial update | `PATCH /users/42` |
| `DELETE` | Yes | No | Delete a resource | `DELETE /users/42` |
| `HEAD` | Yes | Yes | Same as GET, no body | Health checks |
| `OPTIONS` | Yes | Yes | Describe communication options | CORS preflight |
| `CONNECT` | No | No | Tunnel (e.g., HTTPS proxy) | `CONNECT example.com:443` |
| `TRACE` | Yes | No | Echo back the request (diagnostic) | Debugging |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Favourite:** *"Idempotent"* means calling the operation N times produces the same end state as calling it once. `POST` is **not** idempotent (creates duplicates), but `PUT` and `DELETE` **are**.

### HTTP Status Codes — The 5-Class Hierarchy

| Class | Range | Meaning | Common Codes |
|---|---|---|---|
| **1xx Informational** | 100–199 | Request received, continuing | `100 Continue`, `101 Switching Protocols` |
| **2xx Success** | 200–299 | Request successfully received | `200 OK`, `201 Created`, `204 No Content` |
| **3xx Redirection** | 300–399 | Further action needed | `301 Moved Permanently`, `304 Not Modified` |
| **4xx Client Error** | 400–499 | Bad request from client | `400 Bad Request`, `401 Unauthorized`, `403 Forbidden`, `404 Not Found`, `429 Too Many Requests` |
| **5xx Server Error** | 500–599 | Server failed to fulfill valid request | `500 Internal Server Error`, `502 Bad Gateway`, `503 Service Unavailable`, `504 Gateway Timeout` |

---

## 2.3 Web Services Architecture — SOAP vs REST vs GraphQL

### REST (Representational State Transfer) — The De Facto Standard
- **Architectural style** (not a protocol) defined by Roy Fielding in his 2000 dissertation
- Uses **HTTP semantics natively** (GET = read, POST = create, etc.)
- **Stateless** — each request contains all info needed
- **Resource-oriented** — URLs identify resources, not actions
- Data formats: typically JSON (also XML, YAML, HTML)
- **Cacheable**, **Uniform Interface**, **Layered System**

### SOAP (Simple Object Access Protocol)
- **Formal protocol** with strict W3C standards
- XML-based envelope structure
- Transport-agnostic (HTTP, SMTP, JMS, TCP)
- Built-in **WS-Security**, **WS-ReliableMessaging**
- Used in **enterprise** (banking, healthcare, government)

### GraphQL (by Facebook, 2015)
- **Query language** for APIs
- Single endpoint, client specifies exact fields needed
- Eliminates over-fetching and under-fetching
- Strongly typed schema

### Quick Comparison Table

| Feature | REST | SOAP | GraphQL |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Type** | Architectural Style | Protocol | Query Language |
| **Data Format** | JSON (typical) | XML only | JSON |
| **Transport** | HTTP/HTTPS | HTTP/SMTP/TCP | HTTP/HTTPS |
| **Contract** | OpenAPI (optional) | WSDL (mandatory) | SDL Schema |
| **Caching** | Native HTTP caching | Complex | Difficult |
| **Learning Curve** | Low | High | Medium |
| **Cloud-Native Fit** | Excellent | Legacy | Excellent |

---

## 2.4 Web Servers vs Application Servers

This distinction is a **favourite KTU question**.

| Aspect | Web Server | Application Server |
|---|---|---|
| **Primary Role** | Serves static content (HTML, CSS, JS, images) | Executes business logic, dynamic content |
| **Examples** | Nginx, Apache, IIS, Caddy | Tomcat, JBoss, WebLogic, Gunicorn |
| **Protocol** | HTTP | HTTP + proprietary (AJP, IIOP) |
| **Language** | Any (static files) | Java/Python/Node/Ruby/Go |
| **In Cloud Setup** | Edge / Reverse Proxy | Behind the proxy, internal |

> [!NOTE]
> **In Modern Cloud Architecture**, Nginx often serves as a **reverse proxy** in front of multiple Node.js/Python application servers — a pattern called *Reverse Proxy + Load Balancing*.

---

## 2.5 Scalability Patterns in Web Technology

### Vertical Scaling (Scale Up)
- Adding more CPU/RAM/Disk to a *single* server
- **Pros:** Simple, no code changes
- **Cons:** Hardware ceiling, single point of failure

### Horizontal Scaling (Scale Out)
- Adding *more* servers behind a load balancer
- **Pros:** Theoretically infinite, fault-tolerant
- **Cons:** Requires stateless design, session management complexity

### Load Balancing Algorithms

| Algorithm | How It Works | Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| **Round Robin** | Sequential distribution | Equal server capacity |
| **Weighted Round Robin** | Round Robin + weights | Heterogeneous servers |
| **Least Connections** | Send to server with fewest active connections | Long-lived sessions |
| **IP Hash** | Hash(client IP) mod N servers | Session affinity |
| **Least Response Time** | Health + speed-based | Performance-critical |
| **Random** | Uniform random selection | Very large server pools |

---

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following formulas are essential for solving numerical/analytical questions in KTU Module 3 exams.**

### Performance & Capacity Formulas

| Quantity | Formula | Variables | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Response Time (RT)** | $RT = T_{network} + T_{server} + T_{db}$ | $T_{network}$ = network delay, $T_{server}$ = processing, $T_{db}$ = database query | seconds (s) |
| **Throughput (X)** | $X = \dfrac{N}{T}$ | $N$ = requests completed, $T$ = time interval | req/s |
| **Little's Law** | $L = \lambda \cdot W$ | $L$ = avg concurrent users, $\lambda$ = arrival rate, $W$ = avg time in system | dimensionless |
| **Server Utilization (ρ)** | $\rho = \dfrac{\lambda}{\mu}$ | $\lambda$ = arrival rate, $\mu$ = service rate (per server) | 0 ≤ ρ < 1 |
| **M/M/1 Queue Wait Time** | $W_q = \dfrac{\rho}{\mu - \lambda}$ | $\rho$ = utilization, $\mu$ = service rate | seconds |
| **M/M/1 Total Time** | $W = \dfrac{1}{\mu - \lambda}$ | M/M/1 single-server queue | seconds |
| **Bandwidth Required** | $BW = \dfrac{P \cdot S \cdot 8}{T}$ | $P$ = concurrent users, $S$ = avg page size (bytes), $T$ = target time | bits/second |
| **CDN Cache Hit Ratio** | $HR = \dfrac{C_{hit}}{C_{hit} + C_{miss}}$ | $C_{hit}$ = cache hits, $C_{miss}$ = cache misses | 0 to 1 (or %) |
| **Compression Ratio** | $CR = \dfrac{O_{size}}{I_{size}}$ | $O_{size}$ = output size, $I_{size}$ = input size | dimensionless |
| **Storage per User (TB)** | $S_{user} = \dfrac{A \cdot R \cdot 365 \cdot T_{retention}}{10^{12}}$ | $A$ = avg daily data per user (GB), $R$ = replication factor, $T_{retention}$ in years | Terabytes |

### Cost & Cloud Capacity Formulas

| Quantity | Formula | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| **Monthly Bandwidth Cost** | $C_{bw} = B_{GB} \cdot P_{GB}$ | $P_{GB}$ = cloud provider's $/GB egress rate |
| **Total Cost of Ownership (TCO)** | $TCO = C_{capex} + \sum_{t=1}^{n} \dfrac{C_{opex}}{(1+r)^t}$ | Discounted cash flow over $n$ years |
| **Required Servers** | $N_{servers} = \lceil \dfrac{\lambda_{peak}}{\mu \cdot \rho_{safe}} \rceil$ | $\rho_{safe}$ typically 0.6 to 0.7 |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Exam Trap:** Students often write $\rho = \dfrac{\mu}{\lambda}$ — this is **WRONG**. Utilization = arrival rate ÷ service rate. Memorize: *"Utilization is how busy the server is, so it's requests coming in divided by requests the server can handle."*

| **E-Commerce (Amazon, Flipkart)** | REST APIs, microservices, CDN for product images, JWT auth |

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Estimating Required Web Servers Using M/M/1 Queuing Model

This is a **classic KTU numerical problem**. Given user load, service rate, and target utilization, derive the minimum number of servers needed.

### Problem Setup
A cloud-hosted web application receives an average of **$\lambda = 300$ requests per second**. Each server can handle **$\mu = 100$ requests per second** (service rate). The operations team wants to keep server utilization below **$\rho_{safe} = 0.75$** for stability (to avoid queue explosion). How many servers are needed?

---

### Step 1 — Identify the Queueing Model

We model each server as an **M/M/1 queue**:
- **M** = Markovian (Poisson) inter-arrival times
- **M** = Markovian (exponential) service times
- **1** = Single server

For multiple servers, we extend to **M/M/c** (where $c$ is the number of servers).

---

### Step 2 — Compute Per-Server Utilization

$$
\rho = \frac{\lambda}{\mu} = \frac{300}{100} = 3.0
$$

**Interpretation:** One server would be utilized at **300%**, which is impossible. We definitely need multiple servers.

---

### Step 3 — Compute Number of Servers for Theoretical Utilization

If we ignore the safe threshold for a moment:

$$
c_{min} = \left\lceil \frac{\lambda}{\mu} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{300}{100} \right\rceil = 3 \text{ servers}
$$

With 3 servers, utilization would be $\rho = 300 / (3 \times 100) = 1.0$ — **saturated**, leading to infinite queue length. Not acceptable.

---

### Step 4 — Apply the Safe Utilization Threshold

We want per-server utilization $\rho \leq 0.75$:

$$
N_{servers} = \left\lceil \frac{\lambda}{\mu \cdot \rho_{safe}} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{300}{100 \times 0.75} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{300}{75} \right\rceil = \lceil 4.0 \rceil = 4 \text{ servers}
$$

---

### Step 5 — Verify the Actual Utilization

$$
\rho_{actual} = \frac{\lambda}{N \cdot \mu} = \frac{300}{4 \times 100} = 0.75
$$

**Verification:** This matches our target exactly. The system is stable.

---

### Step 6 — Compute Expected Waiting Time (M/M/c Approximation)

For an M/M/c queue, the expected time in system is:

$$
W_q = \frac{P_0 \cdot (c\rho)^c \cdot \rho}{c! \cdot (1-\rho)^2 \cdot \mu \cdot (c - \lambda/\mu)^2}
$$

where $P_0$ is the probability of zero customers in system. For simplicity, using the M/M/1 approximation per server at ρ = 0.75:

$$
W = \frac{1}{\mu - \lambda_{per\_server}} = \frac{1}{100 - 75} = \frac{1}{25} = 0.04 \text{ seconds} = 40 \text{ ms}
$$

$$
T_{total} = \frac{1}{\mu \cdot (1-\rho)} = \frac{1}{100 \times (1 - 0.75)} = \frac{1}{25} = 0.04 \text{ s}
$$

**Conclusion:** With 4 servers, each user waits on average **40 milliseconds** — well within Google's recommended TTFB target of **< 200 ms**.

---

### Step 7 — Compute Throughput Capacity at Peak

If peak load is 2× average = **600 req/s**:

$$
N_{peak} = \left\lceil \frac{600}{75} \right\rceil = \lceil 8.0 \rceil = 8 \text{ servers}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Cloud Auto-Scaling Implication:** This is exactly why AWS Auto Scaling Groups, GCP Managed Instance Groups, and Kubernetes Horizontal Pod Autoscaler exist — they automatically add/remove servers between 4 (normal) and 8 (peak) based on CPU utilization or request count.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Bandwidth Calculation for a Web Application

### Problem Setup
A cloud-hosted web application expects **$P = 500$ concurrent users**. The average webpage size (HTML + CSS + JS + images) is **$S = 2.5$ MB**. The target page load time is **$T = 4$ seconds**. Calculate the required bandwidth.

---

### Step 1 — Convert Units

Page size in bits:

$$
S_{bits} = 2.5 \text{ MB} \times 8 \times 10^6 \text{ bits/MB} = 20 \times 10^6 \text{ bits} = 20 \text{ Mbits}
$$

---

### Step 2 — Apply the Bandwidth Formula

$$
BW = \frac{P \cdot S_{bits}}{T} = \frac{500 \times 20 \times 10^6}{4} = \frac{10 \times 10^9}{4} = 2.5 \times 10^9 \text{ bits/s} = 2.5 \text{ Gbps}
$$

---

### Step 3 — Add 30% Headroom for Bursts and Overhead

$$
BW_{provisioned} = 2.5 \text{ Gbps} \times 1.3 = 3.25 \text{ Gbps}
$$

**Conclusion:** The cloud provider must provision at least **3.25 Gbps** of egress bandwidth for this workload.

---

### Step 4 — Cross-Verification Using Throughput

If each user makes 1 page load per 4 seconds, the request rate is:

$$
\lambda = \frac{500}{4} = 125 \text{ pages/s}
$$

Throughput in bytes per second:

$$
X = 125 \times 2.5 \times 10^6 \text{ bytes/s} = 312.5 \times 10^6 \text{ bytes/s}
$$

Convert to Gbps:

$$
X_{Gbps} = \frac{312.5 \times 10^6 \times 8}{10^9} = 2.5 \text{ Gbps} \checkmark
$$

Both methods agree.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Storage Estimation for a Cloud Photo-Sharing Web Service

### Problem Setup
A photo-sharing web app (like Instagram) has **$U = 10$ million users**. Each user uploads **$A = 50$ MB** of photos per day on average. Photos are replicated across **$R = 3$ Availability Zones** for durability. Calculate **annual storage**.

---

### Step 1 — Daily Data Ingested

$$
D_{daily} = U \cdot A = 10 \times 10^6 \times 50 \times 10^6 \text{ bytes} = 5 \times 10^{14} \text{ bytes} = 500 \text{ TB/day}
$$

---

### Step 2 — With Replication Factor

$$
D_{replicated} = 500 \text{ TB} \times 3 = 1500 \text{ TB/day} = 1.5 \text{ PB/day}
$$

---

### Step 3 — Annual Storage

$$
S_{annual} = 1.5 \text{ PB/day} \times 365 = 547.5 \text{ PB/year}
$$

---

### Step 4 — Apply 80% Compression Ratio (Assume Photos Are Stored as JPEG with avg CR = 0.2)

$$
S_{compressed} = 547.5 \times 0.8 = 438 \text{ PB/year}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Cloud Architecture Insight:** This is why **object storage** (AWS S3, Azure Blob) is used instead of block storage — it's virtually unlimited, costs ~$0.02/GB/month, and supports lifecycle policies to move cold data to **Glacier** at $0.004/GB/month.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — When to Use What

| Scenario | Recommended Web Technology Choice | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Public e-commerce site with 10K users/day | REST + JSON + CDN + Nginx | Simplicity, caching, broad client support |
| Banking transaction system | SOAP + WS-Security + mutual TLS | Strong contract, encryption, audit trail |
| Mobile app with varying data needs | GraphQL + JWT + WebSocket | Single endpoint, real-time updates, no over-fetching |
| Real-time chat / live dashboard | WebSocket + Redis pub/sub | Persistent connection, low latency |
| Public API for 3rd-party developers | REST + OpenAPI 3.0 + OAuth 2.0 + Rate Limiting | Standardization, self-documenting, secure |
| Microservices communication | gRPC + Protocol Buffers + mTLS | High performance, strong typing, low overhead |
| Static documentation website | Static HTML + CDN (S3 + CloudFront) | Cheapest, fastest, no server needed |
| IoT sensor network | MQTT over WebSocket + JSON | Lightweight, low bandwidth, async |

---

## 3.5 Step-by-Step: Converting a Monolithic Web App to Cloud-Native

This is a **favourite 14-mark KTU question** on *Web Technology in Cloud Computing*.

### Original Monolithic Architecture
```
[Browser] → [Single Apache+Tomcat Server] → [Single MySQL DB]
```

### Target Cloud-Native Architecture — 7-Step Refactoring

**Step 1: Separate Static and Dynamic Content**
- Move HTML/CSS/JS/images to **S3/CloudFront CDN**
- Reduces load on application server by 60-70%

**Step 2: Introduce Reverse Proxy + Load Balancer**
- Place **AWS ALB** in front of multiple app servers
- Enables horizontal scaling and TLS termination

**Step 3: Containerize the Application**
- Write a `Dockerfile`, build image, push to **ECR/Docker Hub**
- Example: `FROM node:20-alpine; COPY . /app; CMD ["node", "server.js"]`

**Step 4: Deploy on Orchestrator**
- Use **Kubernetes (EKS/GKE/AKS)** for auto-scaling and self-healing
- Define `Deployment`, `Service`, `Ingress` manifests

**Step 5: Decouple Database**
- Migrate to **Amazon RDS Multi-AZ** for high availability
- Add **ElastiCache (Redis)** for session storage and query caching
- Implement **read replicas** for read-heavy workloads

**Step 6: Async Messaging for Long Tasks**
- Use **SQS/SNS** or **Kafka** for email notifications, image processing
- Improves perceived response time

**Step 7: Observability Stack**
- **Prometheus + Grafana** for metrics
- **ELK Stack (Elasticsearch, Logstash, Kibana)** for logs
- **Jaeger / AWS X-Ray** for distributed tracing

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Result:** The application now scales independently at each layer, has zero downtime during deployments, costs less due to right-sizing, and recovers automatically from server failures.

## 4.3 REST API Resource Lifecycle (CRUD Mapping)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> None : Resource does not exist
    None --> Created : POST /users 201 Created

    Created --> Updated : PUT /users/42 200 OK

    Partial --> Read : GET /users/42

```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Always remember — `POST` returns **201 Created** with a `Location` header pointing to the new resource. `DELETE` returns **204 No Content** (no body). `GET` returns **200 OK** with the resource representation.
