# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model QP]`**

> Explain the three-tier web architecture with a neat diagram. Why is it preferred over the traditional two-tier model in cloud-based deployments?

#### Model Answer (3 marks)

The **three-tier web architecture** separates the application into three logical layers:

1. **Presentation Tier** — The user interface built using HTML5, CSS3, and JavaScript. Runs in the browser.
2. **Application/Logic Tier** — The business logic executed on a server (e.g., Node.js, Django, Spring Boot). Processes user requests, enforces rules, coordinates with the data tier.
3. **Data Tier** — The persistence layer using databases (MySQL, PostgreSQL, MongoDB) or object storage (S3).

**Why preferred over two-tier in cloud:**
- **Independent scalability** — each tier scales on its own demand (web tier scales on user load, DB tier scales on query load)
- **Better security** — the data tier is hidden behind the application tier, not directly exposed
- **Loose coupling** — frontend developers and backend developers can work independently
- **Cloud-native fit** — each tier maps naturally to managed services (S3 + ALB + RDS)

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall:** Students often draw only the two tiers (Client + DB) and call it three-tier. **Examiner's deduction: -1 mark.** Always show the **middle application server** explicitly.

---

### Question 2
**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model QP]`**

> Differentiate between REST and SOAP web services. List any three advantages of REST over SOAP for cloud-native applications.

#### Model Answer (3 marks)

| Aspect | REST | SOAP |
|---|---|---|
| **Type** | Architectural style | Protocol |
| **Data format** | JSON (typical), XML, YAML | XML only |
| **Transport** | HTTP/HTTPS only | HTTP, SMTP, JMS, TCP |
| **Contract** | Optional OpenAPI | Mandatory WSDL |
| **State** | Stateless | Can be stateful |
| **Performance** | Lightweight, faster | Heavier due to XML parsing |

**Three advantages of REST in cloud-native apps:**
1. **Lightweight JSON payload** — reduces bandwidth cost (important in cloud egress pricing)
2. **Stateless & cacheable** — works seamlessly with CDNs and load balancers
3. **Lower learning curve** — easy integration with JavaScript, mobile, IoT clients

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **KTU Rule:** Candidates must answer **ONE** of the two alternatives.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)
**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 / May 2024 Module 3]`**

> **(a)** [7 marks] Describe the complete **HTTP request-response lifecycle** with a sequence diagram. Explain the role of each of the following headers:
>   - `Host`
>   - `User-Agent`
>   - `Accept`
>   - `Authorization`
>   - `Cookie`
>   - `Content-Type`
>   - `Cache-Control`
>
> **(b)** [7 marks] A cloud-hosted e-commerce website receives an average of **800 requests per second**. Each web server has a service rate of **150 req/s**. Design a load-balanced architecture to handle **2× peak load** while keeping per-server utilization below **70%**. Show all calculations clearly.

---

#### Model Solution for Question A

### Part (a) — HTTP Request-Response Lifecycle [7 marks]

The HTTP lifecycle consists of **8 distinct phases**:

**Phase 1 — URL Resolution**
The browser parses the URL into scheme (`https`), host (`api.shop.com`), path (`/products`), and query (`?category=electronics`).

**Phase 2 — DNS Lookup**
Browser checks OS cache → router cache → ISP recursive resolver → authoritative nameserver. Result: IP address like `198.51.100.42`.

**Phase 3 — TCP Connection**
Three-way handshake:
- Client → Server: `SYN` (seq=x)
- Server → Client: `SYN-ACK` (seq=y, ack=x+1)
- Client → Server: `ACK` (ack=y+1)

**Phase 4 — TLS Handshake** (for HTTPS)
- Client sends `ClientHello` with supported cipher suites
- Server responds with `ServerHello`, certificate, and key exchange
- Both derive symmetric session keys
- Total: 1-RTT in TLS 1.3 (was 2-RTT in TLS 1.2)

**Phase 5 — HTTP Request Transmission**

```http
GET /api/products?category=electronics HTTP/1.1
Host: api.shop.com
User-Agent: Mozilla/5.0 (Windows NT 10.0; Win64; x64) AppleWebKit/537.36
Accept: application/json, text/html;q=0.9
Authorization: Bearer eyJhbGciOiJIUzI1NiIsInR5cCI6IkpXVCJ9...
Cookie: session=abc123; cart_id=9876
```

**Header Roles:**

| Header | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `Host` | Identifies the virtual host (required for shared hosting/CDN) |
| `User-Agent` | Identifies client software (browser, OS, version) |
| `Accept` | Content negotiation — tells server which formats client supports |
| `Authorization` | Carries credentials (Bearer token, Basic auth) |
| `Cookie` | Sends previously set session/cart data back to server |
| `Content-Type` | MIME type of request body (only in POST/PUT/PATCH) |
| `Cache-Control` | Directives like `no-cache`, `max-age=3600` |

**Phase 6 — Server Processing**
Web server (Nginx) → application server (Java/Python) → database query → response assembly.

**Phase 7 — HTTP Response Transmission**

```http
HTTP/1.1 200 OK
Content-Type: application/json; charset=utf-8
Cache-Control: public, max-age=300
Set-Cookie: session=xyz789; HttpOnly; Secure; SameSite=Strict
Content-Length: 4096

[{"id":1,"name":"Laptop","price":75000}, ...]
```

**Phase 8 — Connection Management**
`Connection: keep-alive` allows reusing the TCP connection. Browser renders the response, executes JavaScript, fetches additional resources (images, CSS), and may open WebSocket for real-time features.

---

### Part (b) — Load-Balanced Architecture Calculation [7 marks]

**Given:**
- Average load $\lambda_{avg} = 800$ req/s
- Service rate per server $\mu = 150$ req/s
- Peak load factor = $2\times$ → $\lambda_{peak} = 1600$ req/s
- Safe utilization $\rho_{safe} = 0.70$

**Step 1 — Compute Servers Required for Peak**

$$
N = \left\lceil \frac{\lambda_{peak}}{\mu \cdot \rho_{safe}} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{1600}{150 \times 0.70} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{1600}{105} \right\rceil = \lceil 15.238 \rceil = 16 \text{ servers}
$$

**Step 2 — Verify Utilization at Peak**

$$
\rho_{peak} = \frac{\lambda_{peak}}{N \cdot \mu} = \frac{1600}{16 \times 150} = \frac{1600}{2400} = 0.667 \text{ (66.7\%)} \checkmark
$$

**Step 3 — Verify Utilization at Average**

$$
\rho_{avg} = \frac{\lambda_{avg}}{N \cdot \mu} = \frac{800}{16 \times 150} = \frac{800}{2400} = 0.333 \text{ (33.3\%)}
$$

This means **5-6 servers** can handle average load, leaving 10-11 idle for autoscaling bursts.

**Step 4 — Compute Average Response Time at Peak**

Using M/M/1 approximation with $\rho = 0.667$:

$$
W = \frac{1}{\mu \cdot (1 - \rho)} = \frac{1}{150 \times (1 - 0.667)} = \frac{1}{150 \times 0.333} = \frac{1}{50} = 0.02 \text{ s} = 20 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute Cost-Efficient Configuration**

Using **weighted scaling** (50% on-demand + 50% spot instances for AWS):

| Server Tier | Count | AWS Instance Type | Cost/hr |
|---|---|---|---|
| On-Demand (baseline) | 8 | `t3.medium` | $0.0416 × 8 = $0.33/hr |
| Spot (burst) | 8 | `t3.medium` spot | $0.0125 × 8 = $0.10/hr |
| **Total Hourly** | 16 | — | **$0.43/hr** |
| **Monthly (730 hrs)** | — | — | **~$314/month** |

**Architecture Diagram (Textual):**

```
[Users] → [CloudFront CDN] → [Route 53] → [Application Load Balancer]
                                                        ↓
                    ┌──────────────┬──────────────┬──────────────┐
                    ↓              ↓              ↓              ↓
                [Web 1]        [Web 2]  ...   [Web 8]   ...  [Web 16]
                    ↓              ↓              ↓              ↓
                    └──────────────┴──────────────┴──────────────┘
                                        ↓
                    [ElastiCache Redis] + [RDS PostgreSQL Multi-AZ]
```

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> - Forgetting the $\lceil \cdot \rceil$ (ceiling) operation → -1 mark
> - Not verifying $\rho_{actual}$ after computation → -1 mark
> - Not including a CDN in the architecture → -0.5 mark
> - Writing $\rho = \mu / \lambda$ instead of $\lambda / \mu$ → **full marks cut** (common deadly trap)

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice
**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 / May 2024 Module 3]`**

> **(a)** [7 marks] Explain the **REST architectural style** in detail. List and explain the **six guiding constraints** of REST as defined by Roy Fielding. Differentiate between REST and GraphQL.
>
> **(b)** [7 marks] A video streaming web application serves **5 million users**. Average watch time is **40 minutes/day per user**. Bitrate is **5 Mbps**. Calculate the **daily data transfer**, **monthly bandwidth cost** (assume $0.08/GB egress), and recommend the **optimal CDN caching strategy**.

---

#### Model Solution for Question B

### Part (a) — REST Architectural Style [7 marks]

**Definition (Roy Fielding, 2000):**
REST is an **architectural style** for distributed hypermedia systems that emphasizes scalability, generality of interfaces, and independent deployment of components.

**The Six Guiding Constraints:**

**1. Client–Server Separation**
The client (UI) and server (data storage) are independent. Client concerns: rendering, user input. Server concerns: storage, security. They evolve independently.

**2. Statelessness**
Each request from client to server must contain **all information** needed to understand and process it. The server does **not** store client session state between requests. Benefits: visibility, reliability, scalability.

**3. Cacheability**
Responses must explicitly or implicitly indicate whether they are cacheable. `Cache-Control: max-age=3600` tells clients/proxies they can reuse the response for 1 hour, reducing server load by 40-60%.

**4. Uniform Interface**
The fundamental abstraction is the **resource**. Each resource has a unique URI. Standard methods (GET, POST, PUT, DELETE) and standard representations (JSON, XML) decouple implementation from interface.

**5. Layered System**
The architecture is composed of hierarchical layers (CDN → LB → Web → App → DB). Each layer only knows about the layer directly adjacent. Enables load balancing, caching, and security at proxy layers.

**6. Code on Demand (Optional)**
Servers can temporarily extend client functionality by transferring executable code (e.g., JavaScript applets, WASM modules).

**REST vs GraphQL:**

| Aspect | REST | GraphQL |
|---|---|---|
| **Endpoints** | Multiple (`/users`, `/orders`, `/products`) | Single (`/graphql`) |
| **Data fetching** | Fixed shape per endpoint | Client-specified shape |
| **Over-fetching** | Common (returns extra fields) | None (exact fields only) |
| **Under-fetching** | Common (requires multiple calls) | None (one query fetches all) |
| **Caching** | Native HTTP caching | Requires persisted queries |
| **Tooling** | OpenAPI, Swagger | Apollo, Relay, GraphiQL |
| **Best for** | Public APIs, simple CRUD | Complex relational data, mobile apps |

---

### Part (b) — Video Streaming Bandwidth Calculation [7 marks]

**Given:**
- Users $U = 5 \times 10^6$
- Watch time $W = 40$ min/day
- Bitrate $R = 5$ Mbps

**Step 1 — Compute Daily Viewing Hours**

$$
H_{daily} = U \cdot W = 5 \times 10^6 \times \frac{40}{60} = 5 \times 10^6 \times 0.667 = 3.333 \times 10^6 \text{ hours}
$$

**Step 2 — Convert Hours to Seconds**

$$
T_{seconds} = 3.333 \times 10^6 \times 3600 = 1.2 \times 10^{10} \text{ seconds}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Daily Data Transfer in Bits**

$$
D_{bits} = T_{seconds} \cdot R = 1.2 \times 10^{10} \times 5 \times 10^6 = 6 \times 10^{16} \text{ bits}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert to Petabytes**

$$
D_{PB} = \frac{6 \times 10^{16}}{8 \times 10^{15}} = 7.5 \text{ PB/day}
$$

**Step 5 — Convert to GB**

$$
D_{GB} = 7.5 \times 10^6 \text{ GB/day} = 7{,}500{,}000 \text{ GB/day}
$$

**Step 6 — Compute Monthly Data (30 days)**

$$
D_{monthly} = 7{,}500{,}000 \times 30 = 225{,}000{,}000 \text{ GB/month} = 225 \text{ PB/month}
$$

**Step 7 — Compute Monthly Bandwidth Cost**

$$
C_{monthly} = D_{monthly} \cdot P_{GB} = 225 \times 10^6 \times 0.08 = 18{,}000{,}000 = \$18 \text{ million/month}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **This $18M/month figure demonstrates WHY CDN is non-negotiable for video streaming.**

**Step 8 — Optimal CDN Strategy**

Apply **80% cache hit ratio** (typical for popular content):

$$
D_{origin} = D_{monthly} \times (1 - 0.80) = 225 \text{ PB} \times 0.20 = 45 \text{ PB/month}
$$

**Cost after CDN:**

$$
C_{CDN} = 45 \times 10^6 \times 0.08 + (180 \times 10^6 \times 0.01)
$$

$$
C_{CDN} = \$3.6\text{M} + \$1.8\text{M} = \$5.4\text{M/month}
$$

**Savings: $18M − $5.4M = $12.6M/month (70% reduction!)**

**Recommended Architecture:**
1. **Multi-CDN strategy** — Use both AWS CloudFront + Akamai for redundancy and global coverage
2. **Adaptive bitrate streaming** — HLS/DASH with 3 quality levels (1080p, 720p, 480p)
3. **Pre-signed URLs** for premium content
4. **Origin Shield** to reduce origin load by another 50%
5. **Edge compute** (Lambda@Edge) for personalization and ad insertion

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Module 3 — Web Technology**

### 🔹 Core Concepts
- Web Technology = Client + Server + Protocol + Data, all glued together by HTTP/HTTPS
- **3-Tier Architecture**: Presentation + Application + Data tiers
- **HTTP Methods**: `GET` (read), `POST` (create), `PUT` (replace), `PATCH` (partial), `DELETE` (remove)
- **HTTP Status Classes**: 1xx info, 2xx success, 3xx redirect, 4xx client error, 5xx server error
- **Statelessness**: Each HTTP request is independent; state is carried via cookies, tokens, or URLs
- **HTTPS** = HTTP + TLS 1.3; default port **443** (HTTP uses **80**)
- **REST** is an architectural style; **SOAP** is a protocol; **GraphQL** is a query language
- **JWT** (JSON Web Token) for stateless authentication; **OAuth 2.0** for delegated authorization
- **CDN** caches static content at edge locations closer to users, reducing latency and origin load
- **Load Balancer** distributes requests across multiple servers using Round Robin, Least Connections, IP Hash, etc.

### 🔹 Cloud-Native Web Stack
- **Reverse Proxy**: Nginx in front of app servers (TLS termination, gzip, rate limiting)
- **API Gateway**: AWS API Gateway / Kong / Apigee for routing, throttling, auth
- **Object Storage**: S3 / Azure Blob for static assets, backups, big files
- **Container Orchestration**: Kubernetes (EKS/GKE/AKS) for auto-scaling and self-healing
- **Observability**: Prometheus + Grafana (metrics), ELK (logs), Jaeger (tracing)

### 🔹 Performance Formulas (Must Memorize)
- **Little's Law**: $L = \lambda W$
- **Utilization**: $\rho = \lambda / \mu$
- **M/M/1 Response Time**: $W = 1 / (\mu - \lambda)$
- **Bandwidth**: $BW = (P \times S \times 8) / T$
- **CDN Hit Ratio**: $HR = C_{hit} / (C_{hit} + C_{miss})$
- **Servers Required**: $N = \lceil \lambda / (\mu \cdot \rho_{safe}) \rceil$

### 🔹 Security Essentials
- **TLS 1.3** is mandatory for all production web apps (deprecates TLS 1.0/1.1)
- **HTTPS Everywhere** — Google ranks HTTPS sites higher (SEO)
- **OWASP Top 10** — know XSS, CSRF, SQL Injection, Broken Auth, Sensitive Data Exposure
- **CORS** (Cross-Origin Resource Sharing) controls which domains can call your API
- **Rate Limiting**: Token bucket / Leaky bucket to prevent DDoS

### 🔹 KTU Exam Power Phrases (Use These!)
- *"In a cloud-native web architecture, the [layer] is decoupled from the [layer] to enable independent scaling and fault isolation."*
- *"The choice between REST, SOAP, and GraphQL depends on factors such as payload size, schema evolution, caching requirements, and client diversity."*
- *"Horizontal scaling is preferred over vertical scaling in cloud deployments due to its theoretical infinite scalability and built-in fault tolerance."*
- *"CDN reduces the origin server load by caching static content at geographically distributed edge locations, thereby improving TTFB and reducing bandwidth costs."*

> [!WARNING]
> **Final KTU Exam Pitfalls to Avoid:**
> 1. Confusing *web server* (Nginx) with *application server* (Tomcat) — they are **different**
> 2. Saying "HTTP is secure" — it is **NOT**; only **HTTPS** is secure
> 3. Forgetting to include `Content-Type` header in POST/PUT requests
> 4. Writing `PUT` for partial updates — that's `PATCH`; `PUT` is full replacement
> 5. Calculating $\rho$ as $\mu/\lambda$ instead of $\lambda/\mu$
> 6. Confusing authentication (who you are) with authorization (what you can do)
> 7. Drawing a 2-tier architecture when 3-tier is asked — **-2 marks minimum**

---

*End of Module 3 — Web Technology Notes for PECPT525 Cloud Computing (KTU 2024 Scheme)*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Multitenant Technology
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT525 - CLOUD COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Multitenant Technology

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Multitenancy** is a software architecture pattern in which a **single instance** of an application (running on a shared physical or virtual infrastructure) simultaneously serves **multiple distinct customer organizations (tenants)**, where each tenant's data, configuration, and user identities are logically isolated while sharing common compute, storage, and networking resources.

In the context of **Platform-as-a-Service (PaaS)** and **Software-as-a-Service (SaaS)**, multitenancy is the *foundational architectural principle* that enables cloud providers to achieve economies of scale, dynamic elasticity, and per-tenant metering. A *tenant* is formally defined as a group of users (typically belonging to a single organization, department, or project) who share a common access privilege to the software instance.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Apartment Building" Model

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Think of a high-rise apartment building.
> - The **building structure, foundation, elevators, water supply, and electrical wiring** are *shared* by all residents — this represents the shared **application instance, OS, middleware, and hardware**.
> - Each **apartment (flat)** is a *tenant*. Although they all use the same building, residents cannot enter each other's flats, and each flat can be decorated and furnished according to its owner's taste.
> - The **building manager** (equivalent to the *tenant manager / identity service*) handles who enters which flat, allocates parking slots, and ensures that one resident's heavy renovation does not crack the walls of another flat (this is the **"Noisy Neighbor" Problem**).

So, just as an apartment building reduces per-resident construction cost by sharing infrastructure, multitenancy reduces per-customer IT cost by sharing one application instance among many customers.

### 1.3 Key Terminology

- **Tenant**: A single customer organization (e.g., a company, university, or department) using the shared cloud service.
- **Tenant Isolation**: Logical and/or physical separation ensuring that one tenant's data and operations are invisible to others.
- **Tenant-Aware Application**: An application that recognizes the *tenant context* (tenant ID) in every request and routes resources accordingly.
- **Noisy Neighbor**: A tenant whose workload consumes a disproportionate share of shared resources, degrading performance for others.
- **Customization**: Per-tenant configuration of UI, workflows, business rules, and branding without modifying the shared codebase.
- **Elastic Scalability**: The ability of the shared instance to dynamically allocate resources as the *aggregate* load from all tenants changes.

### 1.4 Standard Industry Metrics (KTU Board Highlights)

- **Tenancy Density**: Number of tenants supported per physical server — typically **50–500 tenants** in mature SaaS systems.
- **Hardware Utilization**: **60–80%** (versus **10–15%** in single-tenant deployments).
- **Cost Reduction Factor**: **3x–10x** lower TCO (Total Cost of Ownership) compared to single-tenant hosting.
- **SLA Uptime**: Typically **99.9% (Three-Nines)** or **99.99% (Four-Nines)** for premium tenants.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Layers of a Multitenant System

A multitenant cloud application is logically decomposed into **four hierarchical layers**, each with its own isolation strategy:

1. **Infrastructure Layer (IaaS substrate)**
   - Virtual Machines (VMs), containers, or bare-metal servers.
   - Isolation: Hypervisor-enforced, namespace-based, or hardware-level.
   - Shared by all tenants in a region/zone.

2. **Application Layer (Shared Codebase)**
   - A *single* deployed binary/service.
   - The application is *tenant-aware* — every API call carries a `tenant_id` token verified by an Identity Provider (IdP).

3. **Data Layer (Database Partitioning)**
   - Three canonical partitioning strategies (discussed in §2.3 below).

4. **Presentation Layer (Per-Tenant UI)**
   - Custom branding, themes, locales, and feature flags.
   - Delivered via tenant configuration objects loaded at session initialization.

### 2.2 Why Multitenancy? — The "Why" Behind the Pattern

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Without multitenancy, every customer would require a **dedicated application instance**, dedicated DB, dedicated admin team. This is economically unsustainable at hyperscale. Multitenancy converts **fixed infrastructure cost** into a **shared variable cost**, making cloud computing commercially viable.

- **Cost Efficiency**: One operations team supports N tenants.
- **Resource Pooling**: Statistical multiplexing of workloads — peak loads of different tenants rarely coincide, so overall provisioning is lower.
- **Operational Simplicity**: Patches, upgrades, and security fixes deploy *once* and benefit *all* tenants simultaneously.
- **Faster Onboarding**: A new tenant can be provisioned in minutes via metadata injection rather than full instance deployment.

### 2.3 The Three Canonical Data Isolation Models

> [!NOTE]
> This is a **frequently-tested KTU concept** — memorize the table below.

| Isolation Model | Database | Schema | Tenant Data Location | Trade-off |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Separate Database** | One DB per tenant | One schema per DB | Completely separate DB instance | Highest isolation, highest cost |
| **Shared Database, Separate Schema** | One shared DB | One schema per tenant | Different schemas within same DB | Medium isolation, medium cost |
| **Shared Database, Shared Schema** | One shared DB | One shared schema | Discriminator column `tenant_id` in every table | Lowest isolation, lowest cost |

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are **board-relevant** for numerical and analytical questions.

| # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Variables & Units | KTU Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Per-tenant cost (shared model) | $C_{tenant} = \dfrac{C_{infra} + C_{ops}}{N}$ | $C_{infra}$ = infrastructure cost (₹/month); $C_{ops}$ = ops cost (₹/month); $N$ = number of tenants (dimensionless) | Cost-savings analysis |
| 2 | Cost savings ratio | $S = 1 - \dfrac{C_{shared}}{N \cdot C_{single}}$ | $C_{single}$ = cost of one single-tenant instance; $C_{shared}$ = shared-instance cost | Compare architectures |
| 3 | Aggregate resource utilization | $U_{agg} = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{N} u_i \cdot r_i}{R_{total}}$ | $u_i$ = utilization of tenant $i$; $r_i$ = resources requested; $R_{total}$ = total resources | Capacity planning |
| 4 | Effective SLA availability | $A_{eff} = \prod_{i=1}^{N} A_{i}^{w_i}$ | $A_i$ = availability of component $i$; $w_i$ = weight of component $i$ | Reliability engineering |
| 5 | Tenant density per server | $D = \dfrac{R_{server}}{R_{avg\,tenant}}$ | $R_{server}$ = server capacity; $R_{avg\,tenant}$ = avg. tenant demand | Hardware sizing |
| 6 | Noisy-Neighbor Impact Index | $I_{NN} = \dfrac{\max(u_i)}{\bar{u}}$ | $\max(u_i)$ = peak tenant utilization; $\bar{u}$ = mean tenant utilization | Performance SLAs |
| 7 | Multiplexing gain | $G = \dfrac{\text{Peak Sum of Individual Demands}}{\text{Peak of Aggregate Demand}}$ | Dimensionless ratio $\geq 1$ | Statistical multiplexing proof |

> **Note on Notation:** In the table, vertical bars inside any cell are formatted as `$\vert$` to avoid breaking markdown table syntax.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **SaaS Vendors**: Salesforce, ServiceNow, Microsoft 365, and Google Workspace use multitenancy to serve *millions* of organizations from a single codebase.
- **Database-as-a-Service**: Snowflake, Amazon Redshift, and Azure SQL Database implement *shared-schema* multitenancy with row-level security (RLS).
- **Web Hosting**: WordPress.com, Shopify, and BigCommerce enable merchants to operate independent storefronts on a shared platform.
- **DevOps Platforms**: Kubernetes namespaces provide container-level multitenancy inside a single cluster.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Per-Tenant Cost Reduction

> **Problem:** A single-tenant deployment costs **₹50,000/month** per customer. A multitenancy platform has a fixed infrastructure cost of **₹8,00,000/month** and an operational overhead of **₹2,000/month per tenant**. Find the breakeven number of tenants.

**Step 1 — Define the cost functions.**

Let the per-tenant cost in the *single-tenant* model be $C_{single}$.

Let the per-tenant cost in the *multitenant* model be $C_{multi}$.

We are given:

$$
C_{single} = 50{,}000 \quad \text{(₹/month per customer)}
$$

$$
C_{infra} = 8{,}00{,}000 \quad \text{(₹/month, shared)}
$$

$$
C_{ops} = 2{,}000 \quad \text{(₹/month per tenant)}
$$

**Step 2 — Express the total multitenant cost for $N$ tenants.**

The total cost borne by the multitenancy platform is the fixed infrastructure cost plus the per-tenant operational overhead summed over $N$ tenants.

$$
C_{total}^{multi}(N) = C_{infra} + N \cdot C_{ops}
$$

Substituting the given numerical values:

$$
C_{total}^{multi}(N) = 8{,}00{,}000 + N \cdot 2{,}000
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the total single-tenant cost for $N$ tenants.**

If each tenant has its own dedicated instance:

$$
C_{total}^{single}(N) = N \cdot C_{single} = N \cdot 50{,}000
$$

**Step 4 — Set the breakeven condition.**

Breakeven occurs when both architectures have equal total cost:

$$
C_{total}^{multi}(N^*) = C_{total}^{single}(N^*)
$$

$$
8{,}00{,}000 + 2{,}000 \cdot N^* = 50{,}000 \cdot N^*
$$

**Step 5 — Solve algebraically for $N^*$.**

Bring all $N^*$ terms to one side:

$$
8{,}00{,}000 = 50{,}000 \cdot N^* - 2{,}000 \cdot N^*
$$

$$
8{,}00{,}000 = 48{,}000 \cdot N^*
$$

$$
N^* = \dfrac{8{,}00{,}000}{48{,}000}
$$

$$
N^* = 16.67
$$

**Step 6 — Interpret the result.**

Since $N$ must be an integer, the breakeven point is $\boxed{N^* = 17 \text{ tenants}}$. For any tenant count above 17, the multitenancy architecture is cheaper than hosting each customer on a dedicated instance.

**Step 7 — Verify with a sanity check.**

Plug in $N = 17$:

- Multitenant cost: $8{,}00{,}000 + 17 \times 2{,}000 = 8{,}34{,}000$
- Single-tenant cost: $17 \times 50{,}000 = 8{,}50{,}000$

Multitenant is cheaper by ₹16,000. ✔

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Multiplexing Gain (Statistical Aggregation)

> **Problem:** A server has capacity **100 units**. Three tenants arrive with peak demands: 50, 60, and 40 units respectively. Compute the multiplexing gain.

**Step 1 — Compute the sum of individual peak demands.**

$$
P_{sum} = 50 + 60 + 40 = 150 \text{ units}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the peak of the *aggregate* demand (assuming peaks are uncorrelated and uniformly distributed in time).**

For statistically independent tenants, the *expected* peak of the aggregate is approximated by the square-root-sum rule:

$$
P_{agg} \approx \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{N} p_i^{\,2}}
$$

$$
P_{agg} = \sqrt{50^{2} + 60^{2} + 40^{2}}
$$

$$
P_{agg} = \sqrt{2500 + 3600 + 1600}
$$

$$
P_{agg} = \sqrt{7700}
$$

$$
P_{agg} \approx 87.75 \text{ units}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the multiplexing gain.**

$$
G = \dfrac{P_{sum}}{P_{agg}} = \dfrac{150}{87.75}
$$

$$
\boxed{G \approx 1.71}
$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation.**

A gain of **1.71x** means the cloud provider needs only **~58%** of the provisioning that would be required if tenants were hosted individually. The remaining **~42%** is freed for additional tenants or buffer headroom — the *core economic engine* of cloud computing.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — When to Choose Which Isolation Model

| Criteria | Separate DB | Shared DB / Separate Schema | Shared DB / Shared Schema |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Data Isolation Strength** | Very High (audit-ready) | High | Moderate (RLS-dependent) |
| **Cost per Tenant** | High | Medium | Very Low |
| **Onboarding Speed** | Slow (DB provisioning) | Medium | Fast (row insert) |
| **Custom Schema** | Fully flexible | Limited | Not possible |
| **Compliance (HIPAA, PCI-DSS)** | Easiest | Achievable | Requires care |
| **Best For** | Banking, defense | Mid-market SaaS | High-volume consumer apps |
| **Example Platforms** | Oracle, MSSQL dedicated | PostgreSQL schemas | Snowflake, BigQuery |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Multitenant Architecture (Layered View)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph PRES["PRESENTATION LAYER"]
        UI1["Tenant A Portal"]
        UI2["Tenant B Portal"]
        UI3["Tenant C Portal"]
    end

    subgraph APP["APPLICATION LAYER - SHARED INSTANCE"]
        LB["Load Balancer with Tenant Router"]
        AUTH["Identity Provider - OIDC - SAML"]
        APP1["Application Service Tenant Aware"]
    end

    subgraph MID["MIDDLEWARE LAYER"]
        CACHE["Shared Cache - Tenant Keyed"]
        MQ["Message Queue - Tenant Tagged"]
    end

    subgraph DATA["DATA LAYER - THREE ISOLATION MODELS"]
        DB1["Model 1 - Separate Database"]
        DB2["Model 2 - Shared DB Separate Schema"]
        DB3["Model 3 - Shared DB Shared Schema with RLS"]
    end

    subgraph INFRA["INFRASTRUCTURE LAYER"]
        VM1["Virtual Machine Pool"]
        VM2["Container Orchestrator"]
        NET["Virtual Private Network"]
    end

    UI1 --> LB
    UI2 --> LB
    UI3 --> LB
    LB --> AUTH
    AUTH --> APP1
    APP1 --> CACHE
    APP1 --> MQ
    APP1 --> DB1
    APP1 --> DB2
    APP1 --> DB3
    APP1 --> VM1
    VM1 --> VM2
    DB1 --> NET
    DB2 --> NET
    DB3 --> NET
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Sequence Diagram — Tenant Request Lifecycle

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant T as Tenant User
    participant LB as Load Balancer
    participant IDP as Identity Provider
    participant APP as Shared App Instance
    participant DB as Data Layer
    participant MT as Metering Service

    T->>LB: HTTPS Request with Auth Token
    LB->>IDP: Validate Token
    IDP-->>LB: Tenant ID and Role Claims
    LB->>APP: Forward Request with Tenant Context
    APP->>DB: Query with Tenant Filter
    DB-->>APP: Tenant Scoped Data Only
    APP->>MT: Record Usage Metrics
    MT-->>APP: Acknowledged
    APP-->>LB: Tenant Branded Response
    LB-->>T: Deliver Response
```

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    REQ["Incoming Request"] --> CTX["Extract Tenant ID"]
    CTX --> AUTHZ{"Authorization Check"}
    AUTHZ -->|Denied| REJ["Reject 403"]
    AUTHZ -->|Allowed| ROUTE["Route to Tenant Workspace"]
    ROUTE --> CFG["Load Tenant Config"]
    CFG --> EXEC["Execute Business Logic"]
    EXEC --> DATAOP["Data Operation with Tenant Predicate"]
    DATAOP --> LOG["Audit Log Tenant Tagged"]
    EXEC --> RESP["Build Tenant Branded Response"]
    RESP --> OUT["Send to Client"]
```

### 4.4 Diagram Interpretation Notes (KTU Board Tips)

- The **Load Balancer** is the *first line of tenant context awareness* — it inspects the JWT (JSON Web Token) or session cookie.
- The **Identity Provider (IdP)** issues and validates tenant claims — never trust client-supplied tenant IDs.
- The **Data Layer** is the *most critical isolation boundary*. Even shared-schema deployments must enforce row-level security (RLS) at the database engine level.
- The **Metering Service** is what enables **pay-per-use billing** — a defining feature of cloud computing.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define multitenancy in cloud computing. List any two advantages of a multitenant architecture over a single-tenant architecture.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (2 marks):** Multitenancy is an architectural pattern in which a *single instance* of a software application serves *multiple customers (tenants)*, with each tenant's data logically isolated while sharing common underlying compute, storage, and network resources.
>
> **Advantages (1 mark — any two):**
> 1. **Cost efficiency** — Infrastructure and operational costs are distributed across many tenants, reducing per-tenant TCO.
> 2. **Easier maintenance** — Patches and upgrades are deployed once and benefit all tenants.
> 3. **Better resource utilization** — Statistical multiplexing yields higher hardware utilization (60–80%) compared to single-tenant deployments (10–15%).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**What is the "Noisy Neighbor" problem in a multitenant cloud environment? Suggest one technique to mitigate it.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (2 marks):** The *Noisy Neighbor* problem occurs when one tenant's workload consumes a disproportionate share of shared resources (CPU, memory, I/O, network), thereby degrading the performance and SLA compliance of other co-resident tenants.
>
> **Mitigation technique (1 mark):** **Resource Quotas and Rate Limiting** — assign per-tenant CPU shares, memory caps, and I/O throttling limits so that any single tenant cannot exceed its allocated share. Examples: Linux cgroups, Kubernetes ResourceQuotas, and database connection pool limits.

---

### 5.2 Part B — ESE Module Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Model Paper Adapted]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **three canonical data isolation models** used in multitenant databases. Compare them with respect to isolation strength, cost, and customization flexibility.

**(b) [7 Marks)** A SaaS provider hosts a multitenant application. The fixed infrastructure cost is **₹12,00,000/month** and operational cost is **₹3,000/month per tenant**. If a single-tenant dedicated deployment would cost **₹80,000/month per customer**, determine:
- (i) The breakeven number of tenants.
- (ii) The monthly savings when the platform serves **200 tenants**.
- (iii) The per-tenant cost in both models at $N = 200$.

---

**Model Solution — Question A:**

**(a) Three Data Isolation Models (7 marks):**

| Model | Description | Isolation | Cost | Customization |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Separate Database** | Each tenant gets its own DB instance | **Very High** | High | Full |
| **Shared DB / Separate Schema** | All tenants share one DB, but each gets a distinct schema | High | Medium | Schema-level possible |
| **Shared DB / Shared Schema** | All tenants share the DB *and* schema, differentiated by a `tenant_id` column with Row-Level Security (RLS) | Moderate | Low | Limited (RLS-based filtering) |

> **Key Insight (for 1 extra mark):** The industry trend moves toward *Shared Schema + RLS* for cost efficiency, with **cryptographic tenant separation** (tenant-specific encryption keys) layered on top to compensate for weaker isolation.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:** $C_{infra} = 12{,}00{,}000$, $C_{ops} = 3{,}000$, $C_{single} = 80{,}000$.

**(i) Breakeven:**

$$
12{,}00{,}000 + 3{,}000 \cdot N^* = 80{,}000 \cdot N^*
$$

$$
12{,}00{,}000 = 77{,}000 \cdot N^*
$$

$$
N^* = \dfrac{12{,}00{,}000}{77{,}000} \approx 15.58
$$

$$
\boxed{N^* = 16 \text{ tenants (next integer)}}
$$

**(ii) Monthly savings at $N = 200$:**

Multitenant total cost:

$$
C_{multi}(200) = 12{,}00{,}000 + 3{,}000 \cdot 200 = 12{,}00{,}000 + 6{,}00{,}000 = 18{,}00{,}000
$$

Single-tenant total cost:

$$
C_{single}(200) = 80{,}000 \cdot 200 = 1{,}60{,}00{,}000
$$

Savings:

$$
S = 1{,}60{,}00{,}000 - 18{,}00{,}000 = \boxed{1{,}42{,}00{,}000 \text{ ₹/month}}
$$

**(iii) Per-tenant cost at $N = 200$:**

Multitenant: $18{,}00{,}000 / 200 = \boxed{₹9{,}000/\text{tenant}}$

Single-tenant: $\boxed{₹80{,}000/\text{tenant}}$

**Reduction factor:** $80{,}000 / 9{,}000 \approx 8.89$x cheaper.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2023, Adapted]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, describe the **layered architecture of a multitenant cloud application**. Briefly explain the role of each layer in enabling tenant isolation.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **key challenges** of multitenant systems. For each challenge, propose a suitable engineering solution.

---

**Model Solution — Question B:**

**(a) Layered Architecture (7 marks):**

The multitenant cloud application is structured into **four layers**:

1. **Presentation Layer** — Per-tenant UI customizations (logo, theme, locale). Built using tenant configuration objects.
2. **Application Layer** — A single, shared, *tenant-aware* application instance. Every API call carries a verified `tenant_id` claim.
3. **Data Layer** — Provides one of the three isolation models (separate DB / separate schema / shared schema with RLS).
4. **Infrastructure Layer** — Shared VMs, containers, networking, and storage managed by the cloud provider.

> **Supporting Services (cross-cutting):**
> - **Identity Provider (IdP):** Issues and validates tenant tokens.
> - **Tenant Manager:** Onboards new tenants and provisions resources.
> - **Metering & Billing Service:** Tracks per-tenant consumption.

*(Refer to the Mermaid diagram in SECTION 4 for the block-level schematic — 1 mark is awarded for a clean diagram.)*

**(b) Challenges & Solutions (7 marks):**

| # | Challenge | Engineering Solution |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Data Isolation & Privacy** | Enforce Row-Level Security (RLS), tenant-specific encryption keys, and audit logging. |
| 2 | **Noisy Neighbor** | Per-tenant resource quotas, cgroups, connection-pool throttling, and SLA-based load shedding. |
| 3 | **Customization vs. Shared Codebase** | Use metadata-driven configuration, feature flags, and plugin/micro-app architecture. |
| 4 | **Compliance & Regulatory Audits** | Offer tenants a choice of geographic regions, support per-tenant encryption, and provide compliance dashboards. |
| 5 | **Tenant Onboarding & Offboarding** | Automate provisioning/deprovisioning via Tenant Manager service; ensure data sanitization on offboarding. |
| 6 | **Disaster Recovery Isolation** | Define RPO/RTO per tenant tier; use tenant-tagged backups with selective restoration. |
| 7 | **Performance Predictability** | Capacity planning with statistical multiplexing; auto-scaling policies triggered by aggregate load. |

---

### 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls — Read Carefully Before Writing Your Exam:**
>
> 1. **Confusing "tenant" with "user":** A tenant is an *organization*, not an individual user. Saying "tenants are users" costs 1 full mark.
> 2. **Omitting the `tenant_id` in shared-schema models:** Examiners specifically look for the *discriminator column / RLS* mechanism. Skipping it = −2 marks.
> 3. **Not drawing a labeled block diagram in Part B (a) questions:** A diagram is worth **1–2 marks by itself**. Even a simple hand-drawn rectangle diagram earns credit.
> 4. **Forgetting units in numerical answers:** Always write "₹/month" or "tenants" — bare numbers are penalized.
> 5. **Ignoring the "Noisy Neighbor" problem:** If a question asks about multitenancy *challenges*, this MUST be mentioned.
> 6. **Saying "multitenancy = virtualization":** It is NOT the same. Virtualization isolates *infrastructure*; multitenancy isolates *application/data* per customer. Mixing them up = conceptual failure.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Points — Memorize Before Exam Day:**

- ✅ Multitenancy = **one instance, many tenants, shared resources, isolated data**.
- ✅ A **tenant** is a customer *organization*, not a single user.
- ✅ Three data isolation models: **Separate DB**, **Shared DB / Separate Schema**, **Shared DB / Shared Schema (with RLS)**.
- ✅ The **Noisy Neighbor** problem arises from shared resource contention — mitigated via quotas and throttling.
- ✅ Multitenancy enables **statistical multiplexing** — peak loads of different tenants rarely align, lowering aggregate provisioning.
- ✅ **Breakeven formula:** $C_{infra} = (C_{single} - C_{ops}) \cdot N^*$.
- ✅ **Multiplexing gain** $G = P_{sum} / P_{agg} \geq 1$.
- ✅ **Cloud computing metrics:** 60–80% hardware utilization, 3x–10x cost reduction, 99.9–99.99% SLA.
- ✅ **Identity Provider (IdP)** is the gatekeeper of tenant context — never trust client-side tenant IDs.
- ✅ **Metering & Billing** is what converts shared infrastructure into per-tenant revenue (pay-per-use model).
- ✅ **Real-world examples:** Salesforce, ServiceNow, Google Workspace, Microsoft 365, Snowflake, BigQuery.
- ✅ **Industry trend:** Move toward *Shared Schema + Row-Level Security + Tenant-Specific Encryption Keys* for optimal cost-isolation balance.
- ✅ Multitenancy ≠ Virtualization; Multitenancy ≠ Multi-instance architecture; Multitenancy ≠ Multi-user system.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Service Technology. Resource Provisioning Techniques - Static and Dynamic Provisioning. Open-Source Software Platforms for Private Cloud - OpenStack
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT525 - CLOUD COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Service Technology. Resource Provisioning Techniques - Static and Dynamic Provisioning. Open-Source Software Platforms for Private Cloud - OpenStack

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Service Technology in Cloud Computing

**Service Technology** refers to the underlying framework, tools, protocols, and architectural components that enable the design, deployment, management, orchestration, and delivery of cloud-based services (SaaS, PaaS, IaaS). It encompasses everything from virtualization layers and service-oriented middleware to APIs, Service Level Agreements (SLAs), billing engines, and orchestration platforms that abstract physical infrastructure into usable, on-demand services.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Highlight:** Service Technology is the **enablement layer** that converts raw compute, storage, and network resources into standardized, consumable, billable cloud services accessible via well-defined interfaces (APIs, portals, CLI).

### Conceptual Analogy: The "Electricity Grid" Metaphor

Imagine a city's electricity supply:
- **Power Plants (Physical Servers)** = raw infrastructure
- **Transformers & Power Lines (Service Technology)** = the technology that delivers usable electricity
- **Electrical Sockets in Homes (Cloud Services)** = the standardized endpoint users consume

You don't care which power plant generated your electricity. You just plug into a socket, and you get **standardized, metered, billed service**. **Service Technology is the transformer grid of cloud computing** — it makes heterogeneous, distributed resources appear as uniform, on-demand services.

## 1.2 Resource Provisioning — Formal Definition

**Resource Provisioning** is the process of **allocating, configuring, activating, and managing computing resources** (CPU cores, RAM, storage, bandwidth, IP addresses, middleware, applications) to satisfy user workload requirements within a cloud environment.

Two principal paradigms exist:

### A. Static Provisioning

**Static Provisioning** is a **pre-allocated, fixed-capacity, manual or semi-automated** resource allocation strategy where resources are reserved **in advance** based on **peak-load estimations**, regardless of the actual real-time demand.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Property of Static Provisioning:** *Resources are committed before they are needed and remain reserved for the entire planned duration.* Wastage is the trade-off for predictability.

### B. Dynamic Provisioning

**Dynamic Provisioning** is an **on-demand, elastic, automated** resource allocation mechanism where resources are **allocated, scaled, and de-allocated in real-time** based on current workload metrics, policies, or predictive analytics.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Property of Dynamic Provisioning:** *Resources are tightly coupled to actual demand — provisioned when needed, released when not.* Elasticity is the trade-off, with potential provisioning latency.

### Conceptual Analogy: "Restaurant Table Booking"

- **Static Provisioning** = A restaurant reserves **20 fixed tables every Saturday** because historical data says Saturday is busy. Even if only 5 customers show up, those 15 tables stay empty and cannot be sold. *Predictable, but wasteful.*
- **Dynamic Provisioning** = The restaurant starts with 5 tables, and as customers arrive, staff **quickly add or remove tables** in real-time based on queue length. *Efficient, but staff need time to set up each new table (provisioning latency).*

## 1.3 Open-Source Private Cloud Platforms — OpenStack

**OpenStack** is a **free, open-source, massively scalable cloud operating system** that pools compute, storage, and networking resources across a data center and enables on-demand provisioning of virtual machines, containers, and cloud services through a unified dashboard and API.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Highlight:** OpenStack is the **de-facto standard** for building private and hybrid clouds. It is governed by the **OpenInfra Foundation** (formerly OpenStack Foundation) and released under the **Apache 2.0 License**.

### Conceptual Analogy: "OpenStack as a Cloud-Building LEGO Set"

Think of OpenStack as a giant **LEGO set for building your own AWS or Azure**:
- Each LEGO brick = an individual OpenStack **service/component** (Nova, Neutron, Cinder, etc.)
- You assemble bricks = integrate components
- The finished LEGO model = a working private cloud

You don't have to build bricks yourself (Apache-licensed open code), and you can swap or extend any brick (modular architecture).

## 1.4 Standard Cloud Provisioning Metrics

| Metric | Symbol | Unit | Definition |
|---|---|---|---|
| Provisioning Latency | $T_p$ | seconds | Time from request to resource ready |
| Utilization Ratio | $U$ | % | (Used Capacity) / (Provisioned Capacity) × 100 |
| Over-provisioning Factor | $O_f$ | dimensionless | Provisioned / Required |
| SLA Violation Rate | $S_{violation}$ | % | Requests failing SLA / Total requests |
| Elasticity Coefficient | $E_c$ | dimensionless | (Resources Released) / (Resources Grown) |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of Resource Provisioning

The provisioning lifecycle in any cloud platform follows a standardized pipeline:

### The 5-Stage Provisioning Pipeline

1. **Request Initiation** — User (or autoscaler) submits a request specifying VM type, OS image, region, SLA tier.
2. **Admission Control & Quota Check** — Platform validates the request against tenant quotas, capacity policies, and SLA contracts.
3. **Resource Matching & Placement** — Scheduler/filter selects the optimal physical host(s) using algorithms (e.g., Nova Filter Scheduler, weighted scoring).
4. **Provisioning & Configuration** — Hypervisor (KVM, Xen, Hyper-V) boots the VM, network ports are wired, storage volumes are attached.
5. **Release/Decommission** — Resources are reclaimed, volumes snapshotted or deleted, IPs released, billing finalized.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The difference between *static* and *dynamic* provisioning is **not in the pipeline itself**, but in **WHO triggers each stage and WHEN**:
> - **Static** → Human/Admin triggers stages 1–4 *before* the workload arrives.
> - **Dynamic** → Automated policy engine / load monitor triggers all 5 stages in *real-time* based on metric thresholds.

## 2.2 Static Provisioning — Deep Analysis

### Operational Characteristics
- Resources are **reserved for a fixed time window** (e.g., a semester, a quarter, an event).
- **No real-time scaling** — capacity is "frozen" at deployment time.
- Typically used for **predictable, long-running, mission-critical** workloads (e.g., ERP systems, university exam servers during evaluation periods).

### Advantages
- ✅ Predictable performance
- ✅ Zero provisioning latency at runtime
- ✅ Simpler capacity planning model
- ✅ Lower risk of SLA violations due to capacity exhaustion

### Disadvantages
- ❌ **Severe resource wastage** during low-demand periods
- ❌ Cannot absorb **unexpected demand spikes**
- ❌ Requires accurate **demand forecasting** (often impossible)
- ❌ Higher **Total Cost of Ownership (TCO)** due to idle capacity

### Mathematical Model of Static Provisioning

For a workload with peak demand $D_{peak}$ and average demand $D_{avg}$:

$$C_{static} = C_{unit} \times D_{peak} \times T_{window}$$

where:
- $C_{unit}$ = per-unit cost of resource
- $D_{peak}$ = peak demand (provisioned capacity)
- $T_{window}$ = provisioning window in time units

The **wasted cost** becomes:

$$C_{waste} = C_{unit} \times (D_{peak} - D_{avg}) \times T_{window}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Trap:** Static provisioning is **NOT always inferior**. For workloads with stable demand (variance $\sigma^2 \approx 0$), static provisioning achieves **near-100% utilization** and beats dynamic provisioning in TCO.

## 2.3 Dynamic Provisioning — Deep Analysis

### Operational Characteristics
- Capacity is **continuously adjusted** based on real-time metrics (CPU%, memory, queue depth, request rate).
- Uses **threshold-based, scheduled, or predictive (ML-based)** scaling policies.
- Powered by **autoscalers** (e.g., AWS Auto Scaling Groups, OpenStack Heat, Kubernetes HPA).

### Scaling Modes

| Mode | Trigger | Reaction Time | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Reactive (Threshold)** | CPU > 70% | 1–5 min | Spiky web traffic |
| **Scheduled** | Calendar / Cron | Hours ahead | Business hour load |
| **Predictive (ML)** | Time-series forecast | 10–30 min ahead | Diurnal SaaS patterns |
| **Hybrid** | Forecast + Threshold | 5–15 min | Enterprise production |

### Mathematical Model of Dynamic Provisioning

Let $D(t)$ be the demand function over time. The provisioned capacity $C(t)$ lags the demand by provisioning delay $T_p$:

$$C(t) = \alpha \cdot D(t - T_p) + \beta \cdot \frac{dD}{dt}$$

where:
- $\alpha$ = demand-tracking coefficient (typically $\alpha = 1$)
- $\beta$ = rate-of-change coefficient
- $T_p$ = provisioning latency
- $\frac{dD}{dt}$ = demand velocity

The **elasticity efficiency** can be quantified as:

$$\eta_{elastic} = \frac{\int_0^T \min(C(t), D(t))\, dt}{\int_0^T C(t)\, dt}$$

Ideal elasticity: $\eta_{elastic} = 1$ (provisioning perfectly tracks demand without wastage or starvation).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Key Insight:** Dynamic provisioning can suffer from **oscillation** — if scaling-in and scaling-out thresholds are too close, the system can thrash. The **hysteresis gap** (deadband) between scale-out and scale-in thresholds is critical.

## 2.4 Comparison Matrix: Static vs Dynamic Provisioning

| Parameter | Static Provisioning | Dynamic Provisioning |
|---|---|---|
| **Trigger** | Manual / Scheduled | Automated / Real-time |
| **Latency** | Zero (pre-provisioned) | Non-zero ($T_p$ seconds) |
| **Cost Efficiency** | Low at low utilization | High at variable utilization |
| **Complexity** | Low | High (needs monitoring, policies) |
| **SLA Compliance** | High for known peaks | High if scaled fast enough |
| **Use Cases** | Predictable workloads | Variable / bursty workloads |
| **Risk** | Over-provisioning | Under-provisioning / Oscillation |

## 2.5 OpenStack — The De-Facto Open-Source Private Cloud

### Origin & Governance
- **Originally released:** July 2010 by **NASA** and **Rackspace**
- **Current steward:** **OpenInfra Foundation**
- **License:** **Apache 2.0**
- **Latest release series:** **2024.x / Dalmatian / Caracal** (KTU students should mention "Caracal" or "Dalmatian" as current)
- **Written primarily in:** **Python**

### Architectural Philosophy
OpenStack follows a **modular, microservices-style architecture** where each functional capability is a separate **service (project)** that exposes a **REST API**. This contrasts with monolithic cloud platforms.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Design Principle:** *"A service for every concern."* Compute, networking, storage, identity, orchestration, telemetry — all are independent, swappable services that communicate via message queues and REST.

## 2.6 OpenStack Core Services — High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Service | Code Name | Function | Equivalent AWS Service |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Compute** | **Nova** | Provision & manage VMs | EC2 |
| **Networking** | **Neutron** | Software-Defined Networking (SDN) | VPC |
| **Object Storage** | **Swift** | Scalable object/blob store | S3 |
| **Block Storage** | **Cinder** | Persistent block volumes | EBS |
| **Identity** | **Keystone** | Authentication, authorization, RBAC | IAM |
| **Image** | **Glance** | VM disk image catalog | AMI |
| **Dashboard** | **Horizon** | Web-based UI | AWS Console |
| **Orchestration** | **Heat** | Template-based infra-as-code | CloudFormation |
| **Telemetry** | **Ceilometer** | Metering & monitoring | CloudWatch |
| **Database** | **Trove** | DB-as-a-Service | RDS |
| **DNS** | **Designate** | DNS service | Route 53 |
| **Key Manager** | **Barbican** | Secret/key management | KMS |
| **Shared File Sys** | **Manila** | Shared filesystems | EFS |
| **Bare Metal** | **Ironic** | Physical server provisioning | bare metal EC2 |

### Communication & Message Bus

OpenStack services communicate through:
- **AMQP (Advanced Message Queuing Protocol)** — typically **RabbitMQ**
- **REST APIs** (HTTP/HTTPS) — between services and external clients
- **SQL databases** — typically **MySQL/MariaDB** or **PostgreSQL** for state persistence

## 2.7 OpenStack Logical Architecture

OpenStack's logical architecture comprises three concentric rings:

### Ring 1 — Outer: Presentation Layer
- **Horizon** (Web UI) and CLI clients
- Issues REST API calls

### Ring 2 — Middle: Logical / Orchestration Layer
- **Keystone** (auth), **Heat** (orchestration), **Ceilometer** (monitoring)
- **Glance** (image catalog)
- All communicate via **AMQP (RabbitMQ)** bus

### Ring 3 — Inner: Resource Layer
- **Nova** (compute via hypervisors: KVM, Xen, VMware vSphere)
- **Neutron** (SDN with Open vSwitch, OVS)
- **Cinder** (block storage via LVM, Ceph, NFS)
- **Swift** (object storage with ring topology)

### Shared Services
- **Database (MariaDB/PostgreSQL)** — state
- **Message Queue (RabbitMQ)** — async communication
- **Identity (Keystone)** — auth tokens

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 14-Mark Question Pattern:** "Explain OpenStack architecture with neat diagram. List any 8 core services and their functions." → Answer MUST include the **3-ring model**, **AMQP bus**, **Keystone at the center**, and **hypervisor integration**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Optimal Capacity Under Static Provisioning

### Problem Setup
A university runs an online examination portal. Historical data shows the demand distribution over a 4-hour exam window follows a known mean $\mu$ and standard deviation $\sigma$. The university uses **static provisioning** with a safety factor $k$ (number of standard deviations above mean).

### Step 1: Define the Required Capacity
To guarantee SLA with confidence level $P$, the provisioned capacity must satisfy:

$$D_{peak} = \mu + k \cdot \sigma$$

where $k$ is derived from the inverse cumulative distribution function of the demand distribution.

### Step 2: For a Normal (Gaussian) Demand Distribution
If $D \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma^2)$, then the $z$-score for a given confidence $P$ is:

$$k = z_{P} = \Phi^{-1}(P)$$

where $\Phi^{-1}$ is the inverse standard normal CDF.

### Step 3: Common Confidence Levels
For commonly used confidence intervals:

| Confidence $P$ | $z_P$ (k value) |
|---|---|
| 90% | 1.28 |
| 95% | 1.645 |
| 99% | 2.33 |
| 99.9% | 3.09 |

### Step 4: Worked Numerical Example
Suppose:
- Mean demand $\mu = 500$ VMs
- Std. dev. $\sigma = 80$ VMs
- Desired SLA confidence $P = 99\%$

Then:

$$\begin{aligned}
D_{peak} &= \mu + z_{0.99} \cdot \sigma \\
&= 500 + (2.33)(80) \\
&= 500 + 186.4 \\
&= 686.4 \text{ VMs}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 5: Cost Computation
If each VM costs $C_{unit} = \$0.10$/hour and the exam runs $T = 4$ hours:

$$\begin{aligned}
C_{static} &= C_{unit} \times D_{peak} \times T \\
&= 0.10 \times 686.4 \times 4 \\
&= \$274.56
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 6: Wasted Cost
The average demand was 500 VMs, so the *unutilized* cost is:

$$\begin{aligned}
C_{waste} &= C_{unit} \times (D_{peak} - \mu) \times T \\
&= 0.10 \times (686.4 - 500) \times 4 \\
&= 0.10 \times 186.4 \times 4 \\
&= \$74.56
\end{aligned}$$

The **utilization ratio** is:

$$U = \frac{\mu}{D_{peak}} = \frac{500}{686.4} \approx 72.83\%$$

This is the **stochastic cost of certainty** in static provisioning.

## 3.2 Derivation: Dynamic Provisioning Break-Even Point

### Problem Setup
Compare static vs dynamic provisioning. Under static, you pay for $D_{peak}$ always. Under dynamic, you pay only for the demand $D(t)$ at each instant, but you also pay a **scaling overhead cost** per scale event.

### Step 1: Total Dynamic Cost
$$C_{dynamic} = C_{unit} \cdot \int_0^T D(t)\, dt + C_{event} \cdot N_{events}$$

where $N_{events}$ is the number of scaling events and $C_{event}$ is the per-event overhead (e.g., VM boot time, API calls, license fees).

### Step 2: Total Static Cost
$$C_{static} = C_{unit} \cdot D_{peak} \cdot T$$

### Step 3: Break-Even Condition
Dynamic becomes cheaper when $C_{dynamic} < C_{static}$:

$$C_{unit} \cdot \int_0^T D(t)\, dt + C_{event} \cdot N_{events} < C_{unit} \cdot D_{peak} \cdot T$$

Rearranging:

$$C_{event} \cdot N_{events} < C_{unit} \cdot T \cdot \left(D_{peak} - \bar{D}\right)$$

where $\bar{D} = \frac{1}{T}\int_0^T D(t)\,dt$ is the time-averaged demand.

### Step 4: Interpretation
- **LHS** = total scaling overhead cost (penalty for dynamism)
- **RHS** = total wasted capacity cost (penalty for staticness)
- **Dynamic wins** when: *demand variance × duration × unit cost > scaling overhead*

### Step 5: Worked Example
Let:
- $T = 24$ hours
- $D_{peak} = 1000$ VMs
- $\bar{D} = 400$ VMs
- $C_{unit} = \$0.05$/hour/VM
- $C_{event} = \$0.50$/event
- Demand pattern: 1 scale-out + 1 scale-in per hour ⇒ $N_{events} = 48$

Compute both sides:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{LHS} &= C_{event} \cdot N_{events} = 0.50 \times 48 = \$24.00 \\
\text{RHS} &= C_{unit} \cdot T \cdot (D_{peak} - \bar{D}) \\
&= 0.05 \times 24 \times (1000 - 400) \\
&= 0.05 \times 24 \times 600 \\
&= \$720.00
\end{aligned}$$

Since LHS (\\$24) $\ll$ RHS (\\$720), **dynamic provisioning is 30× more cost-effective** in this scenario.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** Dynamic provisioning is *always* preferred when demand has high variance, **except** when provisioning overhead $C_{event}$ is so high (e.g., legacy mainframes) that it dominates the savings.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Decision Flow for Choosing Provisioning Mode

| Condition | Recommended Mode | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Demand variance $\sigma^2 \approx 0$ | **Static** | No benefit from dynamism |
| Demand has clear peak pattern | **Static (peak-sized)** | One-time sizing optimal |
| Demand is bursty / unpredictable | **Dynamic** | Elasticity saves cost |
| Provisioning latency $T_p$ is huge (mins) | **Static** | Dynamic cannot react fast enough |
| Workload is mission-critical, SLA ≥ 99.99% | **Static + Dynamic buffer** | Belt-and-suspenders strategy |
| $C_{event}$ is very high | **Static** | Scaling overhead exceeds savings |
| Workload is short-lived (minutes) | **Dynamic** | No time for static sizing |
| Workload is long-lived (months) | **Hybrid** | Static baseline + dynamic buffer |

## 3.4 OpenStack Nova — Provisioning Workflow (Step-by-Step)

When a user launches a VM in OpenStack Nova, the following sequence executes:

1. **API Request Reception** → Nova API (`nova-api`) receives POST request with flavor + image ID via Horizon or CLI.
2. **Authentication** → Request token validated against **Keystone** (identity service).
3. **Database Record Creation** → VM state stored in **MariaDB** with status = `BUILDING`.
4. **RPC to Scheduler** → Message published to **RabbitMQ** queue, picked up by `nova-scheduler`.
5. **Filtering & Weighing** → Scheduler runs filters (availability zone, RAM, CPU fit) and weights to pick the best **compute node**.
6. **RPC to Compute Node** → `nova-compute` service on the chosen host receives the launch task.
7. **Image Retrieval** → `nova-compute` asks **Glance** for the image; image is cached locally or streamed via HTTP.
8. **Hypervisor Invocation** → For **KVM** hypervisor, `libvirt` API creates the VM, attaches the disk image, and connects to **Neutron** for the virtual NIC.
9. **Volume Attachment** → If requested, `nova-compute` attaches a **Cinder** volume via iSCSI or RBD.
10. **State Transition** → Database updated to status = `ACTIVE`; **Ceilometer** records the launch event for billing/telemetry.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 14-Mark Favorite Question:** *"Trace the request flow when a user launches a VM in OpenStack. List all services involved."* → The above 10-step trace is the **gold-standard model answer**.

## 3.5 OpenStack Deployment Topologies

| Topology | Description | Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| **Single-Node (All-in-One)** | All services on one VM/laptop | Dev/test, learning |
| **Multi-Node (Controller + Compute)** | Controller hosts control plane, separate compute nodes | Production small-scale |
| **Multi-Node (Controller + Network + Compute + Storage)** | Full disaggregation | Enterprise production |
| **HA (High Availability)** | Multiple controllers, clustered DB, VRRP for API endpoints | Carrier-grade deployments |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

## 4.2 Resource Provisioning Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Workload Request Arrives] --> B{Is Demand Pattern Known?}
    B -- YES Predictable --> C{Is Variance High?}
    B -- NO Unpredictable --> D[Use Dynamic Provisioning]

    C -- NO Low Variance --> E[Use Static Provisioning]
    C -- YES High Variance --> F{Is Provisioning Latency T_p Acceptable?}

    F -- YES --> G[Reactive Dynamic with Auto Scaler]
    F -- NO --> H[Static Baseline + Dynamic Buffer]

    E --> I[Reserve D_peak = mu + k sigma]
    G --> J[Threshold or Predictive Autoscaling]
    H --> K[Hybrid Strategy]

    I --> L[Deploy to Pre-Provisioned Pool]
    J --> M[Monitor Metrics Trigger Scale]
    K --> N[Static for SLA + Dynamic for Peaks]

    L --> O[Continuous Monitoring]
    M --> O
    N --> O

    O --> P{SLA Violated?}
    P -- YES --> Q[Increase Buffer or Tune Thresholds]
    P -- NO --> R[Continue Normal Operation]
```

## 4.3 OpenStack Nova Request Flow

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram

    participant H as Horizon UI
    participant API as Nova API
    participant K as Keystone
    participant SCH as Nova Scheduler

    participant G as Glance
    participant N as Neutron
    participant CI as Cinder

    U->>H: Click Launch Instance
    H->>API: POST /servers with flavor and image
    API->>K: Validate Auth Token
    K-->>API: Token OK with Roles
    API->>MQ: Publish build task
    MQ->>SCH: Pick best host
    SCH-->>MQ: Selected host compute node X
    MQ->>C: Build VM on host X
    C->>G: Fetch image
    G-->>C: Image stream

    C->>CI: Attach block volume
    CI-->>C: Volume attached

    H-->>U: Show VM console
```


# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 / Model]
**Differentiate between static and dynamic resource provisioning in cloud computing.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Static Provisioning | Dynamic Provisioning |
|---|---|---|
| **Trigger** | Manual, pre-planned | Automated, real-time |
| **Allocation Time** | Before workload arrives | When workload demands it |
| **Resource Utilization** | Low (over-provisioned) | High (matches demand) |
| **Provisioning Latency** | None at runtime | $T_p$ seconds at runtime |
| **Cost Model** | Pay for peak always | Pay for actual usage + scaling overhead |
| **SLA Risk** | Low (over-provisioned) | Moderate (under-provisioning risk) |
| **Best Suited For** | Predictable workloads | Variable / bursty workloads |
| **Example** | University exam server, ERP | E-commerce site during festive sales |

> [!NOTE]
> **Valuation Tip:** A simple 3-row table with correct trade-off identification gets full 3 marks. No diagrams needed for 3-mark questions.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024 / Model]
**What is OpenStack? List any four core services of OpenStack with their functions.**

**Model Answer:**

**OpenStack** is an open-source cloud operating system (licensed under **Apache 2.0**) used to build private and hybrid IaaS clouds. It is maintained by the **OpenInfra Foundation** and written primarily in **Python**.

Four core services:

1. **Nova (Compute)** — Provisions and manages virtual machines (VMs) on demand using hypervisors like **KVM**, **Xen**, or **vSphere**.
2. **Neutron (Networking)** — Provides Software-Defined Networking (SDN) with support for VLANs, VXLAN, floating IPs, and routers via Open vSwitch.
3. **Cinder (Block Storage)** — Provides persistent block storage volumes attached to VMs, backed by LVM, Ceph, or NFS.
4. **Keystone (Identity)** — Centralized authentication and authorization service providing tokens, roles, and project/tenant management.

> [!NOTE]
> **Marks Split:** 1 mark for OpenStack definition + 2 marks for service-function pairs (0.5 each).

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam - Model Paper / KTU 2024 Scheme Pattern]**

**(a)** Explain the architecture of OpenStack with a neat block diagram. List any **eight** core services and their functions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare **static** and **dynamic** resource provisioning in detail. Discuss a real-world scenario where each is preferred. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A(a) — OpenStack Architecture (7 marks)

**Introduction (1 mark):**
OpenStack is a modular, open-source cloud platform with separate services for compute, network, storage, and orchestration. Services expose **REST APIs** and communicate via **AMQP (RabbitMQ)** message bus, with state stored in **MariaDB/PostgreSQL**.

**Architecture Diagram (2 marks):** The 3-ring model:

- **Presentation Layer:** Horizon (Dashboard), CLI, REST API endpoints
- **Logical / Orchestration Layer:** Keystone, Glance, Heat, Ceilometer
- **Resource Layer:** Nova, Neutron, Cinder, Swift
- **Shared Backbone:** RabbitMQ, Database, Identity

**Eight Core Services (4 marks — 0.5 per service):**

| # | Service | Function |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Nova** | Compute (VM lifecycle) |
| 2 | **Neutron** | Software-Defined Networking |
| 3 | **Cinder** | Persistent Block Storage |
| 4 | **Swift** | Scalable Object Storage |
| 5 | **Keystone** | Identity & RBAC |
| 6 | **Glance** | VM Image Registry |
| 7 | **Horizon** | Web-based Dashboard |
| 8 | **Heat** | Orchestration (HOT templates, equivalent to CloudFormation) |

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A(b) — Static vs Dynamic (7 marks)

**Definition Block (2 marks):**
- **Static Provisioning:** Resources are pre-allocated based on peak-demand estimation, fixed for a known duration, and not adjusted at runtime.
- **Dynamic Provisioning:** Resources are allocated, scaled, and de-allocated in real-time based on workload metrics and policy triggers.

**Comparative Analysis (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Static | Dynamic |
|---|---|---|
| Cost Efficiency | Low (idle capacity cost) | High (pay-as-you-use) |
| SLA Compliance | High when demand < provisioned | Depends on $T_p$ |
| Complexity | Low | High (needs monitors, policies) |
| Elasticity | None | Full |

**Mathematical Insight (1 mark):**
$$C_{static} = C_{unit} \times D_{peak} \times T$$
$$C_{dynamic} = C_{unit} \times \int_0^T D(t)\,dt + C_{event} \times N_{events}$$

**Real-World Scenarios (1 mark):**
- **Static preferred:** University semester-end exam portal — predictable, fixed window, 4 hours, known peak.
- **Dynamic preferred:** E-commerce portal (Flipkart/Amazon Big Billion Days) — bursty, unpredictable, requires elastic scaling from 1000 to 100,000 VMs within minutes.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert:**
> - ❌ **Common Mistake 1:** Conflating OpenStack with OpenShift. OpenShift = container PaaS (Kubernetes-based). OpenStack = VM IaaS.
> - ❌ **Common Mistake 2:** Writing "OpenStack is a hypervisor" — wrong. OpenStack is an **orchestration platform**; the hypervisor is its **backend** (KVM, Xen, etc.).
> - ❌ **Common Mistake 3:** Confusing **Cinder** (block) with **Swift** (object). Block = mounted disk, byte-level access. Object = REST PUT/GET, flat namespace.
> - ❌ **Common Mistake 4:** Listing Horizon as an orchestration tool — it's only a **dashboard**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam - Model Paper / KTU 2024 Scheme Pattern]**

**(a)** What is dynamic provisioning? Explain the **three scaling modes** of dynamic provisioning with examples. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **OpenStack Nova architecture** and trace the **step-by-step workflow** of launching a virtual machine in OpenStack. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B(a) — Dynamic Provisioning Modes (7 marks)

**Definition (1 mark):**
Dynamic provisioning is the elastic, automated allocation and de-allocation of resources in real-time, governed by monitoring metrics, scheduling rules, or predictive analytics.

**Three Scaling Modes (6 marks — 2 marks each):**

**1. Reactive (Threshold-Based) Scaling**
- Triggers when a metric (CPU%, memory, queue length) crosses a configured threshold.
- Example: AWS Auto Scaling — when average CPU > 70% for 5 minutes, add 2 instances; when CPU < 30%, remove instances.
- **Pros:** Simple, reactive to actual load. **Cons:** Lags behind spikes; scaling-in hysteresis needed to prevent oscillation.

**2. Scheduled Scaling**
- Resources scaled according to a pre-defined calendar/cron schedule.
- Example: A B2B SaaS scales up to 50 instances every weekday 9 AM, scales down to 5 at 7 PM, weekends scale to 0.
- **Pros:** Predictable, no lag. **Cons:** Cannot handle unexpected spikes outside the schedule.

**3. Predictive (ML-Based) Scaling**
- Uses time-series forecasting (ARIMA, LSTM, Prophet) to predict future demand and provision ahead of time.
- Example: AWS Predictive Scaling uses ML on 14-day history to forecast next 2 days and pre-warms capacity.
- **Pros:** Combines benefit of scheduled + reactive. **Cons:** Requires historical data, ML pipeline overhead.

> [!NOTE]
> **Hybrid Scaling** is the modern enterprise default: predictive + reactive + scheduled combined.

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B(b) — OpenStack Nova Workflow (7 marks)

**Nova Architecture (3 marks):**
Nova has four sub-components:
- **`nova-api`** — REST API frontend, receives user requests
- **`nova-scheduler`** — Decides which compute node runs the VM (filter + weigh logic)
- **`nova-compute`** — Daemon on each compute host that manages VMs via hypervisor API
- **`nova-conductor`** — Mediates database access (compute nodes never talk to DB directly — security + scalability)

Supporting services: `nova-novncproxy` (console), `nova-consoleauth`.

**Step-by-Step VM Launch Workflow (4 marks — 0.5 per step):**

1. User issues `openstack server create` via CLI or Horizon → request hits **`nova-api`**.
2. **`nova-api`** authenticates with **Keystone** using user token.
3. Request details (flavor, image, network, keypair) are validated; database entry created with state `BUILDING`.
4. **`nova-api`** publishes a "schedule" message to **RabbitMQ**.
5. **`nova-scheduler`** consumes message, runs filters (AvailabilityZone, RamFilter, ComputeFilter, ImagePropertiesFilter) and weights, selects best compute node.
6. Scheduler publishes a "build" message to the chosen compute node's queue.
7. **`nova-compute`** on that node fetches the image from **Glance** (cached locally after first download).
8. **`nova-compute`** calls **libvirt** (for KVM) to define and start the VM with the specified vCPU/RAM/disk/NIC.
9. Neutron agent wires the vNIC to the appropriate OVS bridge; floating IP associated if requested.
10. Volume attached from **Cinder**; metadata propagated; state changed to `ACTIVE`; **Ceilometer** records the event.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert (Part B specific):**
> - ❌ **Common Mistake 1:** Skipping the role of **`nova-conductor`**. Many students omit this, losing 1 mark. **Conductor is the security boundary that prevents compute nodes from direct DB access.**
> - ❌ **Common Mistake 2:** Saying "Nova uses KVM" — Nova *supports* KVM/Xen/vSphere/VMware; it does not use only KVM.
> - ❌ **Common Mistake 3:** Confusing **AMQP bus** with API. APIs are synchronous HTTP for clients; AMQP is for inter-service async communication.
> - ❌ **Common Mistake 4:** Missing state transition (`BUILDING → ACTIVE`) in the trace.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Must-Know Before Exam**

### Provisioning Fundamentals
- ⭐ **Static Provisioning** = Pre-allocated, fixed, peak-sized, no elasticity, high TCO at low utilization
- ⭐ **Dynamic Provisioning** = Elastic, automated, three modes: **Reactive, Scheduled, Predictive**
- ⭐ Hybrid (Static baseline + Dynamic buffer) is the **enterprise production default**
- ⭐ The 5-stage provisioning pipeline: **Request → Admission → Placement → Provision → Release**
- ⭐ The break-even condition: dynamic wins when $C_{event} \cdot N_{events} < C_{unit} \cdot T \cdot (D_{peak} - \bar{D})$

### OpenStack Must-Know
- ⭐ OpenStack = **Apache 2.0 licensed**, **Python-written**, **OpenInfra Foundation**-governed
- ⭐ Modular architecture — **a service for every concern**
- ⭐ Communication: **REST API** (sync) + **AMQP/RabbitMQ** (async) + **SQL DB** (state)
- ⭐ **Nova = Compute** (equivalent to AWS EC2)
- ⭐ **Neutron = Networking / SDN** (equivalent to AWS VPC)
- ⭐ **Cinder = Block Storage** (equivalent to AWS EBS)
- ⭐ **Swift = Object Storage** (equivalent to AWS S3)
- ⭐ **Keystone = Identity & RBAC** (equivalent to AWS IAM)
- ⭐ **Glance = Image Catalog** (equivalent to AWS AMI registry)
- ⭐ **Horizon = Web Dashboard**
- ⭐ **Heat = Orchestration via HOT templates** (equivalent to AWS CloudFormation)
- ⭐ **Ceilometer = Telemetry & Metering** (equivalent to AWS CloudWatch)
- ⭐ **Nova sub-components:** `nova-api`, `nova-scheduler`, `nova-compute`, `nova-conductor`
- ⭐ **Conductor's role:** Mediates DB access (compute nodes NEVER touch DB directly)

### Examiner High-Yield Traps to Avoid
- ❌ Don't call OpenStack a "hypervisor" — it's an **orchestration platform**
- ❌ Don't confuse **Cinder (block)** with **Swift (object)**
- ❌ Don't confuse **OpenStack** (IaaS) with **OpenShift/Kubernetes** (container PaaS)
- ❌ Don't list Horizon as an orchestrator — it's only a dashboard
- ❌ Always mention **Apache 2.0 license** when defining OpenStack — it's a favorite 1-mark sub-question
- ⭐ **Mnemonic for Nova Workflow:** **"AASC"** — **A**PI → **A**uth (Keystone) → **S**chedule (Filter+Weigh) → **C**ompute (libvirt boot)

### Formulae Quick-Flash
$$\boxed{C_{static} = C_{unit} \times D_{peak} \times T}$$

$$\boxed{C_{dynamic} = C_{unit} \cdot \int_0^T D(t)\,dt + C_{event} \cdot N_{events}}$$

$$\boxed{U = \frac{D_{avg}}{D_{peak}} \times 100\%}$$

$$\boxed{\eta_{elastic} = \frac{\int_0^T \min(C(t), D(t))\,dt}{\int_0^T C(t)\,dt}}$$
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
