<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CloudStack (Apache CloudStack™)** is an open-source, turnkey **Infrastructure-as-a-Service (IaaS)** cloud orchestration platform written predominantly in **Java**. It is used to deploy, orchestrate, and manage large, elastic pools of **virtual machines** across multiple **hypervisors**, **network fabrics**, and **storage backends**.

In the context of the KTU 2024 syllabus (Module 3 – *Cloud Platforms & Cloud Software* emphasis), CloudStack is positioned as a *canonical example* of a self-managed private/hybrid cloud operating system that exposes both a **native RESTful API** and a **legacy Amazon EC2/S3 compatibility API**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The "Apartment Building" Cloud**
> - **CloudStack = The Building Management Office (BMO).**
> - **Management Server = The Reception Desk** that accepts requests ("I want a flat for 4 guests, 3 nights, north-facing") and dispatches the right workers.
> - **Zone = The Apartment Complex.** Several complexes make up a city.
> - **Pod = A single Tower/Block** inside the complex (one floor of networking).
> - **Cluster = A Floor** within a tower, where flats share the same plumbing (storage).
> - **Host = A single Apartment** that actually houses the tenants (VMs).
> - **Primary Storage = The Building's overhead water tank** (always-on disk for live VMs).
> - **Secondary Storage = The Building's warehouse** (where empty flats/templates are kept until needed).

So, when a user clicks "Deploy VM", the **reception desk (Management Server)** chooses an **apartment (Host)** with available resources, copies a **template from the warehouse (Secondary Storage)** to the **water tank (Primary Storage)**, plugs in networking, and hands over the keys — all automatically.

## 1.3 Key Terminology at a Glance

| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| IaaS | Infrastructure-as-a-Service |
| Hypervisor | Software that creates and runs VMs (e.g., KVM, XenServer, VMware ESXi) |
| Orchestration | Automated arrangement, coordination, and management of cloud resources |
| Self-Service Portal | Browser-based GUI allowing tenants to provision their own VMs |
| Tenancy | Logical isolation between users/organizations on shared hardware |

## 1.4 Historical Context (Examiner's Favourite)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **2008:** Developed by *Cloud.com* (originally called *VMOps*).
> - **2011:** Acquired by **Citrix Systems** and released as **Citrix CloudStack**.
> - **2012:** Donated to the **Apache Software Foundation (ASF)** as an *Incubator* project.
> - **2013:** Graduated as **Apache Top-Level Project (TLP)**.
> - Current license: **Apache License 2.0** (permissive, business-friendly).
> - Currently used in production by large telcos (e.g., Bharti Airtel, KT, KDDI, Nokia Networks).

---

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & High-Yield Reference

## 2.1 The CloudStack Logical Hierarchy (Organizational Units)

CloudStack organizes physical and logical infrastructure into a **strict 6-level hierarchy**:

1. **Region** — A collection of one or more *Zones* (geographic grouping).
2. **Zone** — The largest container of physical resources; typically a single **data center**.
3. **Pod** — A rack or collection of racks sharing a common **Layer-2 broadcast domain**.
4. **Cluster** — A group of *homogeneous* Hosts that share the **same Primary Storage**.
5. **Host** — A single physical server running a supported hypervisor.
6. **Primary Storage** — Repository of live VM disk volumes attached to a cluster.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundary Rule:** VMs are scoped to **Clusters**, but cloud user accounts (tenants) span the entire **Zone**.

## 2.2 Core Architectural Components

| Component | Role |
|---|---|
| **Management Server (MS)** | Central brain. Runs the Tomcat servlet + Java services. Hosts the DB, API endpoints, UI. Stateless horizontally-scalable. |
| **MySQL Database** | Backs the Management Server. Stores state of zones, hosts, VMs, networks, users. |
| **Hosts (Computing Nodes)** | Run the hypervisor agent (e.g., libvirt for KVM). The Management Server talks to agents via **agent-direct routing**. |
| **Primary Storage** | Block (iSCSI, FC, Ceph RBD) or File (NFS, Local FS). Holds running VM disk volumes. |
| **Secondary Storage** | Always **NFS** (or object-store via Swift/S3). Holds templates, ISOs, snapshots, volume backups. |
| **Virtual Router (VR)** | Per-Network software appliance providing DHCP, DNS, NAT, VPN, LB, FW. Implements the "Advanced Zone" networking. |
| **Console Proxy VM (CPVM)** | Provides browser-based VNC/HTTP console to user VMs. |
| **Secondary Storage VM (SSVM)** | Handles template/ISO/snapshot download, upload, copy between zones. |

## 2.3 High-Yield Reference Sheet — CloudStack Cheat Card

| Feature | Detail | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| **Source language** | Java | Backend services |
| **License** | Apache 2.0 | Open source |
| **Communication** | HTTP/HTTPS REST + XML/JSON | Native API |
| **DB backend** | MySQL / MariaDB | Required |
| **Multi-hypervisor** | KVM, VMware vSphere, XenServer/XCP-ng, Hyper-V, LXC | Wide support |
| **Networking modes** | Basic, Advanced, Isolated, VPC, Shared | Tenant-controlled |
| **UI** | Web GUI (AngularJS) + API | Browser-based |
| **API parity** | Native CloudStack API + EC2/S3 bridge | Hybrid-cloud friendly |
| **HA feature** | VM HA, Host HA, Management Server HA | Built-in |

## 2.4 Networking Models — The Most Tested Sub-Topic

> [!NOTE]
> CloudStack uses **Network Offerings** to bundle *services* (DHCP, DNS, Firewall, LB, VPN, NAT, etc.) that are then associated with a guest network. Three foundational network types:

### (a) Basic Zone Networking
- One big shared network per zone.
- The Management Server hands out IP/MAC addresses.
- **No** Virtual Router per account.
- Suited for simple test/dev setups.

### (b) Advanced Zone Networking (Default)
- **Per-tenant isolated network** (VLAN, GRE, or VXLAN).
- A **Virtual Router (VR)** is spawned per account/network offering to provide NAT, DHCP, DNS, Firewall, LB, VPN, Userdata.
- The most commonly deployed model in production.

### (c) VPC (Virtual Private Cloud)
- Enterprise-grade: tenant owns a **router**, defines multiple **tiers** (subnets), attaches **ACLs**, **VPN gateways**, **site-to-site IPSec**.
- Supports **private gateways** to corporate datacenters.

## 2.5 Storage Offerings

| Type | Examples |
|---|---|
| **Local** | Local host filesystem |
| **Shared File** | NFS |
| **Shared Block** | iSCSI, Fiber Channel, Ceph RBD, GlusterFS |
| **Object** | Swift, S3 (used as Secondary Storage via S3-HTTP bridge) |

> [!TIP]
> Snapshots are *diff-based* in CloudStack and are stored on Secondary Storage. Volume backups (full copies) are also written to Secondary Storage.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

CloudStack (and similar IaaS orchestrators like **OpenStack**) is the **de-facto platform** in:
- **Telco NFV clouds** (Airtel, Reliance Jio, Vodafone).
- **Academic private clouds** (university teaching/research clusters).
- **Government sovereign clouds** (e.g., IndiaStack, MeghRaj).
- **HPC clouds** for scientific computing.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Walkthrough — "What Happens When You Click Deploy VM"

This is the **single most important KTU-style question**: *Trace the lifecycle of a VM deployment in CloudStack.*

### Step 0 — Pre-conditions (Deterministic Setup)
The Administrator has registered a Zone, Pod, Cluster, Host(s), Primary Storage, and Secondary Storage. A Network Offering and Service Offering exist.

### Step 1 — User Request Ingestion

The authenticated tenant sends an HTTP API call:

$$
\text{POST } / \text{client}/\text{api}?\text{command=deployVirtualMachine}\&\text{zoneId}=z\text{-101}\&\text{templateId}=t\text{-203}\&\text{serviceOfferingId}=s\text{-504}
$$

CloudStack accepts the request at the **API Dispatcher (Tomcat servlet)**. The dispatcher authenticates the session via a SHA-1 signed **API key/secret** pair.

### Step 2 — Resource Allocation Logic (The "Allocator")

The Management Server invokes the **First-Fit Allocator** (or user-selected deployment planner):

$$
\text{SelectHost}(H) = \min_{h \in \text{AvailableHosts}} \Big( \text{Score}(h) \Big)
$$

where

$$
\text{Score}(h) = w_1 \cdot \text{CPU}_{\text{free}}(h) + w_2 \cdot \text{RAM}_{\text{free}}(h) + w_3 \cdot \text{IOPS}_{\text{free}}(h) - w_4 \cdot \text{Load}_{\text{avg}}(h)
$$

Weights $(w_1, w_2, w_3, w_4)$ are tuned by the administrator to bias placement.

### Step 3 — IP Address Assignment

The Management Server queries the **Pod-level IP pool** to allocate an unused guest IP:

$$
\text{GuestIP}_{\text{new}} = \text{IPAllocator}(\text{Pod}_{\text{ID}}, \text{NetworkOffering}_{\text{ID}})
$$

For an Advanced Zone, it also decides whether to start a **Virtual Router (VR)** if not already running for that account/network.

### Step 4 — Volume Provisioning on Primary Storage

A new VM volume is created on Primary Storage:

$$
\text{VolSize} = \max(\text{TemplateSize}, \text{DiskOfferingSize})
$$

The volume is formatted with the hypervisor's native format (qcow2 for KVM, VHD for Hyper-V, VMDK for VMware).

### Step 5 — Template Copy (Secondary → Primary)

If the template resides on Secondary Storage of a different zone, the **Secondary Storage VM (SSVM)** downloads it via HTTP, copies it across zones, and makes it available locally.

$$
\text{Copy}(T_{\text{remote}} \to T_{\text{local}}) \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Volume created from } T_{\text{local}}
$$

### Step 6 — Agent-Direct Push to Host

The Management Server sends an XML/JSON command directly to the **hypervisor agent** on the selected host via **agent-direct routing** (no SSH, no message queue — *important distinction from OpenStack's RabbitMQ*).

$$
\text{Agent}_{\text{host}} \leftarrow \text{CreateVMCMD}(\text{VMName}, \text{VolPath}, \text{NetCfg}, \text{ISO})
$$

### Step 7 — VM Boot & State Transition

The hypervisor starts the VM. The agent reports back status:

$$
\text{State}_{\text{VM}} : \text{Starting} \to \text{Running}
$$

The Management Server updates the **MySQL `vm_instance` table** with `state='Running'`.

### Step 8 — Console Proxy Provisioning

If the user requests console access, the **Console Proxy VM (CPVM)** establishes a VNC tunnel from the user's browser to the VM's hypervisor port.

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    autonumber
    participant U as Tenant

    participant MS as Management Server

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define CloudStack. List any two hypervisors supported by it. **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Set-B]**

**Model Answer:**
CloudStack is an **open-source Infrastructure-as-a-Service (IaaS) cloud orchestration platform** developed originally by Cloud.com, later donated to the **Apache Software Foundation**, currently licensed under **Apache 2.0**. It enables the deployment, management, and automation of large pools of virtual machines, networking, and storage.

### Q2. Differentiate between Primary Storage and Secondary Storage in CloudStack. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Primary Storage | Secondary Storage |
|---|---|---|
| Purpose | Holds **running** VM disk volumes | Holds **templates, ISOs, snapshots** |
| Access pattern | High-IOPS, hot data | Cold data, occasional reads |
| Protocols | NFS, iSCSI, FC, Ceph RBD, Local FS | **NFS only** (native); S3/Swift via bridge |
| Scope | Per Cluster | Per Zone |
| Used during | VM runtime | Template copy / VM provisioning |

## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **architecture of CloudStack**. List its major components and state the function of each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **hierarchical organization of resources** in CloudStack (Region → Zone → Pod → Cluster → Host). Why is the Cluster concept critical for shared Primary Storage? **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution — Question A

#### Part (a) — Architecture (7 marks)

The CloudStack architecture has **four major logical layers**:

**Layer 1 — Client Layer**
- **Web UI (AngularJS)** and **API clients** (curl, CloudMonkey CLI, Terraform provider, AWS-CLI bridge).

**Layer 2 — Management Plane**
- **Management Server** (Tomcat-hosted servlet container, stateless Java services).
- **MySQL Database** (state store: zones, hosts, VMs, networks, accounts).
- Communicates with hosts using **agent-direct HTTP/XML** routing — no message queue.

**Layer 3 — Hypervisor / Compute Layer**
- Hosts running **KVM / XenServer / VMware / Hyper-V**.
- Each host runs a small **agent** that receives commands and reports state.

**Layer 4 — Storage Layer**
- **Primary Storage** (per cluster) – VM volumes.
- **Secondary Storage** (per zone) – templates, ISOs, snapshots.

**Auxiliary Service VMs (spawned on demand):**
- **Console Proxy VM (CPVM)** – browser-based VM console.
- **Secondary Storage VM (SSVM)** – template download/copy.
- **Virtual Router (VR)** – DHCP/DNS/NAT/Load-Balancer per tenant.

**(7 marks: 3 for diagram, 2 for components, 2 for service VMs)**

#### Part (b) — Hierarchy (7 marks)

The hierarchy is **strictly nested** for resource scoping:

$$
\text{Region} \supset \text{Zone} \supset \text{Pod} \supset \text{Cluster} \supset \text{Host}
$$

- **Region** – geographic grouping of zones (used only for API parameter scoping).
- **Zone** – a data center, the largest unit a tenant sees.
- **Pod** – a Layer-2 broadcast boundary (one rack or one switch group).
- **Cluster** – a group of **homogeneous hosts** that share **the same Primary Storage**.
- **Host** – the actual physical server.

**Why Cluster is critical for Primary Storage:**
- A Cluster is the **smallest unit** at which Primary Storage is shared.
- Live migration (VMotion/XenMotion/KVM live-migration) is **only possible within a Cluster** because the shared storage means the VM's disk volume does not need to be copied to a new Primary Storage target.
- Resource limits and capacity planning are typically enforced per-Cluster.

**(7 marks: 3 for hierarchy definition, 4 for Cluster + shared-storage reasoning)**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a)** Explain **Basic Zone**, **Advanced Zone**, and **VPC** network models in CloudStack with suitable diagrams. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write and explain the **step-by-step sequence of events** that occur when a tenant deploys a Virtual Machine using the CloudStack API. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution — Question B

#### Part (a) — Network Models (7 marks)

**Basic Zone**
- Single shared network per zone.
- Management Server directly assigns IP/MAC to VMs.
- **No Virtual Router** — limited services (no per-tenant NAT, no VPN, no LB).
- Use case: dev/test, simple labs.

**Advanced Zone**
- **Isolated per-tenant network** (VLAN/GRE/VXLAN).
- **Virtual Router (VR)** per account provides: **DHCP, DNS, NAT, Firewall, Load Balancer (HAProxy), VPN (IPSec), Userdata**.
- Default production model. Supports multiple guest networks per tenant.

**VPC (Virtual Private Cloud)**
- Tenant owns a **VPC Router** and defines **multiple subnets/tiers**.
- Supports **Network ACLs**, **Site-to-Site IPSec VPN**, **Private Gateway** to corporate DC, **Static NAT**.
- Most enterprise-grade offering.

| Model | Virtual Router | Tenant Isolation | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| Basic | No | None (shared) | Lab |
| Advanced | Yes (per account) | VLAN/GRE/VXLAN | Production |
| VPC | Yes (per VPC) | Multiple subnets + ACL | Enterprise |

#### Part (b) — VM Deploy Lifecycle (7 marks)

(See detailed walkthrough in **Section 3** of these notes.)

Summary steps:
1. **API call received** by Dispatcher; session authenticated via SHA-1 signature.
2. **Allocator selects host** (First-Fit scoring on CPU, RAM, IOPS).
3. **IP reserved** from Pod-level pool.
4. **Volume created** on Cluster Primary Storage.
5. **Template copied** from Secondary Storage (via SSVM if cross-zone).
6. **Virtual Router** spawned/verified for tenant network.
7. **Agent-direct command** sent to host hypervisor.
8. **VM boots**; CPVM ready for console access; state persisted in MySQL.

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common pitfalls — where students lose marks:**
> 1. **Confusing CloudStack with OpenStack.** OpenStack uses RabbitMQ + AMQP + Python; CloudStack uses **agent-direct HTTP** + **Java/MySQL**. Examiners specifically test this distinction.
> 2. **Forgetting SSVM and CPVM** in architecture diagrams — these are *service VMs*, not optional. Always label them.
> 3. **Misstating the license.** It is **Apache 2.0** (not GPL, not BSD).
> 4. **Cluster scope confusion.** Shared Primary Storage is **Cluster-level**, not Zone-level.
> 5. **Ignoring the EC2/S3 API bridge** — a common *differentiator* question.
> 6. **Writing about "OpenStack Nova" in CloudStack answers** — instant 0 for that sub-part.

---

## Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

- **CloudStack** = open-source Java-based IaaS orchestrator, **Apache 2.0**.
- Acquired by **Citrix (2011)** → donated to **Apache (2012)** → **TLP 2013**.
- **Hierarchical scoping:** Region ⊃ Zone ⊃ Pod ⊃ Cluster ⊃ Host.
- **Three storage scopes:** Local (per host), Primary (per cluster), Secondary (per zone).
- **Three service VMs on demand:** **CPVM** (console), **SSVM** (template/snapshot), **VR** (network).
- **Networking models:** Basic, Advanced (default), VPC, Shared.
- **Communication:** **Agent-direct HTTP/XML** (no RabbitMQ — distinguishing feature).
- **Database:** MySQL/MariaDB is mandatory.
- **Hypervisor support:** KVM, XenServer, VMware, Hyper-V, LXC.
- **API:** Native REST + Amazon EC2/S3 compatibility bridge.
- **Use cases:** Telco NFV, academic clouds, sovereign clouds.
- **First-Fit Allocator** selects host based on weighted scoring of CPU, RAM, IOPS.
- **Live migration** restricted to a single Cluster due to shared Primary Storage.
- **HA features:** VM HA, Host HA, MS HA — all built-in.
- **Snapshot storage:** Diff-based, persisted on Secondary Storage.
- **Compare/contrast hooks** for the exam: **CloudStack vs. OpenStack** (Java/Python, agent-direct/AMQP, MySQL-any/SQLite-Postgres).

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & High-Yield Reference

## 2.1 The CloudStack Logical Hierarchy (Organizational Units)

CloudStack organizes physical and logical infrastructure into a **strict 6-level hierarchy**:

1. **Region** — A collection of one or more *Zones* (geographic grouping).
2. **Zone** — The largest container of physical resources; typically a single **data center**.
3. **Pod** — A rack or collection of racks sharing a common **Layer-2 broadcast domain**.
4. **Cluster** — A group of *homogeneous* Hosts that share the **same Primary Storage**.
5. **Host** — A single physical server running a supported hypervisor.
6. **Primary Storage** — Repository of live VM disk volumes attached to a cluster.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundary Rule:** VMs are scoped to **Clusters**, but cloud user accounts (tenants) span the entire **Zone**.

## 2.2 Core Architectural Components

| Component | Role |
|---|---|
| **Management Server (MS)** | Central brain. Runs the Tomcat servlet + Java services. Hosts the DB, API endpoints, UI. Stateless horizontally-scalable. |
| **MySQL Database** | Backs the Management Server. Stores state of zones, hosts, VMs, networks, users. |
| **Hosts (Computing Nodes)** | Run the hypervisor agent (e.g., libvirt for KVM). The Management Server talks to agents via **agent-direct routing**. |
| **Primary Storage** | Block (iSCSI, FC, Ceph RBD) or File (NFS, Local FS). Holds running VM disk volumes. |
| **Secondary Storage** | Always **NFS** (or object-store via Swift/S3). Holds templates, ISOs, snapshots, volume backups. |
| **Virtual Router (VR)** | Per-Network software appliance providing DHCP, DNS, NAT, VPN, LB, FW. Implements the "Advanced Zone" networking. |
| **Console Proxy VM (CPVM)** | Provides browser-based VNC/HTTP console to user VMs. |
| **Secondary Storage VM (SSVM)** | Handles template/ISO/snapshot download, upload, copy between zones. |

## 2.3 High-Yield Reference Sheet — CloudStack Cheat Card

| Feature | Detail | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| **Source language** | Java | Backend services |
| **License** | Apache 2.0 | Open source |
| **Communication** | HTTP/HTTPS REST + XML/JSON | Native API |
| **DB backend** | MySQL / MariaDB | Required |
| **Multi-hypervisor** | KVM, VMware vSphere, XenServer/XCP-ng, Hyper-V, LXC | Wide support |
| **Networking modes** | Basic, Advanced, Isolated, VPC, Shared | Tenant-controlled |
| **UI** | Web GUI (AngularJS) + API | Browser-based |
| **API parity** | Native CloudStack API + EC2/S3 bridge | Hybrid-cloud friendly |
| **HA feature** | VM HA, Host HA, Management Server HA | Built-in |

## 2.4 Networking Models — The Most Tested Sub-Topic

> [!NOTE]
> CloudStack uses **Network Offerings** to bundle *services* (DHCP, DNS, Firewall, LB, VPN, NAT, etc.) that are then associated with a guest network. Three foundational network types:

### (a) Basic Zone Networking
- One big shared network per zone.
- The Management Server hands out IP/MAC addresses.
- **No** Virtual Router per account.
- Suited for simple test/dev setups.

### (b) Advanced Zone Networking (Default)
- **Per-tenant isolated network** (VLAN, GRE, or VXLAN).
- A **Virtual Router (VR)** is spawned per account/network offering to provide NAT, DHCP, DNS, Firewall, LB, VPN, Userdata.
- The most commonly deployed model in production.

### (c) VPC (Virtual Private Cloud)
- Enterprise-grade: tenant owns a **router**, defines multiple **tiers** (subnets), attaches **ACLs**, **VPN gateways**, **site-to-site IPSec**.
- Supports **private gateways** to corporate datacenters.

## 2.5 Storage Offerings

| Type | Examples |
|---|---|
| **Local** | Local host filesystem |
| **Shared File** | NFS |
| **Shared Block** | iSCSI, Fiber Channel, Ceph RBD, GlusterFS |
| **Object** | Swift, S3 (used as Secondary Storage via S3-HTTP bridge) |

> [!TIP]
> Snapshots are *diff-based* in CloudStack and are stored on Secondary Storage. Volume backups (full copies) are also written to Secondary Storage.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

CloudStack (and similar IaaS orchestrators like **OpenStack**) is the **de-facto platform** in:
- **Telco NFV clouds** (Airtel, Reliance Jio, Vodafone).
- **Academic private clouds** (university teaching/research clusters).
- **Government sovereign clouds** (e.g., IndiaStack, MeghRaj).
- **HPC clouds** for scientific computing.

## 2.7 CloudStack vs. OpenStack — KTU Comparison Table

| Aspect | CloudStack | OpenStack |
|---|---|---|
| Origin | Cloud.com → Citrix → Apache | NASA + Rackspace |
| Language | Java | Python |
| DB | MySQL only | SQLite/MySQL/PostgreSQL |
| Message bus | None (agent-direct) | RabbitMQ (AMQP) |
| Hypervisors | KVM, Xen, VMware, Hyper-V, LXC | Primarily KVM (libvirt) |
| Installer | One binary, ~30 mins | Multi-node, modular |
| Curve | Easier for beginners | Steeper, more flexible |

$$

$$

### Step 2 — Resource Allocation Logic (The "Allocator")

$$
\text{SelectHost}(H) = \min_{h \in \text{AvailableHosts}} \Big( \text{Score}(h) \Big)
$$

where

$$
\text{Score}(h) = w_1 \cdot \text{CPU}_{\text{free}}(h) + w_2 \cdot \text{RAM}_{\text{free}}(h) + w_3 \cdot \text{IOPS}_{\text{free}}(h) - w_4 \cdot \text{Load}_{\text{avg}}(h)
$$

Weights $(w_1, w_2, w_3, w_4)$ are tuned by the administrator to bias placement.

### Step 3 — IP Address Assignment

The Management Server queries the **Pod-level IP pool** to allocate an unused guest IP:

$$
\text{GuestIP}_{\text{new}} = \text{IPAllocator}(\text{Pod}_{\text{ID}}, \text{NetworkOffering}_{\text{ID}})
$$

For an Advanced Zone, it also decides whether to start a **Virtual Router (VR)** if not already running for that account/network.

### Step 4 — Volume Provisioning on Primary Storage

A new VM volume is created on Primary Storage:

$$
\text{VolSize} = \max(\text{TemplateSize}, \text{DiskOfferingSize})
$$

The volume is formatted with the hypervisor's native format (qcow2 for KVM, VHD for Hyper-V, VMDK for VMware).

### Step 5 — Template Copy (Secondary → Primary)

If the template resides on Secondary Storage of a different zone, the **Secondary Storage VM (SSVM)** downloads it via HTTP, copies it across zones, and makes it available locally.

$$
\text{Copy}(T_{\text{remote}} \to T_{\text{local}}) \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Volume created from } T_{\text{local}}
$$

### Step 6 — Agent-Direct Push to Host

The Management Server sends an XML/JSON command directly to the **hypervisor agent** on the selected host via **agent-direct routing** (no SSH, no message queue — *important distinction from OpenStack's RabbitMQ*).

$$
\text{Agent}_{\text{host}} \leftarrow \text{CreateVMCMD}(\text{VMName}, \text{VolPath}, \text{NetCfg}, \text{ISO})
$$

### Step 7 — VM Boot & State Transition

The hypervisor starts the VM. The agent reports back status:

$$
\text{State}_{\text{VM}} : \text{Starting} \to \text{Running}
$$

If the user requests console access, the **Console Proxy VM (CPVM)** establishes a VNC tunnel from the user's browser to the VM's hypervisor port.

### Step 9 — Asynchronous Job Tracking

The API returns a `jobId`. The client polls:

$$
\text{queryAsyncJobResult}(\text{jobId}) \Rightarrow \{\text{status}, \text{vmId}, \text{ipAddress}\}
$$

    cluster --> hostA
    cluster --> hostB
    cluster --- pst
    hostA --> guestvmA
    hostB --> guestvmB
    templateRepo -.copies to.- pst
```

## 4.3 VM Deployment Lifecycle Sequence

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    autonumber
    participant U as Tenant
    participant API as API Dispatcher
    participant MS as Management Server
    participant DB as MySQL
    participant SSVM as SSVM
    participant H as Host Agent
    participant V as Virtual Router
    U->>API: deployVirtualMachine(templateId, zoneId, serviceOfferingId)
    API->>MS: validate session + parse parameters
    MS->>DB: check quotas, user rights
    DB-->>MS: OK
    MS->>MS: First-Fit Allocator selects Host H
    MS->>DB: reserve IP from Pod pool
    MS->>SSVM: copy template from Secondary Storage
    SSVM-->>MS: copy complete
    MS->>V: ensure Virtual Router exists for tenant net
    MS->>H: agent-direct CreateVMCMD(volume, net, iso)
    H-->>MS: VM Running
    MS->>DB: update vm_instance.state = Running
    MS-->>U: jobId = success, return VM details
```

## 4.4 Network Model Comparison Block

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph BASIC[Basic Zone]
        b1[Shared physical L2 net]
        b2[MS assigns IP and MAC directly]
        b3[No Virtual Router]
    end
    subgraph ADV[Advanced Zone]
        a1[Per-tenant isolated VLAN or GRE or VXLAN]
        a2[VR provides DHCP DNS NAT LB FW VPN]
        a3[Default production mode]
    end
    subgraph VPC[Virtual Private Cloud]
        v1[Tenant owned VPC router]
        v2[Multiple tiers with subnets]
        v3[ACLs IPSec private gateway]
    end
    BASIC -.less features.- ADV
    ADV -.enterprise upgrades.- VPC
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define CloudStack. List any two hypervisors supported by it. **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Set-B]**

**Model Answer:**
CloudStack is an **open-source Infrastructure-as-a-Service (IaaS) cloud orchestration platform** developed originally by Cloud.com, later donated to the **Apache Software Foundation**, currently licensed under **Apache 2.0**. It enables the deployment, management, and automation of large pools of virtual machines, networking, and storage.

Any two supported hypervisors:
1. **KVM** (Kernel-based Virtual Machine)
2. **VMware vSphere / ESXi**

*(Acceptable alternatives: XenServer / XCP-ng, Microsoft Hyper-V, LXC)*

*(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 each for two hypervisors)*

### Q2. Differentiate between Primary Storage and Secondary Storage in CloudStack. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Primary Storage | Secondary Storage |
|---|---|---|
| Purpose | Holds **running** VM disk volumes | Holds **templates, ISOs, snapshots** |
| Access pattern | High-IOPS, hot data | Cold data, occasional reads |
| Protocols | NFS, iSCSI, FC, Ceph RBD, Local FS | **NFS only** (native); S3/Swift via bridge |
| Scope | Per Cluster | Per Zone |
| Used during | VM runtime | Template copy / VM provisioning |

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **architecture of CloudStack**. List its major components and state the function of each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **hierarchical organization of resources** in CloudStack (Region → Zone → Pod → Cluster → Host). Why is the Cluster concept critical for shared Primary Storage? **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution — Question A

#### Part (a) — Architecture (7 marks)

The CloudStack architecture has **four major logical layers**:

**Layer 1 — Client Layer**
- **Web UI (AngularJS)** and **API clients** (curl, CloudMonkey CLI, Terraform provider, AWS-CLI bridge).

**Layer 2 — Management Plane**
- **Management Server** (Tomcat-hosted servlet container, stateless Java services).
- **MySQL Database** (state store: zones, hosts, VMs, networks, accounts).
- Communicates with hosts using **agent-direct HTTP/XML** routing — no message queue.

**Layer 3 — Hypervisor / Compute Layer**
- Hosts running **KVM / XenServer / VMware / Hyper-V**.
- Each host runs a small **agent** that receives commands and reports state.

**Layer 4 — Storage Layer**
- **Primary Storage** (per cluster) – VM volumes.
- **Secondary Storage** (per zone) – templates, ISOs, snapshots.

**Auxiliary Service VMs (spawned on demand):**
- **Console Proxy VM (CPVM)** – browser-based VM console.
- **Secondary Storage VM (SSVM)** – template download/copy.
- **Virtual Router (VR)** – DHCP/DNS/NAT/Load-Balancer per tenant.

**(7 marks: 3 for diagram, 2 for components, 2 for service VMs)**

#### Part (b) — Hierarchy (7 marks)

The hierarchy is **strictly nested** for resource scoping:

$$
\text{Region} \supset \text{Zone} \supset \text{Pod} \supset \text{Cluster} \supset \text{Host}
$$

- **Region** – geographic grouping of zones (used only for API parameter scoping).
- **Zone** – a data center, the largest unit a tenant sees.
- **Pod** – a Layer-2 broadcast boundary (one rack or one switch group).
- **Cluster** – a group of **homogeneous hosts** that share **the same Primary Storage**.
- **Host** – the actual physical server.

**Why Cluster is critical for Primary Storage:**
- A Cluster is the **smallest unit** at which Primary Storage is shared.
- Live migration (VMotion/XenMotion/KVM live-migration) is **only possible within a Cluster** because the shared storage means the VM's disk volume does not need to be copied to a new Primary Storage target.
- Resource limits and capacity planning are typically enforced per-Cluster.

**(7 marks: 3 for hierarchy definition, 4 for Cluster + shared-storage reasoning)**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a)** Explain **Basic Zone**, **Advanced Zone**, and **VPC** network models in CloudStack with suitable diagrams. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write and explain the **step-by-step sequence of events** that occur when a tenant deploys a Virtual Machine using the CloudStack API. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution — Question B

#### Part (a) — Network Models (7 marks)

**Basic Zone**
- Single shared network per zone.
- Management Server directly assigns IP/MAC to VMs.
- **No Virtual Router** — limited services (no per-tenant NAT, no VPN, no LB).
- Use case: dev/test, simple labs.

**Advanced Zone**
- **Isolated per-tenant network** (VLAN/GRE/VXLAN).
- **Virtual Router (VR)** per account provides: **DHCP, DNS, NAT, Firewall, Load Balancer (HAProxy), VPN (IPSec), Userdata**.
- Default production model. Supports multiple guest networks per tenant.

**VPC (Virtual Private Cloud)**
- Tenant owns a **VPC Router** and defines **multiple subnets/tiers**.
- Supports **Network ACLs**, **Site-to-Site IPSec VPN**, **Private Gateway** to corporate DC, **Static NAT**.
- Most enterprise-grade offering.

| Model | Virtual Router | Tenant Isolation | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| Basic | No | None (shared) | Lab |
| Advanced | Yes (per account) | VLAN/GRE/VXLAN | Production |
| VPC | Yes (per VPC) | Multiple subnets + ACL | Enterprise |

#### Part (b) — VM Deploy Lifecycle (7 marks)

(See detailed walkthrough in **Section 3** of these notes.)

Summary steps:
1. **API call received** by Dispatcher; session authenticated via SHA-1 signature.
2. **Allocator selects host** (First-Fit scoring on CPU, RAM, IOPS).
3. **IP reserved** from Pod-level pool.
4. **Volume created** on Cluster Primary Storage.
5. **Template copied** from Secondary Storage (via SSVM if cross-zone).
6. **Virtual Router** spawned/verified for tenant network.
7. **Agent-direct command** sent to host hypervisor.
8. **VM boots**; CPVM ready for console access; state persisted in MySQL.

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common pitfalls — where students lose marks:**
> 1. **Confusing CloudStack with OpenStack.** OpenStack uses RabbitMQ + AMQP + Python; CloudStack uses **agent-direct HTTP** + **Java/MySQL**. Examiners specifically test this distinction.
> 2. **Forgetting SSVM and CPVM** in architecture diagrams — these are *service VMs*, not optional. Always label them.
> 3. **Misstating the license.** It is **Apache 2.0** (not GPL, not BSD).
> 4. **Cluster scope confusion.** Shared Primary Storage is **Cluster-level**, not Zone-level.
> 5. **Ignoring the EC2/S3 API bridge** — a common *differentiator* question.
> 6. **Writing about "OpenStack Nova" in CloudStack answers** — instant 0 for that sub-part.

---

## Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

- **CloudStack** = open-source Java-based IaaS orchestrator, **Apache 2.0**.
- Acquired by **Citrix (2011)** → donated to **Apache (2012)** → **TLP 2013**.
- **Hierarchical scoping:** Region ⊃ Zone ⊃ Pod ⊃ Cluster ⊃ Host.
- **Three storage scopes:** Local (per host), Primary (per cluster), Secondary (per zone).
- **Three service VMs on demand:** **CPVM** (console), **SSVM** (template/snapshot), **VR** (network).
- **Networking models:** Basic, Advanced (default), VPC, Shared.
- **Communication:** **Agent-direct HTTP/XML** (no RabbitMQ — distinguishing feature).
- **Database:** MySQL/MariaDB is mandatory.
- **Hypervisor support:** KVM, XenServer, VMware, Hyper-V, LXC.
- **API:** Native REST + Amazon EC2/S3 compatibility bridge.
- **Use cases:** Telco NFV, academic clouds, sovereign clouds.
- **First-Fit Allocator** selects host based on weighted scoring of CPU, RAM, IOPS.
- **Live migration** restricted to a single Cluster due to shared Primary Storage.
- **HA features:** VM HA, Host HA, MS HA — all built-in.
- **Snapshot storage:** Diff-based, persisted on Secondary Storage.
- **Compare/contrast hooks** for the exam: **CloudStack vs. OpenStack** (Java/Python, agent-direct/AMQP, MySQL-any/SQLite-Postgres).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Basics of Eucalyptus
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT525 - CLOUD COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Basics of Eucalyptus

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Eucalyptus (Elastic Utility Computing Architecture for Linking Your Programs To Useful Systems)** is an open-source, Linux-based software framework designed to deliver Infrastructure-as-a-Service (IaaS) private and hybrid cloud computing environments. It is fundamentally a collection of modular, web-service-based components that collectively provide a cloud computing platform.

Developed originally as a research project at the University of California, Santa Barbara (UCSB) in 2008, Eucalyptus is engineered to be highly compatible with the **Amazon Web Services (AWS)** public cloud API. This means applications written to interact with AWS (specifically EC2, S3, EBS, IAM, and ELB) can be transparently ported to a Eucalyptus-based private cloud without code modification.

| Attribute | Standard Value / Metric |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Architecture Type** | Modular, Service-Oriented (SOA) |
| **Cloud Deployment Model** | Private Cloud & Hybrid Cloud |
| **API Compatibility** | AWS (EC2, S3, EBS, IAM, ELB) |
| **Operating System Support** | Linux (CentOS, Ubuntu, RHEL, openSUSE) |
| **Hypervisor Support** | KVM, VMware (ESXi), Xen, Hyper-V |
| **Default Web Interface Port** | **8443** (HTTPS) |
| **Current Stable Major Line** | 4.x series |

### 1.2 Intuition (The "Hotel Analogy")

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Hotel Chain vs. Your Own House**
> - **AWS (Public Cloud)** is like staying in a massive international hotel chain (Marriott, Hilton). You book rooms (VMs), use storage (luggage room), but you don't manage the building, electricity, or plumbing. You just pay for what you use.
> - **Eucalyptus (Private Cloud)** is like building a **miniature replica of that exact same hotel chain inside your own private property** (your college campus, your company's data center). The key genius is that the front desk, the booking system, the room keys, and the luggage tags are **identical** to the Marriott standard. So if you ever move out to the international chain, you don't need a new language — the staff and the systems work the exact same way.
> - This is what "AWS API compatibility" means in practice.

### 1.3 Why Eucalyptus Matters in KTU Curriculum

- It is the **canonical example** of an open-source IaaS platform in the KTU PECPT525 syllabus.
- It demonstrates **service-oriented component design** (a core cloud engineering principle).
- It is widely used in academic institutions and research labs to teach cloud deployment without the recurring cost of AWS.
- **Hybrid Cloud bridging**: Enterprises can "burst" workloads from a Eucalyptus private cloud to AWS during peak demand.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The "Why" and "How" Behind Eucalyptus

The core design philosophy of Eucalyptus rests on three engineering pillars:

**Pillar 1: Modularity**
Eucalyptus is intentionally broken into five independent, loosely-coupled web services. Each service can run on a different physical server, can be restarted independently, and communicates only via standardized WSDL (Web Services Description Language) interfaces. This means a failure in one component (e.g., storage) does not crash the others (e.g., compute scheduling).

**Pillar 2: API Fidelity to AWS**
The designers reverse-engineered the public AWS SOAP and Query APIs. When a client runs the `euca2ools` (or `aws-cli`) command `euca-run-instances`, Eucalyptus receives the exact same XML/JSON payload format that EC2 expects. This is achieved through an **API translation layer** at the front-end controller.

**Pillar 3: Hypervisor Agnosticism**
Eucalyptus does not bundle its own hypervisor. Instead, it speaks to any compatible hypervisor on the host (Node Controller) using a thin **VIM (Virtual Infrastructure Manager) adapter layer**. This is why the same Eucalyptus install can manage KVM, VMware, or Xen with minimal reconfiguration.

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Concept Sheet

| Component | Full Name | Role | Default Port |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **CLC** | Cloud Controller | Front-end / Web-services entry point; authenticates users via IAM; routes requests to internal components. | **8773** |
| **Walrus** | (Storage abstraction) | Provides S3-compatible object storage (buckets, objects). | **8773** (via CLC) |
| **CC** | Cluster Controller | Manages a group of Node Controllers; schedules VM instances to specific NCs; provides elastic network addresses. | **8774** |
| **SC** | Storage Controller | Provides EBS-compatible block storage (volumes, snapshots) backed by iSCSI or AoE. | **8775** |
| **NC** | Node Controller | Runs on the actual hypervisor host; controls VM lifecycle (run, stop, describe) on that physical box. | **8775** |
| **VLAN** | Virtual LAN Manager | Optional; creates isolated virtual networks per security group across cluster nodes. | - |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memory Aid (for KTU exams): "C-W-C-S-N"** — *Cloud, Walrus, Cluster, Storage, Node* — read top-down from user-facing to hardware-facing.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Academic Cloud Labs**: Universities deploy Eucalyptus on commodity hardware to give students hands-on EC2/S3 experience without AWS bills.
- **Government & Defense**: Organizations with strict data-sovereignty rules (e.g., Indian Defence, DRDO labs) deploy Eucalyptus to keep all data on-premise while still using AWS-compatible toolchains.
- **Hybrid Bursting**: A private Eucalyptus cloud can offload peak traffic to public AWS — the orchestration code is identical.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 The End-to-End Instance Launch Flow (Step-by-Step Trace)

This is the **most frequently asked derivation-style question** in KTU Module 3. The following is the exhaustive, step-by-step logical path a single `euca-run-instances` request travels through the Eucalyptus architecture.

**Step 1 — Client Authentication**
The end-user issues a CLI command (e.g., `euca-run-instances emi-ABC12345`) from a workstation. The command is signed using the user's **Access Key ID** and **Secret Access Key** (issued by the IAM subsystem embedded in the CLC). The signed request is sent over **HTTPS (port 8443)** to the CLC.

**Step 2 — Cloud Controller (CLC) Receives Request**
The CLC's web-services front end validates the signature against its internal IAM database. Upon successful authentication, the CLC decomposes the request to identify:
- The **Image ID (EMI)** — the Virtual Machine template to launch.
- The **Instance Type** — CPU/RAM footprint.
- The **Availability Zone / Cluster** — the target CC.

**Step 3 — Walrus Resolution (Image Retrieval)**
The CLC asks the **Walrus** component: *"Where is the bits of `emi-ABC12345` stored?"* Walrus returns a **Walrus URL** (e.g., `http://walrus.euca.internal/services/Walrus/bucket/image-store/emi-ABC12345.manifest.xml`). This URL points to the actual raw disk image (KVM's `.qcow2` or VMware's `.vmdk`).

**Step 4 — Cluster Controller (CC) Scheduling**
The CLC forwards the launch request to the appropriate **Cluster Controller**, identified by the user's availability-zone setting. The CC examines the resource utilization of all its registered **Node Controllers** using a simple scheduling heuristic (often "first-available with sufficient resources"). It selects the optimal NC.

**Step 5 — Network Setup (VLAN Manager)**
Before the VM is launched, the CC (in cooperation with the optional **VLAN Manager**) assigns a **private IP address** from the cluster's network pool. It may also create a **dynamic VLAN** across the cluster so that VMs on different NCs but in the same security group can communicate on a virtual Layer-2 network.

**Step 6 — Storage Controller (SC) Volume Attachment**
If the user requested an **EBS-style persistent volume**, the CLC forwards the request to the **Storage Controller**. The SC creates a logical volume (e.g., an LVM volume or a file-backed image) and exports it to the target NC using **iSCSI** (Linux Open-iSCSI initiator) or **ATA-over-Ethernet (AoE)**.

**Step 7 — Node Controller (NC) VM Launch**
The chosen **Node Controller** receives the launch command. It performs the following sub-steps:
1. Downloads the image from the **Walrus URL** obtained in Step 3 (cached locally for future reuse).
2. Allocates local resources (vCPU, RAM) on the underlying hypervisor.
3. Issues the hypervisor-specific command:
   - For **KVM**: `virsh create /var/lib/eucalyptus/instances/i-.../image.xml`
   - For **VMware**: calls the VMware vSphere API to register and power-on the VM.
4. Attaches the **block volume** (from Step 6) to the VM.
5. Plugs the **virtual NIC** into the cluster's VLAN bridge.

**Step 8 — Status Reporting Back Up the Chain**
The NC reports the new VM's state (`pending` → `running`) and its assigned **IP address** back to the CC, which forwards it to the CLC, which finally returns a SOAP/XML response to the user's CLI — completing the request/response cycle.

### 3.2 Logical Decision Matrix for Component Placement

> [!NOTE]
> The following matrix maps a user's question to the correct Eucalyptus component — a **very high-yield KTU question pattern** (often 7–8 marks).

| User Action (API Call) | Eucalyptus Component Responsible |
| :--- | :--- |
| `euca-describe-images` | **Walrus** (lists objects in `image-store` bucket) |
| `euca-create-volume` | **Storage Controller (SC)** |
| `euca-attach-volume` | **Storage Controller (SC)** + **Node Controller (NC)** |
| `euca-allocate-address` | **Cluster Controller (CC)** (owns the IP pool) |
| `euca-run-instances` | **CLC** (entry) → **CC** (schedule) → **NC** (execute) |
| `euca-bundle-image` | Client-side; upload to **Walrus** |
| `euca-modify-instance-attribute` | **CLC** forwards to **NC** |
| User authentication check | **CLC's embedded IAM subsystem** |

### 3.3 Performance & Capacity Heuristics

While Eucalyptus is I/O-bound rather than compute-bound, a useful engineering heuristic for sizing the CLC (often the bottleneck) is:

$$T_{\text{launch}} = T_{\text{CLC\_auth}} + T_{\text{Walrus\_resolve}} + T_{\text{CC\_schedule}} + T_{\text{NC\_boot}}$$

Where:
- $T_{\text{CLC\_auth}}$ ≈ **10–50 ms** (signature verification).
- $T_{\text{Walrus\_resolve}}$ ≈ **20–100 ms** (HTTP HEAD on Walrus).
- $T_{\text{CC\_schedule}}$ ≈ **5–20 ms** (NC selection).
- $T_{\text{NC\_boot}}$ ≈ **30–90 s** (dominated by VM boot time, not Eucalyptus itself).

So the **overhead introduced by the Eucalyptus control plane is typically 0.1–0.2 seconds per launch** — negligible compared to the 30–90 second VM boot.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 High-Level Eucalyptus Architecture (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
graph TD
    userNodeA[End User CLI euca2ools aws cli] -- HTTPS Port 8443 --> clcNodeB[Cloud Controller CLC IAM Authentication Request Routing]
    clcNodeB -- Internal SOAP --> walrusNodeC[Walrus Object Storage S3 Compatible Buckets and Images]
    clcNodeB -- Internal SOAP --> ccNodeD[Cluster Controller CC Scheduler and Network Manager]
    ccNodeD -- Hypervisor API --> ncNodeE[Node Controller NC KVM VMware Xen]
    ccNodeD -- iSCSI AoE --> scNodeF[Storage Controller SC EBS Compatible Volumes]
    scNodeF -- Block Device --> ncNodeE
    walrusNodeC -- HTTP Image Download --> ncNodeE
    ccNodeD -. VLAN Tagging .- ncNodeE
    userNodeA -. Read Write Buckets .-> walrusNodeC
```

### 4.2 Component Deployment Topology

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph Front End Tier
        CLC1[CLC Server]
    end
    subgraph Storage Tier
        WAL1[Walrus]
        SC1[Storage Controller]
    end
    subgraph Cluster Tier
        CC1[Cluster Controller]
    end
    subgraph Compute Tier
        NC1[Node Controller 1]
        NC2[Node Controller 2]
        NC3[Node Controller N]
    end
    CLC1 --- WAL1
    CLC1 --- SC1
    CLC1 --- CC1
    CC1 --- NC1
    CC1 --- NC2
    CC1 --- NC3
    SC1 -. iSCSI .- NC1
    SC1 -. iSCSI .- NC2
    SC1 -. iSCSI .- NC3
    WAL1 -. HTTP .-> NC1
    WAL1 -. HTTP .-> NC2
    WAL1 -. HTTP .-> NC3
```

### 4.3 Request Lifecycle Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    startNode((Start: User Issues euca run instances)) --> step1[Step 1: Signed HTTPS Request to CLC]
    step1 --> step2{Step 2: CLC IAM Auth Valid?}
    step2 -- No --> failNode((Auth Failure Response to User))
    step2 -- Yes --> step3[Step 3: CLC Asks Walrus for Image Location]
    step3 --> step4[Step 4: Walrus Returns Image URL]
    step4 --> step5[Step 5: CLC Forwards to Cluster Controller]
    step5 --> step6[Step 6: CC Selects Best Node Controller]
    step6 --> step7[Step 7: CC Requests Block Volume from SC]
    step7 --> step8[Step 8: SC Exports iSCSI Volume]
    step8 --> step9[Step 9: NC Downloads Image from Walrus]
    step9 --> step10[Step 10: NC Boots VM via Hypervisor]
    step10 --> step11[Step 11: SC Attaches Volume to VM]
    step11 --> step12[Step 12: Status Reported Up the Chain to User]
    step12 --> endNode((End: VM Running))
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`
**Define Eucalyptus. List any two of its key features.**

**Model Answer:**
Eucalyptus is an open-source software framework that implements the IaaS (Infrastructure-as-a-Service) model to deliver **private and hybrid cloud** environments with **API compatibility to Amazon Web Services (AWS)**. It is built as a collection of modular, web-service-based components.

**Key Features (any two):**
1. **AWS API compatibility** — allows tools and applications built for EC2/S3 to run unmodified.
2. **Modular architecture** — components (CLC, Walrus, CC, SC, NC) are decoupled web services, enabling independent scaling and failure isolation.
3. **Hypervisor agnostic** — supports KVM, VMware, Xen via adapter layer.
4. **Hybrid cloud support** — workloads can burst from Eucalyptus private cloud to AWS public cloud.

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`
**What is the role of the Walrus component in Eucalyptus?**

**Model Answer:**
**Walrus** is the **S3-compatible object storage service** in Eucalyptus. It provides a simple storage interface for storing and retrieving data as objects within named containers called **buckets**. Walrus stores:
1. **VM image templates** (EMIs — Eucalyptus Machine Images) in a special `image-store` bucket.
2. **User-uploaded files** via an S3-compatible REST API.

Walrus is loosely coupled from the compute tier, ensuring storage persists independently of running VMs.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks)

> **Module 3 Internal Choice Question**

**Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the Eucalyptus architecture in detail with a neat block diagram. Describe the responsibilities of **CLC, Walrus, and Cluster Controller**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Trace the end-to-end flow of an `euca-run-instances` command through the Eucalyptus components, explaining the role of each component involved. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q.A(a):**

Eucalyptus follows a **hierarchical, service-oriented architecture** consisting of five modular web-service components. The architecture is designed so that a request from a user flows downward from a centralized front-end (CLC) to the physical hypervisor hosts (NCs).

**1. Cloud Controller (CLC) — Front End**
- Acts as the **single point of entry** for all client requests (web services and UI).
- Hosts the embedded **IAM (Identity and Access Management)** subsystem that authenticates users.
- Routes incoming requests to the appropriate lower-level component (CC, Walrus, SC).
- Provides a web-based management dashboard at port **8443**.

**2. Walrus — Object Storage**
- Implements the **S3-compatible** object storage API.
- Stores **VM images (EMIs)** and user data buckets.
- Decouples storage from compute — Walrus can run on a separate, dedicated machine.

**3. Cluster Controller (CC) — Middleware**
- Manages a **logical grouping of Node Controllers** (a "cluster").
- Responsible for **VM instance scheduling** (deciding which NC will host a new VM).
- Manages the **network namespace** for the cluster, including elastic IP allocation and VLAN isolation.
- Brokers the communication between the CLC and the NCs.

*(Block diagram as shown in Section 4.1 of these notes.)*

---

**Model Solution for Q.A(b):**

The end-to-end flow of `euca-run-instances emi-ABC12345` proceeds as follows:

1. **Client Sign & Submit**: The user's CLI (euca2ools) signs the request using AWS-style Access Key and Secret Key, and submits it over HTTPS to the CLC.
2. **CLC Authenticate**: The CLC validates the signature against its IAM database. If invalid, an error is returned immediately.
3. **Image Resolution via Walrus**: The CLC queries Walrus for the location (URL) of the image bits corresponding to `emi-ABC12345`. Walrus responds with a manifest URL.
4. **Forward to Cluster Controller**: The CLC identifies which Cluster Controller owns the requested availability zone and forwards the launch request to it.
5. **Cluster Scheduling**: The CC polls all registered Node Controllers for their resource availability and selects the optimal NC based on CPU/RAM requirements.
6. **Storage Volume Provisioning**: If the user requested a persistent block volume, the CC coordinates with the Storage Controller (SC) to export an iSCSI or AoE target to the chosen NC.
7. **VM Launch on NC**: The chosen Node Controller downloads the image from Walrus, registers it with the local hypervisor (KVM/VMware/Xen), and boots the VM.
8. **Volume Attachment**: The SC volume is attached to the running VM as a virtual block device.
9. **Status Reply**: The VM's state (`running`) and assigned IP are reported upward (NC → CC → CLC → Client).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> A very common mistake is to **omit the Walrus image-resolution step** and claim the NC magically knows the image. The examiner will deduct **2 marks** if you skip Step 3. Also, do **not confuse SC (Storage Controller, EBS-style) with Walrus (Object Store, S3-style)** — they are **two different storage subsystems** serving different purposes.

---

**Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Compare and contrast the **Walrus** and **Storage Controller (SC)** components of Eucalyptus in terms of purpose, API, and storage type. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain how Eucalyptus achieves **AWS API compatibility** and why this is strategically important for hybrid cloud deployments. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q.B(a):**

| Feature | **Walrus** | **Storage Controller (SC)** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **AWS Analog** | Amazon S3 (Simple Storage Service) | Amazon EBS (Elastic Block Store) |
| **Storage Type** | Object storage (flat, key-value) | Block storage (raw volumes) |
| **Data Structure** | Buckets containing objects | Volumes (mountable disks) |
| **Access Pattern** | HTTP REST (PUT/GET/DELETE object) | iSCSI / AoE block device attach |
| **Typical Use** | Store VM images, backups, user files | Boot disks, persistent database volumes |
| **Persistence** | Independent of VM lifecycle | Independent of VM lifecycle |
| **API Style** | S3 REST + SOAP | EBS Query + SOAP |

**Conclusion**: Walrus and SC are **complementary**, not competing. A typical Eucalyptus deployment uses Walrus to store the EMI template and SC to provide the persistent attached volume for the running VM.

---

**Model Solution for Q.B(b):**

**Mechanism of AWS API Compatibility:**
Eucalyptus was designed from inception to mimic the **wire-level protocol** of AWS. Specifically:
1. The CLC implements the same **WSDL/SOAP** and **Query/HTTP** interfaces that EC2 exposes.
2. Client tools (euca2ools, AWS CLI) send identical XML/JSON payloads to either AWS or Eucalyptus.
3. The user simply **changes the endpoint URL** (e.g., from `ec2.amazonaws.com` to `clc.euca.internal:8443`) and the rest of the workflow is identical.

**Strategic Importance for Hybrid Cloud:**
1. **Portability**: An application developed and tested on a local Eucalyptus private cloud can be deployed to public AWS without any code change.
2. **Vendor Lock-in Mitigation**: Organizations are not trapped into a single vendor — they can move workloads freely.
3. **Burst Computing**: When local Eucalyptus resources saturate, additional instances can be spun up in public AWS, governed by the same orchestration scripts.
4. **Cost Optimization**: Sensitive workloads stay on-premise (Eucalyptus); non-sensitive scalable workloads run on AWS.
5. **Disaster Recovery**: VM images (EMIs/AMIs) can be exported from Eucalyptus to AWS S3 for off-site DR.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Must-Remember KTU Bullets for Eucalyptus (Module 3):**

- [x] **Full form**: Elastic Utility Computing Architecture for Linking Your Programs To Useful Systems.
- [x] **Origin**: Research project at **UC Santa Barbara (UCSB)**, 2008.
- [x] **Primary Use Case**: Build **private and hybrid** IaaS clouds.
- [x] **Five Core Components (mnemonic "C-W-C-S-N")**: **C**LC, **W**alrus, **C**luster Controller, **S**torage Controller, **N**ode Controller.
- [x] **Cloud Controller (CLC)** = front-end + IAM authentication + request routing (port **8443** for UI, **8773** for API).
- [x] **Walrus** = S3-compatible object storage (buckets + objects); stores EMIs.
- [x] **Cluster Controller (CC)** = scheduler + network manager for a group of NCs.
- [x] **Storage Controller (SC)** = EBS-compatible block storage using iSCSI or AoE.
- [x] **Node Controller (NC)** = hypervisor host that actually runs VMs.
- [x] **EMI** = Eucalyptus Machine Image (analog of Amazon AMI).
- [x] **AWS API compatibility** is the #1 strategic feature — allows seamless hybrid cloud bursting.
- [x] Supports multiple hypervisors: **KVM, VMware ESXi, Xen, Hyper-V** (no proprietary hypervisor).
- [x] Do **not** confuse Walrus (S3-style object) with Storage Controller (EBS-style block).
- [x] CLC is the single point of authentication; CC handles scheduling; NC executes.
- [x] Default web UI: **https://\<CLC-IP\>:8443/**.
- [x] Open-source under BSD-style license (historical); foundational to OpenStack lineage (some Eucalyptus engineers later contributed to OpenStack).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Open Nebula
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT525 - CLOUD COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Open Nebula

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 MODULE 3 — OPEN NEBULA: CORE FOUNDATIONS

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **OpenNebula** is an **open-source cloud computing platform** designed to manage and orchestrate virtualized data center resources. It provides a simple, flexible, and feature-rich toolkit for building **private, public, and hybrid cloud infrastructures** (IaaS — Infrastructure as a Service).

According to the KTU 2024 syllabus, OpenNebula is positioned as a lightweight alternative to heavyweight platforms like OpenStack, focusing on simplicity, interoperability, and multi-tenancy.

| Attribute | Specification |
|---|---|
| **Type** | Open-Source Cloud Management Platform (CMP) |
| **Initial Release** | 2008 (by Ignacio M. Llorente and Rubén S. Montero, Universidad Complutense de Madrid) |
| **Latest Stable Series** | 6.x (LTS) |
| **License** | Apache 2.0 |
| **Standard Port** | 9869 (Sunstone GUI) |
| **Default Hypervisor** | KVM (also supports VMware, Xen, LXC) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Board Term:** OpenNebula is officially classified as a **Cloud Management Platform (CMP)** — NOT a hypervisor. It *orchestrates* hypervisors and other resources; it does not perform hardware-level virtualization itself.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Hotel Chain Manager"

Imagine OpenNebula as the **central reservation and operations manager of a large hotel chain**:

- 🏨 **Physical Servers** = The hotel buildings (rooms exist physically but cannot be used until managed).
- 🛏️ **Virtual Machines (VMs)** = The rentable rooms prepared for guests.
- 👤 **Users/Cloud Consumers** = The guests.
- 📋 **OpenNebula Core (`oned` daemon)** = The hotel manager who decides which guest goes into which room, when rooms are cleaned, and which rooms are available.
- 🖥️ **Sunstone GUI** = The guest-facing online booking portal.
- 📦 **Datastores** = The hotel's storage rooms where furniture, supplies, and records are kept.

Just as the hotel manager keeps track of all rooms, guests, and bookings centrally, OpenNebula tracks all VMs, hosts, users, and resource allocations from a single management plane.

---

## 1.3 Why OpenNebula? — Core Value Proposition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> OpenNebula was built to address the **"complexity barrier"** of cloud platforms. While OpenStack requires 10+ coupled services, OpenNebula uses a **single, monolithic core daemon** that makes deployment and operations significantly simpler.

**Key Differentiators:**

1. **Lightweight Architecture** — Single binary, minimal dependencies.
2. **Multi-Hypervisor Support** — KVM, VMware vCenter, Xen, LXC, Firecracker.
3. **Multi-Tenancy Built-in** — Native support for groups, users, ACLs, and quotas.
4. **Hybrid Cloud Bridging** — Native connectors for AWS, Azure, Google Cloud.
5. **Federation Capability** — Connect multiple OpenNebula zones as one logical cloud.
6. **Edge & Bare-Metal Cloud Support** — Hot-pluggable drivers (Edge Clusters).

---

## 1.4 Comparison: OpenNebula vs. Other CMPs

| Feature | OpenNebula | OpenStack | Apache CloudStack |
|---|---|---|---|
| Architecture | Monolithic | Distributed Microservices | Monolithic |
| Ease of Deployment | **High (Easy)** | Low (Complex) | Medium |
| Number of Services | **1 Core Daemon** | 10+ Coupled Services | 1 Management Server |
| Best Suited For | SMBs, Edge, HPC | Hyperscale, Telcos | Public Cloud Providers |
| GUI Name | Sunstone | Horizon | CloudStack UI |
| Hybrid Cloud | **Native Connectors** | Third-party | Limited |
| Federation | First-class (Zones) | Limited (Cells) | Limited |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 🔬 DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS — OPEN NEBULA ARCHITECTURE

## 2.1 High-Level Architecture Overview

OpenNebula follows a **two-tier architecture**:
- **Front-End (Management Plane):** Runs all OpenNebula services (Sunstone, scheduler, core daemon).
- **Hosts (Virtualization Plane):** Run the actual VMs using the configured hypervisor.

The two tiers communicate through a secure **SSH-based driver channel** (encrypted XML-RPC over SSH by default).

---

## 2.2 Core Daemon: `oned`

The `oned` (OpenNebula Daemon) is the **brain** of the platform. It manages:

- VM lifecycle (create, suspend, resume, stop, terminate).
- Host and cluster management.
- User/Group/Virtual Data Center (VDC) management.
- Image and Template repositories.
- Scheduling decisions (delegated to the scheduler).

> [!NOTE]
> **The `oned` daemon is stateful** — it maintains the entire data center state in a **MySQL/SQLite backend database**. This is why the front-end can crash and be restarted without losing state.

---

## 2.3 OpenNebula Component Stack

| Component | Function | Communication |
|---|---|---|
| **Sunstone** | Web-based GUI (Ruby on Rails) | HTTP/HTTPS to `oned` |
| **CLI (`onevm`, `onehost`, etc.)** | Command-line management tools | XML-RPC / OneFlow API |
| **Scheduler** | Decides host placement for VMs | Talks to `oned` internally |
| **Core Daemon (`oned`)** | Central orchestrator | XML-RPC, MySQL, SSH to hosts |
| **Information Drivers** | Monitor host resources (IM drivers) | SSH to hosts |
| **Virtualization Drivers** | Deploy VMs (VM drivers — KVM, vCenter) | SSH to hosts |
| **Storage Drivers** | Manage datastores (TM drivers) | SSH / NFS / Ceph |
| **Networking Drivers** | Configure virtual networks (VN_MAD) | 802.1Q, Open vSwitch, eBPF |
| **Marketplace** | Public/Private App Store | HTTP API |
| **OneFlow** | Multi-VM service orchestration | REST API |

---

## 2.4 Datastore Model — The Three-Tier Storage Architecture

OpenNebula separates storage into three logical types:

| Datastore Type | Purpose | Examples |
|---|---|---|
| **System Datastore** | Holds running VM disks (deployment context) | Shared (NFS, Ceph) or SSH-transfer (Local) |
| **Image Datastore** | Stores VM Template Images (OS images) | NFS, Ceph, LVM, S3-compatible, vCenter |
| **Files Datastore** | Generic file storage for users (non-bootable data) | NFS, Ceph, S3 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Focus:** A common question asks *"How does a VM image travel from the Image Datastore to a host for execution?"* — the answer involves the **Transfer Manager (TM) driver**, which can be `shared` (NFS/Ceph mount) or `ssh` (image copied securely via SCP).

---

## 2.5 Virtual Networks in OpenNebula

OpenNebula offers **two distinct networking models**:

1. **Bridged Networking (Ephemeral)** — `dummy` driver; automatically creates Linux bridges per VM. Suitable for simple setups.
2. **VLAN-based Virtual Networks (802.1Q)** — `802.1Q` driver; uses tagged VLANs for network isolation. Production-grade.

Optional advanced drivers:
- **Open vSwitch (`ovswitch`)** — SDN-style networking.
- **eBPF (`ebtables`)** — Modern kernel-bypass datapath.

---

## 2.6 Scheduling Policies

When a VM is submitted, the **Scheduler** evaluates hosts using a **Rank expression**. Common policies:

| Policy | Use Case |
|---|---|
| `Packing` | Maximize consolidation (energy efficiency) |
| `Striping` | Distribute load evenly |
| `Fixed` | Pin VMs to specific hosts |
| `Load-aware` | Custom expression (e.g., rank by free CPU) |

The default rank expression is:

$$
\text{Rank} = -\text{USEDCPU} \quad \text{(i.e., prefer least-loaded host)}
$$

---

## 2.7 Federation & Hybrid Cloud

> [!NOTE]
> **Federation** allows multiple independent OpenNebula instances (called *zones*) to be joined into a single logical cloud, sharing a central **Master zone** with replicated data. This is used to scale geographically and to federate edge clusters.

Hybrid cloud is achieved via the **AWS EC2 driver**, **Azure driver**, and **Google Cloud driver**, allowing burst-out to public clouds.

---

## 2.8 KTU Formula & Reference Cheat Sheet

> [!TIP]
> The following table summarizes every concept that has appeared (or could appear) in KTU Module 3 exam answers for OpenNebula.

| Concept | Definition / Standard | Exam-Significance |
|---|---|---|
| `oned` | Core orchestration daemon (C++ / Ruby) | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| Sunstone | Default web GUI on **port 9869** | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| KVM | Default supported Linux hypervisor | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| XML-RPC | API protocol used between Sunstone / CLI and `oned` | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| SSH | Driver communication channel between front-end and hosts | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| MySQL / SQLite | Default stateful backend | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| System DS | Stores running VM disks | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| Image DS | Stores OS templates | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| Files DS | User file storage | ⭐⭐ |
| Master Zone | Central zone in federation | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| Slave Zone | Replicated zone in federation | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| OneFlow | Service composer (multi-VM apps) | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| 802.1Q | VLAN driver for tenant isolation | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| Marketplace | Repository of ready-to-use VM images | ⭐⭐ |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🧮 STEP-BY-STEP ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 OpenNebula End-to-End VM Provisioning — The Complete Workflow

The provisioning of a Virtual Machine in OpenNebula is a **multi-stage orchestration pipeline**. The KTU board frequently asks for this flow as a 7-mark descriptive answer. Below is the **exhaustive, step-by-step lifecycle**.

---

### **Stage 1: Template Definition**

The user (or administrator) defines a **VM Template** — a declarative document specifying CPU, memory, disk image, network, and contextualization scripts.

Example conceptual template (abstract representation, not actual code):

$$
\text{Template} = \{ \text{NAME}, \text{CPU}, \text{MEMORY}, \text{DISK}_{image}, \text{NIC}_{vnet}, \text{CONTEXT} \}
$$

---

### **Stage 2: Submission to `oned`**

The template is submitted via one of the three interfaces:
- **Sunstone GUI** → HTTP POST → `oned` (Sunstone converts to XML-RPC)
- **CLI** (`onevm create <template>`) → direct XML-RPC
- **REST API** → JSON over HTTP

The `oned` daemon:
1. Validates the template against schema.
2. Assigns a unique **VMID**.
3. Persists the VM record (state = `PENDING`) in the **backend database (MySQL/SQLite)**.
4. Pushes the VM to the scheduler queue.

---

### **Stage 3: Scheduling Decision**

The OpenNebula Scheduler:
1. Retrieves all candidate hosts in the target cluster.
2. Filters hosts that **satisfy capacity requirements** (CPU, memory, datastore reachability, network reachability).
3. Ranks remaining hosts using the configured Rank expression.
4. Selects the **highest-ranked host** and dispatches the VM.

$$
\text{SelectedHost} = \arg\max_{h \in \text{Candidates}} \left( \text{Rank}(h) \right)
$$

With the default policy:

$$
\text{Rank}(h) = -\text{USEDCPU}_{h}
$$

The VM transitions: `PENDING → HOLD` → `PROLOG` (once dispatched).

---

### **Stage 4: Image Staging (TM Driver Operation)**

The **Transfer Manager driver** moves the VM disk from the **Image Datastore** to the **System Datastore** of the selected host:

- If `shared` driver is used → datastores are mounted (NFS/Ceph), so the image is **not copied**, just referenced.
- If `ssh` driver is used → the image is **streamed via SCP** to the host.

$$
\text{DiskState} : \text{Datastore}_{image} \xrightarrow{\text{TM driver}} \text{Datastore}_{system}^{host}
$$

The VM transitions to state: `PROLOG`.

---

### **Stage 5: VM Instantiation (VM Driver Operation)**

The **Virtualization Driver** (`kvm`, `vcenter`, `xen`, etc.) executes the actual hypervisor commands on the target host:

For KVM:
- Creates a `qemu-kvm` process with libvirt XML.
- Attaches virtual disks and NICs.
- Boot order is enforced from the contextualized image.

The VM transitions: `PROLOG → BOOT → RUNNING`.

---

### **Stage 6: Live Monitoring (IM Driver Operation)**

The **Information Manager driver** continuously monitors:
- Host CPU/RAM utilization.
- VM state changes.
- Network connectivity.
- Storage health.

Monitoring data is pushed back to `oned` via XML-RPC over SSH.

---

### **Stage 7: VM Termination / Cleanup**

When a user issues `onevm terminate <vmid>`:
1. `oned` sends a shutdown/destroy signal to the VM driver on the host.
2. The driver kills the hypervisor process and cleans up virtual disks in the System Datastore.
3. The driver de-allocates network resources.
4. `oned` marks the VM state as `DONE`.
5. The VM is optionally archived into the history database for auditing.

$$
\text{VM}_{state} : \text{RUNNING} \rightarrow \text{SHUTDOWN} \rightarrow \text{DONE}
$$

---

## 3.2 Mathematical Summary of States

The full VM state machine can be represented as a directed state graph:

$$
S = \{ \text{PENDING}, \text{HOLD}, \text{PROLOG}, \text{BOOT}, \text{RUNNING}, \text{MIGRATE}, \text{STOPPED}, \text{SUSPENDED}, \text{DONE}, \text{FAILED} \}
$$

Allowed transitions (excerpt):

$$
\text{PENDING} \rightarrow \text{HOLD} \quad \mid \quad \text{HOLD} \rightarrow \text{PROLOG}
$$

$$
\text{PROLOG} \rightarrow \text{BOOT} \quad \mid \quad \text{BOOT} \rightarrow \text{RUNNING}
$$

$$
\text{RUNNING} \rightarrow \text{MIGRATE} \rightarrow \text{RUNNING} \quad \text{(live migration)}$$
$$
\text{RUNNING} \rightarrow \text{STOPPED} \quad \mid \quad \text{STOPPED} \rightarrow \text{DONE}
$$

$$
\text{ANY} \rightarrow \text{FAILED} \quad \text{(on driver error)}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** The most commonly asked states are **PENDING, PROLOG, RUNNING, DONE, and FAILED**. Memorize the arrows in/out of `PROLOG` — this is where image staging happens.

---

## 3.3 Capacity Calculation Example (Exam-Style)

A common numerical question: *Given a host with 32 vCPUs and 128 GB RAM, OpenNebula reports it as 100% reserved. The KVM hypervisor overhead is 4% of total resources. Calculate the effective allocatable resources for VMs.*

**Step 1 — Apply hypervisor overhead:**

$$
\text{Overhead}_{CPU} = 0.04 \times 32 = 1.28 \text{ vCPUs}
$$
$$
\text{Overhead}_{RAM} = 0.04 \times 128 = 5.12 \text{ GB}
$$

**Step 2 — Effective capacity:**

$$
\text{CPU}_{alloc} = 32 - 1.28 = 30.72 \text{ vCPUs} \approx 30 \text{ vCPUs}
$$
$$
\text{RAM}_{alloc} = 128 - 5.12 = 122.88 \text{ GB} \approx 122 \text{ GB}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> OpenNebula uses **KVM hypervisor overhead** as a default reservation factor to avoid resource exhaustion. This is sometimes asked in numerical questions.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🗺️ STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & ARCHITECTURE SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 OpenNebula High-Level Reference Architecture

The following Mermaid diagram shows the **complete OpenNebula reference architecture** with all major components, driver subsystems, and external integrations.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph FE["FRONT-END MANAGEMENT PLANE"]
        GUI["SUNSTONE GUI - PORT 9869"]
        CLI["CLI TOOLS - onevm onehost"]
        API["REST API - ONE FLOW"]
        MARKET["MARKETPLACE APP STORE"]
    end

    subgraph CORE["OPEN NEBULA CORE"]
        ONED["ONED CORE DAEMON"]
        SCHED["SCHEDULER ENGINE"]
        DB["MYSQL OR SQLITE BACKEND"]
        FED["FEDERATION MASTER"]
    end

    subgraph DRIVERS["DRIVER LAYER"]
        VMD["VM DRIVERS - KVM VMWARE XEN LXC"]
        IMD["INFO DRIVERS - HOST MONITORING"]
        TMD["TRANSFER DRIVERS - NFS CEPH SSH"]
        NETD["NETWORK DRIVERS - 8021Q OVS EB PF"]
    end

    subgraph HOSTS["VIRTUALIZATION HOSTS - HYPERVISOR PLANE"]
        H1["HOST 01 - KVM"]
        H2["HOST 02 - KVM"]
        H3["HOST 03 - VMWARE VCENTER"]
    end

    subgraph STORAGE["DATASTORE TIER"]
        IMGDS["IMAGE DATASTORE - OS TEMPLATES"]
        SYSDS["SYSTEM DATASTORE - RUNNING VM DISKS"]
        FILDS["FILES DATASTORE - USER FILES"]
    end

    subgraph HYBRID["HYBRID CLOUD BRIDGE"]
        AWS["AWS EC2 DRIVER"]
        AZURE["AZURE DRIVER"]
        GCP["GCP DRIVER"]
    end

    GUI --> ONED
    CLI --> ONED
    API --> ONED
    MARKET --> ONED
    ONED --> DB
    ONED --> SCHED
    ONED --> FED
    ONED --> VMD
    ONED --> IMD
    ONED --> TMD
    ONED --> NETD
    VMD --> H1
    VMD --> H2
    VMD --> H3
    IMD --> H1
    IMD --> H2
    IMD --> H3
    TMD --> IMGDS
    TMD --> SYSDS
    NETD --> H1
    NETD --> H2
    NETD --> H3
    FED --> AWS
    FED --> AZURE
    FED --> GCP
    FILDS --> H1
```

---

## 4.2 VM Provisioning Sequence Diagram

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant U as USER
    participant SUN as SUNSTONE GUI
    participant O as ONED CORE
    participant DB as DATABASE
    participant SC as SCHEDULER
    participant H as HOST HYPERVISOR
    participant DS as DATASTORE

    U->>SUN: Submit VM Template
    SUN->>O: XML RPC create VM
    O->>DB: Persist VM state PENDING
    O->>SC: Dispatch for placement
    SC->>O: Return selected HOST
    O->>DS: TM driver stage image to host
    DS-->>H: Image now on System DS
    O->>H: VM driver instantiate VM
    H-->>O: VM BOOT succeeded
    O->>DB: Update state to RUNNING
    O-->>SUN: Return VMID and status
    SUN-->>U: Display running VM
```

---

## 4.3 OpenNebula Federation Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ZONEM["MASTER ZONE"]
        ZM["ONED MASTER - AUTH REPL"]
        DB_M["CENTRAL DB"]
    end

    subgraph ZONE1["SLAVE ZONE 1 - EDGE DC"]
        ZS1["ONED SLAVE 1"]
        H1["HOST POOL A"]
    end

    subgraph ZONE2["SLAVE ZONE 2 - REGIONAL DC"]
        ZS2["ONED SLAVE 2"]
        H2["HOST POOL B"]
    end

    subgraph ZONE3["SLAVE ZONE 3 - PUBLIC CLOUD"]
        ZS3["ONED SLAVE 3 - AWS"]
        H3["VIRTUAL HOSTS"]
    end

    ZM <--> ZS1
    ZM <--> ZS2
    ZM <--> ZS3
    ZS1 --> H1
    ZS2 --> H2
    ZS3 --> H3
    DB_M -.->|REPLICATE| ZS1
    DB_M -.->|REPLICATE| ZS2
    DB_M -.->|REPLICATE| ZS3
```

---

## 4.4 Datastore Interaction Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    USER[USER UPLOADS IMAGE] --> IMGREG[REGISTER IN IMAGE DS]
    IMGREG --> TMDRV{TM DRIVER TYPE}
    TMDRV -->|SHARED NFS CEPH| SHARED[DIRECT MOUNT NO COPY]
    TMDRV -->|SSH LOCAL| COPY[SECURE COPY VIA SCP]
    SHARED --> SYSDS[SYSTEM DS ON TARGET HOST]
    COPY --> SYSDS
    SYSDS --> BOOT[VM BOOTS FROM SYSTEM DS]
```

> [!TIP]
> The **TM driver choice** is one of the most commonly confused topics in KTU exams. Always state: *"Shared drivers do NOT copy the image; SSH drivers DO copy the image."*

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📝 KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Question 1: Define OpenNebula. List any four of its key features.**

> `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

**OpenNebula** is an open-source cloud computing management platform used to build and manage virtualized data centers, private clouds, and hybrid cloud environments. It provides Infrastructure-as-a-Service (IaaS) capabilities by orchestrating hypervisors, storage, and networking resources.

**Four Key Features:**

1. **Multi-hypervisor support** — Manages KVM, VMware vCenter, Xen, and LXC from a single control plane.
2. **Multi-tenancy** — Native user/group/ACL/VDCs for isolated cloud partitions.
3. **Hybrid cloud bursting** — Native drivers to AWS, Azure, and Google Cloud.
4. **Lightweight monolithic architecture** — A single `oned` daemon with minimal deployment complexity.

**Common Follow-up in Boards:** *"Differentiate OpenNebula from OpenStack."* — Always mention *monolithic vs. microservice architecture* and *deployment complexity*.

---

### **Question 2: What is Sunstone in OpenNebula? Mention its default port.**

> `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

**Sunstone** is the **default web-based graphical user interface (GUI)** of OpenNebula, built using Ruby on Rails. It allows administrators and users to manage all cloud resources — VMs, hosts, networks, images, users — through a browser-based dashboard.

**Default Port:** `9869` (HTTPS recommended in production).

Sunstone supports **role-based views**: `user`, `admin`, `cloud admin`, `groupadmin`, and `sunstone-admin` — each offering different capabilities and views into the cloud.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Note:** As per KTU ESE (End Semester Exam) Module 3 pattern, students must answer **ONE question from Module 3**. Below are two alternative 14-mark questions with sub-parts.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks): OpenNebula Architecture and VM Lifecycle**

> `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**
**Explain the OpenNebula architecture in detail with a neat diagram. List the responsibilities of the core daemon `oned`.**

**Model Solution:**

The OpenNebula architecture is a **two-tier model** consisting of the **Front-End (Management Plane)** and **Hosts (Virtualization Plane)**. Communication between the two tiers occurs through secure **SSH-based driver channels** carrying XML-RPC payloads.

**Front-End Tier Hosts:**

- **Sunstone GUI** — Web interface on port 9869.
- **CLI Tools** — `onevm`, `onehost`, `oneimage`, etc.
- **OneFlow** — Service orchestration for multi-VM apps.
- **Scheduler** — Decides host placement.
- **Core Daemon (`oned`)** — Central orchestrator.
- **MySQL/SQLite** — Stateful backend database.
- **Marketplace** — App store for VM images.

**Host Tier Hosts:**

- Physical servers running KVM, VMware, Xen, or LXC.
- Managed exclusively through driver SSH channels.

**Responsibilities of `oned` daemon:**

1. Manages the **entire VM lifecycle** (create, suspend, resume, stop, terminate).
2. Maintains **persistent state** in the database.
3. Coordinates **scheduling decisions** with the scheduler.
4. Exposes **XML-RPC API** to Sunstone, CLI, and OneFlow.
5. Orchestrates **driver subsystems** (VM, IM, TM, VN, HM, MARKET).
6. Handles **user/group/VDC/ACL** management.
