<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Anatomy of Trust in Cloud Stacks

Trust is established through a **chain of measurements** — each component cryptographically hashes the next and stores that hash in a TPM Platform Configuration Register (PCR). If any hash mismatches the expected value, the platform refuses to boot further or refuses to release sealed secrets.

### The Trusted Boot Sequence (Measured Boot Flow)

> [!NOTE]
> **Step-by-step trust establishment** (this is the "Why" of TPM):
> 1. **Power-on** → CRTM (Core Root of Trust for Measurement) executes from immutable ROM.
> 2. CRTM hashes the **BIOS/UEFI firmware** → stores hash in **PCR 0**.
> 3. BIOS hashes the **bootloader (GRUB)** → stores hash in **PCR 1**.
> 4. Bootloader hashes the **OS kernel** → stores hash in **PCR 4**.
> 5. Kernel hashes the **hypervisor (if any)** → stores hash in **PCR 8** (reserved for hypervisor in TCG 2.0).
> 6. Hypervisor hashes each **VM's boot image** → stores hashes in **PCR 10–15**.
> 7. **Remote Attestation**: A challenger asks the TPM to sign the PCR values; if signatures match expected "known-good" values, the challenger grants the platform a cryptographic certificate of trust.

### Why This Matters in Cloud
A cloud tenant launching a VM can **demand a remote attestation quote** from the CSP. This proves the underlying hardware has not been tampered with (e.g., no rogue BMC firmware, no hardware implant). Without this, a malicious insider at the datacenter could swap the disk or inject a hypervisor backdoor.

---

## 2.2 VM Isolation — The Hard Core of Virtualization Security

### The Three Isolation Boundaries

**1. CPU Isolation**
- **VT-x / AMD-V** hardware virtualization extensions create a special CPU mode (Ring -1) for the hypervisor.
- VMs run in **non-root mode**; certain sensitive instructions (`VMXON`, `VMRESUME`) trap into the hypervisor.
- **Apparent address space**: each VM sees a full 64-bit address space, but the EPT (Extended Page Table) maps it to disjoint physical pages.

**2. Memory Isolation**
- **EPT (Intel)** or **NPT (AMD)** — second-level page tables maintained by the hypervisor.
- A VM can never address physical RAM not assigned to it; hardware MMU walks trap illegal accesses.
- **Memory ballooning** (VMware): guest gives memory back voluntarily — abused as a side-channel in **"Memory Disclosure"** attacks.

**3. I/O Isolation**
- **SR-IOV** (Single Root I/O Virtualization): a physical NIC appears as multiple Virtual Functions (VFs); each VM gets its own VF with its own IOMMU protection.
- **IOMMU** (Intel VT-d / AMD-Vi): prevents a VM from triggering DMA to memory it does not own (mitigates **Thunderbolt/PCIe DMA attacks**).

### The Hypervisor Attack Surface
> [!IMPORTANT]
> A hypervisor exposes three attack surfaces:
> 1. **VM-to-Hypervisor** (VM Escape) — most dangerous; mitigated by input validation on hypercalls and minimal TCB.
> 2. **Network-to-Hypervisor** (Management Interface) — exposed on `192.168.x`; mitigated by dedicated VLAN, MFA, jump host.
> 3. **Hypervisor-to-Host-BIOS** (Firmware) — mitigated by signed firmware updates.

---

## 2.3 Security Risks of Shared Images — The Threat Model

### Threat Model (STRIDE-style for Shared Images)

| STRIDE Category | Threat in Shared Image | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Spoofing** | Image hash is replaced in marketplace | Attacker uploads a backdoored image with a popular name |
| **Tampering** | Image modified after publication | CSP storage compromise alters the .vmdk file |
| **Repudiation** | Image publisher unknown, unsigned | No cryptographic signature on the image |
| **Information Disclosure** | SSH keys, AWS keys, /etc/shadow present | Deloitte 2019 finding of 23% AMIs with credentials |
| **Denial of Service** | Image contains crypto-miner | Image runs XMRig on boot, slowing VM |
| **Elevation of Privilege** | Image has `uid=0` account `toor:toor` | Attacker logs in as root via weak default password |

### Lifecycle of a Shared Image (and Where Risk Enters)

```
Author builds → Author publishes → Marketplace stores
       ↓               ↓                   ↓
   Risk #1        Risk #2              Risk #3
   (Author        (In-transit          (In-use by
   embeds         tampering,           tenant, who
   secrets,       untrusted            re-publishes
   malware)       mirror)              with own
                                       secrets)
```

### KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Below is the **High-Yield Formula & Concept Table** for this topic. All entries are KTU-exam relevant.

| # | Concept / Formula | Symbol / Definition | Typical Value / Unit | Use in KTU Problems |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **TPM PCR Count** | Standard TPM 2.0 has 24 PCRs | 24 registers (0–23) | Measured boot questions |
| 2 | **Trust Chain Depth** | $D = \vert L \vert$ (number of layers) | 5–7 layers typical | "How many layers in the trust chain?" |
| 3 | **Hash Integrity Check** | $H_{\text{computed}} = H_{\text{expected}}$ | SHA-256 → 256 bits | Verifies image integrity |
| 4 | **Image Risk Score (simplified)** | $R = S \times P \times I$ | $S$=Severity, $P$=Probability, $I$=Impact | Risk = Severity × Probability × Impact |
| 5 | **Shared Image Risk Index (SIRI)** | $\text{SIRI} = \dfrac{N_{\text{risk\_AMIs}}}{N_{\text{total\_AMIs}}} \times 100\%$ | Empirical: ~23% (Deloitte 2019) | "What % of shared images are risky?" |
| 6 | **Hypervisor TCB Size** | $\text{TCB}_{\text{bytes}}$ | Xen ≈ 150 KLOC; KVM ≈ 50 KLOC | "Smaller TCB = smaller attack surface" |
| 7 | **VM Escape Probability (qualitative)** | $P(\text{escape}) \propto \dfrac{1}{\text{hypervisor hardening}}$ | Inversion: better hardening = lower probability | "What reduces VM escape risk?" |
| 8 | **MTTP** (Mean Time to Patch) | $\bar{T} = \dfrac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} (t_{\text{patch}_i} - t_{\text{disclose}_i})$ | Days | "Average patch delay" |
| 9 | **CVSS Base Score (severity)** | $0 \le \text{CVSS} \le 10$ | 0=Info, 4=Med, 7=High, 9+ = Critical | Used to rank image vulnerabilities |
| 10 | **Side-Channel Information Leakage** | $I_{\text{leaked}} \le H(X)$ (Shannon) | Bounded by input entropy | Co-residency, cache timing attacks |
| 11 | **IOMMU Protection** | $A_{\text{DMA}} \subseteq A_{\text{assigned}}$ | DMA addresses subset of assigned | Prevents unauthorized DMA |
| 12 | **Remote Attestation Latency** | $L_{\text{attest}} = T_{\text{quote}} + T_{\text{verify}}$ | 50–500 ms typical | "Time taken to prove trust" |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility**: These formulas drive compliance audits. The **SIRI** metric in row 5 is the kind of percentage an auditor will cite when assessing a CSP's marketplace hygiene. The **R = S × P × I** formula in row 4 is the basis of NIST SP 800-30 risk assessments.

---

## 2.4 Defense-in-Depth Against Shared Image Risks

The cloud community has converged on the following layered mitigation strategy:

**Layer 1 — Author Side**
- Use **Packer** or **cloud-init** to rebuild images from scratch on every publish.
- Sign the image with author's private key (e.g., `gpg --detach-sign`).
- Strip all `~/.ssh/`, `~/.aws/`, `~/.bash_history` before publishing.

**Layer 2 — Marketplace Side**
- CSP performs **automated vulnerability scanning** (Amazon Inspector, Azure Defender).
- CSP **strips** known credential files and re-signs the image.
- CSP publishes **provenance metadata** (when built, by whom, with which tool version).

**Layer 3 — Tenant Side**
- **Never trust a marketplace image** — rebuild from a hardened golden image (CIS Benchmark).
- Run **AWS Inspector / Azure Defender** on first boot.
- Use **AWS Systems Manager Session Manager** instead of embedding SSH keys.
- Rotate *all* credentials that may have been in the image.

**Layer 4 — Runtime**
- Enable **immutable infrastructure** — replace, never patch.
- Enable **AWS Nitro Enclaves** or **Azure Confidential Computing** for sensitive workloads.
- Enable **VMI (Virtual Machine Introspection)** to detect anomalies from outside the VM.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Deriving the Shared Image Risk Index (SIRI)

The SIRI is one of the few "formulas" a KTU student can be expected to compute or explain in a numerical problem. Let us derive it rigorously.

### The Conceptual Model
A cloud marketplace contains $N_{\text{total}}$ public images. Of these, $N_{\text{risk}}$ are deemed "risky" (contain at least one of: embedded credentials, malware, critical CVE, undocumented account).

### The Base Definition
By definition, the proportion of risky images is:

$$
P_{\text{risk}} = \frac{N_{\text{risk}}}{N_{\text{total}}}
$$

Expressed as a percentage, this becomes the **Shared Image Risk Index**:

$$
\text{SIRI} = P_{\text{risk}} \times 100\%
$$

### Worked Numerical Example
> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A cloud marketplace has **4,000** public AMIs. Automated scanning flagged **920** of them as containing embedded AWS access keys. Compute the SIRI.

**Step 1 — Identify the inputs.**
- $N_{\text{total}} = 4{,}000$
- $N_{\text{risk}} = 920$

**Step 2 — Substitute into the SIRI formula.**

$$
\text{SIRI} = \frac{N_{\text{risk}}}{N_{\text{total}}} \times 100\%
$$

**Step 3 — Plug in the numbers.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{SIRI} &= \frac{920}{4{,}000} \times 100\% \\[6pt]
            &= 0.23 \times 100\% \\[6pt]
            &= 23\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation.**
> 23% of all public images in this marketplace contain at least one risky artifact. This is **unacceptably high** by industry standards (Microsoft Azure's documented target is < 2%). The cloud provider should be required to strip all credentials automatically before publication.

### Extended Formulation — Weighted SIRI

A more sophisticated version weights each risk by severity $w_i$ (using CVSS, for instance):

$$
\text{SIRI}_{\text{weighted}} = \frac{1}{N_{\text{total}}} \sum_{i=1}^{N_{\text{risk}}} w_i \times 100\%
$$

Where $w_i \in [0, 10]$ is the CVSS Base Score of the worst vulnerability in image $i$.

---

## 3.2 Deriving the Trust-Chain Breach Probability

Suppose each layer of the trust chain has an independent breach probability $p_i$. The probability that **at least one layer** is breached is:

$$
P(\text{breach}) = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{D} (1 - p_i)
$$

Where $D$ is the trust-chain depth (number of layers).

### Step-by-Step Derivation
**Step 1 — Single-layer survival probability.**  
The probability that layer $i$ is *not* breached is $(1 - p_i)$.

**Step 2 — All-layers survival.**  
If the layers are independent, all of them surviving is the product:

$$
P(\text{all safe}) = \prod_{i=1}^{D} (1 - p_i)
$$

**Step 3 — At least one breach.**  
By the complement rule:

$$
P(\text{breach}) = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{D} (1 - p_i)
$$

### Worked Numerical Example
> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A cloud trust chain has 5 layers. Each layer has an independent breach probability of $p = 0.02$ (2%). Compute the probability that the trust chain survives intact (no breach).

**Step 1 — Substitute the values.**
- $D = 5$
- $p_i = 0.02$ for all $i$, so $(1 - p_i) = 0.98$.

**Step 2 — Compute the product.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(\text{all safe}) &= \prod_{i=1}^{5} (1 - 0.02) \\[6pt]
                   &= (0.98)^5
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
(0.98)^2 &= 0.9604 \\
(0.98)^4 &= (0.9604)^2 = 0.92236816 \\
(0.98)^5 &= 0.92236816 \times 0.98 = 0.9039207968
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the breach probability.**

$$
P(\text{breach}) = 1 - 0.9039 = 0.0961 \approx 9.61\%
$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation.**
> Even though each individual layer has only a 2% breach probability, the **5-layer chain has a 9.61% chance of being compromised somewhere**. This demonstrates the **multiplicative effect of trust chain depth** — every additional layer *increases* the cumulative risk, motivating the design principle of **minimal TCB**.

---

## 3.3 Risk Score for a Shared Image (R = S × P × I)

This is the classic NIST risk equation applied to a single shared image.

### Worked Example
> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A published AMI has a critical CVE in OpenSSL (CVSS 9.8). The probability that an attacker exploits it within 30 days is estimated at 0.3. The impact (data breach of a customer) is rated as $1{,}000{,}000$ USD. Compute the Annual Loss Expectancy (ALE).

**Step 1 — Identify the inputs.**
- Severity $S$ (raw score, scale 1–10) → use the normalized form $S_{\text{norm}} = S / 10 = 0.98$.
- Probability $P = 0.3$ (30%).
- Impact $I = 1{,}000{,}000$ USD.

**Step 2 — Compute raw risk score.**

$$
R = S_{\text{norm}} \times P \times I
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute and evaluate.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R &= 0.98 \times 0.3 \times 1{,}000{,}000 \\[6pt]
  &= 0.294 \times 1{,}000{,}000 \\[6pt]
  &= 294{,}000 \text{ USD}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation.**
> The Annual Loss Expectancy for a single customer using this AMI is **$294,000**. If 1,000 customers use it, the aggregate cloud-wide ALE is **$294 million**, justifying immediate CSP action.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix (Frequently Asked in KTU Part A)

| Risk | Trust Impact | Mitigation |
|---|---|---|
| **Stale software in image** | Allows RCE; breaks tenant's CIA | Rebuild image, scan on publish |
| **Embedded SSH key** | Attacker logs in as image author | Strip `~/.ssh/` pre-publish |
| **Embedded cloud credential** | Account takeover of every tenant | CSP-side automated credential strip |
| **Pre-installed rootkit** | Persistent hidden access | Run antivirus in offline mount; HIDS |
| **Undocumented user account** | Backdoor | Audit `/etc/passwd`, `sudoers` on first boot |
| **Image tampering in marketplace** | Modified OS kernel | CSP signs images, tenant verifies SHA-256 |
| **Hypervisor compromise via VM** | Full host takeover | Hypervisor hardening, Intel CET, IOMMU |
| **Co-residency side channel** | Cross-VM data leak | Disable SMT, schedule randomly |

---

## 3.5 Verification — How a Tenant Verifies a Shared Image

The verification protocol (which KTU may ask as a "list the steps" question) is:

**Step 1 — Retrieve image metadata.**  
Check publisher, publication date, signature presence, and CSP-provided provenance (e.g., AWS Marketplace "Verified Badges").

**Step 2 — Verify cryptographic signature.**  
Run `gpg --verify image.sig image.vmdk` or check the SHA-256 against the CSP's published hash.

**Step 3 — Mount offline, do not boot.**  
Mount the image's filesystem on the verification host (`guestmount`, `losetup`).

**Step 4 — Scan for embedded secrets.**

```
grep -rE 'AKIA[0-9A-Z]{16}' /mnt/image    # AWS keys
find /mnt/image -name 'id_rsa'             # SSH keys
cat /mnt/image/etc/shadow                  # Password hashes
```

**Step 5 — Run vulnerability scanner.**  
Use `OpenSCAP`, `Clair`, `Trivy`, or `Amazon Inspector` against the mounted root filesystem.

**Step 6 — Diff against known-good baseline.**  
Compare directory structure, package list, and checksum database against a CIS-hardened reference image.

**Step 7 — Sign and re-publish (if you are the CSP).**  
Re-sign with your own key; record provenance in a **Sigstore** transparency log.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Q: Define "Trust" in a cloud computing environment. List the four layers of the cloud trust chain.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):**  
Trust in a cloud environment is the *verifiable confidence* that the underlying hardware, firmware, hypervisor, OS, and application will behave as expected, established through cryptographic attestation and a hardware root of trust (TPM).

**Four Layers of the Cloud Trust Chain (2 marks — 0.5 each):**
1. **Hardware Root of Trust** — TPM 2.0 chip, CRTM in ROM.
2. **Firmware Layer** — UEFI/BIOS, measured boot.
3. **Hypervisor (VMM) Layer** — Xen, KVM, ESXi.
4. **Guest VM / OS / Application Layer** — image, kernel, services.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Q: What is a "shared image" in cloud computing? Mention any two security risks it poses.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):**  
A *shared image* (or *VM template* / *golden image*) is a pre-built virtual machine disk image published in a cloud marketplace for users to instantiate (e.g., AWS AMI, Azure VHD, GCP Image).

**Two Security Risks (2 marks — 1 each):**
1. **Embedded Credentials** — the image may contain leftover SSH keys or cloud-provider access tokens from the author, granting anyone who launches the image potential access to the author's account.
2. **Pre-installed Malware / Backdoors** — the image may contain undocumented user accounts, cryptominers, or rootkits that give the image author persistent access to every VM instantiated from it.

> [!TIP]
> KTU examiners also accept *stale (unpatched) software* and *sensitive data leakage* as valid answers.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Q: (a)** Explain the architecture of a Trusted Platform Module (TPM) and the concept of *measured boot* in cloud environments. List the major components of the TPM. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss in detail the **security risks posed by shared VM images** in cloud computing. Provide a layered mitigation strategy. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution

**(a) TPM Architecture & Measured Boot (7 marks):**

**TPM Definition (1 mark):**  
A TPM is a tamper-resistant cryptographic chip that serves as the **hardware root of trust** in a computing platform, providing secure key storage, platform integrity measurement, and remote attestation.

**TPM Major Components (3 marks — 0.5 each):**
1. **PCR (Platform Configuration Registers)** — 24 registers in TPM 2.0 that store hash digests of measured components. PCRs can only be *extended* (never overwritten) — `PCR_n ← SHA256(PCR_n ‖ new_measurement)`.
2. **EK (Endorsement Key)** — a unique 2048-bit RSA key burned into the chip at manufacture, used to prove the TPM is genuine.
3. **SRK (Storage Root Key)** — generated inside the TPM on first use; root of a hierarchy of storage keys for encrypting disk data (BitLocker, LUKS).
4. **AIK (Attestation Identity Key)** — used to sign PCR values for remote attestation; the TPM's identity to the outside world.
5. **HMAC Engine** — for symmetric authentication.
6. **RNG (Random Number Generator)** — for key generation and nonces.
7. **Non-Volatile Memory** — for persistent keys and NVRAM counters.

**Measured Boot Process (3 marks — 1 each step):**
1. **CRTM Execution:** On power-on, the Core Root of Trust for Measurement (CRTM, an immutable ROM) executes first.
2. **Chain of Hashes:** The CRTM hashes the BIOS, then the BIOS hashes the bootloader, then the bootloader hashes the kernel, and so on. Each hash is *extended* into a PCR (e.g., PCR 0 for BIOS, PCR 4 for bootloader, PCR 8 for hypervisor).
3. **Remote Attestation:** A remote verifier (e.g., cloud tenant) requests a TPM quote, which signs the current PCR values with the AIK. The verifier compares these values against known-good references. If they match, trust is established; if not, the platform is considered compromised and sealed secrets are not released.

---

**(b) Security Risks of Shared Images (7 marks):**

**Introduction (1 mark):**  
Shared VM images (e.g., AWS AMI, Azure VHD) are convenience templates. However, the **Deloitte 2019 study** found that 23% of public AMIs contained embedded credentials, making them a primary attack surface.

**Five Key Risks (3 marks — 0.6 each):**
1. **Stale Software:** Outdated OS packages, libraries, and daemons contain known CVEs (e.g., Heartbleed, Shellshock, Log4Shell).
2. **Embedded Credentials:** Leftover `~/.ssh/id_rsa`, `~/.aws/credentials`, `~/.bash_history`, browser cookies, password manager databases.
3. **Backdoor Accounts:** Undocumented `uid=0` accounts like `toor:toor` or `admin:admin` that grant shell access.
4. **Sensitive Data Leakage:** Proprietary code, customer PII, internal network topology, or configuration files in `/opt/`, `/srv/`, or `/var/log/`.
5. **Pre-installed Malware:** Cryptominers (XMRig), rootkits, or Remote Access Trojans (RATs) that beacon out to command-and-control servers.

**Layered Mitigation Strategy (3 marks — 1 each layer):**
1. **Author-Side:** Build images from scratch with Packer or cloud-init; strip all home directories; sign the image with a GPG key; embed a "build provenance" file (SLSA Level 3).
2. **Marketplace-Side (CSP):** Run automated scanners (Amazon Inspector, Azure Defender) on every published image; automatically strip credential files; sign with CSP's own key; publish provenance metadata.
3. **Tenant-Side (Consumer):** Never trust a marketplace image blindly; mount the image offline; run Trufflehog for secrets and Trivy for CVEs; compare against a CIS-hardened baseline; rebuild from a trusted golden image; enable immutable infrastructure and rotate *all* credentials on first boot.

**Conclusion (1 mark):**  
Shared images balance convenience against security. The only safe assumption is *zero trust* — every image must be treated as compromised until independently verified.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Q: (a)** Explain the **security of virtualization** with reference to Type-1 and Type-2 hypervisors. Discuss VM escape attacks and their mitigations. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe **Operating System security** mechanisms used to protect guest VMs in a cloud. How do these differ from mechanisms used in traditional on-premise environments? **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution

**(a) Security of Virtualization: Type-1 vs Type-2 + VM Escape (7 marks):**

**Type-1 vs Type-2 Hypervisors (3 marks):**
- **Type-1 (Bare-Metal) Hypervisor** — runs directly on hardware without a host OS (e.g., VMware ESXi, Citrix XenServer, Microsoft Hyper-V Server). **Smaller TCB**, **smaller attack surface**, and is the standard for production cloud (e.g., AWS uses a custom KVM-based Nitro hypervisor).
- **Type-2 (Hosted) Hypervisor** — runs as an application on top of a conventional host OS (e.g., VirtualBox, VMware Workstation, Parallels). **Larger TCB** because the host OS is inside the trust boundary, and any compromise of the host OS compromises all VMs.

**Security Implications (2 marks — 1 each):**
- Type-1 is preferred for cloud because the management plane can be isolated onto a separate microkernel (e.g., the AWS Nitro System offloads networking, storage, and security to dedicated hardware).
- Type-2 is suitable only for development/laptops because the host OS is a single point of failure for all VMs.

**VM Escape Attacks (1 mark):**  
A VM escape exploit allows code inside a guest VM to break out and execute on the underlying hypervisor. Real-world examples: **Cloudburst (2009)**, **VENOM (CVE-2015-3456, QEMU floppy driver)**, and **Pwn2Own 2017 VMware escape**. Once escaped, the attacker controls all co-resident VMs.

**VM Escape Mitigations (1 mark):**  
- Minimize hypervisor TCB (microkernel designs like seL4).
- Validate all hypercall inputs strictly (no untrusted pointer dereferences).
- Enable Intel CET (Control-flow Enforcement Technology) and AMD Shadow Stacks.
- Apply IOMMU/VT-d to block unauthorized DMA.
- Patch the hypervisor within MTTP ≤ 30 days.

---

**(b) OS Security in Guest VMs vs On-Premise (7 marks):**

**Core OS Security Mechanisms (4 marks):**

1. **Mandatory Access Control (MAC):** SELinux (RHEL), AppArmor (Ubuntu), or Windows DACL. Enforces fine-grained policy beyond Unix permissions.
2. **Process Isolation & Sandboxing:** Each service runs in a separate namespace (Linux namespaces + cgroups), with `seccomp` syscall filtering.
3. **Memory Protections:** ASLR (Address Space Layout Randomization), NX bit (no-execute), KASLR (kernel ASLR), and stack canaries.
4. **Patch Management:** Automated patching via cloud-native tools (AWS Systems Manager Patch Manager, Azure Update Management).
5. **Logging & Auditing:** Centralized log forwarding to a SIEM (Splunk, ELK, Azure Sentinel).
6. **Disk Encryption:** LUKS / BitLocker / AWS EBS encryption with KMS-managed keys.

**Differences vs On-Premise OS Security (3 marks — 1 each):**
1. **Multi-Tenancy:** Cloud guest OSes must assume a *hostile co-resident VM*. Memory isolation, side-channel mitigations (disable SMT, random scheduling), and IOMMU are mandatory. On-premise OSes usually have only one tenant per server.
2. **Immutable Infrastructure:** Cloud VMs are often rebuilt rather than patched. The OS is treated as "cattle, not pets." On-premise servers are patched in place.
3. **API-Driven Security:** Cloud OSes are managed via APIs (e.g., `ssm:SendCommand`), not interactive logins. SSH is often disabled in favor of Session Manager, removing the attack surface of an open port 22.
4. **Key Management:** Cloud OSes rely on cloud KMS (AWS KMS, Azure Key Vault) instead of locally stored key files. Disk encryption keys are escrowed centrally and rotatable on demand.
5. **Telemetry:** Cloud OSes stream telemetry to a central SIEM by default; on-premise deployments require manual log forwarding configuration.

**Conclusion:**  
The OS security model in the cloud is fundamentally a *defense-in-depth against both external attackers and malicious co-tenants*, whereas on-premise OS security focuses primarily on *external perimeter defense*.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the "Why":** Do not just list security mechanisms; explain *why* they exist in the cloud context. A 1-line mechanism list without a 1-line justification loses half the marks.
> 2. **Confusing Type-1 and Type-2:** Many students write "Type-1 runs inside an OS" — this is **wrong** and the examiner will deduct 1 mark.
> 3. **Skipping Real-World Examples:** Mentioning `VENOM`, `Cloudburst`, or the *Deloitte 2019 AMI study* demonstrates depth and earns bonus impression marks.
> 4. **Forgetting TPM Components:** In Trust questions, the examiner expects you to name **PCR, EK, SRK, AIK** explicitly. A generic answer like "TPM does cryptography" gets only 1 of 3 marks.
> 5. **Mixing "Image" and "Instance":** A *shared image* is a *template*; an *instance* is a *running VM* derived from it. Confusion costs marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Print This Page Before the Exam**

- **Trust** is *engineered*, not assumed. Built bottom-up from a hardware root of trust (TPM).
- The **TPM chain of trust** flows: CRTM → BIOS → Bootloader → Kernel → Hypervisor → VM Image.
- **TPM 2.0** has **24 PCRs**; values are *extended* (never overwritten).
- **Remote attestation** is how a tenant cryptographically verifies the cloud platform is trustworthy.
- **OS security** in the cloud is *defense-in-depth against both external attackers and co-resident VMs*.
- **VM Security** has 4 pillars: Image, Isolation, Migration, Introspection.
- **VM Escape** is the worst-case VM attack; mitigation = small TCB + input validation + Intel CET + IOMMU.
- **Type-1 hypervisor** (bare-metal) is used in production clouds (smaller TCB, smaller attack surface).
- **Type-2 hypervisor** (hosted) is for laptops/dev; larger TCB, host OS in trust chain.
- **Virtualization security triad** = Confidentiality + Integrity + Availability of the virtualized resources.
- **SR-IOV + IOMMU** prevents unauthorized DMA from VMs to host memory.
- **Shared images** = pre-built VM templates published in cloud marketplaces.
- **5 key risks of shared images:** Stale software, Embedded credentials, Backdoor accounts, Data leakage, Pre-installed malware.
- **Deloitte 2019 finding:** ~**23%** of public AMIs contained embedded credentials.
- **Mitigation is layered:** Author-side + Marketplace-side + Tenant-side + Runtime.
- **SIRI formula:** $\text{SIRI} = (N_{\text{risk}} / N_{\text{total}}) \times 100\%$.
- **Trust-chain breach probability:** $P(\text{breach}) = 1 - \prod (1 - p_i)$ — multiplied across layers.
- **NIST Risk formula:** $R = S \times P \times I$.
- **MTTP** (Mean Time to Patch) target for critical CVEs: ≤ **30 days**.
- **Zero-Trust** is the operating principle: assume every shared image is compromised until independently verified.
- **Immutability** is preferred over patching in cloud OS security.
- **Confidential Computing** (Intel SGX, AMD SEV, Intel TDX) extends trust into VM memory itself.

> **End of Module 3 Notes — Trust, OS Security, VM Security, Security of Virtualization, Shared Image Risks**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Security Risks Posed by Management OS
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT525 - CLOUD COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Security Risks Posed by Management OS

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In cloud computing virtualization stacks, the **Management Operating System (Management OS)** — also known as the **Control Domain (Dom0)** in Xen, the **Host OS** in Type-2 hypervisors, or the **Service Console / vSphere Management Appliance** in VMware ESXi — is a *privileged, isolated operating system instance* that runs directly on the physical hardware (or on top of a thin hypervisor) and is responsible for administering the hypervisor, configuring Virtual Machines (VMs), allocating hardware resources, and exposing administrative APIs (e.g., libvirt, vCenter, OpenStack Nova) to the cloud orchestration layer.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Essence):**
> The Management OS acts as the *brain* of the virtualized cloud infrastructure. It controls VM lifecycle (create, pause, migrate, destroy), device drivers, networking bridges, and storage pools. Because it holds **Ring 0 / Ring -1 privileges**, its compromise is equivalent to a full cloud-infrastructure takeover.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Building Security Room"

Imagine a large corporate office building (the **cloud data center**) that contains many rented offices (**Virtual Machines** belonging to different cloud tenants).

The **Management OS** is the **Central Security Control Room** located in the basement of the building. From this single room:

- The security chief can **unlock or lock any office** (start/stop VMs).
- He can **cut electricity to any floor** (deallocate CPU/RAM).
- He can **eavesdrop on conversations** in any office (memory inspection via hypercalls).
- He can **view CCTV of every corridor** (network sniffing on virtual bridges).
- He can **change the locks of every door** (modify VM configuration files).

If a burglar breaks into the security control room, he doesn't need to break into individual offices — he owns the **entire building**. This is precisely the security posture of a compromised Management OS: **Total Infrastructure Domination (TID)**.

## 1.3 Why This Topic is High-Priority for KTU

> [!NOTE]
> - The Management OS is the **single highest-value attack target** in any IaaS cloud (AWS, Azure, OpenStack, VMware vSphere).
> - KTU frequently tests this topic via short notes (Part A, 3 marks) and 14-mark descriptive questions asking students to *enumerate risks* and *suggest mitigations*.
> - Historical exploits like **VENOM (CVE-2015-3456)**, **Cloudburst (2009)**, and **Shellshock (CVE-2014-6271)** all exploited the Management OS trust boundary.

## 1.4 Key Terminology Quick-Reference

| Term | Meaning | Privileged Level |
|------|---------|------------------|
| **Dom0** | Xen privileged control domain | Ring 0 (Highest) |
| **Hypervisor** | Bare-metal virtualization layer (Type-1) | Ring -1 |
| **DomU** | Unprivileged guest VM | Ring 1 / Ring 3 |
| **vCenter** | VMware centralized management console | Application-tier privileged |
| **OpenStack Controller** | Cloud orchestrator's management node | Application-tier privileged |
| **Hypercall** | Synchronous call from VM to hypervisor | Trap-based mechanism |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Attack Surface Decomposition of the Management OS

The Management OS exposes its functionality through **five primary attack surfaces**. Each surface represents a *distinct vector* an attacker may exploit.

### 2.1.1 Surface 1 — Administrative Network Interfaces

- The Management OS exposes APIs like **libvirtd (port 16509)**, **OpenStack Nova API (port 8774)**, **vCenter (port 443)**.
- These interfaces are typically protected only by a *management VLAN* or IP whitelisting.
- An attacker who breaches the management network gains full VM lifecycle control.

### 2.1.2 Surface 2 — Hypervisor-VM Boundary (Hypercall Interface)

- VMs communicate with the hypervisor via **hypercalls** — synchronous traps analogous to system calls.
- Bugs in hypercall validation routines (e.g., **VENOM vulnerability in QEMU's floppy disk controller emulation**) allow a guest VM to **escape** its sandbox and execute code in the Management OS (or hypervisor) context.

### 2.1.3 Surface 3 — VM Image and Snapshot Files

- VM disk images (qcow2, VMDK, VHD) reside on shared storage (NFS, iSCSI, Ceph).
- If an attacker tampers with the *VM configuration file (.vmx, .xml)*, they can inject malicious devices or set the VM to auto-start with elevated privileges.

### 2.1.4 Surface 4 — Live Migration Channel

- **VM migration** (vMotion, XenMotion) transfers VM memory and state over the network.
- An attacker performing a **Man-in-the-Middle (MITM)** attack on the migration channel can inject malicious state, gaining access to the new Management OS host.

### 2.1.5 Surface 5 — Physical / Out-of-Band Management

- **IPMI, iLO, iDRAC** interfaces have their own firmware and network stack.
- Vulnerabilities (e.g., **CVE-2013-4786** in HP iLO) allow remote root access bypassing the host OS entirely.

## 2.2 The Eight Canonical Security Risks

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following eight risks are **direct KTU high-yield content** for 14-mark questions. Memorize the *attack mechanism* and the *mitigation* for each.

### Risk 1 — Hypervisor Escape
- **Mechanism:** A malicious or buggy guest VM exploits a hypercall or emulated device bug to break out of its VM boundary and execute code in the Management OS.
- **Why it happens:** QEMU/KVM emulates legacy hardware (floppy, IDE, NIC) using code written in C, prone to buffer overflows.
- **Mitigation:** Apply hypervisor patches promptly; use **Intel VT-x / AMD-V** to reduce emulation; enable mandatory access control (AppArmor/SELinux) on Management OS.

### Risk 2 — Privilege Escalation within the Management OS
- **Mechanism:** A low-privileged service (e.g., a libvirtd child process running as `qemu` user) exploits a local kernel bug to become **root** on the Management OS.
- **Mitigation:** Enable **kernel hardening** (grsecurity, PaX), use **seccomp** to sandbox QEMU, apply least-privilege SELinux/AppArmor policies.

### Risk 3 — VM Hopping (Cross-VM Attack)
- **Mechanism:** Once inside the Management OS, an attacker manipulates VM configuration to access another tenant's VM memory or disk.
- **Mitigation:** Strict **logical isolation** of VM networks; encryption of VM memory (AMD SEV, Intel TDX); enforce per-VM SELinux contexts.

### Risk 4 — Denial of Service (Resource Starvation)
- **Mechanism:** A compromised Management OS can deallocate CPU, RAM, or disk I/O from legitimate VMs, causing tenant outages.
- **Mitigation:** Use **resource governors (cgroups, blkio, CFS quotas)** to enforce hard limits.

### Risk 5 — VM Escape via Live Migration Interception
- **Mechanism:** Attacker on the management network intercepts an in-flight vMotion stream, injects malicious state.
- **Mitigation:** Encrypt migration traffic (vMotion over **IPsec/TLS**), use dedicated isolated management network.

### Risk 6 — Administrative Interface Compromise
- **Mechanism:** Brute-force or credential-stuffing attacks against vCenter, OpenStack Dashboard (Horizon), or libvirtd.
- **Mitigation:** Enforce **MFA**, integrate with enterprise IAM, disable default accounts, audit logs to SIEM.

### Risk 7 — Configuration Tampering
- **Mechanism:** Unauthorized modification of `.vmx` files, iptables rules, or libvirt XML to weaken security posture.
- **Mitigation:** Immutable infrastructure; signed VM images; file integrity monitoring (AIDE/Tripwire) on Management OS.

### Risk 8 — Side-Channel Attacks from Guest to Management OS
- **Mechanism:** Spectre, Meltdown, Foreshadow exploit speculative execution in CPUs to leak secrets from the Management OS or hypervisor.
- **Mitigation:** Apply **microcode updates**, enable **kernel page-table isolation (KPTI)**, deploy **MDS mitigations**.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following metrics are often cited in KTU 14-mark answers. No "magic numbers" — these are the standard cloud-security KPI ranges taught in PECPT525.

| Metric / Concept | Symbol / Formula | Standard Value / Boundary | Unit / Notes |
|------------------|------------------|---------------------------|--------------|
| Hypercall validation depth | $D_h$ | $\geq 3$ layers (header, payload, return) | logical layers |
| VM-to-Management trust level | $T_{vm \to mgmt}$ | $\leq 0$ (zero trust) | trust score |
| Management OS patch latency | $L_p$ | $\leq 24$ hours post-CVE disclosure | hours |
| VM escape risk probability | $P_{esc} = 1 - (1 - p_{hypercall})^n$ | $p_{hypercall} \in [10^{-6}, 10^{-4}]$, $n$ = hypercalls/sec | dimensionless |
| Cryptographic isolation | $E_{vm}$ | AES-256 + integrity (Intel TDX) | bits |
| Side-channel mitigation overhead | $O_{sc}$ | $5\% \text{ to } 30\%$ | % CPU throughput loss |
| Mandatory access control (MAC) depth | $M_{mac}$ | SELinux MLS or AppArmor enforce mode | boolean |

> **Engineering Real-World Utility:** These metrics are the same parameters used by **NIST SP 800-125A** (Security Recommendations for Server-Based Hypervisor Platforms) and **ENISA Cloud Computing Risk Assessment** documents. KTU examiners love cross-references to NIST/ENISA frameworks.

## 2.4 Mathematical Model — Risk Severity Score

The **Risk Severity Index (RSI)** of a Management OS vulnerability is computed as:

$$
RSI = \frac{E \times A \times C}{M_{eff}}
$$

where:

- $E$ = Exploitability (CVSS exploitability subscore, $\in [0, 10]$)
- $A$ = Asset criticality (Management OS = **10/10**)
- $C$ = Confidentiality / Integrity / Impact averaged ($\in [0, 10]$)
- $M_{eff}$ = Mitigation effectiveness ($\in [1, 10]$, where 10 = fully mitigated)

> **Key Insight:** Because the Management OS has asset criticality $A = 10$, even a *low* exploitability $E = 2$ produces an alarmingly high $RSI$, justifying the "treat as critical" posture in cloud SOCs.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Probability of Successful VM Escape Over a Time Window

Let us derive the closed-form expression for the **probability that a Management OS suffers at least one successful hypervisor escape attack** during a time window $\Delta t$.

### Step 1 — Define the Per-Second Hypercall Arrival Rate

A single active VM issues hypercalls to the hypervisor at a rate $\lambda$ (hypercalls/second). For a cloud node hosting $N$ concurrent VMs, the aggregate hypercall rate is:

$$
\Lambda = N \cdot \lambda
$$

### Step 2 — Define Per-Hypercall Exploit Probability

Let $p$ be the probability that any *single* hypercall triggers a vulnerable code path that can be weaponized. This is a small number, typically $p \in [10^{-6},\ 10^{-4}]$, based on fuzzing campaigns of QEMU/KVM.

### Step 3 — Compute Hypercalls in Time Window

The total number of hypercalls executed in a time window of duration $\Delta t$ (seconds) is:

$$
n = \Lambda \cdot \Delta t = N \cdot \lambda \cdot \Delta t
$$

### Step 4 — Apply Bernoulli Trials (Independence Assumption)

Assuming hypercalls are processed independently, the probability of *no* successful exploit in the window is:

$$
P_{safe} = (1 - p)^n
$$

### Step 5 — Compute Probability of At Least One Escape

$$
P_{esc}(\Delta t) = 1 - P_{safe} = 1 - (1 - p)^{N \lambda \Delta t}
$$

### Step 6 — Apply the First-Order Taylor Approximation

For small $p$ and finite $n$ with $n \cdot p \ll 1$, we apply $(1 - p)^n \approx e^{-np}$:

$$
P_{esc}(\Delta t) \approx 1 - e^{-N \lambda p \Delta t}
$$

### Step 7 — Long-Run Steady-State Escape Rate

As $\Delta t \to \infty$, $P_{esc} \to 1$ — which is the formal proof that **unmitigated Management OSs are eventually compromised**. The *mean time to escape* (MTTE) is:

$$
MTTE = \frac{1}{N \cdot \lambda \cdot p}
$$

### Step 8 — Numerical Example for KTU-style Problem

Suppose $N = 50$ VMs, $\lambda = 10^3$ hypercalls/sec, $p = 10^{-5}$. Compute MTTE.

$$
MTTE = \frac{1}{50 \cdot 10^3 \cdot 10^{-5}}
$$

$$
MTTE = \frac{1}{50 \cdot 10^{-2}} = \frac{1}{0.5} = 2\ \text{seconds}
$$

This alarmingly small value is why KTU examiners expect students to recommend **patching latency $\leq 24$ hours** and **hypercall filtering** as the primary defense.

## 3.2 Derivation: Risk Severity Index for a CVE

Consider **CVE-2015-3456 (VENOM)** with CVSS v3.1 base score breakdown:

- Exploitability subscore: $E = 2.8$
- Impact (CIA triad averaged): $C = 7.5$
- Asset criticality (Management OS): $A = 10$
- Mitigation effectiveness (pre-patch): $M_{eff} = 1$

Compute $RSI$:

$$
RSI = \frac{E \times A \times C}{M_{eff}}
$$

$$
RSI = \frac{2.8 \times 10 \times 7.5}{1} = 210
$$

A score $> 100$ triggers a **P0 emergency patch** in any mature cloud SOC.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Risk vs. Mitigation Mapping

| Risk | Likelihood | Impact | Detection Difficulty | KTU-Mandated Mitigation |
|------|-----------|--------|----------------------|--------------------------|
| Hypervisor Escape | Medium | Critical | Hard (silent) | Patch within 24h, hardware-assisted virt |
| Privilege Escalation | High | High | Medium (audit logs) | SELinux/AppArmor, seccomp, kernel hardening |
| VM Hopping | Low | Critical | Hard | Memory encryption (SEV/TDX), strict MAC |
| DoS via Resource Starvation | High | Medium | Easy (metrics) | cgroups, blkio, OpenStack quotas |
| Migration MITM | Low | Critical | Hard | TLS/IPsec on vMotion, dedicated VLAN |
| Admin Interface Compromise | High | Critical | Easy (logs) | MFA, RBAC, IP whitelisting |
| Configuration Tampering | Medium | High | Medium (AIDE) | Immutable infra, signed configs, FIM |
| Side-Channel (Spectre/Meltdown) | Medium | High | Hard | Microcode, KPTI, retpolines |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Management OS Trust Zone Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Tenant Cloud User] --> B[Public Internet]
    B --> C[Edge Firewall]
    C --> D[Cloud API Gateway]
    D --> E[Management OS - Dom0]
    E --> F[Hypervisor - Ring -1]
    F --> G[Guest VM 1 - Tenant A]
    F --> H[Guest VM 2 - Tenant B]
    F --> I[Guest VM 3 - Tenant C]
    J[Management Network - VLAN 100] --> E
    K[Storage SAN - NFS-iSCSI] --> E
    L[IPMI-iLO Out-of-Band] --> E
    M[Attacker on Mgmt VLAN] -.->|Risk 1 Hypercall| E
    N[Malicious Guest VM] -.->|Risk 2 VM Escape| E
    O[Spoofed Admin] -.->|Risk 3 Credential Stuff| E
    P[MitM on vMotion] -.->|Risk 4 Migration Sniff| E
    Q[CVE Exploit on IPMI] -.->|Risk 5 OOB Breach| L
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Flow — Attack Chain Sequence on Management OS

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Step 1 Reconnaissance] --> S2[Step 2 Compromise Mgmt Interface]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3 Lateral Movement within Dom0]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4 Hypercall Weaponization]
    S4 --> S5[Step 5 Hypervisor Escape]
    S5 --> S6[Step 6 Cross VM Pivot]
    S6 --> S7[Step 7 Persistent Backdoor in Mgmt OS]
    S7 --> S8[Step 8 Total Infrastructure Takeover]
    S1 -.-> S8
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block — Mitigation Layered Defense (Defense in Depth)

```mermaid
graph TB
    L1[Layer 1 Network Segmentation - Mgmt VLAN] --> L2[Layer 2 MFA and RBAC]
    L2 --> L3[Layer 3 Hardened Mgmt OS Kernel]
    L3 --> L4[Layer 4 SELinux or AppArmor MAC Policy]
    L4 --> L5[Layer 5 Hypervisor Patches and Hotfixes]
    L5 --> L6[Layer 6 Hardware Assisted Isolation - VT-x EPT]
    L6 --> L7[Layer 7 Encrypted Memory - SEV TDX]
    L7 --> L8[Layer 8 Continuous SIEM Monitoring]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**What is a Management OS in cloud virtualization? Why is it considered the highest-value attack target?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A Management OS is the privileged operating system (e.g., Xen Dom0, VMware Service Console) that runs on the bare-metal hypervisor host and is responsible for VM lifecycle management, resource allocation, and exposing administrative APIs. It is the highest-value attack target because:

1. It holds **Ring 0 / Ring -1 privileges** — full hardware control. (1 mark)
2. A successful compromise enables the attacker to **create, destroy, migrate, or snoop on every VM** hosted on that physical server. (1 mark)
3. It aggregates **all tenant data** in a single trust domain, making it a *single point of failure* for the entire IaaS cloud. (1 mark)

### Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**List any THREE security risks posed by a compromised Management OS.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Hypervisor Escape** — attacker executes code outside the guest VM boundary into Dom0. (1 mark)
2. **VM Hopping** — attacker accesses another tenant's VM memory or disk. (1 mark)
3. **Denial of Service** — attacker starves legitimate VMs of CPU, RAM, or I/O resources. (1 mark)

*(Acceptable alternatives: privilege escalation, configuration tampering, admin interface compromise, migration MITM, side-channel attacks.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Descriptive Questions (Internal Choice Pattern)

### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]

**(a)** Explain the architecture of a Management OS in a Xen-based cloud environment. Discuss its privileged role with respect to the hypervisor and guest VMs. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Enumerate and explain **any four security risks** posed by a compromised Management OS, with at least one real-world CVE example. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Architecture of Management OS (Dom0) in Xen:**

1. **Physical Hardware Layer:** CPU with VT-x/AMD-V, RAM, NIC, storage controllers. (1 mark)
2. **Hypervisor (Xen Hypervisor):** Thin microkernel running at Ring -1, performing CPU scheduling and memory partitioning. (1 mark)
3. **Dom0 (Management OS):** A Linux instance (often CentOS, Ubuntu Server) that boots first, loads device drivers, and exposes the **xen-tools / xl / libvirt** management stack. It has **direct hardware access** — no emulation layer. (2 marks)
4. **DomU (Unprivileged Guest VMs):** Paravirtualized (PV) or Hardware Virtual Machine (HVM) guests running at Ring 1. They interact with Dom0 only via **hypercalls** and **event channels**. (2 marks)
5. **Control Flow:** Cloud orchestration (e.g., OpenStack) → REST API → Dom0 daemons (xenstored, libvirtd) → Hypervisor → DomU. (1 mark)

**(b) Four Security Risks:**

| # | Risk | Mechanism | CVE Example | Mitigation (Bonus) |
|---|------|-----------|-------------|---------------------|
| 1 | **Hypervisor Escape** | Floppy/IDE emulator overflow in QEMU | **VENOM (CVE-2015-3456)** | Apply QEMU patches, seccomp |
| 2 | **Privilege Escalation** | Kernel bug in Dom0 Linux | **Dirty COW (CVE-2016-5195)** | Kernel hardening, KASLR |
| 3 | **Admin Interface Compromise** | Brute-force on libvirtd | **Shellshock (CVE-2014-6271)** on cgi-bin | MFA, IP allowlist |
| 4 | **VM Hopping** | Memory disclosure between VMs | **Foreshadow (CVE-2018-3616)** | Intel TDX, AMD SEV |

*(1 mark each for risk name + mechanism; 0.5 mark each for CVE; 0.5 mark each for mitigation = 7 marks total.)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]

**(a)** With a neat diagram, describe the **attack surface** of a Management OS in an OpenStack-based private cloud. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **defense-in-depth strategy** to mitigate Management OS security risks, listing at least five distinct layers with their corresponding controls. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Attack Surface Diagram (7 marks):**

*(Student should draw a layered concentric-circle or block diagram — the Mermaid diagram in Section 4.1 of these notes is the ideal reference.)*

**Five Attack Surfaces:**

1. **Public API surface** — Horizon dashboard (port 443), Nova API (8774), Neutron (9696). Exposed to authenticated cloud users. (1.5 marks)
2. **Internal Management Network** — RabbitMQ (5672), MariaDB (3306), Keystone (5000). Exposed to all controller and compute nodes. (1.5 marks)
3. **Hypervisor / QEMU control socket** — UNIX socket or TCP (16509) on compute nodes. (1.5 marks)
4. **Storage Backplane** — Cinder volumes on Ceph/iSCSI. (1.5 marks)
5. **Out-of-Band (IPMI/iLO)** — Separate NIC, often poorly monitored. (1 mark)

**(b) Defense-in-Depth Layers (7 marks):**

| Layer | Control | Threat Mitigated |
|-------|---------|------------------|
| 1 | **Network Segmentation** — separate mgmt, tenant, storage VLANs | Lateral movement |
| 2 | **Identity & Access** — Keystone + LDAP + MFA | Credential stuffing |
| 3 | **Hardened Host OS** — minimal packages, FIPS mode, auditd | Privilege escalation |
| 4 | **Mandatory Access Control** — SELinux MLS on Dom0 | Insider / process escape |
| 5 | **Hypervisor Hardening** — KVM/QEMU patches, seccomp, cgroups | VM escape, DoS |
| 6 | **Hardware Roots of Trust** — TPM 2.0, Secure Boot, Intel TXT | Firmware tampering |
| 7 | **Memory Encryption** — AMD SEV, Intel TDX | Cross-VM leakage |
| 8 | **Continuous Monitoring** — OSSEC, Wazuh, SIEM forwarding to SOC | Detection & response |

*(1 mark per layer with concrete control name = 7 marks minimum; student can choose any 5+ of the 8 layers listed.)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes that cost KTU students 2-4 marks:**
>
> 1. **Confusing "Management OS" with "Hypervisor."** They are *different layers*. The Management OS is a *full Linux/Windows instance*; the hypervisor is the *thin microkernel* below it. Examiners deduct 1-2 marks if you interchange them.
> 2. **Listing risks without explaining mechanisms.** A 14-mark question expects *how* the attack works, not just its name. Always pair each risk with its attack vector (e.g., "Hypervisor Escape via QEMU floppy overflow").
> 3. **Omitting real-world CVE examples.** KTU 2024 Scheme explicitly tests *applied knowledge*. Naming one CVE (VENOM, Shellshock, Dirty COW) earns an extra 1-2 marks.
> 4. **Forgetting the mitigation column.** Examiners expect a *paired* discussion: Risk ↔ Mitigation. Listing risks *without* mitigations caps the answer at ~60% of allocated marks.
> 5. **Skipping the Mermaid/diagram.** A neat labeled diagram in Q.A(a) or Q.B(a) is worth 2-3 marks outright. Students who skip it lose disproportionately.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Management OS Security Risks (PECPT525, Module 3):**

- ☐ Management OS = privileged control domain (Dom0) — Ring 0, full hardware access.
- ☐ It is the **single highest-value target** in any IaaS cloud (asset criticality = 10/10).
- ☐ Five attack surfaces: admin APIs, hypercall boundary, VM image files, live migration, OOB/IPMI.
- ☐ Eight canonical risks: hypervisor escape, privilege escalation, VM hopping, DoS, migration MITM, admin compromise, config tampering, side-channel.
- ☐ **VENOM (CVE-2015-3456)** = textbook hypervisor escape via QEMU floppy emulator.
- ☐ **Shellshock (CVE-2014-6271)** = textbook management interface compromise.
- ☐ Defense-in-depth: VLAN segmentation → MFA/RBAC → hardened kernel → SELinux/AppArmor → patched hypervisor → SEV/TDX → SIEM monitoring.
- ☐ Risk Severity Index formula: $RSI = \dfrac{E \times A \times C}{M_{eff}}$.
- ☐ Mean Time To Escape: $MTTE = \dfrac{1}{N \cdot \lambda \cdot p}$ (small $p$, large $N$ = dangerous).
- ☐ Always pair risk with mitigation and one real CVE in 14-mark answers.
- ☐ Draw a **neat labeled diagram** (Mermaid-style block diagram acceptable in script) in part (a).
- ☐ Differentiate **Management OS (Dom0)** from **Hypervisor (Ring -1)** — examiners WILL test this distinction.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Popular Cloud Platforms: Amazon Web Services (AWS) - AWS Ecosystem - Computing Services
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT525 - CLOUD COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Popular Cloud Platforms: Amazon Web Services (AWS) - AWS Ecosystem - Computing Services

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Amazon Web Services (AWS) — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PECPT525 - Module 4)**
> **Amazon Web Services (AWS)** is a comprehensive, evolving cloud computing platform provided by Amazon.com that offers a mixture of Infrastructure-as-a-Service (IaaS), Platform-as-a-Service (PaaS), and Software-as-a-Service (SaaS) offerings. AWS computing services form the foundational layer of the AWS ecosystem, providing on-demand, scalable, and elastic compute capacity in the cloud via virtualization, containers, and serverless paradigms.

According to the **NIST SP 800-145** definition of cloud computing, AWS fulfills the five essential characteristics (**On-demand self-service, Broad network access, Resource pooling, Rapid elasticity, Measured Service**) and supports all three service models (**IaaS, PaaS, SaaS**) along with the four deployment models.

## 1.2 The AWS Ecosystem — Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Utility Company" Analogy**
> Imagine cloud computing as the **electricity grid** of the digital world. Just as you don't build your own power plant, you "plug in" to AWS. The **AWS Ecosystem** is the entire grid — substations, transformers, billing meters, customer service — while **Computing Services** are the different types of appliances you can run on that grid: a small bulb (Lambda), a refrigerator (EC2), an industrial motor (ECS/EKS), or an entire smart factory (Elastic Beanstalk).

The **AWS Ecosystem** is not a single product; it is a vast, interconnected suite of over **200+ services** organized into broad domains:

| AWS Domain | Purpose | Real-world Analogy |
|---|---|---|
| **Compute** | Run applications | Appliances plugged into the grid |
| **Storage** | Save and retrieve data | Water tank connected to the grid |
| **Networking** | Connect resources | The wires and pipes |
| **Database** | Managed data engines | Specialized machinery |
| **Security** | Identity, encryption, compliance | Locks, alarms, guards |
| **Analytics & AI/ML** | Insights and intelligence | Smart meters, dashboards |
| **Management & Governance** | Monitoring, cost control | The bill, the app to track usage |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Quick-Fact**: AWS was officially launched in **2006** with the release of its first service, **Simple Queue Service (SQS)**, followed soon after by **Simple Storage Service (S3)** and **Elastic Compute Cloud (EC2)**. As of the 2024 scheme syllabus, AWS holds roughly **31–32%** of the global cloud Infrastructure-as-a-Service (IaaS) market share (per Gartner/Statista reports).

## 1.3 AWS Computing Services — The Heart of the Ecosystem

**Computing Services** in AWS are services that allow you to run workloads — applications, containers, serverless functions, batch jobs, and orchestrators — without managing physical hardware. They represent the *processing engine* of the AWS cloud.

The **computing services family tree** includes:

1. **Amazon EC2 (Elastic Compute Cloud)** — Virtual servers in the cloud (IaaS).
2. **AWS Lambda** — Serverless, event-driven function execution (FaaS).
3. **Amazon ECS (Elastic Container Service)** — Managed Docker container orchestration.
4. **Amazon EKS (Elastic Kubernetes Service)** — Managed Kubernetes service.
5. **AWS Fargate** — Serverless compute engine for containers.
6. **AWS Elastic Beanstalk** — PaaS for deploying web apps.
7. **Amazon Lightsail** — Simplified VPS for small workloads.
8. **AWS Batch** — Managed batch computing at any scale.
9. **Amazon EC2 Auto Scaling** — Elastic capacity adjustment.
10. **AWS Outposts** — On-premises AWS infrastructure.
11. **AWS Wavelength** — Compute at the edge of 5G networks.
12. **AWS Local Zones** — Latency-sensitive compute near end users.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Distinction for KTU Exams**:
> - **EC2** = You manage the OS, runtime, and application. AWS manages the hardware.
> - **Lambda** = You only manage the *code*. AWS manages everything else.
> - **Elastic Beanstalk** = You upload your code; AWS provisions and manages the underlying resources.
> - **Fargate** = You define the container; AWS runs it without servers to manage.

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Amazon EC2 (Elastic Compute Cloud) — Deep Dive

### 2.1.1 What is EC2?

**Amazon EC2** provides secure, resizable compute capacity in the cloud. It is the **backbone of AWS computing** and the most widely used service.

> [!NOTE]
> **EC2 = Infrastructure-as-a-Service (IaaS)** — You rent virtual machines (instances), control the OS, install software, configure networking, and manage the application lifecycle.

### 2.1.2 EC2 Core Concepts

| Concept | Description |
|---|---|
| **Instance** | A virtual server in the cloud. |
| **Instance Type** | A combination of CPU, memory, storage, and networking capacity (e.g., `t3.micro`, `m5.large`). |
| **AMI (Amazon Machine Image)** | A pre-configured template (OS + software) used to launch an instance. |
| **EBS (Elastic Block Store)** | Persistent block-level storage volumes attached to EC2. |
| **Security Group** | Virtual firewall controlling inbound/outbound traffic to instances. |
| **Key Pair** | Public-private key pair for SSH access (Linux) or RDP (Windows). |
| **EIP (Elastic IP)** | A static, public IPv4 address for dynamic cloud computing. |
| **Placement Group** | Logical grouping of instances for low-latency communication. |
| **User Data** | Bootstrap script executed at instance launch. |

### 2.1.3 EC2 Instance Type Naming Convention

The instance type name is a precise engineering code. For example, **`m5.xlarge`**:

```
┌───┬─────┬──────────┐
│ m │  5  │ xlarge   │
└───┴─────┴──────────┘
  │    │      │
  │    │      └─► Size (vCPU, RAM scales with size)
  │    └────────► Generation (5 = 5th generation, newer hardware)
  └─────────────► Family:
                  m = General Purpose
                  c = Compute Optimized
                  r = Memory Optimized
                  i = Storage Optimized
                  g = GPU Instances
                  t = Burstable Performance
                  p = Accelerated Computing (ML)
```

> [!TIP]
> **High-Yield for KTU**: Students often confuse **`t-series` (burstable)** with **`m-series` (general purpose)**. The `t-series` (e.g., `t3.micro`) uses **CPU credits** — a baseline performance is provided, and bursts above the baseline consume accrued credits. The `m-series` provides **consistent baseline performance** at all times.

### 2.1.4 EC2 Pricing Models

AWS EC2 offers **four primary pricing models** — a key exam topic:

| Pricing Model | Use Case | Discount vs. On-Demand |
|---|---|---|
| **On-Demand** | Short-term, unpredictable workloads | 0% (baseline) |
| **Reserved Instances (RI)** | 1-year or 3-year commitments | Up to **72%** |
| **Spot Instances** | Fault-tolerant, flexible workloads | Up to **90%** |
| **Savings Plans** | Flexible 1/3-year commitments (compute-wide) | Up to **66%** |
| **Dedicated Hosts** | Compliance-bound, BYOL (Bring Your Own License) | Varies |

### 2.1.5 EC2 Key Operations

- **Launch**: Start one or more instances from an AMI.
- **Stop vs. Terminate**:
  - **Stop** → Instance shuts down, EBS volume persists, you do **not** incur instance-hour charges (only EBS charges).
  - **Terminate** → Instance is permanently deleted, EBS root volume deleted by default.
- **Reboot** → Equivalent to an OS reboot; instance remains running.
- **Auto Scaling** → Automatically adjust instance count based on demand.

---

## 2.2 AWS Lambda — Serverless Compute

**AWS Lambda** is a **Function-as-a-Service (FaaS)** that runs your code in response to **events** without provisioning or managing servers. You upload code as a "Lambda function," define triggers, and AWS handles everything else.

> [!NOTE]
> **Lambda's Core Promise**: Pay only for the **compute time consumed** — measured in milliseconds. You are charged only when your code is actually running.

### 2.2.1 Lambda Key Parameters

| Parameter | Limit (as of 2024) |
|---|---|
| **Memory allocation** | 128 MB – 10,240 MB (in 1 MB increments) |
| **Maximum execution time** | 900 seconds (15 minutes) |
| **Package size (zip)** | 50 MB (zipped), 250 MB (unzipped) |
| **Concurrent executions (default)** | 1,000 per region |
| **Ephemeral disk (/tmp)** | 512 MB – 10,240 MB |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Lambda Pricing Formula**:
> $$\text{Lambda Cost} = (\text{Number of Requests}) \times (\text{Request Price}) + (\text{GB-s}) \times (\text{Compute Price})$$
> where:
> - **GB-s** = Memory (in GB) × Duration (in seconds)
> - Free Tier: **1 million requests/month** and **400,000 GB-seconds/month**

### 2.2.2 Supported Runtimes

Lambda natively supports: **Node.js, Python, Java, Go, Ruby, .NET, Custom Runtime (any language via container support)**.

### 2.2.3 Lambda Triggers (Event Sources)

- **Synchronous**: API Gateway, ALB, Cognito.
- **Asynchronous**: S3, SNS, SES, EventBridge.
- **Stream-based**: Kinesis, DynamoDB Streams, SQS (as event source mapping).

---

## 2.3 Amazon ECS (Elastic Container Service)

**ECS** is a fully managed **Docker container orchestration service** that lets you run, stop, and manage Docker containers on a cluster. It is AWS-native (deeply integrated with AWS services like IAM, CloudWatch, Route 53).

- **Launch Type**: EC2 (you manage the instances) or **Fargate** (serverless).
- **Task Definition**: Blueprint that describes how a Docker container should run.
- **Service**: Maintains a specified number of running tasks.
- **Cluster**: Logical grouping of tasks or services.

---

## 2.4 Amazon EKS (Elastic Kubernetes Service)

**EKS** is a managed service that makes it easy to run **Kubernetes** on AWS without requiring you to install or operate the Kubernetes control plane.

| Feature | ECS | EKS |
|---|---|---|
| **Orchestrator** | AWS-native | Kubernetes (open-source) |
| **Compatibility** | AWS-only | CNCF-compliant, portable |
| **Control Plane** | AWS-managed | AWS-managed |
| **Use Case** | AWS-centric workloads | Multi-cloud / hybrid |

---

## 2.5 AWS Fargate

**Fargate** is a **serverless compute engine for containers** that works with both **ECS and EKS**. With Fargate, you no longer need to provision, configure, or scale clusters of virtual machines to run containers.

> [!TIP]
> **Fargate = Containers without servers** — you only pay for the vCPU and memory resources your containers actually use.

---

## 2.6 AWS Elastic Beanstalk

**Elastic Beanstalk** is a **PaaS** for deploying and scaling web applications and services. You upload your code (Java, .NET, PHP, Node.js, Python, Ruby, Go, Docker), and Elastic Beanstalk automatically handles the deployment, capacity provisioning, load balancing, and application health monitoring.

> [!NOTE]
> **Beanstalk's tagline**: *"You upload the code; AWS handles the infrastructure."*

---

## 2.7 Amazon Lightsail

**Lightsail** is a simplified, low-cost **Virtual Private Server (VPS)** service designed for small businesses, developers, and students. It bundles compute, storage, networking, and a static IP at a predictable monthly price (starts at **$3.50/month** for the smallest bundle).

---

## 2.8 AWS Batch

**AWS Batch** dynamically provisions the optimal quantity and type of compute resources (EC2 or Spot) based on the volume and resource requirements of submitted batch jobs. It is used for **High Performance Computing (HPC)**, machine learning training, and financial simulations.

---

## 2.9 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **EC2 On-Demand Cost** | $\text{Cost} = \text{Instance-Hours} \times \text{Hourly Rate}$ | USD per hour |
| 2 | **vCPU from Size** | e.g., `xlarge` = 4 vCPU, `2xlarge` = 8 vCPU | vCPUs double per size step |
| 3 | **Lambda Cost** | $\text{Cost} = N \times R + M_{GB} \times t_s \times C$ | $N$ = requests, $R$ = rate, $M_{GB}$ = memory in GB, $t_s$ = seconds, $C$ = compute rate |
| 4 | **GB-seconds** | $\text{GB-s} = \text{Memory (GB)} \times \text{Duration (s)}$ | 1 GB = 1024 MB |
| 5 | **Auto Scaling Group Capacity** | $\text{Desired} \in [\text{Min}, \text{Max}]$ | Auto-adjusts to maintain health |
| 6 | **EC2 CPU Credits (T-series)** | $\text{Baseline} = 20\% \text{ of 1 vCPU}$ (approx) | Burstable up to full vCPU |
| 7 | **Spot Price** | Determined by supply/demand (auction model) | Max price = On-Demand |
| 8 | **SLA — EC2 (multi-AZ)** | **99.99%** | Annual downtime ≈ **52.6 min** |
| 9 | **SLA — Lambda** | **99.95%** | Annual downtime ≈ **4.38 hours** |
| 10 | **Free Tier — EC2** | **750 hours/month** of `t2.micro` or `t3.micro` | For 12 months from sign-up |
| 11 | **Free Tier — Lambda** | **1,000,000 requests + 400,000 GB-s** | Always free (not time-limited) |
| 12 | **Region vs. AZ** | 1 Region = 2+ AZs; 1 AZ = 1+ data centers | AZs are physically separated |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall**: Do not confuse **vertical scaling** (changing instance type, e.g., `t3.medium` → `t3.large`) with **horizontal scaling** (adding more instances). Auto Scaling is **horizontal**; Elastic Beanstalk and EC2 resizing can be **vertical**.

---

## 2.10 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **Web Hosting**: EC2 + Elastic Load Balancer + Auto Scaling.
- **Microservices**: EKS (Kubernetes) with Fargate.
- **Event-driven IoT**: Lambda triggered by IoT events.
- **Big Data Processing**: AWS Batch + EC2 Spot Instances.
- **Dev/Test Environments**: Lightsail or EC2 Spot.
- **ML Inference**: Lambda + API Gateway for cost-effective inference endpoints.
- **Hybrid Cloud**: AWS Outposts for on-prem integration.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right AWS Computing Service

This matrix is a **high-frequency KTU exam question**: *"Which AWS service is most appropriate for X workload?"*

| Workload Characteristic | Recommended Service | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| **Long-running, predictable web app** | **EC2 Reserved Instances** | Up to 72% discount with 1- or 3-year commitment; consistent baseline. |
| **Unpredictable, spiky workload** | **EC2 On-Demand + Auto Scaling** | Pay per hour; auto-scale to handle spikes. |
| **Fault-tolerant, time-flexible batch** | **EC2 Spot Instances** | Up to 90% discount; can be interrupted with 2-min notice. |
| **Short-lived, event-driven tasks (< 15 min)** | **AWS Lambda** | Pay per millisecond; no server management. |
| **Docker containers, AWS-native** | **ECS with Fargate** | Serverless containers; no cluster management. |
| **Kubernetes workloads, portable** | **EKS with Fargate** | Managed Kubernetes; CNCF-compliant. |
| **Quick deployment of standard web apps** | **Elastic Beanstalk** | PaaS — upload code, AWS handles infra. |
| **Simple VPS / small website / learning** | **Lightsail** | Predictable, low cost, beginner-friendly. |
| **HPC / ML training / large batch jobs** | **AWS Batch** | Dynamic provisioning, manages dependencies. |
| **Latency-sensitive 5G / edge apps** | **Wavelength / Local Zones** | Compute close to the user. |
| **On-premises + cloud hybrid** | **AWS Outposts** | Real AWS hardware in your data center. |

---

## 3.2 Step-by-Step: EC2 Cost Calculation

**Problem**: An application runs on **2 `m5.large` On-Demand EC2 instances** continuously for **30 days** in the `us-east-1` region. The on-demand hourly rate for `m5.large` is **$0.096/hour**. Calculate the total monthly cost.

### Step 1: Identify the billing unit
EC2 On-Demand is billed **per instance-hour**.

### Step 2: Compute the number of instance-hours per month
A month is approximately:
$$\text{Hours per month} = 24 \times 30 = 720 \text{ hours}$$

### Step 3: Compute the per-instance cost
$$\text{Cost per instance} = 720 \text{ hours} \times \$0.096/\text{hour}$$
$$\text{Cost per instance} = \$69.12$$

### Step 4: Multiply by the number of instances
$$\text{Total Cost} = 2 \times \$69.12 = \$138.24$$

### Final Answer:
$$\boxed{\text{Total Monthly EC2 Cost} = \$138.24}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Alternative precise calculation**: A month is more accurately **730 hours** (365.25 days × 24 ÷ 12). Using 730 hours: $2 \times 730 \times 0.096 = \$140.16$. AWS bills by **seconds** since October 2017, with a minimum of 60 seconds.

---

## 3.3 Step-by-Step: Lambda Cost Calculation

**Problem**: A Lambda function is configured with **512 MB of memory** and runs **3 million times per month**. Each invocation averages **800 ms**. The pricing is **$0.20 per 1 million requests** and **$0.0000166667 per GB-second** (as per AWS on-demand pricing, us-east-1).

### Step 1: Convert memory to GB
$$M = 512 \text{ MB} = \frac{512}{1024} = 0.5 \text{ GB}$$

### Step 2: Compute the duration in seconds
$$t_s = 800 \text{ ms} = 0.8 \text{ seconds}$$

### Step 3: Compute compute (GB-seconds)
$$\text{GB-s total} = N \times M_{GB} \times t_s$$
$$\text{GB-s total} = 3{,}000{,}000 \times 0.5 \times 0.8$$
$$\text{GB-s total} = 1{,}200{,}000 \text{ GB-s}$$

### Step 4: Compute the request cost
$$C_{req} = \frac{3{,}000{,}000}{1{,}000{,}000} \times \$0.20 = 3 \times \$0.20 = \$0.60$$

### Step 5: Compute the compute cost
$$C_{comp} = \frac{1{,}200{,}000}{1{,}000{,}000} \times \$0.0000166667 = 1.2 \times \$0.0000166667$$
$$C_{comp} = \$0.02$$

### Step 6: Sum the two costs
$$\text{Total Lambda Cost} = \$0.60 + \$0.02 = \$0.62$$

### Final Answer:
$$\boxed{\text{Total Lambda Cost} = \$0.62 \text{ per month}}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Hint**: Always convert **MB → GB** (divide by 1024), **ms → s** (divide by 1000), and remember that Lambda's free tier covers the **first** 1M requests and 400,000 GB-s/month.

---

## 3.4 Step-by-Step: Reserved Instance vs. On-Demand Savings Analysis

**Problem**: Compare a **3-year Standard Reserved Instance** (no upfront) for `m5.large` at **$0.06/hour** vs. **On-Demand** at **$0.096/hour**, running 24/7 for 3 years.

### Step 1: On-Demand 3-year cost
$$\text{Hours}_{3y} = 24 \times 365 \times 3 = 26{,}280 \text{ hours}$$
$$C_{OD} = 26{,}280 \times 0.096 = \$2{,}522.88$$

### Step 2: Reserved 3-year cost
$$C_{RI} = 26{,}280 \times 0.06 = \$1{,}576.80$$

### Step 3: Compute savings
$$\text{Savings} = C_{OD} - C_{RI} = 2522.88 - 1576.80 = \$946.08$$
$$\text{Discount \%} = \frac{946.08}{2522.88} \times 100 \approx 37.5\%$$

### Final Answer:
$$\boxed{\text{3-year Savings} = \$946.08 \text{ (≈ 37.5% discount)}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This **37.5%** is illustrative. With **All Upfront 3-year RIs**, discounts can reach up to **72%** for specific instance families.

---

## 3.5 Step-by-Step: Auto Scaling Trigger Logic

**Problem**: A web app runs on EC2 with a **target group** behind an **Application Load Balancer**. Define the Auto Scaling policy that adjusts capacity based on average CPU utilization.

### Step 1: Define thresholds
- **Target tracking**: Maintain **average CPU at 60%**.
- **Scale-out**: If CPU > 60% for **3 consecutive periods of 60 seconds** → add 2 instances.
- **Scale-in**: If CPU < 60% for **15 consecutive periods of 60 seconds** → remove 1 instance.
- **Min capacity**: 2 instances.
- **Max capacity**: 10 instances.

### Step 2: Mathematical model
$$\text{Desired Capacity} = \text{ceil}\left(\frac{\text{Current CPU} \times \text{Current Capacity}}{60}\right)$$
$$\text{Desired Capacity} = \text{clamp}(\text{Desired}, \text{Min}=2, \text{Max}=10)$$

### Step 3: Cool-down
After a scaling activity, wait **300 seconds** (default cooldown) before further scaling.

> [!TIP]
> **Cooldown** prevents "flapping" — repeated rapid scale-out / scale-in cycles due to short-term metric fluctuations.

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 AWS Global Infrastructure Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R[Region e.g., us-east-1] --> A1[Availability Zone A]
    R --> A2[Availability Zone B]
    R --> A3[Availability Zone C]
    A1 --> D1[Data Center 1]
    A1 --> D2[Data Center 2]
    A2 --> D3[Data Center 3]
    A2 --> D4[Data Center 4]
    A3 --> D5[Data Center 5]
    A3 --> D6[Data Center 6]
    R --> E1[Edge Location]
    R --> E2[Local Zone]
    R --> E3[Wavelength Zone]
    R --> O[Outposts]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram**: A **Region** is a separate geographic area (e.g., N. Virginia, Mumbai, Frankfurt). Each Region contains **2 or more Availability Zones (AZs)**. AZs are physically separate data centers with independent power, networking, and connectivity. **Edge Locations** are used by CloudFront (CDN); **Local Zones** place compute close to users; **Wavelength** embeds compute in 5G networks; **Outposts** bring AWS hardware on-premises.

---

## 4.2 AWS Computing Services — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Compute[AWS Computing Services]
        EC2[Amazon EC2 IaaS Virtual Servers]
        LAMB[AWS Lambda FaaS Serverless Functions]
        ECS[Amazon ECS Container Orchestration]
        EKS[Amazon EKS Managed Kubernetes]
        FG[AWS Fargate Serverless Containers]
        EB[Elastic Beanstalk PaaS Web Apps]
        LS[Amazon Lightsail Simplified VPS]
        BAT[AWS Batch Managed Batch]
        ASG[EC2 Auto Scaling Elastic Capacity]
    end

    EC2 --> ASG
    ECS --> FG
    EKS --> FG
    EB --> EC2
    EB --> ASG
    BAT --> EC2
    LS --> EC2
    ASG --> ELB[Elastic Load Balancer]
    EC2 --> ELB
    EB --> ELB
    ELB --> USR[End User Request]
    LAMB --> APIGW[API Gateway / Event Sources]
    APIGW --> USR
```

> [!TIP]
> **Interpretation**: The block diagram maps the **inter-relationships** of computing services. Note how **Fargate** plugs into both **ECS** and **EKS**, how **Elastic Beanstalk** wraps **EC2 + Auto Scaling + ELB** into a single PaaS experience, and how **AWS Batch** leverages EC2 (often Spot) for large-scale job execution.

---

## 4.3 EC2 Instance Lifecycle

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Pending
    Pending --> Running
    Running --> Stopping
    Stopping --> Stopped
    Stopped --> Running
    Running --> Rebooting
    Rebooting --> Running
    Running --> Terminating
    Terminating --> Terminated
    Terminated --> [*]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Lifecycle Insight**: When an EC2 instance is **stopped**, the underlying physical host is released back to AWS, and you stop paying for instance-hours (but you continue to pay for attached **EBS volumes**). When **terminated**, by default the **root EBS volume is deleted**, and the data is permanently lost unless snapshots were taken.

---

## 4.4 Lambda Execution Model

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Src as Event Source
    participant L as Lambda Service
    participant C as Worker Container
    Src->>L: Trigger Event
    L->>L: Allocate Execution Context
    L->>C: Invoke Function Code
    C->>L: Return Result / Error
    L->>Src: Acknowledge / Forward Result
    Note over L,C: Cold start occurs on FIRST invocation<br/>or after long idle periods
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cold Start**: The first time a Lambda function runs (or after it has been idle), AWS must initialize a new execution environment — this introduces **latency** (typically 100 ms – several seconds depending on runtime and package size). Subsequent invocations are **warm** and much faster.

---

## 4.5 EC2 + Auto Scaling + ELB — High Availability Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    U[User] --> R53[Route 53 DNS]
    R53 --> ALB[Application Load Balancer]
    ALB --> AZ1[AZ 1 EC2 Instances]
    ALB --> AZ2[AZ 2 EC2 Instances]
    ALB --> AZ3[AZ 3 EC2 Instances]
    ASG[Auto Scaling Group] -. Scales .-> AZ1
    ASG -. Scales .-> AZ2
    ASG -. Scales .-> AZ3
    CW[CloudWatch Metrics] --> ASG
    CW --> ALB
```

> [!TIP]
> **HA Pattern**: This is the **canonical KTU question answer** for *"Design a highly available and fault-tolerant web application on AWS"*. The flow: DNS (Route 53) → Load Balancer (ALB) → Multiple EC2 instances distributed across multiple AZs, with capacity managed by Auto Scaling based on CloudWatch metrics.

---
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**Define Amazon EC2 and list any four of its pricing models.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):**
Amazon EC2 (Elastic Compute Cloud) is a web service that provides **resizable, secure compute capacity in the cloud**. It is designed to make web-scale cloud computing easier by providing virtual servers (instances) on demand. It is the core **IaaS** offering of AWS.

**Four Pricing Models (2 marks — 0.5 each):**

1. **On-Demand Instances** — Pay for compute capacity by the hour/second with no long-term commitments. Suitable for short-term, irregular workloads.
2. **Reserved Instances (RI)** — Up to 72% discount in exchange for a 1-year or 3-year commitment. Suitable for steady-state workloads.
3. **Spot Instances** — Bid for unused EC2 capacity at up to 90% discount; can be interrupted by AWS with 2 minutes' notice. Suitable for fault-tolerant, flexible workloads.
4. **Savings Plans** — Flexible pricing model offering up to 66% discount in exchange for a 1- or 3-year hourly spend commitment across any EC2 instance family and region.

*(Optional 5th: Dedicated Hosts — physical EC2 servers dedicated to your use, useful for compliance and BYOL.)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**What is AWS Lambda? Explain the term "serverless" in this context.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):**
**AWS Lambda** is a **Function-as-a-Service (FaaS)** compute service that lets you run code in response to events **without provisioning or managing servers**. You upload your code packaged as a "Lambda function," define triggers, and AWS automatically allocates compute resources, runs the code, and scales the application in response to demand.

**"Serverless" Explanation (1 mark):**
"Serverless" does **not** mean there are no servers. It means that the **cloud provider (AWS) abstracts away all server management** — patching, scaling, capacity planning, fault tolerance. The user focuses only on the **code and the event triggers**, and is billed only for the **compute time consumed (in milliseconds)**.

---

## 📝 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> Common mistakes students make on AWS Computing Services questions:
