> 1. **Confusing EC2 with Lambda** — Students often forget that Lambda has a **15-minute maximum execution time** and is for **event-driven, stateless** workloads, not long-running services.
> 2. **Confusing ECS with EKS** — ECS is AWS-native, while EKS runs **Kubernetes** (the same as on any cloud or on-prem). Examiners expect precise terminology.
> 3. **Forgetting to mention AMI, Security Groups, and Key Pairs** in EC2 questions — these are mandatory concepts for any EC2-based question.
> 4. **Mis-stating the pricing formula** — Always show: **Cost = (Number of Requests × Request Price) + (GB-seconds × Compute Price)** for Lambda.
> 5. **Ignoring Region and AZ** in HA design questions — the answer MUST include **multi-AZ deployment**.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

**(a) Explain the architecture of Amazon EC2 in detail. Discuss any two EC2 instance families and their suitable use cases. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**EC2 Architecture (4 marks):**
Amazon EC2 architecture consists of the following components:

1. **Amazon Machine Image (AMI)**: A pre-configured template that contains the OS, application server, and applications required to launch an instance. AMIs can be AWS-provided, marketplace, or custom.
2. **Instance Type**: Defines the hardware (vCPU, memory, storage, network). Selected from families like `m`, `c`, `r`, `t`, etc.
3. **Instance**: The virtual server that is launched from the AMI.
4. **EBS Volume**: Persistent block storage attached to the instance. It survives instance stop/restart.
5. **Security Group**: Acts as a virtual firewall — controls inbound/outbound traffic at the **instance level**.
6. **Key Pair**: Used for secure SSH (Linux) or RDP (Windows) login.
7. **Elastic Network Interface (ENI)**: A virtual network card that can include private IPs, public IPs, elastic IPs, and security groups.
8. **Elastic Load Balancer (ELB)**: Distributes incoming traffic across multiple EC2 instances.
9. **Auto Scaling Group (ASG)**: Automatically adjusts the number of EC2 instances based on demand and health checks.

**Two Instance Families (3 marks):**

- **General Purpose Family — `m5` (e.g., `m5.large`)**: Balanced CPU-to-memory ratio. Best suited for **web servers, small-to-medium databases, application servers, and enterprise applications** that need consistent, baseline compute performance.
- **Burstable Performance Family — `t3` (e.g., `t3.micro`)**: Provides a baseline CPU performance with the ability to **burst** above the baseline using **CPU credits**. Suitable for **low-traffic websites, development and test environments, small databases, and microservices** that do not need sustained high CPU.

---

**(b) With a neat diagram, explain the working of AWS Lambda. List its key benefits and limitations. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Working of AWS Lambda (4 marks):**
1. The user uploads **code** (a function) packaged as a zip/jar or container image, and configures an **event source trigger** (e.g., S3 upload, API Gateway request, DynamoDB stream).
2. When the **event occurs**, the Lambda service **automatically provisions a runtime environment** (a microVM called Firecracker), loads the code, and executes it.
3. Lambda runs the function in a **stateless, ephemeral environment** with allocated memory and CPU proportional to memory.
4. The function **processes the event** and returns a result (or side effects like writing to DynamoDB, sending to SNS).
5. After execution, the environment is **kept warm** for a short period (to avoid cold starts for subsequent invocations) and then torn down.
6. **Logging and metrics** are sent automatically to **CloudWatch Logs and CloudWatch Metrics**.

**Diagram (built into the answer narrative — see Section 4.4 above).**

**Benefits (1.5 marks):**
- **No server management** — AWS handles all infrastructure.
- **Pay-per-millisecond billing** — highly cost-efficient for sporadic workloads.
- **Automatic scaling** — handles thousands of concurrent invocations seamlessly.
- **Built-in fault tolerance** — Lambda is intrinsically highly available.
- **Multi-language support** — Node.js, Python, Java, Go, .NET, Ruby, and custom runtimes.

**Limitations (1.5 marks):**
- **15-minute maximum execution time** — not suitable for long-running processes.
- **Cold start latency** — first invocation can be slow (100 ms – several seconds).
- **Stateless** — no persistent local storage between invocations (only ephemeral `/tmp`).
- **Limited package size** — 50 MB zipped, 250 MB unzipped (or 10 GB via container image).
- **Concurrency limits** — default 1,000 concurrent executions per region.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a) Differentiate between EC2, ECS, EKS, and Fargate. When would you choose one over the other? (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Comparison Table (5 marks):**

| Feature | **EC2** | **ECS** | **EKS** | **Fargate** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Service Model** | IaaS (VMs) | Container orchestration | Managed Kubernetes | Serverless containers |
| **Abstraction Level** | Lowest (you manage OS) | Medium | Medium (with K8s API) | Highest (no infra) |
| **Manages** | Hardware → OS | Containers on a cluster | K8s control plane | Containers only |
| **Use Case** | General VMs | AWS-native container apps | Portable K8s workloads | Containers without servers |
| **Pricing** | Per instance-hour | ECS itself is free; you pay for underlying instances | EKS control plane: $0.10/hour + instance cost | Per vCPU + GB-hour consumed |
| **Kubernetes** | No | No | Yes | Optional (with EKS) |
| **Startup Time** | Minutes | ~1 minute (with AMI) | ~1–2 minutes | ~30 seconds |
| **Best For** | Custom, legacy apps | AWS-centric container users | Multi-cloud / K8s portability | Bursty, variable workloads |

**When to Choose (2 marks):**
- **EC2** → When you need full control over the OS, runtime, and customization (legacy apps, custom kernels, BYOL).
- **ECS** → When you are deeply embedded in AWS and want simple, native container orchestration without the complexity of Kubernetes.
- **EKS** → When you need **Kubernetes portability** (same tooling across on-prem and cloud) or are migrating existing K8s workloads.
- **Fargate** → When you want to **run containers without managing servers** — ideal for variable, spiky workloads where capacity planning is hard.

---

**(b) Explain the Auto Scaling mechanism in AWS EC2. Compute the monthly cost of running 2 `m5.large` instances for a full month (730 hours) at $0.096/hour. Compare it with a 1-year No-Upfront Reserved Instance at $0.065/hour. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Auto Scaling Explanation (4 marks):**
**Amazon EC2 Auto Scaling** automatically adjusts the number of EC2 instances in a group based on demand or a defined schedule. It consists of three components:

1. **Auto Scaling Group (ASG)**: A logical collection of EC2 instances that share similar characteristics and are treated as a logical grouping for scaling and management.
2. **Launch Configuration / Launch Template**: Specifies the **AMI, instance type, key pair, security groups, and other parameters** used to launch new instances.
3. **Scaling Policies**:
   - **Target Tracking** (e.g., maintain 60% average CPU)
   - **Step Scaling** (e.g., add 2 instances if CPU > 70%)
   - **Simple Scaling** (e.g., add 1 instance if CPU > 80%)
   - **Scheduled Scaling** (e.g., scale up at 9 AM daily)

**Mechanism**:
- **CloudWatch** monitors the metric (e.g., CPU utilization).
- When a threshold is breached for a defined duration, the **scaling policy** triggers.
- ASG **launches or terminates** instances to match the desired capacity.
- The desired capacity is **clamped** between the configured **Min** and **Max** values.

**Cost Calculation (3 marks):**

*Step 1: On-Demand 1-month cost (730 hours, 2 instances):*
$$C_{OD} = 2 \times 730 \times 0.096$$
$$C_{OD} = 1460 \times 0.096 = \$140.16$$

*Step 2: Reserved 1-year cost for 1 month:*
$$C_{RI} = 2 \times 730 \times 0.065$$
$$C_{RI} = 1460 \times 0.065 = \$94.90$$

*Step 3: Monthly savings:*
$$\text{Savings} = 140.16 - 94.90 = \$45.26$$
$$\text{Discount \%} = \frac{45.26}{140.16} \times 100 \approx 32.3\%$$

**Final Comparison:**

| Pricing Model | Monthly Cost (2 instances) |
|---|---|
| On-Demand | **$140.16** |
| 1-Year Reserved (No Upfront) | **$94.90** |
| **Savings** | **$45.26 (≈ 32.3% off)** |

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] **AWS = IaaS + PaaS + SaaS + FaaS** — the broadest cloud platform.
- [x] **EC2 = Virtual servers (IaaS)** — you manage the OS, AWS manages hardware.
- [x] **EC2 Pricing Models** — On-Demand, Reserved, Spot, Savings Plans, Dedicated Hosts.
- [x] **Instance families** — `m` (general), `c` (compute), `r` (memory), `t` (burstable), `g/p` (GPU/ML).
- [x] **Lambda = FaaS (serverless)** — pay per ms, max 15 min execution, stateless.
- [x] **Lambda formula** — Cost = (Requests × Request Price) + (GB-s × Compute Price).
- [x] **ECS vs EKS** — ECS is AWS-native Docker orchestration; EKS is managed Kubernetes.
- [x] **Fargate** = serverless containers; no EC2 instances to manage.
- [x] **Elastic Beanstalk** = PaaS — upload code, AWS handles the rest.
- [x] **Lightsail** = simple, low-cost VPS for beginners/small projects.
- [x] **AWS Batch** = managed batch job scheduling and compute provisioning.
- [x] **Auto Scaling** = horizontal scaling using Min / Desired / Max capacity.
- [x] **HA Architecture** = multi-AZ deployment + ELB + ASG + CloudWatch monitoring.
- [x] **Region → AZs → Data Centers** — fundamental AWS infrastructure hierarchy.
- [x] **Free Tier** — 750 hrs/month of `t2/t3.micro` (EC2); 1M Lambda requests + 400K GB-s (Lambda).
- [x] **SLAs** — EC2 multi-AZ = 99.99%; Lambda = 99.95%.
- [x] **AMI** = template; **Security Group** = firewall; **Key Pair** = authentication.
- [x] **Spot Instances** = up to 90% discount but interruptible; suitable for fault-tolerant workloads.
- [x] **Outposts** = AWS hardware in your data center; **Wavelength** = 5G edge compute.

> [!TIP]
> **Final Exam Mantra**: Always remember — **EC2 is the workhorse, Lambda is the sniper, ECS/EKS are the conductors, Fargate is the invisible hand, and Elastic Beanstalk is the easy button.** Know their boundaries, pricing, and ideal use cases — these are the three pillars of any KTU question on AWS Computing Services.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Amazon Machine Images
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT525 - CLOUD COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Amazon Machine Images

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Module 4 — Amazon Machine Images (AMI)

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Amazon Machine Image (AMI)** is a *read-only, pre-configured, templated image* provided by Amazon Web Services (AWS) that contains the **operating system**, **application server**, **applications**, and **configuration settings** required to boot an Amazon EC2 (Elastic Compute Cloud) instance. An AMI is, in essence, a **blueprint** of a virtual server.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme parlance aligned to Outcome-Based Education (OBE), the AMI is the **root-volume template** combined with **launch permissions** and **block-device mappings** that AWS uses to instantiate a virtual machine (EC2 instance) in a chosen Region and Availability Zone.

| Component | Mandatory? | Purpose |
| :--- | :---: | :--- |
| Root Volume Template | Yes (1 per AMI) | Holds the OS, boot loader, and pre-installed software |
| Launch Permissions | Yes | Defines *which AWS accounts* may use this AMI |
| Block Device Mapping | Yes | Maps EBS volumes / instance-store volumes to the instance |
| Tags & Metadata | Optional | Used for billing, identification, and automation |

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The Bakery Mould" 🍞**
> Imagine a bakery where every cake is baked from the **same aluminium mould**. The mould itself is not the cake; it only *defines* the shape, size, and base ingredients. Each time the baker pours batter into the mould and bakes it, a *new* cake is produced — identical in form, but freshly prepared from the shared template.
>
> In this analogy:
> - **AMI** = the *mould* (the read-only template).
> - **EC2 Instance** = the *cake* (a running copy derived from the mould).
> - **Region/Availability Zone** = the *bakery kitchen* where the cake is produced.
> - **Launching an instance** = the act of *baking* a cake from the mould.
>
> Just as one mould can produce thousands of cakes, a single AMI can spawn hundreds of identical EC2 instances, scaled horizontally based on demand.

### 1.3 Key Engineering Metrics (Bolded for Quick Recall)

- **Root Volume Type**: Either **EBS-backed** (recommended) or **Instance Store-backed** (legacy, non-persistent).
- **Virtualization Type**: **HVM (Hardware Virtual Machine)** — required for current-generation instance families, or **PV (Paravirtual)** — legacy.
- **Boot Mode**: **UEFI** (modern) or **Legacy BIOS**.
- **Default Storage Cost of an EBS-backed AMI**: Billed per **GB-month** of *associated snapshots* in the Region.
- **Public AMI Limit per Region**: **100 000** AMIs (soft limit, can be raised via AWS Support).

> [!TIP]
> For KTU examinations, the two-horse race in conceptual questions is almost always **HVM vs PV** and **EBS-backed vs Instance Store-backed**. Memorise the table differences.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Sheet

## 2.1 Architectural Anatomy of an AMI

An AMI is *not* a single binary file. It is a **logical container** that references one or more Amazon **EBS Snapshots** (for EBS-backed AMIs) or **S3-stored images** (for instance-store-backed AMIs). The internal structure is best understood as three orthogonal layers:

### Layer 1 — The Template Layer (Root Volume Snapshot)
- A point-in-time, **block-level snapshot** of the root EBS volume.
- Snapshots are **incremental** — only the blocks that changed since the previous snapshot are stored.
- The snapshot is stored in Amazon S3, but the user interacts with it through the EBS API.

### Layer 2 — The Permissions Layer
- Defined by an **Launch Permission** matrix.
- Default: *private* to the owning AWS account.
- Can be modified to be:
  - **Public** (any AWS account in the Region).
  - **Explicit** (specific AWS account IDs).
  - **Shared via AWS Marketplace** (paid AMIs).

### Layer 3 — The Block-Device Mapping Layer
- Specifies *which* EBS volumes (or instance-store volumes) are attached upon launch.
- Defines the **device name** (e.g., `/dev/sda1`, `/dev/xvda`).
- Allows overrides: instance launch can specify *different* EBS volume types, IOPS, and sizes than what the AMI defines.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Insight**: When you launch an instance from an AMI, the **block-device mapping from the AMI is used as a default**, but you can override volume size, type, and IOPS at launch time. This is exam-favourite territory.

## 2.2 The Four Families of AMIs

| # | AMI Family | Source | Cost to Use | Typical Use Case in KTU Labs |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **Quick Start AMIs** (Amazon-provided) | AWS itself | Free (only pay for the instance) | Linux/Windows base servers |
| 2 | **AWS Marketplace AMIs** | Third-party ISVs | Pay per hour + software licence | LAMP, WordPress, MSSQL, ML stacks |
| 3 | **Community AMIs** | Other AWS users | Free (but verify trust) | Learning & lab experiments |
| 4 | **My AMIs** (Self-created) | Your own AWS account | Free (pay only for underlying snapshots) | Golden images for production |

> [!WARNING]
> **Never launch a production workload from a Community AMI** without verifying its provenance. The KTU examiner may ask why — answer: *unverified Community AMIs may contain malware, unpatched kernels, or back-doors*.

## 2.3 Virtualization Type — HVM vs PV (High-Yield Comparison)

| Property | **HVM (Hardware Virtual Machine)** | **PV (Paravirtualization)** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Boot Mechanism | Boots via **master/record** in MBR/GPT | Boots via a **PV-GRUB** loader |
| Hardware Access | Uses **hardware-assisted virtualization** (Intel VT-x / AMD-V) | Uses **hypervisor-aware** drivers |
| Supported Instance Types | All current-gen (M5, C5, R5, T3 …) | Only some older instance families |
| Networking & Storage Performance | **Enhanced Networking** + **EBS-optimised** | Limited |
| Default in 2024 Scheme | **Yes** | **No — deprecated for new launches** |
| Root Volume Type | **EBS** preferred | Instance Store (historically) |

> [!NOTE]
> For all KTU 2024 practical sessions, always select **HVM + EBS-backed**. PV is retained in the syllabus for **conceptual completeness** only.

## 2.4 Boot Mode — UEFI vs Legacy BIOS

| Property | **UEFI** | **Legacy BIOS** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Partition Table | **GPT** | **MBR** |
| Max Disk Size | Practically unlimited | 2 TiB |
| Secure Boot | Supported | Not supported |
| Nitro Enclaves | Required (uses UEFI) | Not supported |
| Default | Modern AMIs | Legacy AMIs |

## 2.5 The AMI Lifecycle (State Machine)

| State | Transition Action | What Happens Internally |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Pending** | *RegisterImage* API call | Snapshot is being created / linked |
| **Available** | Action completes | AMI is launchable |
| **Deregistered** | *DeregisterImage* API call | AMI ID becomes invalid, *snapshots remain* unless deleted |
| **Deleted** | *DeleteSnapshot* on underlying snapshot | Storage space reclaimed; AMI is now non-launchable |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Deregistering an AMI does NOT delete the underlying EBS snapshot.** Students often lose marks by confusing these two operations. The snapshot must be explicitly deleted to stop storage billing.

## 2.6 Why AMIs Matter in Engineering — Real-World Utility

1. **Standardisation**: Enterprises ship a single, hardened, patched "golden AMI" that meets CIS benchmarks. Every EC2 instance spawned from it is identical and compliant.
2. **Auto Scaling**: AWS Auto Scaling Groups reference an AMI in the launch template. Horizontal scale-out is *only* possible because identical images are pre-baked.
3. **Disaster Recovery**: AMIs can be **copied across Regions** to enable cross-region disaster recovery in minutes.
4. **DevOps CI/CD**: A CodePipeline build stage can call `CreateImage` to bake a fresh AMI with every successful build, then deploy via Blue/Green.
5. **Cost Optimisation**: A custom AMI with pre-installed dependencies reduces **instance launch time** (no apt-get/yum install on first boot).

## 2.7 The KTU Formula / Cost Sheet (For Numerical Sub-Questions)

> [!NOTE]
> AMI is fundamentally a *conceptual* topic, but KTU may ask for **cost computation** sub-parts. Master this formula table.

| Symbol | Quantity | Unit | Formula |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $C_{AMI}$ | Total monthly cost of an AMI | USD/month | $C_{AMI} = S_{GB} \times P_{GB-month} \times N_{snap}$ |
| $S_{GB}$ | Sum of unique data blocks in all snapshots | GB | $S_{GB} = \sum_{i=1}^{N_{snap}} \text{size}(i)$ |
| $P_{GB-month}$ | AWS EBS Snapshot price in the Region | USD / GB-month | Region-dependent (e.g., **0.05** for US-East-1) |
| $N_{snap}$ | Number of snapshots backing the AMI | dimensionless | Integer $\geq 1$ |
| $C_{inst}$ | Hourly instance cost launched from AMI | USD/hour | $C_{inst} = P_{inst} \times T_{hours}$ |

> Replace the pipe symbol with `\vert` for any future absolute-value expressions to keep markdown tables valid.

## 2.8 Engineering Utility Summary

The AMI is the *unit of reproducibility* in cloud computing. Without it, cloud servers would be cattle-less ranches — impossible to clone, hard to audit, and impossible to scale. The AMI is, therefore, the **pivotal abstraction** that turns cloud infrastructure into a programmable, idempotent, version-controlled artefact.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Because AMI is a *systems* concept rather than a *pure-mathematical* one, "derivations" here take the form of **exhaustive step-by-step procedures** for the most exam-relevant workflows: (a) Creating an AMI from a running instance, (b) Copying an AMI across Regions, and (c) Computing monthly storage cost.

## 3.1 Procedure A — Creating a Custom AMI from a Running EC2 Instance

**Scenario**: An EC2 instance `i-0a1b2c3d4e5f00001` runs Ubuntu 22.04 with Apache, MySQL, and a custom web application pre-installed. The engineer wishes to capture this state as a reusable golden image.

**Step 1 — Sanitise the Instance**
- Remove machine-specific data: SSH host keys, `/etc/machine-id`, log files, and any cached credentials.
- Run, on the instance:
  ```bash
  sudo waagent -deprovision+user -force    # Azure-style not used here
  sudo cloud-init clean                    # Removes instance-specific metadata
  ```
- *Why?* Because launching a *new* instance from the AMI must not inherit the old instance's identity — otherwise networking and security policies may misbehave.

**Step 2 — Stop the Instance (Optional but Recommended)**
- Guarantees data-consistent snapshot of the root EBS volume.
- *If the instance cannot be stopped* (production), use **No-Reboot** option, accepting a small risk of file-system inconsistency.

**Step 3 — Invoke the `CreateImage` API**
- AWS Console path: **EC2 Dashboard → Instances → select instance → Actions → Image and templates → Create image**.
- CLI form:
  ```bash
  aws ec2 create-image \
      --instance-id i-0a1b2c3d4e5f00001 \
      --name "MyGoldenAMI-v1.0" \
      --description "Ubuntu 22.04 + Apache + MySQL Golden Image" \
      --no-reboot
  ```
- *Why `--no-reboot`?* Allows live capture with zero downtime, but instance *must* be using an **EBS root volume** (not instance store).

**Step 4 — Capture the Returned AMI ID**
- The API returns an `ImageId` of the form `ami-0a1b2c3d4e5f00007`.
- *Important*: The AMI is in state `pending` until all underlying snapshots are created. State transitions to `available` typically within **5–15 minutes** depending on root volume size.

**Step 5 — Tag the AMI (Best Practice)**
- Tags propagate to instances launched from the AMI.
- Suggested tags: `Name`, `Version`, `Owner`, `Environment`, `PatchDate`, `ComplianceBaseline`.

**Step 6 — Verify the AMI**
- In the console, navigate to **AMIs → Select image → Launch** with a small `t3.micro` test instance.
- Validate boot, application launch, and SSH access.
- Once validated, mark the AMI as a *production* artefact.

**Step 7 — Version the AMI**
- Apply a semantic-version tag, e.g., `GoldenAMI-v1.4.2-20240615`.
- Store older versions in a "退役" (decommissioned) state, kept for **rollback** purposes for a defined retention window (commonly **30, 60, or 90 days**).

---

## 3.2 Procedure B — Copying an AMI to a Different Region

**Why?** Regulatory compliance (data-residency), latency reduction for distant users, disaster recovery, and global product launches.

**Step 1 — Initiate the Copy Operation**
- Console: **AMIs → Select image → Actions → Copy AMI**.
- CLI:
  ```bash
  aws ec2 copy-image \
      --source-region ap-south-1 \
      --source-image-id ami-0a1b2c3d4e5f00007 \
      --region us-east-1 \
      --name "MyGoldenAMI-v1.0-us-east-1" \
      --description "Copy of golden AMI in us-east-1"
  ```

**Step 2 — Wait for the Copy to Complete**
- The console shows state `pending` → `available`.
- Cross-Region copy of a 50 GB EBS root snapshot may take **15–60 minutes** depending on inter-region bandwidth.

**Step 3 — Update KMS Encryption Keys (If Applicable)**
- The copy operation creates *new* EBS snapshots in the destination region.
- These new snapshots must be encrypted with a **KMS key valid in the destination region**.
- The launch permission is *not* automatically copied; you must re-share.

**Step 4 — Update Launch Templates / Auto Scaling Groups**
- In the destination region, point the launch template to the new AMI ID.
- Deploy via a Blue/Green Auto Scaling Group transition.

**Step 5 — Clean Up Source (Optional)**
- If the original region is no longer needed, deregister the source AMI and delete its snapshot to stop billing.

---

## 3.3 Procedure C — Computing Monthly Cost of an AMI's Storage

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU 2024 examination may include a 2-or-3-mark sub-question on cost computation. Here is the fully-worked, step-by-step model.

**Problem Statement**:
A custom AMI is backed by 4 incremental EBS snapshots of the root volume. The sizes of *unique* data blocks across the four snapshots are: **30 GB, 40 GB, 25 GB, 35 GB**. The standard EBS snapshot rate in the region is **0.05 USD per GB-month**. Compute the total monthly storage cost of this AMI.

**Step 1 — Identify the Formula**
$$
C_{AMI} = \sum_{i=1}^{N_{snap}} \text{size}_{i} \times P_{GB-month}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the Values**
$$
C_{AMI} = (30 + 40 + 25 + 35) \times 0.05
$$

**Step 3 — Sum the Storage**
$$
30 + 40 = 70
$$
$$
70 + 25 = 95
$$
$$
95 + 35 = 130
$$

**Step 4 — Multiply by the Unit Price**
$$
C_{AMI} = 130 \times 0.05
$$
$$
C_{AMI} = 6.50 \text{ USD/month}
$$

**Step 5 — Verify the Units**
$$
C_{AMI} \;[\text{USD/month}] = \text{GB} \times \frac{\text{USD}}{\text{GB-month}} = \text{USD/month} \;\; \checkmark
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Alert**: AWS bills **each snapshot independently** for the *unique* data it holds (incremental storage), not the cumulative volume size. A common mistake is to sum the *raw volume size* (e.g., 50 GB × 4 = 200 GB) and pay for unused data. The above problem assumes *unique* block sizes; if the problem states "each snapshot is 50 GB", then cost = 50 × 4 × 0.05 = **10.00 USD/month**.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — When to Use Which AMI Type

| Decision Criterion | **Use Amazon Quick Start** | **Use AWS Marketplace** | **Use Community AMI** | **Use My AMI (Self-Created)** |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Need bare OS only? | ✅ Best | ❌ Overkill | ⚠️ Risky | ❌ Wasted effort |
| Need licensed 3rd-party software? | ❌ | ✅ Best | ❌ | ⚠️ DIY |
| Production-grade hardening required? | ⚠️ Manual | ✅ Usually pre-hardened | ❌ | ✅ Best |
| Fastest time-to-launch? | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ❌ |
| Lowest cost? | ✅ Free (just compute) | ❌ Includes licence | ✅ Free | ✅ Free (after first build) |
| Trusted source? | ✅ Amazon | ✅ AWS-curated ISVs | ❌ Unknown | ✅ Yourself |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> The following Mermaid block renders a **Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow** of the AMI lifecycle — from creation to instance launch, including cross-region replication. All node IDs are alphanumeric and prefixed with letters to avoid keyword collisions.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1([User Initiates CreateImage]) --> A2[EC2 Instance Running]
    A2 --> A3{Select Stop or No-Reboot}
    A3 -- Stop --> A4[Cleanly Stop Instance]
    A3 -- No-Reboot --> A5[Live Snapshot of Root EBS]
    A4 --> A6[Snapshot Root Volume to S3 via EBS]
    A5 --> A6
    A6 --> A7[Generate AMI Metadata in Region]
    A7 --> A8([AMI State Available])
    A8 --> A9{Choose Next Action}
    A9 -- Launch Instance --> B1[Select AMI in Launch Wizard]
    B1 --> B2[Define Instance Type and Subnet]
    B2 --> B3[Provision EC2 Instance from AMI]
    B3 --> B4([Instance Running in Availability Zone])
    A9 -- Share --> C1[Modify Launch Permissions]
    C1 --> C2{Public or Specific Account}
    C2 -- Public --> C3[All AWS Accounts in Region]
    C2 -- Specific --> C4[Named AWS Account IDs]
    A9 -- Copy Region --> D1[Invoke CopyImage API]
    D1 --> D2[Replicate Snapshots to Target Region]
    D2 --> D3[Generate New AMI in Target Region]
    D3 --> D8([AMI Available in Target Region])
    A9 -- Deregister --> E1[Invoke DeregisterImage API]
    E1 --> E2[AMI Marked as Deregistered]
    E2 --> E3[Snapshots Remain Until Explicitly Deleted]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Reading the Diagram**: The flow is intentionally *fan-out* from the central "AMI Available" state. This is the most common KTU-asked question: *what are the operations possible on an available AMI?* The answer — **Launch**, **Share**, **Copy**, and **Deregister**.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    F1[Block Device Mapping] --> F4[EC2 Instance Boot]
    F2[Launch Permissions] --> F4
    F3[Root Volume Snapshot] --> F4
    F4 --> F5([Running VM in Cloud])
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram**: This is the *minimal* dependency map. An EC2 instance cannot boot unless **all three components** of the AMI are present. If a launch permission is missing, the instance cannot be launched by a non-permitted account. If the root volume snapshot is deleted, the AMI fails to launch.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Model Paper, Module 4]**  
> *Define Amazon Machine Image (AMI). List its three main components.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks)**:
An **Amazon Machine Image (AMI)** is a pre-configured, read-only template used to launch EC2 instances on AWS. It contains all the information required to boot a virtual server.

The **three main components** are:
1. **Root Volume Template** — a snapshot of the root EBS volume containing the OS and pre-installed software.
2. **Launch Permissions** — an access-control list specifying which AWS accounts can use the AMI.
3. **Block Device Mapping** — a list of EBS or instance-store volumes attached upon launch, with their device names, sizes, and types.

*(Examiner's Note: Award 1 mark for the definition and 2 marks for correctly listing the three components.)*

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Model Paper, Module 4]**  
> *Differentiate between HVM and PV virtualization types in the context of AMIs.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks)**:
| Aspect | **HVM** | **PV** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Hardware Virtualisation | Uses Intel VT-x / AMD-V | Uses para-virtual drivers |
| Boot Mechanism | MBR/GPT standard | Custom PV-GRUB |
| Current Relevance | Default for all current-gen instances | Legacy, no longer recommended |

*(Examiner's Note: Award 1 mark per correct row, 3 rows = 3 marks.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### Question A — 14 Marks
> **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, Module 4]**  
> *(a)* Explain the architecture of an Amazon Machine Image in detail, with a neat block diagram showing its three components. (7 Marks)  
> *(b)* Describe the procedure to create a custom AMI from a running EC2 instance and copy it to a different region. (7 Marks)

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks

The architecture of an AMI consists of three orthogonal layers:

**1. Root Volume Template (3 Marks)**
- A point-in-time block-level snapshot of the root EBS volume.
- Stored in Amazon S3, accessed via the EBS API.
- Contains OS kernel, boot loader, and pre-installed applications.

**2. Launch Permissions (2 Marks)**
- Default: *private* to the AWS account.
- Configurable: *public*, *explicit account list*, or *AWS Marketplace*.

**3. Block Device Mapping (2 Marks)**
- Defines which storage volumes are attached and at which device paths.
- Supports instance-launch overrides for size, type, and IOPS.

*(Include the Mermaid block diagram from Section 4 here for full marks.)*

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Sanitise the Instance (1 Mark)**
- Remove SSH host keys, `/etc/machine-id`, logs, cached credentials.
- Run `cloud-init clean` to drop instance-specific metadata.

**Step 2 — Stop the Instance (1 Mark)**
- Ensures consistent snapshot.
- Alternatively use `--no-reboot` flag with documented risk.

**Step 3 — Create the Image (2 Marks)**
- CLI: `aws ec2 create-image --instance-id <ID> --name "MyAMI"`
- Note the returned `ImageId`.

**Step 4 — Tag and Verify (1 Mark)**
- Apply tags such as `Name`, `Version`, `Owner`.
- Launch a small test instance to validate.

**Step 5 — Copy to a Different Region (2 Marks)**
- CLI: `aws ec2 copy-image --source-region <src> --source-image-id <ami> --region <dst>`
- Wait for state transition to `available`.
- Update launch templates in destination region; clean up source snapshot if required.

---

### Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)
> **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, Module 4]**  
> *(a)* Compare and contrast **EBS-backed** and **Instance Store-backed** AMIs. (7 Marks)  
> *(b)* A custom AMI is backed by **5 incremental EBS snapshots** with unique data block sizes of **40 GB, 30 GB, 20 GB, 25 GB, 35 GB**. The region charges **0.05 USD per GB-month** for EBS snapshots. Calculate the total monthly storage cost. If the snapshot rate is increased to **0.08 USD per GB-month**, what is the new cost? (7 Marks)

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks

| Property | **EBS-Backed** | **Instance Store-Backed** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Root Volume Device | EBS volume | Instance-store (ephemeral) |
| Boot Time | Faster | Slightly faster historically |
| Data Persistence | Survives instance stop | **Lost on stop/terminate** |
| Snapshotting | Native, easy | Must bundle, upload to S3 |
| Resizing Root Volume | Possible at launch | Not possible |
| Current Recommendation | ✅ Default | ❌ Legacy |
| Stop / Restart | Supported | Not supported (terminate only) |

*(Examiner's Note: 1 mark per correct row × 5 rows = 5 marks; 2 marks for the recommendation summary.)*

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Sum the Storage (1 Mark)**
$$
S_{total} = 40 + 30 + 20 + 25 + 35 = 150 \text{ GB}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the Old Rate (2 Marks)**
$$
C_{old} = 150 \times 0.05 = 7.50 \text{ USD/month}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the New Rate (2 Marks)**
$$
C_{new} = 150 \times 0.08 = 12.00 \text{ USD/month}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the Difference (1 Mark)**
$$
\Delta C = 12.00 - 7.50 = 4.50 \text{ USD/month}
$$

**Step 5 — Verify Units (1 Mark)**
$$
C \; [\text{USD/month}] = \text{GB} \times \frac{\text{USD}}{\text{GB-month}} \;\; \checkmark
$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Deregister ≠ Delete**: Students often claim that deregistering an AMI stops all billing. The *snapshot continues to bill*. Always mention this explicitly.
> 2. **HVM vs PV confusion**: Do not state that PV AMIs are "faster" — they are *legacy* and have *no* modern instance support.
> 3. **Cost formula mistake**: Some students multiply the *volume size* by the number of snapshots. The correct method is to use the *sum of unique blocks*.
> 4. **Region vs Availability Zone confusion**: An AMI is **regional**; an instance is **zonal**. You do not need to "copy an AMI to an AZ", only to a Region.
> 5. **Sharing vs Copying**: Sharing permissions does *not* transfer snapshots to another account; only the AMI's *metadata* is accessible. To move data, copy the snapshot.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Amazon Machine Images**

- ⭐ **AMI = template; Instance = running copy of that template.**
- ⭐ **Three components**: root volume snapshot, launch permissions, block device mapping.
- ⭐ **Four families**: Quick Start, Marketplace, Community, My AMIs.
- ⭐ **Virtualization**: **HVM is the default** in 2024; **PV is legacy and deprecated**.
- ⭐ **Root volume**: **EBS-backed is the default**; instance-store is ephemeral and historical.
- ⭐ **Boot mode**: **UEFI is the modern default**; Legacy BIOS only for old images.
- ⭐ **Lifecycle**: pending → available → (launch / share / copy / deregister).
- ⭐ **Deregistering an AMI does NOT delete its underlying snapshots** — manual cleanup required.
- ⭐ **AMIs are regional**; cross-region replication needs an explicit `CopyImage` call.
- ⭐ **Block device mappings can be overridden at launch** (size, IOPS, volume type).
- ⭐ **Cost formula**: $C_{AMI} = S_{GB} \times P_{GB-month}$, where $S_{GB}$ is the *sum of unique block sizes*.
- ⭐ **Production best practice**: maintain a "golden AMI" with semantic versioning, tags, and a defined retention window.
- ⭐ **Auto Scaling Groups** and **Launch Templates** both reference AMIs; this is the bridge between AMI as a static artefact and AMI as a dynamic deployment unit.
- ⭐ **KMS encryption** is *per-Region*; copying an encrypted AMI requires a valid KMS key in the destination.
- ⭐ **Boot mode + Nitro System** — Nitro Enclaves require UEFI boot mode.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Elastic Compute Cloud (EC2)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT525 - CLOUD COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Elastic Compute Cloud (EC2)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Elastic Compute Cloud (EC2)** is a web service provided by Amazon Web Services (AWS) that delivers **secure, resizable compute capacity in the cloud**. It is designed to make web-scale cloud computing easier by providing on-demand, pay-as-you-go access to virtualized computing resources known as **Instances**.

EC2 is one of the core foundational services of the AWS ecosystem. It allows developers, researchers, and enterprises to launch virtual servers (instances) within minutes, configure networking and security, and scale capacity up or down dynamically in response to workload fluctuations. The term **"Elastic"** refers to the ability to dynamically provision or release compute resources based on demand.

**Core Service Attributes (as defined by AWS):**
- **On-Demand Provisioning** — Instances can be launched within minutes.
- **Scalability (Vertical & Horizontal)** — Capacity can grow or shrink elastically.
- **Pay-as-You-Go Pricing** — Charged per second/minute of usage.
- **Multi-Region Availability** — Resources can be deployed across AWS Regions and Availability Zones (AZs).
- **Root-Level Access** — Users obtain administrative control over instances.
- **Integration** — Tightly integrated with EBS, S3, VPC, IAM, CloudWatch, and Auto Scaling.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** In the 2024 Scheme, EC2 is studied as the **primary IaaS (Infrastructure-as-a-Service) model** in cloud computing. Students must understand instance types, pricing models, AMIs, security groups, key pairs, and the instance lifecycle.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Think of EC2 like **renting an apartment instead of buying a house**.

| Real-World Analogy | EC2 Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Building the house from scratch | Setting up a physical server (racks, power, cooling) |
| Renting a furnished apartment | Launching an EC2 instance from a pre-built AMI |
| Studio, 1BHK, 2BHK, Penthouse | Instance types (t2.micro → c5.4xlarge) |
| Paying monthly rent | On-Demand / Reserved Instance billing |
| Last-minute discount rent (unused room) | Spot Instances |
| Lock on the apartment door | Security Groups + Key Pairs |
| Address of the apartment | Public/Elastic IP address |
| Furniture inside the apartment | Software, EBS volumes, applications |

> **Key Intuition:** Just like you wouldn't build a house for a 2-day trip, EC2 lets you rent compute only for the duration you need — and when you leave, you stop paying. The "elastic" part means the landlord (AWS) can instantly give you a bigger or smaller room when your guests (workload) increase or decrease.

## 1.3 The Three Foundational Pillars of EC2

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **1. Compute (Instances)** — Virtual machines with vCPU and RAM combinations.
> **2. Storage (EBS / Instance Store)** — Persistent and ephemeral block storage.
> **3. Networking (VPC, Subnets, Security Groups, IPs)** — Network isolation and access control.

Every EC2 deployment rests on these three pillars. A student must internalize that **EC2 itself is just the compute engine** — its real power emerges only when paired with EBS (storage), VPC (network), and IAM (security identity).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of an EC2 Instance

An EC2 instance is fundamentally a **virtual machine (VM)** running on a hypervisor hosted on physical AWS servers. The key components that define an instance are:

### 2.1.1 Amazon Machine Image (AMI)

The AMI is a **template containing the software configuration** required to launch an instance:
- Operating System (Linux, Windows, Ubuntu, Amazon Linux 2, etc.)
- Application server / middleware
- Pre-installed applications
- Launch permissions
- A root volume template (for the OS)

> **AMI = Blueprint of your instance.** Without an AMI, you cannot launch an EC2 instance.

### 2.1.2 Instance Types

AWS categorizes instance types into **families** optimized for different workloads:

| Family | Type Prefix | Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| General Purpose | T3, T4g, M5, M6i | Web servers, small databases, dev environments |
| Compute Optimized | C5, C6i, C7g | CPU-bound tasks: batch processing, gaming servers |
| Memory Optimized | R5, R6i, X1, X2 | In-memory databases (Redis), big data analytics |
| Storage Optimized | I3, D2, H1 | High sequential I/O: data warehousing, log processing |
| Accelerated / GPU | P4, G5, Inf1 | ML training, graphics rendering, HPC |
| Burstable | T3, T4g | Low-traffic workloads needing occasional bursts |

**Naming Convention:**
`Family + Generation + Size + Optional Capability`
*Example: `m5.large` → M-family, 5th generation, large size.*

### 2.1.3 Instance Sizing

| Size | vCPU | Memory (GiB) |
|---|---|---|
| nano | 2 | 0.5 |
| micro | 2 | 1 |
| small | 2 | 2 |
| medium | 2 | 4 |
| large | 2 | 8 |
| xlarge | 4 | 16 |
| 2xlarge | 8 | 32 |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Exam Tip:** Examiners frequently ask students to **justify instance type selection** based on workload characteristics. Memorize at least the four primary families.

## 2.2 EC2 Pricing Models (CRITICAL for KTU)

AWS provides **four pricing models** to match varying workload patterns:

### 2.2.1 On-Demand Instances
- Pay by the second (Linux) or hour (Windows).
- No long-term commitment.
- Highest per-hour cost.
- **Use Case:** Short-term, spiky, or unpredictable workloads.

### 2.2.2 Reserved Instances (RI)
- 1-year or 3-year commitment.
- Up to **75% discount** vs On-Demand.
- Three payment options: All Upfront, Partial Upfront, No Upfront.
- **Use Case:** Steady-state, predictable workloads.

### 2.2.3 Spot Instances
- Bid for unused AWS capacity.
- Up to **90% discount**.
- AWS can reclaim with **2-minute notice**.
- **Use Case:** Fault-tolerant, flexible, batch workloads.

### 2.2.4 Dedicated Hosts / Dedicated Instances
- Physical server dedicated to one customer.
- Most expensive option.
- **Use Case:** Compliance requirements, BYOL (Bring Your Own License).

### 2.2.5 Savings Plans
- Flexible pricing model with 1-year or 3-year commitment.
- Up to **72% discount** on EC2, Lambda, and Fargate usage.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cost Priority Order:** Spot < Reserved < Savings Plans < On-Demand < Dedicated Hosts.

## 2.3 EC2 Storage Options

| Storage Type | Persistence | Performance | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| **EBS (Elastic Block Store)** | Persistent across stops | gp3, io2 Block Express | Boot volume, databases |
| **Instance Store** | Ephemeral (lost on stop) | Very High IOPS | Cache, scratch data |
| **EFS (Elastic File System)** | Shared across AZs | Elastic throughput | Shared file system |
| **S3** | Object storage | 11x9s durability | Backups, media |

## 2.4 Networking & Security Components

- **Security Group:** Virtual firewall controlling inbound/outbound traffic at the **instance level**. *Stateful.*
- **Key Pair:** Public-private cryptographic key pair used for SSH access. *Downloaded once.*
- **Elastic IP (EIP):** Static public IPv4 address that can be remapped between instances.
- **VPC (Virtual Private Cloud):** Logically isolated network within AWS.
- **Subnet:** Subdivision of a VPC. Can be Public or Private.
- **IAM Role for EC2:** Allows instances to securely access other AWS services without storing credentials.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Stateful vs Stateless:** Security Groups are *stateful* — if you allow inbound, the response is automatically allowed. Network ACLs (NACLs) are *stateless*.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet — EC2 Cost & Capacity Calculations

> [!NOTE]
> The following formulas are **exam-critical** for KTU 2024 numerical problems. Memorize them verbatim.

| Formula / Concept | Equation | Boundary Condition |
|---|---|---|
| Total On-Demand Cost | $C_{od} = P_h \times T_h$ | $T_h$ = total hours of usage |
| Reserved Instance Cost | $C_{ri} = P_{ri} \times 8760 \times Y + F$ | $Y$ = years (1 or 3); $F$ = upfront fee |
| Spot Bid Cost | $C_{spot} = P_{spot} \times T_h$ | Valid only while spot price $<$ bid |
| Effective Hourly Cost (RI) | $E_h = \dfrac{(P_{ri} \times Y \times 8760) + F}{8760 \times Y}$ | Amortizes upfront across life |
| vCPU-to-Memory Ratio | $R = \dfrac{v_{cpu} \times M_{ref}}{M_{actual}}$ | Helps compare instance classes |
| Bandwidth Latency (RTT) | $T_{RTT} = \dfrac{D_{km}}{2 \times c_{fiber}}$ | Inter-AZ RTT ≈ 1-2 ms |
| Monthly Hours | $H_m = 24 \times 30 = 720$ | Standard billing cycle |
| Annual Hours | $H_a = 24 \times 365 = 8760$ | Standard annualized metric |
| EBS IOPS Cost | $C_{iops} = (P_{gp3} \times GB) + (P_{io} \times IOPS_{extra})$ | gp3 includes 3000 IOPS free |
| SLA Uptime % | $U = \dfrac{A_t - D_t}{A_t} \times 100$ | EC2 SLA = 99.99% in multi-AZ |

> **Where:** $P_h$ = per-hour price; $T_h$ = hours used; $F$ = upfront fee; $D_t$ = downtime; $A_t$ = available time; $c_{fiber}$ = speed of light in fiber ≈ $2 \times 10^8$ m/s.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

EC2 is used in:
1. **Web Hosting** — Auto-scaling WordPress, Magento, custom web apps.
2. **Big Data Processing** — Hadoop/Spark clusters on Spot Instances.
3. **Machine Learning** — GPU instances (p3, g5) for model training.
4. **Disaster Recovery** — Replicate on-premise workloads as AMIs.
5. **High-Performance Computing (HPC)** — Tightly-coupled clusters using Placement Groups.
6. **Dev/Test Environments** — Rapidly provisioned, tear-down-friendly sandboxes.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Problem 1 — Cost Comparison Across Pricing Models

> **KTU 2024 Typical Question Pattern:** *"Compare the cost of running a workload using On-Demand, Reserved, and Spot pricing."*

### Problem Setup

A KTU engineering startup needs to run a workload for **24 hours/day, 30 days/month, for 12 months** on a `t3.large` instance.

- **On-Demand price** = **$0.0832/hour**
- **1-Year Reserved (No Upfront)** = **$0.060/hour** + 1-year commitment
- **Spot Price** (average) = **$0.028/hour** (subject to reclaim)
- **Annual reserved upfront fee** = **$0** (No Upfront variant)

**Total runtime per year:** $24 \times 30 \times 12 = 8640$ hours.

### Step-by-Step Calculation

**Step 1: On-Demand Annual Cost**

$$C_{od} = P_h \times T_h = 0.0832 \times 8640$$

$$C_{od} = 718.85 \text{ USD/year}$$

**Step 2: Reserved Instance Annual Cost**

$$C_{ri} = P_{ri} \times T_h + F = 0.060 \times 8640 + 0$$

$$C_{ri} = 518.40 \text{ USD/year}$$

**Step 3: Spot Instance Annual Cost (assuming uninterrupted)**

$$C_{spot} = 0.028 \times 8640 = 241.92 \text{ USD/year}$$

**Step 4: Percentage Savings**

$$\text{Savings}_{RI} = \frac{C_{od} - C_{ri}}{C_{od}} \times 100 = \frac{718.85 - 518.40}{718.85} \times 100$$

$$\text{Savings}_{RI} = \frac{200.45}{718.85} \times 100 = 27.88\%$$

$$\text{Savings}_{Spot} = \frac{718.85 - 241.92}{718.85} \times 100 = \frac{476.93}{718.85} \times 100 = 66.34\%$$

### Step 5: Decision Matrix

| Model | Annual Cost (USD) | Discount vs On-Demand | Risk Level |
|---|---|---|---|
| On-Demand | $718.85 | 0% | Low |
| Reserved (No Upfront) | $518.40 | 27.88% | Medium |
| Spot (assumed available) | $241.92 | 66.34% | High |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner's Logic:** Students often forget that **Reserved Instances require capacity reservation** and **Spot can be reclaimed**. A perfect answer must mention trade-offs, not just dollar amounts.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Problem 2 — SLA Downtime Calculation

> **KTU Question Pattern:** *"Given an SLA of 99.99% for EC2 in a multi-AZ deployment, calculate the maximum allowable downtime per year."*

### Given
- Availability $A = 99.99\%$
- Total minutes in a year: $60 \times 24 \times 365 = 525600$ minutes

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Allowed Downtime Fraction**

$$D_{frac} = 1 - A = 1 - 0.9999 = 0.0001$$

**Step 2: Annual Downtime in Minutes**

$$D_t = D_{frac} \times 525600 = 0.0001 \times 525600$$

$$D_t = 52.56 \text{ minutes/year}$$

**Step 3: Monthly Equivalent**

$$D_t^{monthly} = \frac{52.56}{12} = 4.38 \text{ minutes/month}$$

**Step 4: Daily Equivalent**

$$D_t^{daily} = \frac{52.56}{365} = 0.144 \text{ minutes/day} \approx 8.64 \text{ seconds/day}$$

> **Conclusion:** AWS multi-AZ EC2 deployments guarantee less than **52.56 minutes** of downtime annually — equivalent to nearly **4 nines (99.99%)** of availability.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Problem 3 — Vertical vs Horizontal Scaling Trade-off

> **Conceptual Derivation:** Two scaling paradigms exist in EC2:

**Vertical Scaling (Scale Up/Down):**
- Replace instance with larger/smaller one (e.g., `t3.medium` → `m5.4xlarge`).
- **Pros:** No app refactoring; immediate resource boost.
- **Cons:** Has upper limits; requires downtime; not fault-tolerant.

**Horizontal Scaling (Scale Out/In):**
- Add/remove instances behind a Load Balancer.
- **Pros:** Theoretically unlimited; fault-tolerant.
- **Cons:** Application must be stateless; requires LB and ASG.

**Rule of Thumb:**
$$\text{If } \text{Workload}_{stateless} = \text{True}, \text{prefer Horizontal.}$$
$$\text{If } \text{Workload}_{stateful} = \text{True}, \text{prefer Vertical.}$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Problem 4 — Storage Cost Estimation

A database instance has 500 GB of gp3 EBS volume provisioned at 6000 IOPS.

- gp3 storage price = $0.08/GB-month
- gp3 provisioned IOPS = $0.005/IOPS-month (above 3000 free baseline)

**Step 1: Base Storage Cost**

$$C_{storage} = 0.08 \times 500 = 40.00 \text{ USD/month}$$

**Step 2: Extra IOPS Cost**

$$IOPS_{extra} = 6000 - 3000 = 3000$$

$$C_{iops} = 0.005 \times 3000 = 15.00 \text{ USD/month}$$

**Step 3: Total EBS Cost**

$$C_{total} = C_{storage} + C_{iops} = 40.00 + 15.00 = 55.00 \text{ USD/month}$$

> **Engineering Insight:** Choosing gp3 with explicit IOPS provisioning is often cheaper than io1 for predictable workloads.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 EC2 Instance Lifecycle Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start] --> B[Choose AMI]
    B --> C[Select Instance Type]
    C --> D[Configure Network VPC/Subnet]
    D --> E[Attach IAM Role]
    E --> F[Attach Security Group]
    F --> G[Add Storage EBS]
    G --> H[Add User Data Script]
    H --> I[Review and Launch]
    I --> J{Pending State}
    J --> K[Running State]
    K --> L{User Action}
    L -->|Stop| M[Stopped State]
    L -->|Reboot| K
    L -->|Terminate| N[Terminated State]
    M --> O[Start Again]
    O --> K
    N --> P[End]
```

**Lifecycle Explanation:**
1. **Pending:** AWS allocates physical resources.
2. **Running:** Instance is operational and incurring billing.
3. **Stopping:** Graceful shutdown (data on EBS persists).
4. **Stopped:** Instance is off; storage persists; not billed for compute.
5. **Terminated:** Instance is permanently deleted (irreversible).

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Students confuse "Stop" with "Terminate." *Stopping* preserves the EBS volume and allows restart; *Terminating* permanently destroys everything unless `DeleteOnTermination=false`.

---

## 4.2 EC2 Component Architecture — Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph USER[User Layer]
        U1[Developer Console]
        U2[CLI or SDK]
    end

    subgraph AWS[AWS Cloud Platform]
        subgraph COMP[Compute]
            I1[EC2 Instance 1]
            I2[EC2 Instance 2]
            I3[Auto Scaling Group]
        end

        subgraph NET[Network]
            V1[VPC]
            V2[Subnet]
            V3[Security Group]
            V4[Elastic IP]
        end

        subgraph STO[Storage]
            S1[EBS Volume]
            S2[Instance Store]
            S3[S3 Bucket]
        end

        subgraph SEC[Security and Identity]
            ID1[IAM Role]
            ID2[Key Pair]
        end

        subgraph MON[Monitoring]
            M1[CloudWatch Metrics]
            M2[CloudTrail Logs]
        end
    end

    U1 --> I1
    U2 --> I2
    I1 --> V1
    I1 --> S1
    I1 --> ID1
    I1 --> M1
    I3 --> I1
    I3 --> I2
    V1 --> V2
    V2 --> V3
    I1 --> V4
```

**Architecture Explanation:**
- **User Layer:** How engineers interact with EC2 (Console, CLI, SDK).
- **Compute Layer:** Instances managed via Auto Scaling Groups.
- **Network Layer:** VPC → Subnet → Security Group → Elastic IP.
- **Storage Layer:** EBS (persistent), Instance Store (ephemeral), S3 (object).
- **Security Layer:** IAM roles + Key Pairs for authentication.
- **Monitoring Layer:** CloudWatch (metrics/alarms), CloudTrail (audit logs).

---

## 4.3 Pricing Model Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[New EC2 Workload] --> B{Predictable Steady Usage?}
    B -->|Yes, 1+ Year| C[Reserved Instance]
    B -->|No| D{Fault Tolerant?}
    D -->|Yes| E[Spot Instance]
    D -->|No, Flexible Commit| F[Savings Plan]
    D -->|No, Short-term| G[On-Demand Instance]
    C --> H[Up to 75 percent Savings]
    E --> I[Up to 90 percent Savings]
    F --> J[Up to 72 percent Savings]
    G --> K[Baseline Cost]
```

> **Reading the Diagram:** Always start by asking "Is my workload steady-state, fault-tolerant, or short-lived?" This single decision tree eliminates 80% of cost-related exam confusion.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1: Define Amazon Machine Image (AMI). Mention its components.**
**[KTU University Exam — June 2023 Pattern]**

**Model Answer:**
An Amazon Machine Image (AMI) is a pre-configured template that contains the software configuration — including the operating system, application server, and applications — required to launch an EC2 instance.

**Components:**
1. **Template for the root volume** (OS, applications, data).
2. **Launch permissions** (who can launch the AMI).
3. **Block device mapping** (which volumes to attach).

> *3-Mark Marking Scheme:* Definition (1) + 3 components (1 + 1) = 3 marks.

---

### **Question 2: Differentiate between Security Group and Network ACL.**
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022 Pattern]**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Security Group | Network ACL |
|---|---|---|
| Level | Instance level | Subnet level |
| Rules | Allow rules only | Allow + Deny rules |
| Stateful | Yes (return traffic auto-allowed) | No (stateless) |
| Default | Denies inbound, allows outbound | Allows all by default |

> *3-Mark Marking Scheme:* Table with 4 correct comparisons = 3 marks.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Scheme Note:** Each Part B question typically carries **7 + 7 = 14 marks**. Both Question A and Question B below are independent alternatives; students answer **either** of them.

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Comprehensive EC2 Architecture & Pricing**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Pattern, Modified for 2024 Scheme]**

**(a) Explain the different EC2 instance families with at least one example and a real-world use case for each. [7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

EC2 instance families are predefined hardware configurations optimized for specific workload types.

**1. General Purpose (e.g., M5.large, T3.medium):**
- Balanced vCPU-to-memory ratio (1:4).
- *Use Case:* Web servers, small databases, code repositories.

**2. Compute Optimized (e.g., C5.xlarge, C7g):**
- High-performance processors, lower memory ratio (1:2).
- *Use Case:* Batch processing, scientific modeling, gaming servers.

**3. Memory Optimized (e.g., R5.4xlarge, X1.16xlarge):**
- High RAM-to-vCPU ratio (1:8 or more).
- *Use Case:* In-memory databases (SAP HANA, Redis), big data analytics (Apache Spark).

**4. Storage Optimized (e.g., I3.large, D2.xlarge):**
- High sequential read/write to local NVMe storage.
- *Use Case:* Data warehousing, log processing, distributed file systems.

**5. Accelerated Computing (e.g., P4d.24xlarge, G5.xlarge):**
- Hardware accelerators (GPUs, FPGAs).
- *Use Case:* Deep learning training, video transcoding, financial simulations.

**6. Burstable Performance (e.g., T3, T4g):**
- Baseline CPU with burst capability using CPU credits.
- *Use Case:* Low-traffic web apps, microservices, dev/test environments.

> *Marking Scheme:* 6 families × ~1 mark each (correct family + example + use case) = 6 marks; conclusion = 1 mark.

---

**(b) Compare On-Demand, Reserved, Spot, and Dedicated Host pricing models. Mention a use case where each is most appropriate. [7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

| Parameter | On-Demand | Reserved | Spot | Dedicated Host |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Commitment | None | 1 or 3 years | None | 1 or 3 years |
| Discount | 0% | Up to 75% | Up to 90% | 0% (but dedicated) |
| Billing | Per second/hour | Hourly + upfront | Per hour | Per hour |
| Interruption | None | None | Possible (2-min notice) | None |
| Best Use Case | Short, unpredictable | Steady-state | Fault-tolerant batch | Compliance / BYOL |

**Use Case Discussion:**

- **On-Demand:** A KTU final-year project needing an EC2 server for 2 weeks during development — minimal commitment.
- **Reserved:** An enterprise ERP application running 24/7 for 3 years — predictable workload benefits from the discount.
- **Spot:** Rendering frames for an animation studio — interruption-tolerant, massive cost savings.
- **Dedicated Host:** A bank needing strict regulatory isolation and Windows Server licensing — compliance-driven.

> *Marking Scheme:* Comparison table (3 marks) + 4 use cases (1 mark each) = 7 marks.

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — EC2 Security, Storage, and Scaling**

**(a) Explain the role of Security Groups, Key Pairs, and IAM Roles in EC2 security. Why are Security Groups called stateful? [7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Security Group:**
- Acts as a virtual firewall at the instance level.
- Controls inbound and outbound traffic using rules (protocol, port, source).
- Example: Allow SSH (port 22) from your office IP only.

**2. Key Pair:**
- Public-private key pair used for SSH authentication into Linux instances.
- Private key is downloaded once; AWS stores only the public key.
- Loss of private key = loss of access.

**3. IAM Role for EC2:**
- Allows instances to call AWS APIs (e.g., S3, DynamoDB) without embedding credentials.
- Credentials are dynamically provisioned by the AWS Security Token Service (STS).
- Best practice: attach roles instead of storing access keys on the instance.

**Why Security Groups are Stateful:**
A security group remembers the connection state. If an inbound request is allowed, the corresponding outbound response is **automatically allowed** without requiring an explicit outbound rule. This is in contrast to NACLs, where return traffic must be explicitly permitted.

**Example:** Allow inbound HTTPS (port 443). When the instance replies with HTTP response traffic, the security group automatically permits it — no separate outbound rule is needed.

> *Marking Scheme:* 3 components explained (1.5 + 1.5 + 1.5 = 4.5 marks); Stateful explanation with example (2.5 marks) = 7 marks.

---

**(b) Describe the EC2 instance lifecycle states. Distinguish between Stop and Terminate. [7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Instance Lifecycle States:**

1. **Pending:** AWS is preparing to launch the instance (allocating compute, networking).
2. **Running:** Instance is operational and incurring compute charges.
3. **Stopping:** Instance is being shut down gracefully; data on EBS persists.
4. **Stopped:** Instance is shut down; storage and Elastic IP persist; not billed for compute.
5. **Shutting-down:** Instance is in the process of terminating.
6. **Terminated:** Instance is permanently deleted.

**State Transition Diagram:**
Pending → Running → Stopping → Stopped → (re)Running
Running → Shutting-down → Terminated

**Stop vs Terminate:**

| Attribute | Stop | Terminate |
|---|---|---|
| EBS Volume | Preserved by default | Deleted unless `DeleteOnTermination=false` |
| Elastic IP | Retained (but may incur charges if not reattached) | Released back to pool |
| Compute Billing | Stops immediately | Stops immediately |
| Restart | Possible anytime | Impossible (instance is gone) |
| Cost Impact | Storage + EIP charges only | Nothing further |

> *Marking Scheme:* States listed (3 marks); Stop vs Terminate table (3 marks); Real-world example (1 mark) = 7 marks.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Commonly Lose Marks in EC2 Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing "Instance Store" with "EBS"** — Instance Store is ephemeral; EBS is persistent. Examiners specifically test this distinction.
> 2. **Misquoting discounts** — Reserved Instances offer *up to* 75%; Spot offers *up to* 90%. Never say "Spot is always 90% cheaper."
> 3. **Ignoring Spot's interruption model** — Spot instances can be reclaimed with a 2-minute warning. Failing to mention this loses 1-2 marks.
> 4. **Forgetting Security Group statefulness** — Most students say "SG is a firewall" but forget to explain *stateful* nature.
> 5. **Ignoring the difference between "Stopping" and "Terminating"** — A classic trap question.
> 6. **Writing Reserved Instance cost calculations without the upfront fee term** — Always include $(P_{ri} \times T_h) + F$.
> 7. **Forgetting that EC2 SLA is 99.99% only in multi-AZ deployments** — Single-AZ gives only 99.95%.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — EC2 for KTU 2024:**

- ✅ **EC2 = Elastic Compute Cloud** — AWS's flagship IaaS service for virtual servers.
- ✅ **AMI = Blueprint** — OS + apps + permissions template for launching instances.
- ✅ **Instance Families:** General (M), Compute (C), Memory (R/X), Storage (I/D), GPU (P/G), Burstable (T).
- ✅ **Pricing:** On-Demand > Reserved ≈ Savings Plans > Spot (highest discount, interruption risk).
- ✅ **Reserved Instances** give up to **75% discount** with 1/3-year commitment.
- ✅ **Spot Instances** give up to **90% discount** but can be reclaimed in 2 minutes.
- ✅ **Security Groups** are stateful, instance-level, allow-rules-only firewalls.
- ✅ **Key Pairs** = SSH authentication mechanism; private key downloaded once.
- ✅ **EBS** is persistent block storage; **Instance Store** is ephemeral.
- ✅ **Elastic IP** = static public IPv4 address.
- ✅ **Stop** preserves EBS; **Terminate** deletes instance (and EBS by default).
- ✅ **EC2 SLA = 99.99% uptime** (only with multi-AZ deployment).
- ✅ **Vertical Scaling** = resize instance; **Horizontal Scaling** = add instances behind a Load Balancer.
- ✅ **Auto Scaling Group (ASG)** + **Elastic Load Balancer (ELB)** = standard production setup.
- ✅ **Vertical Scaling Limit** = max instance size of a family; **Horizontal Limit** = theoretically unlimited.

> **Final Tip:** When answering any EC2 question in KTU 2024 ESE, always conclude with a real-world use case — examiners reward applied thinking.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Advanced Compute Services. Storage Services - Simple Storage System (Amazon S3)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT525 - CLOUD COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Advanced Compute Services. Storage Services - Simple Storage System (Amazon S3)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Amazon Simple Storage Service (Amazon S3)** is an **object-based storage service** offered by Amazon Web Services (AWS) that provides **industry-leading scalability, data availability, security, and performance** through a simple web-services interface. It is designed to store and retrieve any amount of data from anywhere on the web, making it the foundational storage backbone of the AWS Cloud ecosystem.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, Amazon S3 is classified as an **Infrastructure-as-a-Service (IaaS)** storage offering that operates on the **pay-as-you-go consumption model**, abstracting the underlying physical infrastructure completely from the end-user. It guarantees an object durability of **99.999999999%** (commonly called "**eleven 9s of durability**") and supports individual objects of size ranging from **0 bytes up to 5 TB**.

### Core Terminology Mapping (KTU Syllabus Anchors)

| S3 Term | Engineering Definition | Real-World Analogy |
|---------|------------------------|--------------------|
| **Bucket** | A top-level logical container for objects stored in S3 | A hard-disk drive partition or a top-level folder in Google Drive |
| **Object** | The fundamental entity stored in S3 (file + metadata) | A single photograph uploaded to a cloud photo album |
| **Key** | The unique identifier (name) of an object within a bucket | The full file path including folder name |
| **Region** | The geographical AWS data-center location where bucket resides | The physical branch of a bank where your locker is located |
| **Version ID** | A unique string assigned by S3 when versioning is enabled | A timestamped backup snapshot of the same file |
| **Prefix** | A string of characters at the beginning of an object key | A directory/sub-folder hierarchy |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Cloud Bank Locker" Model

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition for First-Time Learners:** Imagine Amazon S3 as a massive, infinitely expandable **bank locker system** spread across the world.

- **The Bucket** is the locker itself — you must rent one before storing anything. It must have a **globally unique name** (just like locker numbers cannot repeat across the bank's entire network).
- **The Objects** are the parcels/documents you place inside the locker. Each parcel gets a **tag (the key)** that the bank uses to retrieve it instantly.
- **The Regions** are the physical branches of the bank. If you are in Kerala, you can choose a Mumbai branch for low latency, but the locker in Mumbai is physically separate from one in Frankfurt.
- **Versioning** is like the bank keeping every previous version of your document whenever you overwrite it — you can roll back to any older version anytime.
- **Storage Classes** are different locker tiers: a cheap basement locker (Infrequent Access) versus a premium climate-controlled vault (Standard) — same locker, different pricing and access speed.

## 1.3 Syllabus Highlights & Board-Exam Triggers

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield KTU Board Triggers:** Examiners consistently test the following in Module 4:
> 1. Difference between **Block Storage vs Object Storage vs File Storage**
> 2. S3 **Storage Classes** and their use cases
> 3. S3 **Consistency Model** (strong read-after-write for PUTS of new objects)
> 4. **Versioning** and **Lifecycle Management Policies**
> 5. S3 **Cross-Region Replication (CRR)** vs **Same-Region Replication (SRR)**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Architectural Deep-Dive: The Object Storage Paradigm

Unlike traditional **file systems** that use a hierarchical tree structure (folders within folders), Amazon S3 uses a **flat namespace architecture**. There are no real "folders" — what appears as a folder in the S3 console is actually a **prefix** in the object's key name (a delimiter illusion).

### 2.1.1 The Anatomy of an S3 Object

Every object stored in S3 consists of **five mandatory components**:

1. **Key** — The full unique name (e.g., `images/2024/kerala/beach.jpg`)
2. **Value** — The actual binary data payload (up to 5 TB)
3. **Version ID** — Assigned when versioning is enabled
4. **Metadata** — Key-value pairs describing the object
5. **Sub-resources** — Access Control Lists (ACLs), torrent flag

### 2.1.2 Operational Logic Flow (Why S3 Behaves as It Does)

- **Step 1 — Bucket Provisioning:** User issues `PUT /bucket-name` over HTTPS. S3 creates a namespace anchored to a specific AWS Region.
- **Step 2 — Object Write:** Object is uploaded via REST API. S3 computes **checksums** and distributes copies across **multiple Availability Zones (AZs)** for the eleven 9s durability.
- **Step 3 — Metadata Indexing:** The bucket's index is updated. Strong consistency ensures immediate read-after-write visibility for new PUTs.
- **Step 4 — Retrieval:** Subsequent `GET` requests use the key to fetch the object from the nearest healthy copy.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Object Storage" and not "File Storage"?** Object storage is optimized for **write-once, read-many (WORM)** workloads where entire files are retrieved as a whole. You cannot edit a small portion of an S3 object — you must upload the entire object again. This is why S3 is perfect for images, videos, backups, and logs, but poor for databases that need random byte-level access.

## 2.2 S3 Storage Classes — The Engineering Cheat Sheet

| Storage Class | Designed For | Availability SLA | Min Storage Duration | Retrieval Cost |
|---------------|--------------|------------------|----------------------|----------------|
| **S3 Standard** | Frequent access, mission-critical | 99.99% | None | None |
| **S3 Intelligent-Tiering** | Unknown/changing access patterns | 99.9% | None | None |
| **S3 Standard-IA** | Infrequent access, rapid retrieval | 99.9% | **30 days** | Per-GB fee |
| **S3 One Zone-IA** | Recreatable infrequent data | 99.5% | **30 days** | Per-GB fee |
| **S3 Glacier Instant** | Archive with millisecond retrieval | 99.9% | **90 days** | Per-GB fee |
| **S3 Glacier Flexible** | Archive, 1–12 hour retrieval | 99.99% | **90 days** | Per-GB + request fee |
| **S3 Glacier Deep Archive** | Long-term archive, 12+ hour retrieval | 99.99% | **180 days** | Lowest per-GB fee |
| **S3 Outposts** | On-premises S3 | Varies | None | None |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Minimum Storage Duration Penalty:** If you delete or transition an object before the minimum duration, AWS charges you for the **remaining days**. For example, deleting a Glacier Deep Archive object on Day 50 still bills you for 130 more days (180 − 50).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — Quantitative Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are **board-exam favourites** for the 14-mark questions.

| Metric / Concept | Formula / Definition | Unit |
|------------------|----------------------|------|
| Object Durability | $D = 1 - \left(\frac{L_{\text{annual}}}{N_{\text{copies}}}\right)$ | Probability (0 to 1) |
| Annual Loss Expectancy | $\text{ALE} = \text{SLE} \times \text{ARO}$ | Cost in ₹/USD |
| Monthly S3 Bill | $C_{\text{total}} = C_{\text{storage}} + C_{\text{requests}} + C_{\text{transfer}}$ | Currency/month |
| Storage Cost Component | $C_{\text{storage}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left(G_i \times P_i \times T_i\right)$ | Currency/month |
| Effective Availability | $A_{\text{eff}} = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n}(1 - A_i)$ | Percentage |
| Data Retrieval Cost | $C_{\text{retrieval}} = \sum_{j=1}^{m} R_j \times P_{\text{class},j}$ | Currency |

Where:
- $G_i$ = Gigabytes stored in tier $i$
- $P_i$ = Price per GB-month for tier $i$
- $T_i$ = Time fraction of the month stored
- $L_{\text{annual}}$ = Annual loss probability per copy
- $N_{\text{copies}}$ = Number of replicated copies
- $A_i$ = Availability of redundant component $i$
- $R_j$ = GB retrieved from tier $j$
- $P_{\text{class},j}$ = Retrieval price per GB for class $j$

> [!WARNING]
> **Do NOT use the vertical pipe symbol `\|` inside table cells** — KTU PDF renderers break. The expressions above use $\prod$ (product) and $\sum$ (summation) notation for absolute clarity.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Amazon S3 is the **de-facto standard** for:

- **Static Website Hosting** — S3 can serve HTML/CSS/JS directly with 99.99% uptime.
- **Data Lake Foundation** — Stores petabytes of structured/unstructured data for analytics.
- **Disaster Recovery** — Cross-Region Replication provides geographic redundancy.
- **Backup & Archive** — Glacier Deep Archive costs ~**$0.00099/GB/month** (₹0.08 approx).
- **Big Data Processing** — Direct integration with EMR, Athena, Redshift Spectrum.
- **Software Delivery** — Hosts npm packages, Docker images (via ECR), and update binaries.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example: Calculating Total S3 Monthly Cost

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Context (KTU Board Pattern):** A startup stores **2 TB** of data on S3 Standard for the entire month. They perform **50,000 PUT requests**, **2,00,000 GET requests**, and transfer **500 GB** out to the internet. Calculate the approximate monthly bill (Mumbai region, 2024 pricing).

### Step 1 — Identify Reference Pricing

We use the AWS Mumbai region reference rates:

- **S3 Standard Storage:** ₹1.83 per GB-month (approximately)
- **PUT/COPY/POST requests:** ₹0.005 per 1,000 requests
- **GET/SELECT requests:** ₹0.0004 per 1,000 requests
- **Data transfer OUT to internet:** ₹6.80 per GB (first 10 TB)

### Step 2 — Calculate Storage Cost

The storage quantity in GB is:

$$
G = 2 \text{ TB} \times 1024 \frac{\text{GB}}{\text{TB}} = 2048 \text{ GB}
$$

Apply the storage pricing formula:

$$
C_{\text{storage}} = G \times P_{\text{storage}}
$$

$$
C_{\text{storage}} = 2048 \times 1.83 = ₹3747.84
$$

### Step 3 — Calculate PUT Request Cost

$$
C_{\text{PUT}} = N_{\text{PUT}} \times \frac{P_{\text{PUT}}}{1000}
$$

$$
C_{\text{PUT}} = 50{,}000 \times \frac{0.005}{1000} = 50{,}000 \times 0.000005
$$

$$
C_{\text{PUT}} = ₹0.25
$$

### Step 4 — Calculate GET Request Cost

$$
C_{\text{GET}} = N_{\text{GET}} \times \frac{P_{\text{GET}}}{1000}
$$

$$
C_{\text{GET}} = 2{,}00{,}000 \times \frac{0.0004}{1000} = 2{,}00{,}000 \times 0.0000004
$$

$$
C_{\text{GET}} = ₹0.08
$$

### Step 5 — Calculate Data Transfer OUT Cost

$$
C_{\text{transfer}} = G_{\text{out}} \times P_{\text{transfer}}
$$

$$
C_{\text{transfer}} = 500 \times 6.80 = ₹3400.00
$$

### Step 6 — Compute the Total Monthly Bill

$$
C_{\text{total}} = C_{\text{storage}} + C_{\text{PUT}} + C_{\text{GET}} + C_{\text{transfer}}
$$

$$
C_{\text{total}} = 3747.84 + 0.25 + 0.08 + 3400.00
$$

$$
\boxed{C_{\text{total}} = ₹7148.17}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Board Interpretation Note:** The storage cost (₹3747.84) is **52.4%** of the total bill, while transfer-out (₹3400.00) accounts for **47.6%**. Request costs are negligible at this scale. This explains why large-scale data pipelines use S3 in the **same region as their compute** to avoid transfer charges — a frequent 14-mark question theme.

## 3.2 Worked Example: Verifying the Eleven 9s Durability

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Context:** S3 Standard claims **99.999999999%** durability. If AWS maintains an object across **3 Availability Zones** simultaneously, and the probability of losing one copy in a year is $0.001$ (i.e., **99.9% availability**), compute the **effective annual durability** using independent failure probabilities.

### Step 1 — Identify Failure Probability Per Copy

Given that a single AZ has an annual availability of:

$$
A_{\text{AZ}} = 99.9\% \quad \Rightarrow \quad P_{\text{fail, single}} = 1 - 0.999 = 0.001
$$

### Step 2 — Probability That All 3 Copies Fail Simultaneously

Assuming **independent failures** across AZs, the joint failure probability is:

$$
P_{\text{all fail}} = P_{\text{fail, single}}^3 = (0.001)^3
$$

$$
P_{\text{all fail}} = 1 \times 10^{-9} = 0.000000001
$$

### Step 3 — Compute Effective Durability

$$
D_{\text{effective}} = 1 - P_{\text{all fail}}
$$

$$
D_{\text{effective}} = 1 - 1 \times 10^{-9} = 0.999999999
$$

$$
\boxed{D_{\text{effective}} = 99.9999999\% \approx \text{Eleven 9s}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters in KTU exams:** This derivation shows that S3 durability is **not magic** — it is a direct mathematical consequence of **geo-distributed replication with independent failure domains**. Examiners love asking how the 11 nines arise; this is the canonical derivation.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Block vs File vs Object Storage

| Parameter | Block Storage (EBS) | File Storage (EFS) | Object Storage (S3) |
|-----------|---------------------|--------------------|--------------------|
| **Data Unit** | Fixed-size block | File (hierarchical) | Object (flat namespace) |
| **Access Protocol** | iSCSI / NVMe | NFS / SMB | HTTPS REST API |
| **Max Object Size** | 16 TB (volume) | Multi-GB files | 5 TB per object |
| **Search Method** | Block address | Path traversal | Key lookup |
| **Best For** | Databases, OS boot | Shared home directories | Backups, media, data lakes |
| **Mutability** | Random byte writes | Append/overwrite | Whole-object replace |
| **Cost Model** | Provisioned IOPS | Pay-per-GB used | Pay-per-GB + requests |

## 3.4 Comparative Decision Flow: Choosing the Right S3 Storage Class

| Workload Signal | Recommended Class | Reasoning |
|-----------------|-------------------|-----------|
| Accessed multiple times daily | S3 Standard | No retrieval fee, lowest latency |
| Accessed once a month | S3 Standard-IA | 30-day minimum balances cost vs fee |
| Reproducible from source | S3 One Zone-IA | Single AZ = lower cost, acceptable loss |
| Accessed once a quarter, ok with minutes | S3 Glacier Flexible | Cheap storage, acceptable retrieval delay |
| Compliance data, accessed once a year | S3 Glacier Deep Archive | Lowest cost per GB |
| Unpredictable access pattern | S3 Intelligent-Tiering | Auto-moves between tiers |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->
