# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 S3 Object Storage Architecture — Block-Level Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Client["Client Application"]
    APIGW["AWS S3 REST API Gateway"]
    Auth["IAM Authentication and ACL Check"]
    Region["Region Selection Mumbai"]
    Bucket["Logical Bucket Container"]
    Obj1["Object Key images photo1 jpg"]
    Obj2["Object Key videos lecture mp4"]
    Obj3["Object Key docs report pdf"]
    Meta["Metadata and Versioning Index"]
    AZ1["Availability Zone 1a Physical Copies"]
    AZ2["Availability Zone 1b Physical Copies"]
    AZ3["Availability Zone 1c Physical Copies"]

    Client --> APIGW
    APIGW --> Auth
    Auth --> Region
    Region --> Bucket
    Bucket --> Obj1
    Bucket --> Obj2
    Bucket --> Obj3
    Bucket --> Meta
    Obj1 --> AZ1
    Obj1 --> AZ2
    Obj1 --> AZ3
    Obj2 --> AZ1
    Obj2 --> AZ2
    Obj2 --> AZ3
    Obj3 --> AZ1
    Obj3 --> AZ2
    Obj3 --> AZ3
```

## 4.2 Lifecycle Management Transition Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Start["New Object Uploaded"] --> S1["S3 Standard Hot Tier 0 to 30 days"]
    S1 --> S2["S3 Standard IA Warm Tier 30 to 90 days"]
    S2 --> S3["S3 Glacier Flexible Cold Tier 90 to 365 days"]
    S3 --> S4["S3 Glacier Deep Archive Frozen Tier more than 365 days"]
    S4 --> Exp["Expiration Rule Permanent Deletion after N years"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Guide:** Each arrow in the lifecycle flow is governed by a **Lifecycle Policy Rule** (an XML/JSON configuration). Transitions are **asynchronous** and occur in the background; you are not billed for the transition itself, only for the resulting storage class occupancy.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

> **Q: Differentiate between Object Storage and Block Storage. Give one real-world use case for each.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Criteria | Block Storage | Object Storage |
|----------|---------------|----------------|
| **Data Organization** | Divides data into fixed-size blocks addressed by LBA | Stores data as discrete objects identified by a unique key |
| **Metadata** | Minimal (block address only) | Rich custom metadata supported |
| **Access Pattern** | Low-latency random read/write | High-throughput sequential read/write |
| **Use Case** | EC2 root EBS volumes, databases | S3 buckets for images, logs, backups |

**Block Storage Use Case:** Amazon EBS volume attached to an EC2 instance hosting a MySQL database — needs random byte-level access.

**Object Storage Use Case:** A photo-sharing mobile app stores user uploads directly to an S3 bucket — needs massive scale and HTTP-based retrieval.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

> **Q: What is the meaning of "Eleven 9s of Durability" in Amazon S3? State its numerical value.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Amazon S3 Standard provides an object durability of **99.999999999%** (i.e., 99. followed by nine 9s).

This means if you store **10,000,000 objects** in S3, you can expect to lose **a single object once every 10,000 years** on average. This exceptional durability is achieved by:
- Storing object copies across **at least 3 Availability Zones** within a region
- Performing continuous integrity **checksumming** of stored data
- Automatic self-healing repair upon detecting corrupted copies

**Numerical Expression:**

$$
D = 1 - 10^{-11} = 0.99999999999
$$

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

> **(a)** Explain the various **S3 Storage Classes** with their use cases, minimum storage duration, and availability SLAs. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** A company wants to design a **disaster recovery strategy** using S3. Explain how **Versioning, Cross-Region Replication (CRR), and Lifecycle Policies** work together to achieve RTO < 1 hour and RPO near zero. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a) — Storage Classes (7 Marks)

The **eight** S3 storage classes can be categorized along three dimensions: **access frequency**, **retrieval latency**, and **cost**.

**1. S3 Standard — Hot Tier**
- Designed for **frequent access** with millisecond retrieval
- Availability SLA: **99.99%**, Durability: **99.999999999%**
- Stored across **≥ 3 AZs**, no minimum storage duration
- Use case: Web hosting, gaming, content distribution

**2. S3 Intelligent-Tiering — Auto-Tier**
- Uses ML to **automatically move objects** between Frequent and Infrequent tiers based on access patterns
- Small monthly monitoring fee per object, but **no retrieval charges**
- Use case: Data lakes with unpredictable access

**3. S3 Standard-IA — Warm Tier**
- For **infrequent access** requiring rapid retrieval
- Minimum storage duration: **30 days**, retrieval fee applies
- Availability: **99.9%**, stored across ≥ 3 AZs
- Use case: Disaster recovery backups, long-tail media

**4. S3 One Zone-IA — Warm Single-AZ Tier**
- Data stored in **only 1 AZ**, lower cost (20% less than Standard-IA)
- Availability: **99.5%**, durability: **99.999999999%**
- Use case: Reproducible thumbnails, secondary backups

**5. S3 Glacier Instant Retrieval — Cold Millisecond Tier**
- Millisecond retrieval for archive data accessed **once per quarter**
- Minimum storage: **90 days**, retrieval fee applies
- Use case: Medical images, news media archives

**6. S3 Glacier Flexible Retrieval — Cold Tier**
- Configurable retrieval times: **Expedited (1–5 min), Standard (3–5 hrs), Bulk (5–12 hrs)**
- Minimum storage: **90 days**
- Use case: Compliance archives, regulatory data

**7. S3 Glacier Deep Archive — Frozen Tier**
- Lowest cost: ~**$0.00099/GB/month**
- Retrieval: **Standard (12 hrs), Bulk (48 hrs)**
- Minimum storage: **180 days**
- Use case: Long-term retention (7–10 year compliance)

**8. S3 Outposts — On-Premises Tier**
- S3 delivered via **AWS Outposts** hardware on customer premises
- Used for data residency requirements

**Summary Table:**

| Class | Min Duration | Availability | Retrieval |
|-------|--------------|--------------|-----------|
| Standard | None | 99.99% | Free |
| Intelligent-Tiering | None | 99.9% | Free |
| Standard-IA | 30 days | 99.9% | Charged |
| One Zone-IA | 30 days | 99.5% | Charged |
| Glacier Instant | 90 days | 99.9% | Charged |
| Glacier Flexible | 90 days | 99.99% | Charged |
| Glacier Deep Archive | 180 days | 99.99% | Charged |

#### Model Solution for (b) — DR Strategy with Versioning + CRR + Lifecycle (7 Marks)

A robust disaster recovery architecture for a Kerala-based fintech company can use **three S3 features in concert**:

**Step 1 — Enable Versioning on the Primary Bucket**

- Versioning must be enabled **before** any CRR can be configured
- Every PUT/DELETE creates a new version rather than overwriting
- Protects against **accidental deletions** and **application bugs**

**Step 2 — Configure Cross-Region Replication (CRR)**

- Source bucket: `mumbai-prod-data` (primary region)
- Destination bucket: `singapore-dr-data` (DR region)
- IAM Role grants S3 permission to replicate objects
- Only **new objects** (and updates) are replicated; to replicate existing data, use **S3 Batch Replication**
- Status: **Enabled**, with delete marker replication optional

**Step 3 — Apply Lifecycle Policies**

- Move objects older than **30 days** from Standard → Standard-IA
- Move objects older than **90 days** to Glacier Flexible Retrieval
- Expire noncurrent versions after **90 days** to control storage costs

**How This Achieves the RTO/RPO Targets:**

| Metric | Target | Mechanism Achieving It |
|--------|--------|------------------------|
| **RPO ≈ 0** | Near-zero data loss | CRR is **asynchronous but near-real-time** (typically seconds) |
| **RTO < 1 hr** | Restore within 60 minutes | Destination bucket is **already warm** with replicated data; just promote DNS |
| **Cost Optimization** | Lower TCO | Lifecycle policy auto-tiers cold data to Glacier |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** Students often **lose 3 marks** by:
> 1. Forgetting that **Versioning MUST be enabled on BOTH source and destination** buckets before CRR works.
> 2. Confusing **CRR (Cross-Region)** with **SRR (Same-Region)** — CRR is for DR, SRR is for compliance/log aggregation.
> 3. Stating "Replication is real-time" — it is **asynchronous**. The correct term is "near-real-time" with seconds of lag.

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` (Alternative Choice)

> **(a)** Describe the **S3 Consistency Model**. Why does S3 provide strong read-after-write consistency for all operations since 2020? Illustrate with a scenario. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** Explain the **S3 Object Lock** and **WORM (Write Once Read Many)** compliance features. How do they differ from versioning? **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a) — S3 Consistency Model (7 Marks)

**Definition:** Amazon S3 provides **strong read-after-write consistency** for all PUT and GET operations across all storage classes and regions, automatically, without changes to performance or availability, and without additional cost.

**What This Means:**

After a successful `PUT` of a new object or an overwrite of an existing object, any subsequent `GET` request will immediately return the latest version of that object. Similarly, after a `DELETE`, any subsequent `GET` will return a 404 error.

**Why This Was Introduced (Historical Context):**

Before **December 2020**, S3 was only **eventually consistent** for overwrite PUTs and DELETEs. This caused bugs in distributed applications that read immediately after writing. Since December 2020, AWS re-architected the S3 metadata layer to use a strongly consistent distributed system, enabling the new guarantees without any user code changes.

**Illustrative Scenario:**

Consider an e-commerce website uploading a user's profile photo:

```
Step 1: Client PUT /bucket/profile.jpg (uploads new photo)
Step 2: Immediately reads back GET /bucket/profile.jpg
Step 3: Receives the new photo bytes correctly (strong consistency)
```

**Before 2020:** The GET might return the old cached photo for several seconds, causing the user to see a stale image.

**After 2020:** The GET always returns the new photo instantly.

**Benefits for Engineering:**

- Simplifies database cache-invalidation logic
- Enables safe object-level locking patterns
- Removes need for application-side retry loops

#### Model Solution for (b) — Object Lock and WORM (7 Marks)

**S3 Object Lock:**

A feature that allows you to store objects using a **WORM (Write Once Read Many)** model. Once locked, an object cannot be deleted or overwritten for the duration of the retention period.

**Two Retention Modes:**

1. **Governance Mode:** Protects objects from deletion by most users, but the **root account** can still delete (useful for testing).
2. **Compliance Mode:** Even the **root account cannot delete** an object until the retention period expires. Used for strict regulatory compliance (e.g., SEC Rule 17a-4, HIPAA).

**Legal Hold:**

An indefinite hold that prevents deletion until explicitly removed. Used during legal investigations.

**Configuration Requirement:** Object Lock works **only on new buckets** — you must enable it at bucket creation time. It also requires **Versioning** to be enabled.

**Differences from Versioning:**

| Feature | Versioning | Object Lock |
|---------|-----------|-------------|
| **Purpose** | Track every version of an object | Prevent deletion/overwrite for a defined period |
| **Mutability** | All versions remain modifiable | Locked versions become immutable |
| **Compliance Use** | None | SEC, HIPAA, FINRA |
| **Delete Allowed?** | Yes (any version) | No (within retention period) |
| **Can Overwrite?** | Yes (creates new version) | No (rejected with 403) |

**Use Case Example:** A stockbroker must retain trade records for **7 years** under SEC regulations. By placing these records in an S3 bucket with Object Lock in Compliance Mode for 7 years, no administrator — even with root credentials — can tamper with them, ensuring legal defensibility.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Module 4 — Amazon S3**

- **Object Storage Fundamentals:** S3 stores **objects** (not files in folders) in **buckets** within a chosen **AWS Region**. The namespace is **flat**; folder appearance is a prefix illusion.
- **Durability:** **99.999999999% (11 nines)** — achieved by storing across **≥ 3 AZs** with checksumming and self-healing.
- **Availability SLA:** Ranges from **99.5%** (One Zone-IA) to **99.99%** (Standard/Glacier Flexible).
- **Maximum Object Size:** **5 TB**, maximum upload via single PUT is **5 GB** (use Multipart Upload for larger).
- **Storage Class Selection:** Choose based on **access frequency** and **retrieval latency tolerance**.
- **Minimum Duration Penalty:** Deleting early from IA/Glacier classes still bills the full minimum (30/90/180 days).
- **Versioning:** Must be enabled on the **source bucket** before CRR/SRR can be configured.
- **Replication:** **CRR** = Cross-Region (DR use case), **SRR** = Same-Region (aggregation use case). Both are **asynchronous**.
- **Consistency Model:** **Strong read-after-write** since December 2020 for ALL operations.
- **Security:** Supports **IAM policies, Bucket Policies, ACLs, SSE-S3, SSE-KMS, SSE-C** encryption options.
- **Object Lock:** Provides **WORM** compliance; works **only with versioning** and on **new buckets**.
- **Lifecycle Policies:** Automate transitions between tiers and expiration of noncurrent versions.
- **Cost Formula:** $C_{\text{total}} = C_{\text{storage}} + C_{\text{requests}} + C_{\text{transfer-out}}$
- **Common KTU Traps:** Confusing bucket policies with IAM policies; assuming replication is real-time; forgetting that Object Lock cannot be retroactively enabled.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Exam Tip (KTU 2024 Scheme):** When answering any 14-mark question on S3, **always draw the architectural diagram first** (even a rough sketch) before writing prose. Examiners allocate **2 marks** implicitly for visual representation, and well-labeled diagrams often compensate for partially-correct textual explanations.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Elastic Block Store (Amazon EBS)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT525 - CLOUD COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Elastic Block Store (Amazon EBS)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Amazon Elastic Block Store (Amazon EBS)** is a cloud-based, **block-level persistent storage service** designed specifically for use with Amazon EC2 (Elastic Compute Cloud) instances. Each EBS volume is automatically replicated within its Availability Zone (AZ) to provide high availability and durability, and it can be attached to a single EC2 instance (or multiple, via the Multi-Attach feature) as a raw, unformatted block device.

From the perspective of the **KTU 2024 Scheme Cloud Computing syllabus**, EBS falls under the **AWS Storage & Database Services** cluster, categorized as an **IaaS-level storage offering** that provides the following standard guarantees:

- **Data Persistence:** Independent of EC2 instance lifecycle (by default, volumes persist even after the EC2 is terminated).
- **Block-Level Access:** The OS sees the volume as a raw disk drive, allowing file system creation (`ext4`, `xfs`, `NTFS`, etc.).
- **Granularity:** Operates at the block level, not object level (unlike S3).
- **Single AZ Scope:** Volumes reside in one specific Availability Zone and must be attached to an EC2 in that same AZ.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "External SSD" Model

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of EBS as a high-performance external SSD that you "plug into" a virtual computer (EC2) running in a specific data center rack (Availability Zone).**

Imagine the following physical-world setup:

| Real-World Object | AWS Equivalent | Explanation |
|---|---|---|
| External SSD drive | EBS Volume | Raw, formatted storage device |
| Your laptop | EC2 Instance | The "computer" reading/writing data |
| USB-C / SATA port | AWS Storage Area Network | Internal AWS connectivity fabric |
| Cloud photo backup of the SSD | EBS Snapshot (stored in S3) | A point-in-time backup |
| Data center rack (e.g., Rack A) | Availability Zone | EBS volumes are confined to ONE rack |

**Why this analogy works:**
- Just as you can detach the SSD from one laptop and plug it into another, an EBS volume can be detached from one EC2 instance and re-attached to another (in the same AZ).
- Just as a "rack" houses the SSD physically, an AZ confines the EBS volume — if you want it in another rack, you must take a snapshot and copy it over.
- Just as a corrupted SSD can be replaced without affecting the laptop's other drives, an EBS volume failure does not affect the EC2 instance's other mounted volumes.

---

## 1.3 Key Terminology & Physical/Logical Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core EBS Metrics Every KTU Student Must Memorize:**

- **IOPS (Input/Output Operations Per Second):** The number of read/write operations a volume can handle per second. Standard unit: **operations/second**.
- **Throughput:** The volume of data transferred per second. Standard unit: **MiB/s** or **MB/s**.
- **Volume Size (GiB):** Logical storage capacity, ranging from **1 GiB to 64 TiB** (depending on volume type).
- **Latency:** Time taken to complete a single I/O operation, typically in the **single-digit millisecond** range for SSD-backed volumes.
- **Burst Balance (gp2-specific):** Accumulated I/O credits, measured as a percentage (0% to 100%).

> [!NOTE]
> EBS uses **binary prefixes (GiB)** for volume sizing but throughput is often quoted in **MiB/s** for I/O operations. Be careful — **1 GiB = 1024³ bytes**, while **1 GB = 1000³ bytes**.

---

## 1.4 Where EBS Sits in the AWS Storage Family

```
              AWS STORAGE FAMILY (KTU Module 4 Context)
   ┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
   │                                                    │
   │   BLOCK          FILE           OBJECT            │
   │   ─────          ────           ──────            │
   │   [EBS]      [EFS / FSx]       [S3]              │
   │   (EC2)      (Multi-EC2)       (HTTP API)        │
   │                                                    │
   │   [Instance Store]   ← ephemeral, tied to host    │
   └────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** EBS is **block storage**, NOT file storage and NOT object storage. It is **NOT accessible via HTTP** (unlike S3). It is **NOT a Network Attached File System** (unlike EFS). Confusing these is one of the highest-frequency errors in KTU theory exams.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Architecture of an EBS Volume

EBS does not "live inside" an EC2 instance. Instead, it resides on the **EBS Storage Infrastructure** — a redundant fleet of storage servers physically attached to the AWS backbone. When an EC2 instance issues an I/O request, the request traverses the **AWS internal network** and reaches the EBS volume on a remote storage host.

### Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown:

1. **Provisioning Phase:** The user creates an EBS volume in a specific AZ (e.g., `ap-south-1a`) with a chosen volume type and size.
2. **Attachment Phase:** The volume is attached to an EC2 instance **in the same AZ** via an EBS-optimized network path.
3. **OS Recognition:** The EC2's operating system detects the new block device (`/dev/xvdf` on Linux, `xvdf`).
4. **Filesystem Layer:** The user formats the volume (e.g., `mkfs.ext4`) and mounts it to a directory.
5. **I/O Path:** Application writes/reads flow from EC2 → EBS-optimized network → EBS storage server → replicated copies on multiple physical disks.
6. **Replication (Automatic):** Each volume is **synchronously replicated** within the AZ to prevent data loss from hardware failure.
7. **Snapshot/Backup Phase:** Point-in-time snapshots are stored durably in **Amazon S3** (managed transparently by EBS).
8. **Detachment:** Volume can be detached from one EC2 and attached to another in the same AZ.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "synchronous" replication matters:** EBS uses synchronous replication, meaning a write is acknowledged only AFTER it is committed to multiple replicas. This provides durability at the cost of a slight latency increase — typically **< 1 ms** additional overhead.

---

## 2.2 The Four Pillars of EBS Volume Design

Every EBS volume is characterized by four engineering trade-offs, which form the basis of most KTU theory questions:

| Pillar | Definition | KTU-Expected Keyword |
|---|---|---|
| **Size** | Logical capacity in GiB | "Provisioned capacity" |
| **Throughput** | Data rate (MiB/s) | "Streaming workload" |
| **IOPS** | Operations per second | "Random I/O workload" |
| **Latency** | Time per operation | "Sub-millisecond" |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rule of Thumb for KTU Exams:**
> - **Database workloads (OLTP)** → prioritize **IOPS**.
> - **Streaming/Big Data (Hadoop, log processing)** → prioritize **Throughput**.
> - **Boot volumes / general purpose** → balanced.

---

## 2.3 EBS Volume Type Catalog

Amazon EBS offers **two storage backends** (SSD and HDD) and **six volume types** (as of 2024 KTU-relevant syllabus):

### 🔹 SSD-Backed Volumes (Optimized for IOPS & Latency)

| Type | Use Case | Volume Size | Max IOPS/volume | Max Throughput/volume |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **gp3** (General Purpose SSD) | Boot volumes, dev/test, mid-range DBs | 1 GiB – 16 TiB | **16000** | **1000 MiB/s** |
| **gp2** (Legacy General Purpose SSD) | Boot volumes, low-latency apps | 1 GiB – 16 TiB | 16000 (burst) | 250 MiB/s |
| **io2 Block Express** | Mission-critical, 99.999% durability | 4 GiB – 64 TiB | **256000** | 4000 MiB/s |
| **io1** (Provisioned IOPS SSD) | High-performance OLTP | 4 GiB – 16 TiB | 64000 | 1000 MiB/s |

### 🔸 HDD-Backed Volumes (Optimized for Throughput, NOT IOPS)

| Type | Use Case | Volume Size | Max Throughput/volume |
|---|---|---|---|
| **st1** (Throughput Optimized HDD) | Big data, log processing, Kafka | 125 GiB – 16 TiB | **500 MiB/s** |
| **sc1** (Cold HDD) | Infrequent access, archives | 125 GiB – 16 TiB | 250 MiB/s |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Trap:** Students often confuse `st1` and `sc1` with SSD volumes. They are **HDD-backed** and **cannot be used as boot volumes** because EC2 boot requires SSD.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following formulas are essential for KTU numerical/analytical questions on EBS provisioning.

### 📋 Table A: Performance Formulas

| Formula Name | Equation | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| **IOPS Density (gp2)** | $\text{IOPS} = 3 \times \text{Size (GiB)}$ | gp2 baseline IOPS at any size | operations/sec |
| **Burst IOPS (gp2)** | $\text{BurstIOPS} = 3000$ (if size $\leq 1000$ GiB) | gp2 burst ceiling | operations/sec |
| **Burst Credits Accumulation** | $\text{Credits/sec} = (\text{BurstIOPS} - \text{BaseIOPS}) / 60 \times 60$ | gp2 I/O credit accrual | credits |
| **Burst Duration (gp2)** | $\text{T} = \dfrac{\text{Bucket Size}}{\text{BurstIOPS} - \text{WorkloadIOPS}}$ | Max burst time at given workload | seconds |
| **Bucket Size (gp2)** | $\text{Bucket} = 5.4 \times 10^6 \text{ bytes}$ (≈ 5.4 MB) | Maximum accrued burst credits | bytes |
| **Throughput (gp3)** | $\text{Throughput} = 125 + 0.25 \times \text{Size (GiB)}$ | gp3 baseline throughput | MiB/s |
| **Throughput (io2)** | $\text{Throughput} = \min(\text{ProvisionedIOPS}/1024,\; 4000)$ | io2 max streaming throughput | MiB/s |
| **Throughput (st1)** | $\text{Throughput} = 40 + 0.125 \times \text{Size (GiB)}$ | st1 baseline throughput | MiB/s |
| **Cost (Simple)** | $\text{Cost} = (\text{GB-Mo} \times \text{Price/GB-Mo}) + (\text{PIOPS} \times \text{Price/PIOPS-Mo})$ | Monthly bill estimation | USD |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Critical:** The `\vert` and `\mid` LaTeX delimiters above are used in place of the vertical pipe symbol `|` to ensure the markdown table does not break rendering. If you see this in your study notes, do not "correct" them to pipes.

---

### 📋 Table B: Volume Type Selection Matrix

| Workload Profile | Recommended Type | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| MySQL/PostgreSQL OLTP database | **io2** or **io1** | High random read/write IOPS, low latency |
| Boot volume for EC2 | **gp3** or **gp2** | Balanced, low-latency, SSD required |
| Hadoop / MapReduce logs | **st1** | Sequential reads, high throughput, low cost |
| Cold archival data, rarely accessed | **sc1** | Lowest cost HDD, low throughput |
| Enterprise SAP HANA | **io2 Block Express** | Sub-ms latency, 256K IOPS |
| Web server static content cache | **gp3** | Balanced general purpose |
| Video streaming server | **st1** | Sequential streaming throughput |

---

## 2.5 Real-World Utility in Engineering

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry Use Cases (Aligns with KTU Outcome-Based Education employability goals):**

1. **Healthcare / Electronic Medical Records (EMR):** Hospitals use `io2` volumes for patient databases requiring HIPAA compliance, encryption, and 99.999% durability.
2. **Financial Trading Platforms:** Stock exchanges deploy `io2 Block Express` for tick-data ingestion where every microsecond of latency affects arbitrage outcomes.
3. **AI/ML Training Pipelines:** ML engineers attach `st1` volumes to GPU EC2 instances for reading massive training datasets sequentially.
4. **DevOps CI/CD:** Build servers use `gp3` for ephemeral yet persistent workspace storage across multiple EC2 lifecycle events.
5. **E-commerce:** Black Friday traffic spikes rely on EBS Multi-Attach `io2` volumes shared across a clustered application fleet.

---

## 2.6 EBS Snapshots — The Time-Machine Feature

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **EBS Snapshots are incremental, point-in-time backups stored durably in Amazon S3.**

Key engineering properties:

- **Incremental Nature:** Only the blocks that changed since the last snapshot are saved. Subsequent snapshots are therefore cheap and fast.
- **Lifecycle Ordering:** Snapshots must be deleted in reverse chronological order (or AWS automatically handles retention).
- **Cross-AZ / Cross-Region Copy:** Snapshots can be copied to other AZs (to restore a volume there) or other AWS Regions (for disaster recovery).
- **Encryption Inheritance:** If the source volume is encrypted, snapshots are automatically encrypted using the same KMS key.
- **Snapshot Sharing:** Snapshots can be shared across AWS accounts (public sharing is discouraged).

---

## 2.7 EBS Encryption (Security Module Integration)

EBS integrates with **AWS Key Management Service (KMS)** for at-rest encryption:

| Encryption Layer | Mechanism | Algorithm |
|---|---|---|
| Data at rest | AES-256-XTS | Symmetric block cipher |
| Data in transit (EC2 ↔ EBS) | TLS 1.2 / TLS 1.3 | Industry-standard transport |
| Snapshot encryption | KMS-wrapped DEKs | Envelope encryption |
| Key management | AWS KMS / Customer Managed Keys (CMK) | FIPS 140-2 validated |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Frequent Question:** *"What is the encryption overhead of EBS?"*
> **Answer:** Negligible — typically **< 1%** performance impact because AES-NI hardware acceleration is used in Nitro System instances.

---

## 2.8 EBS Multi-Attach Feature

By default, an EBS volume attaches to **only one EC2 instance**. The **EBS Multi-Attach** feature (available for `io1` and `io2` volume types) allows a single volume to be attached to **up to 16 EC2 instances** in the **same Availability Zone**.

> [!WARNING]
> Multi-Attach provides **concurrent block-level access but NOT a shared file system**. Applications must use **cluster-aware file systems** (e.g., OCFS2, GFS2) to coordinate writes and prevent corruption.

# 📘 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: gp2 Burst Duration Calculation

This is the **single most common KTU numerical problem** on EBS. We are given a workload and asked to calculate how long a burst can sustain.

### Problem Setup (KTU Style):
> An EC2 instance has an attached **500 GiB gp2 EBS volume**. A workload demands **3500 IOPS** continuously. Compute the **maximum burst duration** in seconds.

### Step-by-Step Deduction:

**Step 1 — Determine baseline IOPS for gp2 at 500 GiB**

For gp2 volumes, baseline IOPS = **3 IOPS per GiB**, capped at 3000 IOPS for sizes up to 1000 GiB.

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{BaseIOPS} &= 3 \times \text{Size} \\
&= 3 \times 500 \\
&= 1500 \;\text{IOPS}
\end{aligned}$$

Since 1500 < 3000, this is the baseline. No additional IOPS from size (capping rule not triggered).

**Step 2 — Identify the Burst IOPS ceiling**

For gp2 volumes $\leq$ 1000 GiB, the burst IOPS ceiling is fixed at 3000.

$$\text{BurstIOPS} = 3000$$

**Step 3 — Establish the Burst Bucket Capacity**

The gp2 burst bucket starts at full capacity = **5.4 × 10⁶ I/O credits**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why 5.4 × 10⁶?** AWS documentation states: *Bucket size = 5.4 × 10⁶ bytes*. In gp2 semantics, each credit represents one IO operation at the burst rate. The 5.4 × 10⁶ figure is derived from the time it takes to refill an empty bucket (300 seconds) at the maximum refill rate.

**Step 4 — Compute Credits Refill Rate**

Credits accrue at the rate of 3 credits per second per provisioned IOPS above baseline.

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Refill Rate} &= \text{BurstIOPS} - \text{BaseIOPS} \\
&= 3000 - 1500 \\
&= 1500 \;\text{credits/sec} \quad \text{(this is also the refill rate at burst ceiling)}
\end{aligned}$$

But for **draining** (the case during burst), the spend rate is workload IOPS minus baseline:

$$\text{Spend Rate} = \text{WorkloadIOPS} - \text{BaseIOPS}$$

**Step 5 — Compute the Effective Spend Rate**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Spend Rate} &= 3500 - 1500 \\
&= 2000 \;\text{credits/sec}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6 — Compute Maximum Burst Duration**

We assume the bucket starts at maximum capacity (worst case for longest burst).

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{T} &= \dfrac{\text{Bucket Size}}{\text{Spend Rate}} \\
&= \dfrac{5.4 \times 10^{6}}{2000} \\
&= 2700 \;\text{seconds} \\
&= 45 \;\text{minutes}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 7 — Interpretation**

> **After 45 minutes of sustained 3500 IOPS, the burst bucket will be empty. The volume will then throttle back to 1500 IOPS baseline.** If the workload drops back to ≤ 1500 IOPS, the bucket will refill at ~1500 credits/sec and reach full capacity in 3600 seconds (1 hour).

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: gp3 Throughput Provisioning

### Problem Setup:
> You need a volume of **2 TiB** for streaming data. Using gp3, compute the **baseline throughput**, the **maximum throughput if you provision extra throughput credits**, and the **IOPS you get for free**.

### Step-by-Step Deduction:

**Step 1 — Convert TiB to GiB**

$$2 \;\text{TiB} = 2 \times 1024 = 2048 \;\text{GiB}$$

**Step 2 — Baseline Throughput of gp3**

gp3 baseline throughput = 125 MiB/s regardless of size, **but** AWS documentation quotes the standard formula:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Throughput}_{\text{baseline}} &= 125 \;\text{MiB/s} \quad (\text{for sizes} \geq 400 \;\text{GiB})
\end{aligned}$$

For sizes < 400 GiB, the formula scales linearly: $125 \times \dfrac{\text{Size}}{400}$. Since 2048 GiB ≫ 400 GiB, we use 125 MiB/s.

**Step 3 — Free IOPS at 2048 GiB**

gp3 baseline IOPS scales as:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{BaseIOPS}_{\text{gp3}} &= 3000 \quad (\text{for sizes} \geq 400 \;\text{GiB})
\end{aligned}$$

So you get **3000 IOPS for free**.

**Step 4 — Cost Calculation for Provisioned Performance**

To increase IOPS to 10000 (additional 7000 IOPS) and throughput to 500 MiB/s (additional 375 MiB/s):

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Cost}_{\text{extra IOPS}} &= 7000 \times \$0.000005/\text{PIOPS-hr} \times 730 \;\text{hrs} \\
\text{Cost}_{\text{extra Throughput}} &= 375 \times \$0.00004/\text{MiBps-hr} \times 730 \;\text{hrs}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam note:** Exact pricing is not required. KTU questions typically ask for the **formulaic relationship**, not USD figures, unless explicitly provided in the question paper.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: IO2 Throughput Ceiling from Provisioned IOPS

### Problem Setup:
> An `io2` volume is provisioned with **100000 PIOPS**. Calculate the maximum sustained throughput.

### Step-by-Step Deduction:

For io2 volumes, throughput is the **minimum** of two constraints:

$$\text{Throughput}_{\max} = \min\left(\dfrac{\text{Provisioned IOPS}}{1024},\; 4000 \;\text{MiB/s}\right)$$

**Step 1 — Compute IOPS-derived Throughput**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{T}_{\text{IOPS}} &= \dfrac{100000}{1024} \\
&\approx 97.66 \;\text{MiB/s}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the Volume-Size Based Ceiling**

For io2 volumes, throughput also depends on size:

$$\text{T}_{\text{size}} = 0.256 \times \text{Size (GiB)}$$

But the absolute maximum is 4000 MiB/s. Since 97.66 is well below 4000:

$$\text{Throughput}_{\max} = 97.66 \;\text{MiB/s}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why divide by 1024?** This is the AWS-specific conversion assuming **4 KiB blocks** (the minimum I/O size for io2 volumes). Higher per-I/O sizes (e.g., 256 KiB) increase throughput proportionally, up to the ceiling.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — gp2 vs gp3 vs io2

| Attribute | gp2 | gp3 | io2 |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Backend** | SSD | SSD | SSD |
| **Size Range** | 1 GiB – 16 TiB | 1 GiB – 16 TiB | 4 GiB – 64 TiB |
| **Baseline IOPS** | $3 \times \text{Size}$ (cap 3000) | 3000 (size ≥ 400 GiB) | User-defined up to 256000 |
| **Max IOPS** | 16000 (burst) | 16000 (provisioned) | 256000 (Block Express) |
| **Baseline Throughput** | 128 – 250 MiB/s | 125 MiB/s | Variable |
| **Max Throughput** | 250 MiB/s | 1000 MiB/s | 4000 MiB/s |
| **Durability** | 99.8% – 99.9% | 99.8% – 99.9% | **99.999%** |
| **Burst Model** | Credit-based | Provisioned (no bursting) | Provisioned |
| **Latency** | Single-digit ms | Single-digit ms | Sub-millisecond |
| **Cost Predictability** | Variable (burst) | **High** (predictable) | **High** (predictable) |

---

## 3.5 Worked Example: Estimating Required Volume for a Database

**Scenario:** A PostgreSQL database requires:
- 1 TiB storage
- 5000 sustained IOPS
- 200 MiB/s throughput

**Solution:**

1. **Check gp3 fit:**
   - Storage: ✅ (≤ 16 TiB)
   - IOPS: Requires provisioning **5000 - 3000 = 2000 extra IOPS** at additional cost.
   - Throughput: Requires provisioning **200 - 125 = 75 MiB/s** extra.

2. **Check io2 fit (alternative):**
   - Provision 5000 PIOPS directly.
   - Throughput = $5000/1024 \approx 4.88$ MiB/s (TOO LOW).
   - **Therefore io2 is unsuitable** — need higher PIOPS to meet throughput.

3. **Final Recommendation: gp3** with provisioned extra IOPS + throughput, since the workload is balanced.
