# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Q: Define Google App Engine. List any two distinguishing features between its Standard and Flexible environments.**

**Model Answer:**

**Google App Engine (GAE)** is a fully managed **Platform as a Service (PaaS)** offering from Google Cloud Platform that enables developers to build and deploy applications on Google's infrastructure without managing the underlying hardware or runtime.

**Two distinguishing features:**

1. **Runtime Support** — Standard Environment supports only pre-defined Google-managed language runtimes (e.g., Python 3.12, Java 21, Node.js 20). Flexible Environment supports **any custom runtime** via Docker containers.

2. **Scaling Speed & Cold Starts** — Standard scales in **milliseconds to seconds** with low cold-start latency. Flexible scales in **seconds to minutes** with longer cold starts because entire Docker containers must boot.

3. *(Extra, if needed)* **Pricing Granularity** — Standard charges per-instance-class per-hour; Flexible charges per-vCPU, per-memory, per-second.

*(2 distinguishing features = 2 marks; definition = 1 mark)*

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Q: What is a "cold start" in GAE? How does it affect application latency?**

**Model Answer:**

A **cold start** is the latency overhead incurred when a new GAE instance is created and initialized to handle an incoming request, as opposed to serving from an already-warm instance.

**Effects on latency:**
- The first request to a cold instance experiences **additional latency** ranging from **~100 ms (Standard)** to **~15–30 s (Flexible)**.
- It includes container boot time, language runtime initialization, and application code loading.
- It can be **mitigated** by setting `min_instances` to a non-zero value, which keeps a warm pool of instances always available.
- It is **most noticeable** during traffic spikes when auto-scaling rapidly spins up new instances.

*(Concept definition = 1.5 marks; effect on latency = 1.5 marks)*

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the architecture of Google App Engine with a neat block diagram. Describe the role of each layer.

**Model Answer:**

The architecture of Google App Engine consists of **five logical layers**, as detailed below.

**1. Front-End Layer (Load Balancer & Routing):**
The Front-End receives all incoming HTTP/HTTPS requests at `<project-id>.appspot.com`. It performs **geographic routing** to the nearest Google data center, performs **SSL termination**, and dispatches requests across healthy application instances.

**2. Application Sandbox / Runtime Layer:**
Each application instance runs inside a **secure sandbox** that isolates memory, restricts system calls, and enforces resource limits. In Standard Environment, the sandbox is language-specific (e.g., Java JVM, CPython interpreter). In Flexible Environment, it is a full Docker container.

**3. Application Code Layer:**
The user-deployed code version. Each deployment creates an **immutable version ID**. Traffic can be routed across multiple versions (canary releases, A/B testing).

**4. Backend Services Layer:**
GAE exposes a set of **managed backend APIs** including:
- **Datastore / Firestore** — managed NoSQL data store
- **Memcache** — in-memory key-value cache
- **Task Queues** — asynchronous job execution
- **Cloud SQL** — managed relational database
- **Blobstore / Cloud Storage** — large binary object storage
- **Mail, User, Search** — auxiliary services

**5. Infrastructure Layer:**
This includes the **physical servers, networking, storage, and operating system** — all fully managed by Google and invisible to the user.

**Block Diagram (already shown in Section 4.1 — refer to it during the exam).**

*(5 layers × 1 mark each = 5 marks; neat block diagram = 2 marks)*

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** Compare the various **scaling modes** supported by GAE. Under what conditions would you choose Automatic Scaling over Manual Scaling? Justify.

**Model Answer:**

GAE supports **four scaling modes**:

| Mode | Behavior | Trigger | When to Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Automatic** | Instances are dynamically created/destroyed based on request rate, latency, CPU | Defined in `app.yaml` via `automatic_scaling` block | Variable, bursty, unpredictable traffic |
| **Manual** | A fixed number of instances always run | Defined in `app.yaml` via `manual_scaling.instances` | Steady, predictable load |
| **Basic** | Instances are created on demand, shut down during idle | Default for older apps | Low-traffic cost-sensitive apps |
| **Idle / min_instances** | Reserve a minimum pool of warm instances | `automatic_scaling.min_idle_instances` | Latency-sensitive apps where cold starts are unacceptable |

**When to choose Automatic over Manual:**

Choose **Automatic Scaling** when:
1. Traffic is **highly variable or unpredictable** (e.g., e-commerce flash sales, news website during breaking news).
2. You want **cost optimization** — only pay for instances that are actually serving load.
3. The application can tolerate **brief cold-start latency**.
4. You want **hands-off scalability** without an operator monitoring load.

Choose **Manual Scaling** when:
1. Traffic is **steady and predictable** (e.g., internal cron job, fixed-load service).
2. You need **absolute control** over the number of instances.
3. The application does **not** benefit from elastic scaling.

**Justification:** Automatic Scaling is preferred for **modern web/mobile applications** with fluctuating traffic because it directly aligns compute cost with user demand, eliminates the need for capacity planning, and provides resilience against traffic spikes — a hallmark advantage of cloud-native PaaS platforms.

*(4 scaling modes identified = 4 marks; conditions for choosing = 2 marks; justification = 1 mark)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **services and APIs** provided by Google App Engine. How does GAE abstract infrastructure management from the developer?

**Model Answer:**

GAE provides a rich set of **managed backend services and APIs** that allow developers to focus purely on business logic.

**Major Services:**

1. **Datastore / Firestore** — A managed, schemaless, highly available NoSQL document database with built-in transactions, indexes, and queries. Developers do not provision, patch, or replicate databases.

2. **Memcache** — A distributed in-memory caching layer for low-latency read access. No need to install/configure Redis or Memcached servers.

3. **Task Queues** — Enable asynchronous background job processing. Developers push tasks; GAE handles scheduling, retry, and execution.

4. **Cloud SQL** — Managed MySQL/PostgreSQL instances with automatic patching and backups.

5. **Cloud Storage / Blobstore** — For storing and serving large files (images, videos, logs).

6. **User Service** — Built-in authentication using Google accounts or federated identity.

7. **Mail API** — Send transactional emails from the application.

8. **Search API** — Full-text search over documents.

9. **URL Fetch Service** — Make outbound HTTP/HTTPS calls to other services (Standard env).

**How GAE abstracts infrastructure:**

- The developer **never sees a VM, OS, or container configuration** (in Standard).
- **Scaling, load balancing, health checks, patching, and high availability** are all handled automatically.
- The developer writes `app.yaml` to declaratively specify runtime, scaling, and service configuration.
- The developer calls a **simple API** (e.g., `Datastore.put(entity)`) and Google manages the database.
- This aligns with the **PaaS principle**: the platform handles *how* the application runs, leaving the developer to focus on *what* the application does.

*(Listing 6+ services = 4 marks; explanation of abstraction = 3 marks)*

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** A startup deploys a web application on GAE Standard using **F2 instance class** (price = $0.10/hour). The app runs **5 instances** continuously for a 30-day month. Calculate the monthly bill, taking the free tier (28 instance-hours/day) into account. Also explain the difference between **horizontal** and **vertical** scaling in the GAE context.

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1: Total instance-hours per day.**

$$
H_{day} = 5 \times 24 = 120 \text{ instance-hours}
$$

**Step 2: Total instance-hours per month.**

$$
H_{month} = 120 \times 30 = 3600 \text{ instance-hours}
$$

**Step 3: Free tier quota for the month.**

$$
Q_{free} = 28 \times 30 = 840 \text{ instance-hours}
$$

**Step 4: Billable instance-hours.**

$$
H_{billable} = H_{month} - Q_{free} = 3600 - 840 = 2760 \text{ instance-hours}
$$

**Step 5: Final cost.**

$$
C = H_{billable} \times R_{hourly} = 2760 \times 0.10 = 276.00 \text{ USD}
$$

**Final Monthly Bill = $276.00**

**Horizontal vs Vertical Scaling in GAE:**

- **Horizontal Scaling (Scale Out / In):** Adding or removing **more instances** of the same size. GAE Standard **primarily supports horizontal scaling** via Automatic and Manual modes. This is the **default and recommended approach** because it is stateless-friendly and provides better fault tolerance.

- **Vertical Scaling (Scale Up / Down):** Increasing or decreasing the **resources (CPU, RAM)** of an existing instance. In GAE Standard, vertical scaling is done by switching to a **larger instance class** (F2 → F4 → F4_1G). Vertical scaling typically requires a brief instance restart.

**In the GAE context**, horizontal scaling is the natural fit because:
1. App Engine instances are **stateless by design** (state is stored in Datastore/Memcache).
2. Horizontal scaling provides **better high availability** (no single point of failure).
3. It is **automated** by GAE based on traffic metrics, requiring zero developer intervention.

*(Cost calculation steps = 4 marks; horizontal vs vertical explanation = 3 marks)*

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Common ways students LOSE marks on GAE questions:**
> 1. **Writing "GAE is a SaaS"** — GAE is **PaaS**, NOT SaaS. Markers deduct 1 full mark for this classification error.
> 2. **Confusing "cold start" with "load balancing"** — Cold start = instance initialization latency; load balancing = traffic distribution. These are different concepts.
> 3. **Skipping the diagram in 7-mark questions** — KTU mandates a "neat diagram" for full marks on architecture questions. Always draw even a basic block diagram.
> 4. **Using wrong CLI command syntax** — Writing `gcloud deploy` instead of `gcloud app deploy` loses 1 mark.
> 5. **Ignoring the free tier in cost problems** — Many students calculate gross cost and forget to subtract the **28 free instance-hours/day** quota.
> 6. **Mixing up GAE Standard vs Flexible** — A common trap: "Does Standard support Docker?" — **No**. "Does Flexible support SSH?" — **Yes**. Getting these backwards costs marks.
> 7. **Not defining "sandbox" in architecture questions** — Mentioning "sandbox" without explaining it (memory isolation, restricted syscalls) loses 0.5–1 mark.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- GAE = **PaaS** from Google Cloud; first launched in **2008**.
- Two environments: **Standard** (predefined runtimes, fast scaling) and **Flexible** (custom Docker, full Linux).
- Architecture has **5 layers**: Front-End, Sandbox/Runtime, Application Code, Backend Services, Infrastructure.
- Four **scaling modes**: Automatic, Manual, Basic, Idle (min_instances).
- Built-in services: **Datastore/Firestore, Memcache, Task Queues, Cloud SQL, Blobstore, User, Mail, Search**.
- **Cold start** = first-request latency on a newly spawned instance; mitigated by `min_instances`.
- **Deployment command** = `gcloud app deploy`.
- **Free tier** = 28 instance-hours/day (subject to Google Cloud policy changes).
- **Horizontal scaling** is GAE's natural model; vertical scaling done by changing instance class.
- Configuration is **declarative** via `app.yaml` (YAML file).
- GAE apps are served at `https://<project-id>.appspot.com`.
- GAE supports **traffic splitting** across multiple versions (canary deployments).
- **No persistent local disk** in Standard Environment; Flexible has ephemeral disk.
- GAE's sandbox in Standard **restricts outbound networking** to HTTP/HTTPS only.
- KTU high-yield terms: *serverless PaaS, auto-scaling, managed services, sandbox, instance class*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Exam Tip:** When a KTU question says *"Explain GAE as a PaaS offering"*, the answer should always include: (1) definition, (2) which layer is managed by Google (everything below the application), (3) at least 3 examples of managed services, and (4) a comparison vs IaaS/SaaS to show why it is PaaS.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Storage Services
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECPT525 - CLOUD COMPUTING > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Storage Services # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 What is Cloud Storage? **Cloud Storage** is a service model in which data is transmitted and stored on remote storage systems, maintained, managed, backed up, and made available to users over a network — typically the public Internet. It is a major component of the cloud computing stack, providing **scalability, durability, availability, and pay-per-use economics** that traditional on-premise storage cannot match. > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Definition (Syllabus-Aligned):** > "Cloud storage is a cloud computing model that stores data on the Internet through a cloud computing provider who manages and operates data storage as a service. It is delivered on demand with just-in-time capacity and costs, and eliminates buying and managing your own data storage infrastructure." ### Three Foundational Storage Paradigms Cloud storage is broadly classified into three primary data service abstractions. These are *not* competitors — they coexist, and the choice depends on the **use case, access pattern, and data structure**. | Abstraction | Granularity | Access Method | Typical Use Case | |---|---|---|---| | **Object Storage** | Whole object (blob) | RESTful HTTP API (`GET`, `PUT`, `DELETE`) | Media files, backups, Big Data, static website assets | | **Block Storage** | Fixed-size block (typically 16 KB – 256 KB) | Mounted as a raw disk via iSCSI/FC | Databases, OS boot volumes, low-latency transactional workloads | | **File Storage** | Hierarchical file/folder | NFS, SMB/CIFS mount | Shared home directories, lift-and-shift legacy apps, content management | --- ## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Smart Warehouse Network" > [!IMPORTANT] > **Conceptual Analogy: The Three Warehouses** Imagine you run a national logistics business. You have **three kinds of warehouses**, each optimized for a different cargo type: 1. **Object Warehouse (e.g., Amazon S3)** Think of a massive, automated warehouse where every item is placed in a **sealed box** with a unique barcode (the *key*). Workers cannot walk in and rearrange items — they only fetch the whole box by its barcode. **You cannot edit the contents; you replace the entire box.** *Best for:* Photos, videos, documents, backups — anything read or replaced as a whole. 2. **Block Warehouse (e.g., Amazon EBS)** Picture a private, dedicated storage room attached to a single office. The room is divided into **uniform numbered bins (blocks)**. The office worker can read/write to any bin anytime, very fast. Only that one office uses the room. *Best for:* Databases, operating system disks — workloads needing millisecond latency and random writes. 3. **File Warehouse (e.g., Amazon EFS / Azure Files)** Imagine a **shared corporate filing cabinet** accessible by many offices simultaneously. Files live in folders, folders live in folders, and everyone follows the same organizational rules (NFS/SMB). *Best for:* Shared application data, home directories, content repositories. This analogy maps directly onto the three real-world cloud services used by every KTU 2024 student. --- ## 1.3 Key Quality Metrics Every Cloud Storage System Guarantees > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Metrics You MUST Memorize (KTU Board-Exam Hot List):** - **Durability:** Probability that data will *not* be lost over a year. Example: Amazon S3 Standard offers **99.999999999 % (11 nines)** durability, i.e., if you store **10 million objects**, you can expect to lose **one object every 10,000 years**. - **Availability:** Percentage of time the service is operational and accessible. Example: S3 Standard = **99.99 %** availability (≈ 52.6 minutes downtime/year). - **Latency:** Time between a request and the first byte of response (ms or µs). - **Throughput:** Data transferred per unit time (MB/s, GB/s). - **IOPS:** Input/Output Operations Per Second — critical for block storage. - **Consistency Model:** Strong, eventual, or read-your-writes. > [!NOTE] > **SLA Convention:** 99.9 % = "three nines", 99.99 % = "four nines", 99.999 % = "five nines". A jump from three nines to four nines cuts allowed downtime by 90 %. --- # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Architectural Layers of a Cloud Storage System A production cloud storage system is built as a **layered stack**. Understanding the layers is essential for KTU Module-4 questions on architecture and design. ### Layer 1 — Frontend (Access Interface) - **REST/HTTP API** for object storage (S3, Azure Blob, GCS). - **POSIX-compliant mount** for block and file storage. - **SDKs** in Java, Python, Node.js, etc. ### Layer 2 — Control Plane (Metadata & Management) - Stores **metadata** (object name, size, content-type, version, ACL). - Manages **authentication & authorization** (IAM, OAuth, signature v4). - Enforces **quotas, replication policies, lifecycle rules**. ### Layer 3 — Data Plane (Storage Backbone) - The actual disks (HDDs, SSDs, NVMe). - Often uses **Distributed Hash Table (DHT)** to locate objects. - Implements **erasure coding** or **replication** for durability. ### Layer 4 — Backend Infrastructure (Physical) - Geographically distributed **data centers** organized into **Regions** and **Availability Zones (AZs)**. - Region = a physical geographic area (e.g., `ap-south-1` Mumbai). - AZ = one or more discrete data centers with redundant power/networking. --- ## 2.2 Consistency Models — A Board-Favorite Topic A consistency model defines **how recent / synchronized** the data returned by a read is, after a write. | Model | Guarantee | Example Service | |---|---|---| | **Strong Consistency** | After a write completes, *all* subsequent reads (anywhere) return the new value. | Amazon S3 (post-2020), Azure Cosmos DB (configurable) | | **Eventual Consistency** | If no new updates are made, *eventually* all reads will return the latest value. | Older S3, DynamoDB (default), DNS | | **Read-your-writes** | A process that wrote a value will always see that value in its own subsequent reads. | S3, DynamoDB | | **Causal Consistency** | Operations causally related are seen in the same order by all nodes. | Some NoSQL systems | --- ## 2.3 Durability and Availability — The Engineering Math Cloud providers achieve **11 nines of durability** by combining **redundancy** with **failure-domain isolation**. ### Replication Strategy - **Synchronous Replication:** Write is acknowledged only after being copied to *all* replicas. Slow but safe. - **Asynchronous Replication:** Write is acknowledged after being written to the primary; replicas catch up later. Fast but vulnerable to failure window. ### Erasure Coding (Reed-Solomon) Splits an object into $k$ data fragments and computes $m$ parity fragments. The original object can be reconstructed from **any $k$ of the $k+m$ fragments**. $$n = k + m$$ where: - $k$ = number of data fragments - $m$ = number of parity fragments - $n$ = total fragments stored **Example:** With $k = 10$ and $m = 4$, the system tolerates the simultaneous loss of any 4 fragments. ### The Durability Formula If each fragment has independent probability $p$ of surviving one year, durability $D$ is: $$D = \sum_{i=k}^{n} \binom{n}{i} p^{i} (1-p)^{n-i}$$ > [!NOTE] > For a 14-nine durability target with $k=10, m=4$, individual fragment survival $p$ must be ≥ 0.9999… (very high), justifying why hyperscalers use specialized hardware and AZ-isolated placement. --- ## 2.4 Storage Classes / Service Tiers (AWS S3 as Reference Model) | Tier | Availability | Use Case | Retrieval Cost | |---|---|---|---| | **S3 Standard** | 99.99 % | Frequently accessed data | None | | **S3 Intelligent-Tiering** | 99.9 % | Unknown/changing access | None | | **S3 Standard-IA** | 99.9 % | Infrequent access, rapid retrieval | Per-GB | | **S3 One Zone-IA** | 99.5 % | Re-creatable, infrequent | Per-GB | | **S3 Glacier Instant Retrieval** | 99.9 % | Archive, millisecond retrieval | Per-GB | | **S3 Glacier Flexible Retrieval** | 99.99 % | Archive, minutes-to-hours | Per-GB + request | | **S3 Glacier Deep Archive** | 99.99 % | Long-term archive, 12+ hours | Lowest \$ | > [!IMPORTANT] > Azure equivalent: Hot, Cool, Cold, Archive Blob tiers. > GCP equivalent: Standard, Nearline, Coldline, Archive. --- ## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Use | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Erasure Coding fragments | $n = k + m$ | Determine storage overhead | | 2 | Storage overhead ratio | $\text{OH} = \dfrac{n}{k} = 1 + \dfrac{m}{k}$ | Capacity planning | | 3 | Durability (simplified) | $D \approx 1 - (1-p)^n$ when $k=1$ | Quick estimate | | 4 | Availability → downtime | $\text{DT} = (1 - A) \times 525{,}600 \text{ min/yr}$ | SLA conversion | | 5 | Replication factor | $R$ = number of copies | Cost vs. durability | | 6 | Replication storage cost | $C_{\text{repl}} = R \times C_{\text{primary}}$ | Total storage cost | | 7 | Cross-region replication latency | $L_{\text{cr}} = 2 \times RTT + W$ | Async write time | | 8 | S3 standard durability | $D_{\text{S3}} = 0.99999999999$ | 11 nines | | 9 | Storage cost per GB-month | $C = \dfrac{\text{Monthly \$}}{\text{GB stored}}$ | TCO analysis | | 10 | CDN cache hit ratio | $H = \dfrac{\text{Hits}}{\text{Hits + Misses}}$ | Cache efficiency | > Real-world utility: erasure coding and replication math are used by **NetApp, Azure Blob (LRC), Facebook (HDFS-RAID), and Google Colossus** — every KTU viva must reference at least one real implementation. --- # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Worked Derivation 1 — Converting SLA to Allowed Annual Downtime The KTU board frequently asks: *"A cloud storage service offers 99.95 % availability. How many minutes of downtime are permitted per year?"* **Step 1 — Total minutes in a (non-leap) year.** $$\text{Total} = 365 \times 24 \times 60 = 525{,}600 \text{ min}$$ **Step 2 — Compute the allowed downtime fraction.** $$\text{DT} = (1 - A) \times \text{Total min}$$ **Step 3 — Substitute $A = 0.9995$.** $$\text{DT} = (1 - 0.9995) \times 525{,}600$$ **Step 4 — Simplify the bracket.** $$1 - 0.9995 = 0.0005$$ **Step 5 — Final multiplication.** $$\text{DT} = 0.0005 \times 525{,}600 = 262.8 \text{ min/year}$$ **Step 6 — Convert to hours for intuition.** $$262.8 \text{ min} \div 60 = 4.38 \text{ hours/year}$$ > **Engineering Insight:** A 99.95 % SLA sounds impressive but permits ~4.4 hours of annual downtime — acceptable for batch analytics, **NOT** for OLTP financial trading systems which require 99.999 % (≈ 5.26 min/year). --- ## 3.2 Worked Derivation 2 — Erasure Coding Storage Overhead **Problem:** A cloud object store uses Reed-Solomon erasure coding with $k = 6$ data fragments and $m = 3$ parity fragments. A user uploads a 300 GB file. Calculate: 1. Total fragments stored. 2. Size of each fragment. 3. Total physical storage used. 4. Storage overhead ratio. 5. Maximum simultaneous fragment losses tolerated. **Step 1 — Total fragments.** $$n = k + m = 6 + 3 = 9$$ **Step 2 — Size of each fragment.** $$\text{size per fragment} = \dfrac{300 \text{ GB}}{k} = \dfrac{300}{6} = 50 \text{ GB}$$ **Step 3 — Total physical storage.** $$\text{Total} = n \times \text{size per fragment} = 9 \times 50 = 450 \text{ GB}$$ **Step 4 — Storage overhead ratio.** $$\text{OH} = \dfrac{n}{k} = \dfrac{9}{6} = 1.5 \; (50\% \text{ overhead})$$ **Step 5 — Fault tolerance.** The system can lose any $m = 3$ fragments and still reconstruct the original 300 GB file. The loss of any 4th fragment makes reconstruction impossible. > **Comparison with 3-way replication (R = 3):** > Replication overhead = $\frac{3}{1} = 3.0$ (200 % overhead). For the same fault tolerance of 3 simultaneous failures, erasure coding uses **half the storage** — a critical engineering trade-off. --- ## 3.3 Worked Derivation 3 — S3 Durability Calculation **Problem:** A startup stores 1 billion objects in S3 Standard. Using the published durability of 99.999999999 % (11 nines), calculate the expected annual data loss. **Step 1 — Loss probability per object per year.** $$P_{\text{loss}} = 1 - D = 1 - 0.99999999999 = 1 \times 10^{-11}$$ **Step 2 — Expected losses.** $$E[\text{losses}] = N \times P_{\text{loss}}$$ **Step 3 — Substitute $N = 1{,}000{,}000{,}000$.** $$E[\text{losses}] = 10^{9} \times 10^{-11} = 10^{-2} = 0.01 \text{ objects/year}$$ **Step 4 — Interpretation.** A user storing 1 billion objects can statistically expect to lose **0.01 objects per year**, i.e., **one object every 100 years** — which is the engineering basis for S3's reputation as the gold standard of cloud storage. --- ## 3.4 Worked Derivation 4 — Cross-Region Replication (CRR) Write Latency **Problem:** An object is written to a primary region with synchronous local write latency $W_{\text{local}} = 10$ ms. It is asynchronously replicated to a backup region 3000 km away (round-trip network RTT ≈ 30 ms). The replication daemon polls every $T_{\text{cycle}} = 5$ s. Estimate the **average** time until the object exists in the backup region. **Step 1 — Local write acknowledgment time.** $$T_{\text{write}} = W_{\text{local}} = 10 \text{ ms}$$ **Step 2 — Worst-case lag before replication cycle picks the object.** On average, a new object waits half a polling cycle: $$T_{\text{wait}} = \dfrac{T_{\text{cycle}}}{2} = \dfrac{5000}{2} = 2500 \text{ ms}$$ **Step 3 — Network transfer time.** $$T_{\text{net}} = \text{RTT} = 30 \text{ ms}$$ **Step 4 — Total average replication lag.** $$T_{\text{repl}} = T_{\text{write}} + T_{\text{wait}} + T_{\text{net}}$$ **Step 5 — Substitute values.** $$T_{\text{repl}} = 10 + 2500 + 30 = 2540 \text{ ms} \approx 2.54 \text{ s}$$ > **Engineering Insight:** The CRR RPO (Recovery Point Objective) is therefore ≈ 2.5 s on average. A region failure could lose up to ~5 s of writes — acceptable for most apps, **unacceptable** for stock trading. --- ## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Block vs File vs Object | Dimension | Block Storage | File Storage | Object Storage | |---|---|---|---| | **Access Protocol** | iSCSI, FC, NVMe-oF | NFS, SMB | HTTP/REST, S3 API | | **Data Unit** | Block (16 KB – 256 KB) | File (KB – TB) | Object (KB – 5 TB) | | **Mutability** | Random read/write | Random read/write | Replace whole object | | **Metadata** | Limited (LBA, size) | POSIX (owner, perms) | Rich custom metadata | | **Search Capability** | None | Filename/path only | Key prefix, tags, SQL | | **Typical Latency** | sub-millisecond | 1–10 ms | 10–100 ms | | **Throughput** | Very high | High | Moderate–High | | **Scalability** | Limited by single VM | Limited by file system | Virtually unlimited (exabyte) | | **Cost / GB-month** | Highest | Medium | Lowest | | **Concurrent Clients** | Single attach (mostly) | Hundreds–thousands | Millions | | **Best Provider Example** | Amazon EBS, Azure Disk | Amazon EFS, Azure Files | Amazon S3, Azure Blob | | **Use Case** | Database, OS disk | Shared file shares, CMS | Media, backups, data lake | --- # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Cloud Storage Layered Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph CLIENT["CLIENT LAYER"] A1[Web Browser] A2[Mobile App] A3[EC2 VM] A4[Lambda Function] end subgraph ACCESS["ACCESS / API LAYER"] B1[HTTPS REST API] B2[POSIX Mount NFS] B3[Block Device Driver iSCSI] end subgraph CONTROL["CONTROL PLANE"] C1[Authentication IAM] C2[Metadata Service] C3[Lifecycle Policies] C4[Replication Manager] end subgraph DATA["DATA PLANE"] D1[Index Service DHT] D2[Storage Nodes Disk] D3[Erasure Coding Engine] end subgraph PHYSICAL["PHYSICAL INFRASTRUCTURE"] E1[Region ap-south-1] E2[Availability Zone 1a] E3[Availability Zone 1b] E4[Availability Zone 1c] end A1 --> B1 A2 --> B1 A3 --> B1 A3 --> B2 A3 --> B3 A4 --> B1 B1 --> C1 B2 --> C1 B3 --> C1 C1 --> C2 C1 --> C3 C1 --> C4 C2 --> D1 C3 --> D2 C4 --> D3 D1 --> D2 D3 --> D2 D2 --> E2 D2 --> E3 D2 --> E4 E2 --> E1 E3 --> E1 E4 --> E1 ``` ## 4.2 Storage Service Decision Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD Q1[Need to store data?] --> Q2{Access pattern?} Q2 -->|Random low-latency writes| R1[Block Storage
EBS Azure Disk] Q2 -->|Shared file system| R2[File Storage
EFS Azure Files] Q2 -->|Whole objects over HTTP| R3[Object Storage
S3 Azure Blob] Q2 -->|Archive cold data| R4[Glacier / Archive Blob] R1 --> X1[Database
OS Boot
Transaction Logs] R2 --> X2[CMS
Home Directories
Lift and Shift] R3 --> X3[Media Files
Static Website
Data Lake
Backups] R4 --> X4[Compliance Archives
Disaster Recovery] ``` ## 4.3 S3 Object Storage Internal Anatomy ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph OBJ["S3 OBJECT"] M1[Key: photos/2024/kerala.jpg] M2[Value: Binary Data Bytes] M3[Version ID: abc123] M4[Metadata: Content-Type size ACL] M5[Tags: env=prod] end subgraph BUCKET["S3 BUCKET my-kerala-bucket"] OBJ end subgraph REGL["REGION ap-south-1"] AZA[AZ a] AZB[AZ b] AZC[AZ c] BUCKET -.replicated.-> AZA BUCKET -.replicated.-> AZB BUCKET -.replicated.-> AZC end ``` ## 4.4 Content Delivery Network (CDN) Flow ```mermaid flowchart LR U[User in Kochi] -->|HTTPS GET image.jpg| EDGE[CloudFront Edge Location Mumbai] EDGE -->|Cache MISS| ORIGIN[S3 Bucket Origin ap-south-1] ORIGIN -->|Returns object| EDGE EDGE -->|Caches for TTL| EDGE EDGE -->|HTTPS 200 image.jpg| U U2[User in Delhi] --> EDGE U2 -->|Cache HIT served from edge| EDGE ``` ## 4.5 Lifecycle Policy State Machine ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> Standard Standard --> StandardIA: After 30 days no access StandardIA --> Glacier: After 90 days no access Glacier --> DeepArchive: After 365 days no access DeepArchive --> [*]: Expire delete after 7 years Standard --> [*]: Explicit delete StandardIA --> Standard: Access detected ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## Part A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions ### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model]` **Differentiate between Object Storage and Block Storage in cloud computing.** **Model Answer:** | Aspect | Object Storage | Block Storage | |---|---|---| | **Data Unit** | Whole object (blob) accessed via key | Fixed-size blocks addressed by LBA | | **Access Method** | RESTful HTTP API | Mounted as raw disk (iSCSI/FC) | | **Mutability** | Replace whole object | Random read/write to any block | | **Latency** | 10–100 ms | sub-millisecond | | **Scalability** | Virtually unlimited (exabytes) | Limited to single VM attachment | | **Use Case** | Media, backups, data lakes | Databases, OS boot disks | | **Example** | Amazon S3, Azure Blob | Amazon EBS, Azure Disk | Object storage is optimized for **throughput and scale**; block storage is optimized for **low-latency random I/O**. --- ### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Model]` **What is meant by "11 nines of durability" in cloud storage? Calculate the expected annual data loss for 100 million objects.** **Model Answer:** "11 nines of durability" means a **99.999999999 %** probability that an object will *not* be lost over a year. It is the durability guarantee offered by Amazon S3 Standard. **Calculation:** $$P_{\text{loss}} = 1 - 0.99999999999 = 10^{-11}$$ $$E[\text{loss}] = 10^{8} \times 10^{-11} = 10^{-3} = 0.001 \text{ objects/year}$$ Interpretation: statistically, **one object lost every 1000 years** for 100 million stored objects. --- ## Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **(a)** Explain the three primary cloud storage service models — Object, Block, and File storage — with suitable use cases and one real-world provider example for each. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Describe the architecture of a typical cloud object storage system. With a neat diagram, explain the roles of the access layer, control plane (metadata), and data plane (storage nodes). Discuss how **erasure coding** improves durability over simple replication. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution for Q.A(a) The three primary cloud storage service models are: **1. Object Storage** - Stores data as discrete units called *objects*, each containing the data, metadata, and a unique identifier (key). - Accessed via RESTful HTTP APIs (`GET`, `PUT`, `DELETE`). - Cannot be mounted as a filesystem; applications must use the API or SDKs. - **Use case:** Storing unstructured data such as images, videos, backups, log files, data lakes for analytics. - **Example:** Amazon S3, Azure Blob Storage, Google Cloud Storage. **2. Block Storage** - Divides data into equally sized blocks; each block is addressed by a Logical Block Address (LBA). - Attached to a single virtual machine as if it were a physical disk. - Supports random read/write with very low latency. - **Use case:** Boot volumes for VMs, relational databases (MySQL, Oracle), transactional workloads requiring high IOPS. - **Example:** Amazon EBS, Azure Managed Disks, Google Persistent Disk. **3. File Storage** - Provides a traditional hierarchical filesystem with folders and files. - Mounted by multiple compute instances simultaneously over NFS (Linux) or SMB (Windows). - POSIX-compliant, so legacy applications work without modification. - **Use case:** Shared home directories, content management systems (WordPress, Drupal), lift-and-shift of legacy applications. - **Example:** Amazon EFS, Azure Files, Google Filestore. > **Conclusion:** The choice depends on access pattern, latency, scalability, and cost — object for scale, block for performance, file for shared access. --- #### Model Solution for Q.A(b) A typical cloud object storage architecture has four logical layers: **1. Access Layer** - Provides the public interface — RESTful HTTP API over HTTPS, SDKs (Java/Python/Go), and CLI tools. - Authenticates requests using AWS Signature V4, OAuth 2.0, or Azure AD tokens. - Routes requests to the appropriate metadata and data services. **2. Control Plane (Metadata Service)** - Manages *where* an object lives — bucket policies, ACLs, versioning, object locks, lifecycle rules. - Stores metadata in a separate, highly consistent database (often NoSQL like DynamoDB or Cassandra). - Handles namespace management (bucket creation, listing). **3. Data Plane (Storage Backbone)** - The actual storage nodes containing the binary object data. - Uses a **Distributed Hash Table (DHT)** or consistent hashing to map object keys to storage nodes. - Stores data on commodity HDDs/SSDs, often grouped into **chunksets**. **4. Physical Infrastructure** - Geographically distributed across multiple **Availability Zones (AZs)** within a **Region**. - Each AZ has independent power, cooling, and networking — failure of one AZ does not affect others. **Erasure Coding vs. Replication:** | Scheme | Storage Overhead | Fault Tolerance | Best For | |---|---|---|---| | 3-way Replication (R=3) | 200 % | Lose any 2 of 3 copies | Hot data, small scale | | Erasure Coding (k=10, m=4) | 40 % | Lose any 4 of 14 fragments | Cold data, petabyte+ scale | Erasure coding splits an object into $k$ data fragments and $m$ parity fragments, distributed across AZs. The original is recoverable from **any $k$ fragments**. This achieves comparable durability to 3-way replication at **less than one-quarter the storage cost**, making it the preferred technique in modern systems (Azure LRC, Facebook HDFS-RAID, Google Colossus). --- ### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Model]` **(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the working of a **Content Delivery Network (CDN)** integrated with object storage. Discuss cache hit ratio, TTL, and origin shield. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Compare the various **storage tiers** offered by cloud providers (using AWS S3 as reference). Describe how lifecycle policies automate data movement across tiers. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution for Q.B(a) A **Content Delivery Network (CDN)** is a geographically distributed network of proxy servers (edge locations) that cache content close to end-users, reducing latency and origin load. **Working Mechanism:** 1. A user in Kochi requests `https://cdn.example.com/image.jpg`. 2. DNS routes the request to the nearest **edge location** (e.g., Mumbai). 3. The edge server checks its local cache: - **Cache HIT** → returns the object immediately (typically < 30 ms). - **Cache MISS** → fetches the object from the **origin** (S3 bucket in `ap-south-1`), serves it to the user, and caches it for future requests based on TTL. **Key Concepts:** - **Cache Hit Ratio (H):** $H = \dfrac{\text{Cache Hits}}{\text{Cache Hits + Cache Misses}}$. A high H (typically > 90 %) is essential for cost efficiency. Higher H → fewer origin fetches → lower origin cost. - **TTL (Time-To-Live):** Each cached object has a TTL set via response headers (`Cache-Control: max-age=…`). After TTL expiry, the next request re-validates with the origin. - **Origin Shield:** A dedicated intermediate caching layer between all edge locations and the origin. It collapses multiple simultaneous cache misses for the same object into a single origin request, dramatically reducing origin load during traffic spikes (e.g., a viral video). **Benefits:** reduced latency, offloaded origin traffic, DDoS mitigation, global reach. --- #### Model Solution for Q.B(b) Cloud storage **tiers** balance cost against access frequency and retrieval time. Using AWS S3 as reference: | Tier | Availability | Min Storage | Retrieval Time | Use Case | |---|---|---|---|---| | S3 Standard | 99.99 % | None | Instant | Frequently accessed | | Intelligent-Tiering | 99.9 % | None | Instant | Unknown/changing access | | Standard-IA | 99.9 % | 30 days | Instant | Infrequent, rapid retrieval | | One Zone-IA | 99.5 % | 30 days | Instant | Re-creatable, infrequent | | Glacier Instant | 99.9 % | 90 days | Milliseconds | Archive, fast access | | Glacier Flexible | 99.99 % | 90 days | Min–Hrs | Archive, low cost | | Glacier Deep Archive | 99.99 % | 180 days | 12+ Hrs | Long-term compliance | **Lifecycle Policy Automation:** A lifecycle rule is a JSON/XML configuration that automatically transitions objects between tiers or deletes them based on age or access patterns. **Example lifecycle policy (S3):** ```json { "Rules": [ { "ID": "ArchiveOldLogs", "Status": "Enabled", "Filter": { "Prefix": "logs/" }, "Transitions": [ { "Days": 30, "StorageClass": "STANDARD_IA" }, { "Days": 90, "StorageClass": "GLACIER" }, { "Days": 365, "StorageClass": "DEEP_ARCHIVE" } ], "Expiration": { "Days": 2555 } } ] } ``` **Result:** Logs are automatically moved from Standard → Standard-IA after 30 days → Glacier after 90 days → Deep Archive after 1 year, and permanently deleted after 7 years — **all without manual intervention**. This is the standard practice for compliance-driven workloads (HIPAA, GDPR, RBI archival). --- ## 5.1 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Deduction Traps (Direct from Valuation Experience):** > 1. **Confusing durability with availability** — Durability = survival of data; Availability = uptime of service. Most students mix them up; lose 1–2 marks per question. > 2. **Writing "EBS is shared storage"** — EBS is **single-attach** (except EBS Multi-Attach for limited use cases). Use EFS for shared file access. > 3. **Forgetting units in calculations** — Always write "minutes/year" or "GB" explicitly; bare numbers lose 0.5 marks. > 4. **Saying S3 has folders** — S3 has a **flat namespace**; the "folder" view is a UI illusion. Buckets contain keys with `/` in them. > 5. **Mixing up replication and erasure coding** — Replication = full copies; Erasure coding = mathematical fragments. Question 5–10 frequently tests this distinction. > 6. **Not drawing a diagram when asked** — A neat labeled diagram is worth **at least 1–2 marks** on its own in Part B. --- ## 5.2 Topic Recap — Rapid-Revision Checklist > [!IMPORTANT] > **Memorize these bullet points before walking into the exam hall:** - **Three storage types:** Object (HTTP), Block (mount), File (NFS/SMB) — know one provider, one protocol, one use case each. - **11 nines durability** = 99.999999999 % → S3 Standard benchmark. - **Region vs AZ** — Region is a geographic area; AZ is a data center within a region. - **Erasure coding formula** $n = k + m$; tolerate loss of any $m$ fragments. - **Replication storage overhead** = $R$ times (e.g., R=3 → 3× storage). - **Consistency models:** Strong (S3 post-2020), Eventual (older S3, DynamoDB default), Read-your-writes, Causal. - **Storage tiers** — Standard, IA, Glacier, Deep Archive (AWS) / Hot, Cool, Cold, Archive (Azure) — match to access frequency. - **Lifecycle policies** automate tier transitions based on age/access. - **CDN** = distributed edge cache; key metrics = cache hit ratio, TTL, origin shield. - **Cross-region replication** is **asynchronous** by default → expect ~seconds of RPO. - **S3 is not a filesystem** — flat namespace, no real folders, no random writes inside an object. - **Always quote units** in numerical answers: minutes/year, GB, ms, %. - **Draw a diagram for every 7-mark sub-question** that asks for architecture or workflow — guaranteed marks. ---

topics.title: Application Services
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT525 - CLOUD COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Application Services

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Application Services** in cloud computing refer to the suite of software-based, on-demand, network-accessible services that enable the development, deployment, integration, and management of distributed applications over the internet. These services abstract the underlying infrastructure and platform complexities, delivering ready-to-use application-level functionality to end-users, developers, and enterprises.

In the context of the **NIST (National Institute of Standards and Technology) Cloud Computing Reference Model**, Application Services primarily manifest through the **Software as a Service (SaaS)** delivery model, supplemented by platform-level application services such as APIs, web services, and integration middleware offered through **Platform as a Service (PaaS)** environments.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PECPT525 Module 4)**
> Application Services encompass the **service-oriented constructs** that allow software systems to be built, consumed, composed, and scaled as reusable, loosely-coupled, network-distributed components. The core pillars include:
> - **Web Services** (SOAP-based and RESTful)
> - **Service-Oriented Architecture (SOA)**
> - **Web 2.0 / Mashup Services**
> - **Software as a Service (SaaS)** delivery characteristics
> - **Application Programming Interfaces (APIs)** and Gateway services

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition Builder)

> [!TIP]
> **🍽️ The "Food Court Analogy" for Application Services**
>
> Imagine a giant, indoor **food court** (this is your *Cloud*).
>
> - The **building, electricity, water, tables, and chairs** = *Infrastructure* (IaaS — raw compute, storage, network).
> - The **shared kitchen, stoves, utensils, refrigeration, and recipe books** = *Platform* (PaaS — OS, middleware, runtime, DBs).
> - The **actual food stalls serving finished dishes** (biryani stall, juice counter, ice-cream parlor) = ***Application Services***. You don't cook; you just consume the *final product* over the counter.
>
> Just as a food court can:
> - Offer standardized menus (✓ reusable services),
> - Allow multiple stalls to combine a burger + milkshake into a "combo meal" (✓ **mashups**),
> - Serve thousands via a single common counter (✓ **multi-tenancy**),
>
> …cloud Application Services let you **consume, compose, and scale ready-made software capabilities** over the internet — paying only for what you eat.

## 1.3 Taxonomy of Application Services

| Layer | Service Type | Who Uses It | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **End-User** | SaaS Applications | Non-technical users | Gmail, Microsoft 365, Salesforce |
| **Developer** | API & Web Services | Application developers | Twitter API, Google Maps API, Stripe |
| **Integration** | Mashups / iPaaS | Solution architects | Weather+Map combined dashboards |
| **Composition** | SOA / Microservices | Enterprise architects | Banking service bus, ESB patterns |
| **Collaboration** | Web 2.0 Services | Social/crowdsourced users | Wikipedia, Flickr, Twitter |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Three Mandatory Characteristics of any Cloud Application Service (per NIST SP 800-145):**
> 1. **On-Demand Self-Service** — provision automatically without human interaction.
> 2. **Broad Network Access** — reachable over standard protocols (HTTP/HTTPS).
> 3. **Measured Service** — usage is monitored, metered, and billed (pay-per-use).
>
> The other two (Resource Pooling & Rapid Elasticity) apply to the underlying infrastructure that *hosts* the application service.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Service-Oriented Architecture (SOA) Backbone

SOA is the **architectural paradigm** that underpins all modern cloud application services. It treats software functionality as a **reusable, discoverable, loosely-coupled service** with the following **four cardinal properties**:

1. **Loose Coupling** — services know little about each other; interaction is via messages.
2. **Reusability** — a service is designed for multiple consumers.
3. **Autonomy** — service logic is encapsulated; the consumer does not see internals.
4. **Discoverability** — services publish themselves in a registry (e.g., **UDDI**) for lookup.

### 2.1.1 SOA Triad: Roles of a Web Service

A web service operates under three cooperative roles, often illustrated as a triangle (the **SOA Triangle / Web Service Triangle**):

- **Service Provider** — creates, hosts, and publishes the service.
- **Service Registry** — a searchable directory (e.g., UDDI) where the service description (WSDL) is stored.
- **Service Requestor (Consumer)** — locates the service in the registry and invokes it.

The three core XML-based technologies that implement this triad are:

- **WSDL** (Web Services Description Language) — *What* the service does.
- **SOAP** (Simple Object Access Protocol) — *How* messages are exchanged.
- **UDDI** (Universal Description, Discovery, and Integration) — *Where* to find the service.

> [!NOTE]
> **Modern shift:** While classical SOA uses SOAP/WSDL/UDDI, contemporary cloud application services predominantly use **REST + JSON + API Gateways**, which are lighter and more web-friendly.

## 2.2 RESTful Application Services (The Modern Default)

**REpresentational State Transfer (REST)** is an architectural *style* (defined by **Roy Fielding, 2000**) for distributed hypermedia systems. A service is *RESTful* when it conforms to these **six guiding constraints**:

| # | Constraint | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Client–Server** | Separation of concerns; UI and storage are independent. |
| 2 | **Stateless** | Each request carries all info the server needs; no session memory. |
| 3 | **Cacheable** | Responses must define themselves as cacheable or not. |
| 4 | **Uniform Interface** | Standardized resource identification (URIs) + HTTP verbs. |
| 5 | **Layered System** | Client cannot tell if it's talking to the end server or a proxy. |
| 6 | **Code-on-Demand** *(optional)* | Server can transfer executable code (e.g., JavaScript). |

### 2.2.1 The Standard HTTP Verbs in REST

| Verb | CRUD Action | Idempotent? | Safe? |
|---|---|---|---|
| `GET` | Read | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes |
| `POST` | Create | ❌ No | ❌ No |
| `PUT` | Update / Replace | ✅ Yes | ❌ No |
| `PATCH` | Partial Update | ❌ No | ❌ No |
| `DELETE` | Delete | ✅ Yes | ❌ No |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept:** **Idempotency** = performing the operation multiple times produces the *same result*. This is a *favourite* KTU question for differentiating REST verbs.

## 2.3 SOAP vs REST — The Battle of the Web Service Titans

| Feature | SOAP (Simple Object Access Protocol) | REST (REpresentational State Transfer) |
|---|---|---|
| **Type** | Protocol | Architectural style |
| **Data Format** | XML only | JSON, XML, YAML, HTML, plain text |
| **Transport** | HTTP, SMTP, TCP, JMS | HTTP/HTTPS only |
| **Statefulness** | Can be stateful or stateless | Strictly stateless |
| **Contract** | WSDL (mandatory) | OpenAPI/Swagger (optional) |
| **Security** | WS-Security, SSL, SAML | HTTPS, OAuth 2.0, JWT |
| **Performance** | Slower (verbose XML) | Faster (lightweight JSON) |
| **Use Case** | Enterprise, banking, transactions | Web, mobile, public APIs |
| **Standardized by** | W3C | W3C / IETF (informal) |

## 2.4 Web 2.0 — The User-Generated Application Service Era

**Web 2.0** is *not* a technology — it is a **second-generation paradigm** of the World Wide Web emphasizing user collaboration, openness, and network effects. Coined by **Tim O'Reilly (2004)**.

### 2.4.1 Core Web 2.0 Characteristics (High-Yield for KTU)

1. **User as Contributor** — Wikipedia, YouTube, blogs.
2. **Rich User Interface (RIA)** — AJAX, asynchronous JavaScript updates without full page reloads.
3. **Social Networking** — Facebook, LinkedIn, X (Twitter).
4. **Tagging / Folksonomies** — collaborative classification (e.g., Delicious, Flickr tags).
5. **Mashups** — combining multiple web services into a new composite application.
6. **Software as a Service (SaaS)** — applications delivered over the web.
7. **Lightweight Programming Models** — REST + JSON + single-page apps.

> [!NOTE]
> **AJAX (Asynchronous JavaScript and XML)** is the *technical enabler* of Web 2.0's rich UI. It allows a browser to fetch small JSON/XML payloads from a server and update only the relevant section of a page — the foundational concept behind modern **Single Page Applications (SPAs)** such as Gmail.

## 2.5 Mashups — Composing Application Services

A **Mashup** is a web application that **combines data or functionality from two or more external sources (APIs)** to create a new, integrated service. The classic example is plotting **real-estate listings (Zillow API)** on **Google Maps (Google Maps API)**.

### 2.5.1 The Three Architectural Types of Mashups

| Type | What It Combines | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Consumer Mashup** | Multiple public data sources (browser-side) | HousingMaps.com (Zillow + Google Maps) |
| **Data Mashup** | Multiple data sources, server-side processing | Business intelligence dashboards |
| **Application / Business Mashup** | Multiple enterprise APIs into one workflow | CRM + ERP + Analytics unified portal |

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet — Application Service Metrics

> [!TIP]
> The following mathematical relations are tested in KTU numericals for SaaS / application-service scalability problems.

| # | Formula | Meaning / Variables | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\text{Availability} \; A = \dfrac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$ | **MTBF** = Mean Time Between Failures; **MTTR** = Mean Time To Repair | dimensionless (ratio 0–1) or **%** |
| 2 | $\text{Downtime/year} = (1 - A) \times 365 \times 24$ | Hours of unavailability per year (assuming $A$ is a fraction) | hours/year |
| 3 | $\text{Nines} = -\log_{10}(1 - A)$ | Number of "9"s in SLA (e.g., 0.999 → 3 nines) | dimensionless |
| 4 | $\text{Throughput} \; \lambda = \dfrac{N_{\text{req}}}{T_{\text{obs}}}$ | Requests served per unit time | requests/sec |
| 5 | $\text{Response Time} \; R = \text{Service Time} + \text{Wait Time}$ | End-to-end latency | seconds (s) or ms |
| 6 | $\text{Capacity Planning: } \; C_{\text{total}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} C_i \cdot f_i$ | $C_i$ = capacity of tier $i$, $f_i$ = scaling factor | users/sec |
| 7 | $\text{Multi-tenant Efficiency: } \; \eta = \dfrac{N_{\text{tenants}} \times U_{\text{avg}}}{C_{\text{total}}}$ | Ratio of total used resources to provisioned | dimensionless (0–1) |
| 8 | $\text{Little's Law: } \; L = \lambda \cdot W$ | $L$ = concurrent users, $\lambda$ = arrival rate, $W$ = avg time in system | users, /sec, sec |

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why does this matter to a KTU engineer?**
> - **Building modern apps:** Almost every web/mobile app you build uses at least **one external application service** (payment gateway, auth, maps).
> - **System design interviews:** REST, SOAP, microservices, API gateways are *core* topics.
> - **Cost estimation:** $A$ and *nines* directly translate to **SLA penalties** and infrastructure choices in B.Tech final-year projects hosted on AWS/Azure/GCP.
> - **KTU Lab & Mini-Project:** Computing availability, throughput, and capacity is a regular viva question.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Converting "Nines" of Availability to Yearly Downtime

> **Problem:** A cloud SaaS provider advertises *"Four Nines"* availability ($A = 0.9999$). Compute the maximum allowed yearly downtime in **minutes**.

### Step 1 — Identify the formula

From the KTU formula sheet:
$$\text{Downtime/year} = (1 - A) \times 365 \times 24$$

### Step 2 — Substitute the value

$$A = 0.9999 \;\Rightarrow\; 1 - A = 1 - 0.9999 = 0.0001$$

### Step 3 — Compute the yearly downtime in hours

$$\text{Downtime/year} = 0.0001 \times 365 \times 24 \; \text{hours}$$

$$\text{Downtime/year} = 0.0001 \times 8760 \; \text{hours}$$

$$\text{Downtime/year} = 0.876 \; \text{hours}$$

### Step 4 — Convert hours to minutes

$$\text{Downtime/year} = 0.876 \times 60 \; \text{minutes}$$

$$\boxed{\text{Downtime/year} = 52.56 \; \text{minutes} \approx 52 \; \text{minutes } 34 \; \text{seconds}}$$

### Step 5 — Engineering interpretation

> This means the service is **legally allowed to be down for only ~53 minutes per year**. Anything more incurs an SLA breach penalty.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Availability from MTBF and MTTR

> **Problem:** A cloud-based application service has a **Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF) of 800 hours** and a **Mean Time To Repair (MTTR) of 2 hours**. Compute its availability $A$ and the equivalent number of nines.

### Step 1 — Recall the availability formula

$$A = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$$

### Step 2 — Substitute the numerical values

$$A = \frac{800}{800 + 2} = \frac{800}{802}$$

### Step 3 — Evaluate the fraction

$$A = 0.99750623\ldots$$

$$\boxed{A \approx 0.9975 \;\; \text{or} \;\; 99.75\%}$$

### Step 4 — Convert to nines

Using the nines formula:
$$\text{Nines} = -\log_{10}(1 - A)$$

$$1 - A = 1 - 0.99750623 = 0.00249377$$

$$\text{Nines} = -\log_{10}(0.00249377) = -\left(-2.6034\right) = 2.6034$$

$$\boxed{\text{Nines} \approx 2.60 \;\; \text{(between 2-nines and 3-nines)}}$$

### Step 5 — Yearly downtime

$$\text{Downtime/year} = (1 - 0.99750623) \times 8760 \; \text{hours}$$

$$\text{Downtime/year} = 0.00249377 \times 8760 \approx 21.85 \; \text{hours/year}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Capacity Planning using Little's Law

> **Problem:** A SaaS-based ticket-booking system has an average **arrival rate of $\lambda = 50$ requests/second**, and each request spends on average $W = 0.4 \; \text{seconds}$ in the system. Find:
> 1. The **average number of concurrent requests** $L$.
> 2. The **server pool size** required if each server can handle 10 concurrent requests.

### Step 1 — Apply Little's Law

$$L = \lambda \times W$$

### Step 2 — Substitute the values

$$L = 50 \; \text{req/s} \times 0.4 \; \text{s}$$

$$\boxed{L = 20 \; \text{concurrent requests}}$$

### Step 3 — Determine the number of servers

If each server can process $C_{\text{server}} = 10$ concurrent requests, the number of servers $N_{\text{servers}}$ required is:

$$N_{\text{servers}} = \left\lceil \frac{L}{C_{\text{server}}} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{20}{10} \right\rceil = \lceil 2.0 \rceil$$

$$\boxed{N_{\text{servers}} = 2 \; \text{servers (minimum)}}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering practice:** Always round **up** (ceiling function) for capacity planning, and add a **safety margin of 20–30%** for traffic spikes — so 2 → provision **3 servers** in real deployments.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — SOAP vs REST vs gRPC

| Dimension | SOAP | REST | gRPC |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Wire Format** | XML | JSON / XML | Protobuf (binary) |
| **Contract First** | WSDL | OpenAPI | `.proto` schema |
| **Transport** | HTTP/SMTP/TCP | HTTP | HTTP/2 |
| **Speed** | Slow (XML parsing) | Medium | **Fastest** (binary + multiplexing) |
| **Streaming** | Limited | No native | **Yes (bi-directional)** |
| **Browser Friendly** | Poor | Excellent | Needs `grpc-web` |
| **Error Handling** | SOAP Faults | HTTP Status Codes | Status + rich error model |
| **KTU Use Case** | Banking, ERP | Web/Mobile APIs | Microservices, IoT |

---

## 3.5 Worked Example: Multi-Tenant SaaS Resource Pooling

> **Problem:** A multi-tenant SaaS ERP system hosts $N_{\text{tenants}} = 100$ tenants. The average per-tenant resource utilization is $U_{\text{avg}} = 0.3$ of one VM. The cloud provider has provisioned a total of $C_{\text{total}} = 40$ virtual machines. Compute the multi-tenant efficiency $\eta$ and comment on whether the pool is over- or under-provisioned.

### Step 1 — Recall the efficiency formula

$$\eta = \frac{N_{\text{tenants}} \times U_{\text{avg}}}{C_{\text{total}}}$$

### Step 2 — Substitute the values

$$\eta = \frac{100 \times 0.3}{40} = \frac{30}{40}$$

$$\boxed{\eta = 0.75 \;\; \text{or} \;\; 75\%}$$

### Step 3 — Interpretation

A utilization of **75%** is healthy in cloud economics (target band is typically **70–85%**). Below 70% → **over-provisioned** (wasting cost). Above 85% → **risk of contention and SLA violation**.

> Hence, this SaaS configuration is **optimally provisioned**.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Web Service Triangle (SOA Triad) — Service Discovery & Invocation

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["Service Requestor (Consumer App / Client)"] -- "1. LOOKUP service via UDDI" --> B["Service Registry (UDDI)"]
    B -- "2. RETURNS WSDL contract" --> A
    A -- "3. INVOKES service via SOAP message" --> C["Service Provider (Application Service)"]
    C -- "4. PUBLISHES WSDL to registry" --> B
    C -- "5. RETURNS SOAP response" --> A
```

### Block-Level Functional Flow Explanation

1. **Provider** publishes a WSDL description of its service into the **Registry** (publish step).
2. **Consumer** queries the Registry using a lookup protocol.
3. Registry returns the WSDL contract.
4. Consumer uses WSDL to construct a **SOAP envelope** and sends it to the Provider.
5. Provider executes the business logic and returns a **SOAP response**.

---

## 4.2 Layered Architecture of a Cloud Application Service Platform

```mermaid
graph TD
    L1["LAYER 5 — End-User Applications (SaaS: Gmail, Salesforce)"] --> L2
    L2["LAYER 4 — Application Services: Web 2.0, Mashups, APIs, REST/SOAP"] --> L3
    L3["LAYER 3 — Platform Services: PaaS Runtimes, App Servers, Middleware, ESB"] --> L4
    L4["LAYER 2 — Infrastructure Services: Virtualized Compute, Storage, Network (IaaS)"] --> L5
    L5["LAYER 1 — Physical Hardware: Servers, SANs, Routers, Data Centers"]
```

### Block-Level Explanation

- **Layer 1 — Hardware:** Raw physical resources.
- **Layer 2 — IaaS:** Virtualization layer offering VMs, block storage, VLANs.
- **Layer 3 — PaaS:** Application runtimes (Tomcat, Node.js, Python), databases, message queues.
- **Layer 4 — Application Services:** The **focus of this module** — REST/SOAP, APIs, mashups, Web 2.0 features.
- **Layer 5 — SaaS:** End-user-facing software delivered over the browser.

---

## 4.3 REST Request–Response Lifecycle

```mermaid
graph TD
    C["Client (Mobile / SPA / Browser)"] -- "HTTP GET /api/users/42" --> A1["API Gateway: Auth, Rate-limit, Routing"]
    A1 -- "Forward to User-Service" --> S1["User Microservice (Stateless)"]
    S1 -- "Query" --> DB[("Shared Database / Cache")]
    DB -- "User Record" --> S1
    S1 -- "200 OK + JSON Body" --> A1
    A1 -- "200 OK + JSON Body" --> C
```

### Block-Level Explanation

- Client sends an **HTTP GET** to a stable URI: `/api/users/42`.
- The request hits an **API Gateway**, which enforces cross-cutting concerns: authentication, throttling, logging.
- The gateway routes to the appropriate **stateless microservice**.
- The microservice queries the data store, returns a JSON document.
- The gateway forwards the JSON back to the client.
- All interactions are **stateless** (per REST constraint #2) and **cacheable** (constraint #3).

---

## 4.4 Mashup Composition Architecture (Consumer Mashup)

```mermaid
graph TD
    Z["Zillow Real-Estate API (Listings)"] --> M
    G["Google Maps API (Geocoding + Tiles)"] --> M
    W["Weather API (Forecast)"] --> M
    M["Browser-Side Mashup (JavaScript / AJAX)"] -- "Unified Map View" --> U["End User"]
```

### Block-Level Explanation

- Three independent **public REST APIs** (Zillow, Google Maps, Weather) expose their data over HTTP/JSON.
- A **client-side mashup** written in JavaScript (using AJAX) fetches data from all three concurrently.
- The browser composites them into a single, unified UI: properties on a map, each with a weather tooltip.
- **Key insight:** The mashup itself is *stateless* and *lightweight* — all intelligence lives in the APIs.

---

## 4.5 Web 2.0 Application Service Flow (AJAX-driven)

```mermaid
graph TD
    U["User Action (e.g., Type in Search Box)"] --> JS["JavaScript Event Handler in Browser"]
    JS -- "XHR / fetch POST request" --> AJAX["AJAX Engine (XMLHttpRequest / fetch API)"]
    AJAX -- "Async HTTP Request" --> SVR["Web Service (REST API Endpoint)"]
    SVR -- "JSON / XML Payload" --> AJAX
    AJAX -- "DOM Update (Partial Page Refresh)" --> JS
    JS --> DOM["Updated Web Page Section (No Full Reload)"]
```

### Block-Level Explanation

- Traditional web pages require a **full HTTP round-trip** to refresh.
- Web 2.0 / AJAX sends **asynchronous** requests in the background.
- The server returns only the *delta* (typically JSON) — **not a full HTML page**.
- The browser's **Document Object Model (DOM)** is partially updated.
- This gives a *desktop-app feel* — and is the technical foundation of **SPA frameworks** like React, Angular, and Vue.
