## 5.2 PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

### 🅰️ QUESTION A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

> **Q(A)(a)** Explain the **Service-Oriented Architecture (SOA)** paradigm in detail. With a neat diagram, describe the **Web Service Triangle** and the role of **WSDL, SOAP, and UDDI**. **(7 marks)**
>
> **Q(A)(b)** A cloud-based application service has **MTBF = 1200 hours** and **MTTR = 3 hours**. Compute: **(i)** the system availability $A$, **(ii)** the equivalent number of "nines," and **(iii)** the maximum allowable yearly downtime in **minutes**. Conclude whether the service meets a *Three-Nines SLA* ($A \geq 0.999$). **(7 marks)**

#### 🔹 Model Answer for Q(A)(a) — 7 Marks

**Definition (1.5 marks):**
SOA is an architectural paradigm that structures software as a collection of **loosely-coupled, interoperable, reusable services** communicating via messages over a network. Each service:
- performs a **discrete business function**,
- is **platform-independent**,
- is **discoverable** through a registry,
- and is **composable** with other services.

**The Web Service Triangle (3 marks — diagram + explanation):**

The three roles in any web-service interaction are:

1. **Service Provider:** Designs, implements, and hosts the service. Publishes its description to the registry.
2. **Service Registry (UDDI):** Acts as a *Yellow Pages* directory where providers publish and consumers search.
3. **Service Requestor (Consumer):** The application or client that needs the service. Looks up the registry, obtains the WSDL contract, and binds to the provider.

```
       Service Provider
              /\
             /  \
   PUBLISH  /    \  INVOKE
   (WSDL)  /      \  (SOAP)
           /        \
          /          \
  Service  ------  Service
  Registry        Requestor
       \           /
        \---------/
          DISCOVER
           (UDDI)
```

**Roles of WSDL, SOAP, UDDI (2.5 marks — 0.83 each):**

- **WSDL (Web Services Description Language):** An **XML-based** machine-readable contract that describes **what** the service does — its operations, inputs, outputs, and how to invoke it.
- **SOAP (Simple Object Access Protocol):** An **XML-based messaging protocol** that defines **how** a service request and response are formatted and exchanged. Uses an *envelope* containing a *header* (metadata) and a *body* (payload).
- **UDDI (Universal Description, Discovery and Integration):** A **registry standard** used to **publish and discover** web services. It stores WSDL documents and allows keyword-based or category-based search.

#### 🔹 Model Answer for Q(A)(b) — 7 Marks

**Given:** MTBF = 1200 h, MTTR = 3 h.

**(i) Availability $A$ (2 marks):**
$$A = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}} = \frac{1200}{1200 + 3} = \frac{1200}{1203}$$

$$A = 0.997506 \approx 0.9975 \;\; \text{or} \;\; 99.75\%$$

**(ii) Number of Nines (2.5 marks):**
$$1 - A = 1 - 0.997506 = 0.002494$$
$$\text{Nines} = -\log_{10}(1 - A) = -\log_{10}(0.002494)$$
$$\log_{10}(0.002494) = -2.6034$$
$$\boxed{\text{Nines} = 2.6034}$$

**(iii) Yearly Downtime in Minutes (2.5 marks):**
$$\text{Downtime/year} = (1 - A) \times 365 \times 24 \times 60 \; \text{minutes}$$
$$= 0.002494 \times 525{,}600 \; \text{minutes}$$
$$= 1310.78 \; \text{minutes}$$
$$\approx 21 \; \text{hours } 50 \; \text{minutes } 47 \; \text{seconds}$$

**Conclusion (bonus mark):** A 3-Nines SLA requires $A \geq 0.999$. Our computed $A = 0.9975 < 0.999$. **Therefore, the service does NOT meet the Three-Nines SLA.**

> [!WARNING]
> **🛑 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> - Students **frequently forget to convert hours → minutes** at the final step, losing 1 mark.
> - **Use ceiling/worst-case** for SLA questions — partial nines don't round up automatically.
> - Always **state the conclusion explicitly** (meets / does not meet SLA) — 1 mark is reserved for it.

---

### 🅱️ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

> **Q(B)(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **REST architectural style**. List its **six guiding constraints**. Differentiate between **PUT and PATCH** with an example. **(7 marks)**
>
> **Q(B)(b)** What are **Mashups** in the context of cloud application services? Explain the **three types of mashups** with real-world examples. List the **four security challenges** of mashups. **(7 marks)**

#### 🔹 Model Answer for Q(B)(a) — 7 Marks

**Definition (1.5 marks):**
REST (REpresentational State Transfer) is an **architectural style** for distributed hypermedia systems, introduced by **Roy Fielding in his 2000 doctoral dissertation**. It treats every piece of data as a **resource** identified by a **URI**, manipulated using **standard HTTP verbs**, and transferred as representations (JSON/XML).

**Neat Diagram (1.5 marks):**

```
  Client (Browser/Mobile)
        |
        |   HTTP Request:  GET /api/products/101
        v
  +--------------------------------------+
  |         RESTful API Endpoint         |
  |--------------------------------------|
  | Resource: /api/products/101          |
  | Representation: JSON { ... }         |
  | Stateless: each request independent  |
  +--------------------------------------+
        |
        v
  Application Server --> Database
        |
        |   HTTP Response: 200 OK
        v
  Client renders the JSON
```

**Six REST Constraints (3 marks — 0.5 each):**

1. **Client–Server** — clear separation of UI and data storage.
2. **Stateless** — no client context stored on the server between requests.
3. **Cacheable** — responses must indicate cacheability to improve performance.
4. **Uniform Interface** — resources identified by URIs; manipulated via standard verbs.
5. **Layered System** — client unaware of intermediate proxies/gateways.
6. **Code-on-Demand** *(optional)* — server can send executable code (e.g., JavaScript).

**PUT vs PATCH (1 mark — 0.5 each):**

| Aspect | PUT | PATCH |
|---|---|---|
| **Semantics** | **Replace** the entire resource | **Partially update** the resource |
| **Example** | `PUT /users/42 { "name":"Anu", "email":"a@x.com", "age":21 }` | `PATCH /users/42 { "age":22 }` |
| **Idempotent?** | ✅ Yes | ❌ No (multiple PATCHes accumulate changes) |

#### 🔹 Model Answer for Q(B)(b) — 7 Marks

**Definition (1 mark):**
A **Mashup** is a web application that **integrates data, presentation, or functionality from two or more external sources (APIs)** to deliver a new, unified user experience. Mashups are a hallmark of Web 2.0 and are typically built using REST/JSON APIs.

**Three Types of Mashups (3 marks — 1 each):**

1. **Consumer / Presentation Mashup:** Combines multiple **client-side** data sources into one visual interface. Example: **HousingMaps.com** overlays Zillow property listings on Google Maps.
2. **Data Mashup:** Combines and processes data from multiple sources **server-side**, often with analytics. Example: A BI dashboard merging data from Salesforce CRM + Google Analytics + Stripe payments.
3. **Application / Business Mashup:** Combines **multiple enterprise APIs** into a single, integrated workflow. Example: A unified corporate portal that surfaces SAP (ERP) + ServiceNow (ITSM) + Slack (Collaboration).

**Four Security Challenges of Mashups (3 marks — 0.75 each):**

1. **Cross-Site Scripting (XSS):** Malicious code from one source can be injected into the mashup and executed in the user's browser.
2. **Cross-Site Request Forgery (CSRF):** A logged-in user is tricked into triggering unintended actions via the combined service.
3. **API Key / Token Leakage:** Embedding API keys in client-side JavaScript exposes them to attackers.
4. **Data Privacy & Compliance:** Mixing data from multiple providers may breach GDPR, HIPAA, or local data-protection laws.
5. *(Bonus: Inconsistent authentication mechanisms across APIs.)*

> [!WARNING]
> **🛑 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Mashup Question):**
> - Many students **forget to mention both the type AND a real-world example** for each mashup type. Always do **both**.
> - Confusing *Consumer* mashup (browser-side) with *Application* mashup (server-side) is the **#1 mistake**.
> - For security challenges, listing only the *names* without a one-line explanation costs you 0.5 marks each.

---

## 5.3 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **📌 Rapid-Revision Checklist (Pin this in your brain the night before the exam):**

- ⭐ **Application Services** = software-level capabilities delivered over the cloud → primarily **SaaS** plus **APIs/Web Services**.
- ⭐ **SOA Triangle** = **Provider** + **Registry** + **Requestor**, glued by **WSDL + SOAP + UDDI**.
- ⭐ **REST ≠ Protocol**; it is an **architectural style** with **6 constraints** (Stateless, Cacheable, Uniform Interface, etc.).
- ⭐ **SOAP = Protocol + XML only**; **REST = Style + JSON/XML**; both communicate over HTTP.
- ⭐ **HTTP Verbs** — `GET` (safe, idempotent), `PUT` (idempotent), `DELETE` (idempotent), `POST/PATCH` (neither safe nor idempotent).
- ⭐ **Web 2.0** = user-generated content, AJAX, mashups, SaaS, RIA — *not* a technology but a paradigm.
- ⭐ **AJAX** = technical heart of Web 2.0's rich UI (asynchronous, partial page refresh).
- ⭐ **Mashup** = 3 types → **Consumer**, **Data**, **Application** — HousingMaps.com is the *classic* example.
- ⭐ **Availability formula:** $A = \dfrac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$ — memorize this verbatim.
- ⭐ **Nines formula:** $\text{Nines} = -\log_{10}(1 - A)$ — direct conversion to SLA tier.
- ⭐ **Yearly downtime** in minutes = $(1-A) \times 365 \times 24 \times 60$ — don't forget the $\times 60$ for minutes.
- ⭐ **Little's Law** $L = \lambda W$ is the *go-to* for capacity-planning numericals.
- ⭐ **Multi-tenant efficiency** in healthy range = **70–85%**; below 70% = over-provisioned.
- ⭐ **API Gateway** sits in front of microservices, handling **auth, throttling, routing, logging**.
- ⭐ **Stateless** REST is *the reason* cloud apps can scale horizontally — no session affinity needed.
- ⭐ **Web 2.0 ≠ Cloud** — Web 2.0 is a *user-experience* paradigm; Cloud is an *infrastructure/delivery* paradigm. They overlap but are not the same.
- ⭐ **Kerala-specific tip:** KTU loves asking *availability-from-MTBF/MTTR* numericals and *SOAP vs REST* comparison tables — practise these **three times** before the exam.

> **🎯 Final Mantra:** "Application Services in cloud = SOA + Web Services + Web 2.0 + SaaS, all glued together by APIs, JSON, and statelessness."
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Compute Services
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT525 - CLOUD COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Compute Services

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

**Compute Services** in cloud computing refer to the on-demand, scalable, and metered provisioning of **processing power, memory, storage, and networking resources** delivered to consumers over the internet as a managed service. These services abstract away the underlying physical hardware, allowing users to deploy and run applications, workloads, and services without owning, managing, or maintaining the physical servers themselves.

According to the **NIST SP 500-292 Reference Architecture**, compute services fall under the *Resource Abstraction and Control Layer* and are delivered through three principal service models:

1. **Infrastructure-as-a-Service (IaaS)** – Virtual Machines, Bare-Metal Servers
2. **Platform-as-a-Service (PaaS)** – Managed runtimes, containers, orchestration
3. **Software-as-a-Service (SaaS)** – Ready-to-use applications

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight**: Module 4 explicitly emphasizes the differentiation between **Virtual Machines, Containers, and Serverless Computing** as the three primary compute abstraction paradigms, along with their corresponding hyperscaler offerings (AWS EC2, Azure Virtual Machines, Google Compute Engine, AWS Lambda, Azure Functions, Google Cloud Run).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Three Tiers of Restaurant Dining**

Imagine you want to serve a meal to your guests. You have three options:

| Abstraction Level | Restaurant Analogy | Cloud Equivalent |
|---|---|---|
| **High Control (IaaS)** | Rent a full kitchen — you buy groceries, cook, plate, and serve | Virtual Machines (EC2, Azure VM) |
| **Medium Control (PaaS)** | Rent a kitchen with a chef — you decide the menu, chef cooks | Containers (ECS, AKS, GKE) |
| **Zero Control (Serverless)** | Order from a restaurant menu — they cook, deliver, and clean up | Functions (Lambda, Azure Functions) |

The trade-off is simple: **more control = more responsibility**. You choose based on how much management overhead you want to retain versus delegate to the cloud provider.

### 1.3 Core Taxonomy of Compute Services

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Three Pillars of Cloud Compute Abstraction**

- **Virtualization-Based (VMs):** Hardware-level virtualization using hypervisors. Each VM runs its own OS kernel. Strong isolation, slow boot (seconds to minutes).
- **Container-Based:** OS-level virtualization sharing the host kernel. Lightweight, fast boot (milliseconds to seconds). Package-based deployment.
- **Serverless / Function-as-a-Service (FaaS):** Event-driven, stateless execution of small code units. Zero infrastructure management, sub-second billing granularity.

### 1.4 Standard Metrics & Constants

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry-Standard Compute Metrics (Bolded for Emphasis)**

- **vCPU** = **1 vCPU ≈ 1 physical CPU thread** (typically backed by a single hyperthread on x86_64 architecture)
- **ECU (EC2 Compute Unit)** = Equivalent CPU capacity of a **1.0–1.2 GHz 2007 AMD Opteron or 2007 Intel Xeon** processor (legacy AWS metric)
- **Memory-to-vCPU ratio:** Standard is **4 GiB RAM per vCPU** (1:4 ratio), with memory-optimized instances reaching **8:1 or 30:1**
- **Cold start latency (Lambda):** typically **100 ms – 5 seconds** depending on runtime and package size
- **Container density:** up to **10–100× higher** than VMs on equivalent hardware
- **Hypervisor Type-1 (Bare-Metal):** VMware ESXi, Microsoft Hyper-V, KVM
- **Hypervisor Type-2 (Hosted):** VirtualBox, VMware Workstation

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Virtual Machines — Operational Breakdown

A **Virtual Machine (VM)** is an emulation of a physical computer system that executes programs like a real machine. VMs rely on a **hypervisor** (Virtual Machine Monitor) to multiplex one physical host into multiple isolated virtual environments.

**Operational Logic Steps:**

1. **Resource Pooling:** Physical hardware (CPU, RAM, Disk, NIC) is aggregated into a resource pool.
2. **Hypervisor Insertion:** A Type-1 (bare-metal) or Type-2 (hosted) hypervisor sits between hardware and VMs.
3. **VM Instantiation:** Each VM receives virtualized resources (vCPU, vRAM, vDisk).
4. **Guest OS Boot:** Each VM independently boots its own OS kernel.
5. **Workload Execution:** Applications run inside the guest OS, unaware of virtualization.
6. **Hardware-Assisted Virtualization:** Modern CPUs use **Intel VT-x** or **AMD-V** to accelerate VM execution via ring de-privileging.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why VMs Exist in Cloud:** VMs provide **strong isolation boundaries** (separate kernels, separate memory spaces), making them ideal for multi-tenant cloud environments where customers must not interfere with each other.

### 2.2 Containers — Operational Breakdown

Containers package an application together with its **dependencies, libraries, and configuration files** into a single lightweight, portable unit. They share the host OS kernel but maintain isolated user-space via **Linux namespaces** and **cgroups**.

**Operational Logic Steps:**

1. **Image Build:** Application code + dependencies are packaged into a container image (Dockerfile → Docker Image).
2. **Layered Filesystem:** Images use a **Union Filesystem (OverlayFS)** with read-only layers and a writable top layer.
3. **Namespace Isolation:** Each container gets its own PID, Network, Mount, UTS, IPC, and User namespaces.
4. **cgroup Resource Limits:** CPU shares, memory caps, and I/O bandwidth are enforced per container.
5. **Container Runtime:** **containerd** or **CRI-O** executes containers per the OCI specification.
6. **Orchestration:** **Kubernetes** schedules containers across a cluster of nodes.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Containers Exist:** Containers solve the **"works on my machine"** problem by packaging the entire runtime environment, enabling consistent deployment from dev → staging → production.

### 2.3 Serverless Computing — Operational Breakdown

**Serverless** does not mean "no servers" — it means **servers are managed entirely by the cloud provider**. Developers write functions triggered by events; the provider handles provisioning, scaling, patching, and capacity planning.

**Operational Logic Steps:**

1. **Function Authoring:** Developer writes a stateless function (e.g., Python, Node.js, Java).
2. **Event Source Configuration:** Trigger is bound (HTTP request, S3 upload, queue message, schedule).
3. **Cold Start Path:** Provider provisions a sandbox, loads runtime, initializes function code.
4. **Warm Execution:** Subsequent invocations reuse the existing sandbox (sub-100 ms).
5. **Auto-Scaling:** Provider scales concurrent executions based on incoming load.
6. **Billing:** Charged per **GB-second** of compute and **number of invocations**.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Compute Metrics Cheat Sheet (KTU Board Exam Ready)**

| Metric | Formula / Definition | Unit | Boundary Condition |
|---|---|---|---|
| **vCPU Allocation** | $vCPU = \left\lceil \frac{\text{Required MIPS}}{1 \text{ vCPU MIPS}} \right\rceil$ | count | Always round up |
| **EC2 Compute Unit (ECU)** | $ECU = \frac{\text{Reference vCPU cycles}}{2007 \text{ Opteron baseline}}$ | ECU | Legacy metric |
| **Memory-to-vCPU Ratio** | $R = \frac{\text{RAM (GiB)}}{\text{vCPU}}$ | ratio | $R \in [0.5, 30]$ |
| **Cold Start Latency** | $T_{cold} = T_{sandbox} + T_{runtime} + T_{init}$ | ms | $T_{cold} \geq 100$ ms |
| **Container Density Gain** | $D = \frac{N_{containers\_host}}{N_{VMs\_host}}$ | ratio | Typically $D \in [10, 100]$ |
| **Serverless Cost** | $C = (N_{invocations} \times P_{inv}) + (T_{exec\_sec} \times RAM_{GB} \times P_{GBs})$ | USD | $P_{GBs} \approx \$0.0000166667$ (Lambda) |
| **Auto-Scaling Threshold** | $N_{instances} = \left\lceil \frac{\text{Current Load}}{\text{Instance Capacity}} \right\rceil$ | count | Triggered at 60–70% CPU |
| **Hypervisor Overhead** | $OH = 1 - \frac{\text{VM Throughput}}{\text{Bare-Metal Throughput}}$ | % | Typically $5\% - 15\%$ |
| **Load Balancer Distribution** | $L_{i} = \frac{W_{i}}{\sum_{j=1}^{n} W_{j}} \times 100\%$ | % | $\sum L_{i} = 100\%$ |
| **High Availability SLA** | $A = \frac{T_{uptime}}{T_{total}} \times 100\%$ | % | AWS EC2 SLA $\geq 99.99\%$ |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall Alert:** Never use the vertical pipe `\vert` symbol inside markdown tables for this sheet — all delimiters are structural. For absolute values in equations, always use $\vert x \vert$ LaTeX syntax outside tables.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Compute Paradigm | Production Use Case | Why Chosen |
|---|---|---|
| VMs | Legacy ERP systems, monolithic databases | OS-level control, mature ecosystem |
| Containers | Microservices, CI/CD pipelines, ML inference | Portability, fast scaling |
| Serverless | Image processing, IoT backends, chatbots | Zero ops, event-driven bursts |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Serverless Total Cost of Ownership

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Worked Numerical Example — Lambda Cost Calculation**

A developer deploys a Lambda function that processes **5 million invocations per month**, with each invocation averaging **200 ms execution time** and using **512 MB RAM**.

**Given Parameters:**

- $N = 5{,}000{,}000$ invocations/month
- $T_{exec} = 200 \text{ ms} = 0.2 \text{ seconds}$
- $RAM_{GB} = \frac{512}{1024} = 0.5 \text{ GB}$
- $P_{inv} = \$0.20 \text{ per 1 million invocations}$
- $P_{GBs} = \$0.0000166667 \text{ per GB-second}$

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

$$
C_{total} = (N \times P_{inv}) + (T_{exec\_sec} \times RAM_{GB} \times P_{GBs} \times N)
$$

$$
C_{invocations} = \frac{5{,}000{,}000}{1{,}000{,}000} \times \$0.20 = 5 \times \$0.20 = \$1.00
$$

$$
C_{compute} = 0.2 \times 0.5 \times \$0.0000166667 \times 5{,}000{,}000
$$

$$
C_{compute} = 0.1 \times \$0.0000166667 \times 5{,}000{,}000
$$

$$
C_{compute} = \$0.00000166667 \times 5{,}000{,}000 = \$8.3335
$$

$$
C_{total} = \$1.00 + \$8.3335 = \$9.3335 \text{ per month}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The compute cost dominates invocations by ~8× in this scenario. Optimizing function duration or memory allocation yields greater savings than reducing invocation count.

### 3.2 Derivation: Auto-Scaling Decision Logic

Consider an application with the following scaling parameters:

- **Current CPU Utilization:** $U_c = 75\%$
- **Scale-Out Threshold:** $T_{out} = 70\%$
- **Scale-In Threshold:** $T_{in} = 30\%$
- **Current Instance Count:** $N_c = 4$
- **Min Instances:** $N_{min} = 2$, **Max Instances:** $N_{max} = 10$

$$
\text{Decision} = \begin{cases}
\text{Scale Out}, & U_c \geq T_{out} \\
\text{Scale In}, & U_c \leq T_{in} \\
\text{Hold}, & T_{in} < U_c < T_{out}
\end{cases}
$$

Since $U_c = 75\% \geq T_{out} = 70\%$, the system **scales out**. With a default scale-out step of $+2$ instances (AWS ASG default):

$$
N_{new} = \min(N_c + \Delta_{out}, N_{max}) = \min(4 + 2, 10) = 6
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Cooldown Periods Matter:** Without cooldown, scale-out oscillations would cause thrashing. AWS uses a default cooldown of **300 seconds** to stabilize the cluster.

### 3.3 Derivation: Container vs VM Density Ratio

Given:
- Host with $M = 64$ GB RAM
- VM footprint: $R_{VM} = 4$ GB RAM per VM (including OS overhead)
- Container footprint: $R_{ct} = 256$ MB RAM per container

$$
N_{VM} = \left\lfloor \frac{M}{R_{VM}} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor \frac{64}{4} \right\rfloor = 16 \text{ VMs}
$$

$$
N_{ct} = \left\lfloor \frac{M}{R_{ct}} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor \frac{64}{0.25} \right\rfloor = 256 \text{ containers}
$$

$$
D = \frac{N_{ct}}{N_{VM}} = \frac{256}{16} = 16
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Density Insight:** Containers achieve a **16× density improvement** in this scenario. In practice, with proper cgroup tuning, ratios of **50–100×** are achievable.

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master Comparison Table — VMs vs Containers vs Serverless (Board Exam Favorite)**

| Parameter | Virtual Machines | Containers | Serverless |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Isolation Level** | Process + Kernel | Process only | Process + sandbox |
| **Boot Time** | 30 s – 5 min | 100 ms – 5 s | 1 ms – 5 s (warm) |
| **OS Dependency** | Independent Guest OS | Shared host kernel | Managed runtime |
| **Resource Overhead** | High (GB per VM) | Low (MB per container) | None (auto) |
| **Scaling Granularity** | Instance-level | Container-level | Request-level |
| **Persistence** | Stateful (preferred) | Ephemeral (preferred) | Stateless only |
| **Hypervisor Required** | Yes | No | No |
| **Billing Unit** | Hourly | Per second | Per 100 ms |
| **Max Execution Time** | Unlimited | Unlimited | 15 min (Lambda) |
| **Cold Start** | None (pre-allocated) | None (running) | Yes (100 ms – 5 s) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Cloud Compute Service Stack (High-Level Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph ClientLayer["CLIENT ACCESS LAYER"]
        C1[Web Browser]
        C2[Mobile App]
        C3[CLI Tool]
        C4[SDK API Call]
    end

    subgraph EdgeLayer["EDGE AND DELIVERY LAYER"]
        E1[CDN CloudFront]
        E2[Load Balancer ALB]
        E3[API Gateway]
    end

    subgraph ComputeLayer["COMPUTE ABSTRACTION LAYER"]
        direction LR
        subgraph IAAS["IaaS Virtual Machines"]
            VM1[EC2 Instance]
            VM2[Azure VM]
            VM3[GCE Instance]
        end
        subgraph PAAS["PaaS Containers"]
            CT1[ECS Fargate]
            CT2[AKS Pod]
            CT3[GKE Deployment]
        end
        subgraph SAAS["Serverless FaaS"]
            FN1[Lambda Function]
            FN2[Azure Function]
            FN3[Cloud Function]
        end
    end

    subgraph OrchestrationLayer["ORCHESTRATION AND MANAGEMENT"]
        O1[Auto Scaling Group]
        O2[Kubernetes Controller]

    end

    subgraph InfraLayer["PHYSICAL INFRASTRUCTURE"]
        P1[Bare Metal Servers]

    C1 --> E1
    C2 --> E2
    C3 --> E3

```

### 4.2 Virtual Machine Internal Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph AppLayer["APPLICATION LAYER"]
        A1[App Process A]
        A2[App Process B]
        A3[System Libraries]
    end

    subgraph GuestOS["GUEST OPERATING SYSTEM"]
        G1[Guest Kernel]
        G2[Guest Device Drivers]
        G3[Guest File System]
    end

    subgraph Hypervisor["HYPERVISOR VIRTUAL MACHINE MONITOR"]
        H1[VM Scheduler]
        H2[Memory Manager]
        H3[Emulated Hardware NIC Disk]
        H4[Hardware Assisted Intel VT-x]
    end

    subgraph HostHW["HOST PHYSICAL HARDWARE"]
        HW1[CPU Cores]
        HW2[DDR Memory]
        HW3[SSD Storage]
        HW4[10GbE NIC]
    end

    A1 --> G1
    A2 --> G1
    A3 --> G1
    G1 --> H1
    G2 --> H3
    G3 --> H3
    H1 --> HW1
    H2 --> HW2
    H3 --> HW3
    H4 --> HW4

```

### 4.3 Container Architecture with Namespace Isolation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph HostKernel["HOST LINUX KERNEL SHARED"]
        K1[PID Namespace]
        K2[Network Namespace]
        K3[Mount Namespace]
        K4[cgroup Resource Limits]
    end

    subgraph C1["CONTAINER 1 NODE JS APP"]
        C1A[App Code]
        C1B[Node Runtime]
        C1C[App Files]
    end

    subgraph C2["CONTAINER 2 PYTHON APP"]
        C2A[App Code]
        C2B[Python Runtime]
        C2C[App Files]
    end

    subgraph C3["CONTAINER 3 REDIS CACHE"]
        C3A[Redis Binary]
        C3B[Config Files]
        C3C[Data Volume Mount]
    end

    subgraph Runtime["CONTAINER RUNTIME LAYER"]
        R1[containerd]
        R2[OCI Image Registry]
    end

    C1 --> R1
    C2 --> R1
    C3 --> R1
    R1 --> K1
    R1 --> K2
    R1 --> K3
    R1 --> K4
    R2 --> R1
```

### 4.4 Serverless Event-Driven Execution Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Event Source S3 Upload]) --> Trigger[EventBridge Rule Match]
    Trigger --> Invoke[Lambda Invoke API]
    Invoke --> Cold{Cold Start Check}
    Cold -->|Yes Cold| Provision[Provision Sandbox]
    Cold -->|No Warm| Execute[Execute Handler]
    Provision --> Load[Load Runtime Layer]
    Load --> Init[Run Init Code]
    Init --> Execute
    Execute --> Logic[Business Logic Execution]
    Logic --> Log[Write CloudWatch Logs]
    Logic --> SideEffect[Optional Side Effects S3 DynamoDB]
    SideEffect --> Bill[Bill GB Seconds]
    Bill --> End([Response Returned])
    Log --> End
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Question 1.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
> **Define Compute Services in cloud computing. List any three characteristics of cloud compute services.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition:** Compute services refer to the on-demand delivery of computing resources — including processing power, memory, storage, and networking — over the internet on a pay-per-use basis, abstracting the underlying physical infrastructure.

**Three Characteristics:**

1. **Elasticity:** Resources can be scaled up or down automatically based on workload demand.
2. **Metered Billing:** Users pay only for the compute capacity consumed (per second, per hour, or per invocation).
3. **Self-Service Provisioning:** Resources can be provisioned via APIs or consoles without human intervention from the provider.
4. **Resource Pooling:** Multiple tenants share the same physical infrastructure with logical isolation.

*(Any three well-explained — full 3 marks)*

---

> **Question 2.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
> **Differentiate between Virtualization and Containerization. Give one example of each.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Virtualization | Containerization |
|---|---|---|
| Abstraction | Hardware-level | OS-level |
| OS | Each VM has its own guest OS | Shares host OS kernel |
| Boot Time | Seconds to minutes | Milliseconds |
| Size | Gigabytes | Megabytes |
| Hypervisor | Required (VMware, KVM) | Not required |
| Example | AWS EC2, Azure VM | Docker, Kubernetes Pods |

**Examples:**
- **Virtualization:** AWS EC2 running Ubuntu Server 22.04 LTS on a t3.medium instance.
- **Containerization:** Docker container running an Nginx web server from the official `nginx:alpine` image.

---

### 📕 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

> **Question A (14 Marks).** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
> **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the three service models of cloud computing (IaaS, PaaS, SaaS) with suitable examples and responsibility distribution between provider and consumer.
> **(b) [7 Marks]** With a neat diagram, explain the architecture and working of a Virtual Machine including the role of hypervisors. Discuss Type-1 vs Type-2 hypervisors.

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]

**Cloud Service Models — Responsibility Distribution:**

| Layer | IaaS | PaaS | SaaS |
|---|---|---|---|
| Application | User | User | Provider |
| Data | User | User | Provider |
| Runtime | User | Provider | Provider |
| Middleware | User | Provider | Provider |
| OS | User | Provider | Provider |
| Virtualization | Provider | Provider | Provider |
| Server/Storage/Networking | Provider | Provider | Provider |

**IaaS (Infrastructure-as-a-Service):** Provides raw compute, storage, and networking. The user installs OS, middleware, and applications. *Examples: AWS EC2, Azure Virtual Machines, Google Compute Engine, DigitalOcean Droplets.*

**PaaS (Platform-as-a-Service):** Provides a managed runtime environment. The user only develops and uploads application code. *Examples: AWS Elastic Beanstalk, Azure App Service, Google App Engine, Heroku.*

**SaaS (Software-as-a-Service):** Provides ready-to-use applications accessed via browser. The user simply consumes. *Examples: Gmail, Microsoft 365, Salesforce, Slack, Dropbox.*

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]

**Virtual Machine Architecture:**

```
+--------------------------------+
|      Application Layer         |
+--------------------------------+
|     Guest Operating System     |   <-- VM
+--------------------------------+
|        Hypervisor (VMM)        |   <-- Virtualization Layer
+--------------------------------+
|    Host Operating System       |   <-- (Type-2 only)
+--------------------------------+
|      Physical Hardware         |
+--------------------------------+
```

**Working of a VM:**

1. The **hypervisor** creates virtual versions of CPU, memory, storage, and network (vCPU, vRAM, vDisk, vNIC).
2. The guest OS and applications run inside this virtualized environment, completely unaware of the underlying physical host.
3. The hypervisor schedules physical resources among multiple VMs.

**Type-1 vs Type-2 Hypervisors:**

| Aspect | Type-1 (Bare-Metal) | Type-2 (Hosted) |
|---|---|---|
| Installation | Directly on hardware | On top of host OS |
| Performance | High (production-grade) | Lower (development-grade) |
| Examples | VMware ESXi, Microsoft Hyper-V, Xen, KVM | VirtualBox, VMware Workstation, Parallels |
| Use Case | Enterprise data centers | Desktop testing, learning |

**Examples in Cloud:** AWS EC2 uses a customized Xen/KVM-based Type-1 hypervisor for production workloads.

---

> **Question B (14 Marks).** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
> **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain Container technology in detail. Discuss Docker architecture and the role of Kubernetes in container orchestration.
> **(b) [7 Marks]** With a neat diagram, explain Serverless Computing architecture. Discuss AWS Lambda as a representative FaaS platform with its pricing model.

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]

**Container Technology Overview:**

A **container** is a lightweight, portable, self-contained execution unit that packages application code together with its dependencies, libraries, and configuration. Containers share the host OS kernel but run in isolated user spaces using **Linux namespaces** (process, network, mount isolation) and **cgroups** (resource limits).

**Docker Architecture:**

```
+----------------------------------+
|       Docker Client (CLI)        |
+----------------+-----------------+
                 | (REST API)
                 v
+----------------------------------+
|      Docker Daemon (dockerd)     |
+----------------+-----------------+
                 |
   +-------------+-------------+
   |             |             |
   v             v             v
+-------+   +--------+   +---------+
|Images |   |Containers|  |Volumes  |
|       |   |         |   |Networks |
+-------+   +--------+   +---------+
                 |
                 v
+----------------------------------+
|  Container Runtime (containerd)  |
+----------------------------------+
|       Host Linux Kernel          |
+----------------------------------+
```

**Docker Components:**

- **Dockerfile:** Declarative recipe to build an image.
- **Docker Image:** Read-only template packaged in layers (UnionFS/OverlayFS).
- **Docker Container:** Running instance of an image.
- **Docker Hub / Registry:** Repository to store and distribute images.

**Kubernetes (K8s) Orchestration:**

Kubernetes is an open-source container orchestration platform that automates deployment, scaling, and management of containerized applications.

**Key K8s Components:**

| Component | Role |
|---|---|
| **Pod** | Smallest deployable unit (1+ containers) |
| **Deployment** | Manages ReplicaSets and rolling updates |
| **Service** | Stable network endpoint for Pods |
| **Ingress** | HTTP routing into the cluster |
| **Node** | Worker machine running Pods |
| **Control Plane** | API Server, Scheduler, etcd, Controller Manager |
| **kubectl** | CLI to interact with the cluster |

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]

**Serverless Computing Architecture:**

```
Event Source  --->  Trigger Service  --->  Function Platform
(S3 / API GW)        (EventBridge)         (AWS Lambda)
                                                  |
                                                  v
                                          +-------+-------+
                                          |   Sandbox     |
                                          | + Init Code   |
                                          | + Handler     |
                                          +---------------+
                                                  |
                                                  v
                                          CloudWatch Logs
                                          + Side Effects
                                          (DynamoDB/S3)
```

**Working of Serverless:**

1. Developer authors a stateless function and uploads it to the platform.
2. An **event source** (HTTP request, file upload, queue message, schedule) triggers the function.
3. The platform **provisions a sandbox**, loads the runtime, runs initialization code (**cold start**), then executes the handler.
4. Subsequent invocations may reuse the warm sandbox, reducing latency.
5. The platform **auto-scales** horizontally up to the account's concurrency limit.
6. Billing is computed per **invocation** + **GB-seconds of compute**.

**AWS Lambda — Representative FaaS:**

- **Supported Runtimes:** Node.js, Python, Java, Go, Ruby, .NET, custom runtimes.
- **Memory Range:** 128 MB – 10,240 MB (CPU scales linearly with memory).
- **Max Execution Time:** 15 minutes per invocation.
- **Deployment Package Limit:** 50 MB (zipped), 250 MB (unzipped) for direct upload; 10 GB via container images.

**Lambda Pricing Model:**

$$
C_{total} = (N_{inv} \times \$0.20 / 1M) + (T_{sec} \times RAM_{GB} \times \$0.0000166667)
$$

- **Free Tier:** 1 million requests + 400,000 GB-seconds per month (always free).
- **Concurrency Limit:** Default 1,000 concurrent executions per region (soft limit, can be raised).

> [!WARNING]
> **🛑 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Compute Services Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Confusing FaaS with SaaS:** FaaS = backend code execution; SaaS = end-user applications. Do NOT interchange these in long answers.
> 2. **Hypervisor Naming Errors:** Type-1 is **bare-metal** (installed on hardware), Type-2 is **hosted** (installed on an OS). Reversing this is an instant 1-mark deduction.
> 3. **Container ≠ Lightweight VM:** Containers share the host kernel; they are **NOT** full VMs. Writing "container is a small VM" loses 2 marks.
> 4. **Cold Start Confusion:** Cold start applies to **serverless**, NOT to VMs or containers. Misattributing cold start to EC2 = wrong concept.
> 5. **Missing Diagrams:** KTU board values diagrams heavily. A 14-mark question without a labeled architecture diagram typically caps at 8–9 marks. Always draw even a hand-sketched block diagram.
> 6. **Cost Calculation Units:** Lambda memory is billed in **GB-seconds**, NOT MB-seconds. Wrong unit conversion = -1 mark.

---

### 🧠 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Compute Services**

- ✅ **Compute Services** = on-demand, metered, elastic provisioning of CPU + RAM + Storage + Network over the internet.
- ✅ **Three Abstraction Paradigms:** Virtual Machines (IaaS), Containers (PaaS), Serverless (FaaS).
- ✅ **Hypervisor:** Type-1 (bare-metal, e.g., ESXi, KVM, Xen) vs Type-2 (hosted, e.g., VirtualBox). Cloud providers use **Type-1 exclusively** for production.
- ✅ **Containers** use **Linux namespaces + cgroups** for isolation; share the host kernel.
- ✅ **Docker** = image (read-only template) + container (running instance) + registry (storage).
- ✅ **Kubernetes** orchestrates containers via Pods, Deployments, Services, and a Control Plane.
- ✅ **Serverless** = event-driven, stateless, auto-scaled, billed per **invocation + GB-second**.
- ✅ **AWS Lambda limits:** max 15-min execution, 128 MB – 10,240 MB memory, default 1,000 concurrency.
- ✅ **VM density** vs **Container density**: containers achieve **10–100×** higher density on identical hardware.
- ✅ **Auto-Scaling** uses threshold-based rules with cooldown periods (default **300 s** in AWS).
- ✅ **Load Balancer** distributes traffic using algorithms like round-robin, least-connections, or weighted.
- ✅ **Hyperscaler Compute Services Map:** AWS = EC2 + Lambda; Azure = Virtual Machines + Functions; GCP = Compute Engine + Cloud Functions + Cloud Run.
- ✅ **Cold start latency:** typically **100 ms – 5 s** depending on runtime and package size.
- ✅ **High Availability:** Use **Multi-AZ deployments** with at least 2 instances across different zones to meet the **99.99% SLA**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Database Services
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECPT525 - CLOUD COMPUTING > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Database Services # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Cloud Database Services** refer to a class of **Database-as-a-Service (DBaaS)** offerings delivered through the cloud computing paradigm, wherein database management, provisioning, scaling, backup, patching, and optimization are outsourced to a managed cloud provider. These services abstract the underlying hardware, operating system, and database engine, exposing them as on-demand, elastic, pay-per-use resources accessible over standard network protocols. In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020 aligned)** for the course *PECPT525 – Cloud Computing*, Module 4 situates Database Services within the broader **Platform Layer of the Cloud Stack (PaaS)**, treating them as horizontally scalable, fault-tolerant distributed data stores that decouple data persistence from application logic. The formal definition expands to: $$ \text{Cloud DB Service} = f(\text{Multi-tenancy}, \text{Elasticity}, \text{Distribution}, \text{Consistency Model}, \text{SLA}) $$ where each parameter captures a critical engineering characteristic mandated by cloud delivery. --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Plain-English Analogy — "The Self-Service Storage Warehouse"** Imagine you run a small but rapidly expanding e-commerce business. Initially, you store all your customer records, order data, and inventory in a personal filing cabinet (a traditional on-premise database). As the business grows, you need: - More cabinet space (storage scaling) - Faster retrieval (performance) - Automatic backups after floods (disaster recovery) - Multiple clerks accessing the same data without conflicts (concurrency) Instead of building a new office, buying filing cabinets, hiring clerks, and installing fire-safety systems, you simply **rent a fully-managed warehouse** from a company like Amazon or Google. You tell them: 1. How much shelf space you need 2. The type of records (Excel-style tables? receipts? photo IDs?) 3. Your uptime expectations They handle **security, backups, scaling, hardware failures, and software updates**. You simply walk in, deposit your data, and query it whenever needed. This is exactly what **Cloud Database Services** do for software applications. --- ## 1.3 Taxonomy of Cloud Database Services > [!IMPORTANT] > **Core Classification — KTU Board Examiner Frequently Asked** | Service Class | Description | Typical Example | |---|---|---| | **SQL (RDBMS) DBaaS** | Relational, schema-bound, ACID-compliant | Amazon RDS, Google Cloud SQL, Azure SQL DB | | **NoSQL DBaaS** | Schema-less, distributed, BASE-oriented | DynamoDB, MongoDB Atlas, Cosmos DB | | **NewSQL DBaaS** | Distributed SQL with horizontal scale + ACID | Google Spanner, CockroachDB, FaunaDB | | **In-Memory DBaaS** | RAM-resident, microsecond latency | Amazon ElastiCache, Redis Enterprise Cloud | | **Graph DBaaS** | Native relationship traversal | Amazon Neptune, Neo4j Aura | | **Time-Series DBaaS** | Optimized for IoT/metrics workloads | Amazon Timestream, InfluxDB Cloud | | **Ledger DBaaS** | Immutable, cryptographically verifiable | Amazon QLDB | --- ## 1.4 Key Engineering Metrics (Bolded for Recall) > [!NOTE] > The following constants/metrics form the **high-frequency vocabulary** expected in any 14-mark KTU answer: > - **ACID** properties: **A**tomicity, **C**onsistency, **I**solation, **D**urability — **4** properties of RDBMS transactions. > - **CAP Theorem (Brewer's Theorem, 2000)**: only **2 of 3** guarantees (Consistency, Availability, Partition Tolerance) hold simultaneously. > - **BASE properties**: **B**asically **A**vailable, **S**oft state, **E**ventually consistent — NoSQL alternative. > - **Polyglot Persistence**: using **multiple database types** in a single application. > - **Sharding**: horizontal partitioning — typically a power of 2: **2, 4, 8, 16, 32, 64, 128, 256** shards. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Architecture of Cloud DB Services Cloud database services are delivered through a layered architectural stack. Understanding each layer is **critical for KTU 14-mark derivation questions**. ### Layer 1: Physical Infrastructure Layer - Commodity **x86/x64 servers** arranged in racks - **NVMe SSD** or **3D XPoint** persistent storage - **10/25/100 Gbps** east-west fabric (e.g., AWS Nitro, Azure FPGAs) ### Layer 2: Virtualization & Multi-Tenancy Layer - Hypervisor-based isolation (KVM, Hyper-V, Nitro Hypervisor) - **Multi-tenant resource slicing** with hardware-enforced boundaries (SR-IOV, DPDK) ### Layer 3: Distributed Storage Layer - Replicated log-structured stores (e.g., **DynamoDB storage nodes**, **Cosmos DB's Atom-Record-Sequencer**) - Consensus protocols: **Paxos**, **Raft** ### Layer 4: Query Processing Engine - Cost-based optimizers for SQL - Document/object mappers for NoSQL ### Layer 5: API & Control Plane - RESTful control plane APIs (CreateDBInstance, ReadEndpoint) - **API quotas**: default **200 RPS** for control plane (AWS RDS) --- ## 2.2 SQL vs NoSQL vs NewSQL — The Engineering Trade-off > [!NOTE] > **Why this matters in KTU valuation**: This comparison table appears in 80%+ of Module 4 questions. | Parameter | SQL (RDBMS) | NoSQL | NewSQL | |---|---|---|---| | Data Model | Tables with fixed schema | Key-Value, Document, Column, Graph | Tables with fixed schema | | Consistency | **Strong ACID** | **Eventual (BASE)** | **Strong ACID** + distributed | | Scaling | **Vertical (scale-up)** | **Horizontal (scale-out)** | **Horizontal** | | Schema | Rigid, predefined | Dynamic, flexible | Rigid | | Query Language | **ANSI SQL** | API-based (no SQL or SQL-like) | **ANSI SQL** | | Typical Latency | 1–10 ms | 1–5 ms (KV) | 5–20 ms | | Use Case | OLTP, ERP, Banking | IoT, Social, Catalog | Global OLTP, Fintech | | Examples | MySQL, PostgreSQL | DynamoDB, Cassandra | Spanner, CockroachDB | --- ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > This cheat sheet consolidates every equation, metric, and rule needed for Module 4 numerical/analytical KTU questions. | Concept | Formula / Rule | Description | |---|---|---| | **Replication Factor (RF)** | $RF = \frac{N_{\text{copies}}}{N_{\text{unique}}}$ | Number of copies per data item | | **Write Quorum** | $W = \lfloor \frac{N+R}{2} \rfloor$ (Dynamo-style) | Minimum acks for successful write | | **Read Quorum** | $R = \lfloor \frac{N+R}{2} \rfloor$ | Minimum acks for successful read | | **CAP Trade-off** | $C + A + P \le 2$ | Only 2 of 3 properties simultaneously | | **Eventual Consistency Time** | $t_{EC} \approx \frac{d}{c}$ where $d$=datacenter distance, $c$=propagation speed | Approx. propagation-based sync time | | **Sharding Distribution** | $S_{\text{node}} = \frac{D_{\text{total}}}{N_{\text{shards}}}$ | Even distribution per shard | | **Throughput Equation** | $T = \frac{N_{\text{requests}}}{t_{\text{window}}}$ | Requests per unit time | | **Mean Time To Recovery (MTTR)** | $MTTR = \frac{\sum t_{\text{down}}}{N_{\text{failures}}}$ | Avg. recovery duration | | **Availability (SLA)** | $A = \frac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$ | Service uptime fraction | | **Polyglot Score** | $P = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot DB_i$ | Weighted sum of DB types in stack | --- ## 2.4 The CAP Theorem — Detailed Engineering Breakdown > [!IMPORTANT] > **CAP (Brewer's Theorem, 2000)** is the single most asked Module 4 concept in KTU examinations. ### Definition In any distributed data store, when a **network partition (P)** occurs between nodes, the system must choose between: - **Consistency (C)**: every read returns the most recent write - **Availability (A)**: every request receives a (non-error) response **Formal CAP Inequality:** $$ C + A + P \leq 2 \quad \text{(i.e., at most 2 of 3)} $$ ### CAP Trade-offs in Real Systems | System | CAP Choice | Reason | |---|---|---| | **Amazon DynamoDB** | **AP** (Eventually Consistent) | Shopping cart, session data — must remain writable | | **Google BigTable** | **CP** (Strong Consistent) | Search indexing — stale reads unacceptable | | **HBase** | **CP** | Strong consistency over availability | | **Cassandra** | **AP** (tunable to CP) | Tunable consistency via quorum | | **MongoDB** | **CP** (default) | Single-master replication | | **Google Spanner** | **CP** (with TrueTime) | Globally distributed ACID | > [!NOTE] > **Real-world utility**: CAP analysis drives **disaster recovery architecture** — banks choose CP (Spanner), while social media platforms choose AP (DynamoDB). --- ## 2.5 ACID vs BASE — The Fundamental Duality ### ACID (Atomicity, Consistency, Isolation, Durability) | Property | Meaning | |---|---| | **Atomicity** | All or nothing — transaction either fully commits or fully rolls back | | **Consistency** | Database transitions from one valid state to another | | **Isolation** | Concurrent transactions appear serialized | | **Durability** | Committed data survives crashes, power loss | ### BASE (Basically Available, Soft state, Eventual consistency) | Property | Meaning | |---|---| | **Basically Available** | System responds even during failures | | **Soft State** | State may change without input due to eventual consistency propagation | | **Eventual Consistency** | Replicas converge to same value given enough time | --- ## 2.6 NewSQL — Bridging the Divide > [!NOTE] > **NewSQL** is a class of modern relational databases that preserves the **ACID semantics** of SQL systems while delivering the **horizontal scalability** of NoSQL. **Engineering Mechanics:** 1. **Distributed consensus** via Paxos/Raft for leader election 2. **TrueTime API** (Google Spanner) — synchronized clocks with bounded uncertainty **≤ 7 ms** 3. **MVCC (Multi-Version Concurrency Control)** for non-blocking reads 4. **Automatic sharding** with transparent routing --- ## 2.7 Polyglot Persistence > [!IMPORTANT] > **Polyglot Persistence** = the practice of using **multiple specialized data stores** within a single application architecture. Introduced by **Martin Fowler (2011)**. **Example — E-Commerce Platform:** | Data Type | Optimal Database | Reason | |---|---|---| | User profiles | PostgreSQL | Relational, structured | | Product catalog | MongoDB | Document, flexible schema | | Shopping cart | Redis | In-memory, key-value | | Recommendations | Neo4j | Graph relationships | | Search | Elasticsearch | Full-text inverted index | | Analytics | Cassandra | Wide-column, write-heavy | --- ## 2.8 Database Migration Strategies (Lift & Shift, Refactor) | Strategy | Description | Cloud Effort | |---|---|---| | **Rehost (Lift & Shift)** | Move as-is to VM | **Low** | | **Replatform** | Minor engine swap (e.g., MySQL → Aurora) | **Medium** | | **Refactor** | Re-architect using cloud-native DBs | **High** | | **Repurchase** | Replace with SaaS (e.g., Oracle → Salesforce) | **Medium** | | **Retire** | Decommission obsolete DBs | **Low** | | **Retain** | Keep on-premise (hybrid) | **None** | # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix > [!IMPORTANT] > **This section provides exhaustive mathematical derivations of every CAP, replication, and quorum-related concept — exactly what KTU Board Examiners demand for full 14-mark credit.** --- ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Quorum Intersection in Distributed Databases ### Problem Statement In a Dynamo-style distributed key-value store, data is replicated across **$N$** nodes. Determine the values of read quorum **$R$** and write quorum **$W$** such that **strong consistency** is guaranteed. ### Step 1: Define the System Parameters $$ N = \text{Number of replicas per data item} $$ $$ W = \text{Number of acknowledgments required for successful write} $$ $$ R = \text{Number of replicas contacted for a read} $$ ### Step 2: Establish the Consistency Constraint For strong consistency, **the read set and the write set must intersect**. If they did not, a read could miss the most recent write entirely. $$ \text{Intersection Constraint: } (R + W) > N $$ ### Step 3: Solve for Strong Consistency $$ R + W > N \implies W > N - R $$ $$ \text{Choose } R = W = \left\lceil \frac{N+1}{2} \right\rceil $$ For example, with $N = 3$: $$ R = W = \left\lceil \frac{3+1}{2} \right\rceil = \left\lceil 2 \right\rceil = 2 $$ Verification: $$ R + W = 2 + 2 = 4 > 3 = N \quad \checkmark $$ ### Step 4: Compute Availability Trade-off For $N = 3$, $R = W = 2$, the system tolerates: $$ t_{\text{failures}} = N - W = 3 - 2 = 1 \text{ node failure during write} $$ $$ t_{\text{read failures}} = N - R = 3 - 2 = 1 \text{ node failure during read} $$ ### Step 5: Generalize to N = 5 $$ R = W = \left\lceil \frac{5+1}{2} \right\rceil = 3 $$ $$ R + W = 3 + 3 = 6 > 5 = N \quad \checkmark $$ $$ t_{\text{tolerance}} = 5 - 3 = 2 \text{ failures} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Insight**: Higher $R$ and $W$ values give stronger consistency but reduce availability. This is the **fundamental CAP trade-off** in mathematical form. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2: CAP Theorem Availability Bound ### Step 1: Define System Model Let $S$ be a distributed system with three properties: - $C$ = Consistency (linearizability) - $A$ = Availability (every request gets a response) - $P$ = Partition tolerance (system continues despite arbitrary message loss) ### Step 2: Network Partition Scenario Assume two nodes, $N_1$ and $N_2$, separated by a partition. A write to $N_1$ must propagate to $N_2$ for consistency, but the partition blocks propagation. ### Step 3: Inconsistency Arises If $N_2$ accepts the read, it returns stale data — violates $C$. If $N_2$ rejects the read, it returns an error — violates $A$. ### Step 4: Formal Result $$ \text{During partition } P: \quad C \land A = \text{false} \implies C + A \leq 1 $$ Combined with partition tolerance requirement ($P = 1$ always, since networks fail): $$ C + A + P \leq 2 $$ ### Step 5: Quantify SLAs For a system with target availability $A = 99.99\%$ ("four nines"), downtime per year is: $$ D_{\text{year}} = 365.25 \times 24 \times 60 \times (1 - 0.9999) = 52.56 \text{ minutes/year} $$ --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3: Sharding Performance Analysis ### Step 1: Define Workload Parameters - Total data size: $D_{\text{total}}$ in TB - Number of shards: $S$ - Per-shard capacity: $C_{\text{shard}}$ in TB - Per-shard throughput: $T_{\text{shard}}$ in QPS (queries per second) ### Step 2: Compute Per-Shard Distribution Assuming uniform hash distribution: $$ D_{\text{per shard}} = \frac{D_{\text{total}}}{S} $$ $$ T_{\text{total}} = S \times T_{\text{shard}} $$ ### Step 3: Compute Latency Model Network latency $L_{\text{net}}$, disk I/O latency $L_{\text{disk}}$, query processing latency $L_{\text{qp}}$: $$ L_{\text{total}} = L_{\text{net}} + L_{\text{disk}} + L_{\text{qp}} $$ For a cross-shard join (joining two tables across shards): $$ L_{\text{join}} = 2 \times L_{\text{net}} + 2 \times L_{\text{disk}} + L_{\text{qp}} $$ ### Step 4: Numerical Example Given: - $D_{\text{total}} = 100 \text{ TB}$ - $S = 16$ shards - $C_{\text{shard}} = 10 \text{ TB}$ - $T_{\text{shard}} = 5000 \text{ QPS}$ - $L_{\text{net}} = 0.5 \text{ ms}$ - $L_{\text{disk}} = 1.0 \text{ ms}$ - $L_{\text{qp}} = 0.2 \text{ ms}$ Compute: $$ D_{\text{per shard}} = \frac{100}{16} = 6.25 \text{ TB} $$ $$ T_{\text{total}} = 16 \times 5000 = 80{,}000 \text{ QPS} $$ $$ L_{\text{total}} = 0.5 + 1.0 + 0.2 = 1.7 \text{ ms} $$ $$ L_{\text{join}} = 2(0.5) + 2(1.0) + 0.2 = 3.2 \text{ ms} $$ --- ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Cost & Performance > [!IMPORTANT] > This matrix is **extremely high-yield** for 14-mark KTU comparison questions. | Database Service | Type | CAP Choice | Read Latency | Write Latency | Cost Model | Best For | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | **Amazon RDS (MySQL)** | SQL | CA → CP | 5–10 ms | 5–10 ms | Instance-hours | Traditional OLTP | | **Amazon Aurora** | NewSQL | CP | 1–5 ms | 5–10 ms | Per-query + instance | High-performance SQL | | **Amazon DynamoDB** | NoSQL KV | AP | 1–5 ms | 5–10 ms | Per-request units | Serverless apps | | **Google Cloud Spanner** | NewSQL | CP (TrueTime) | 5–20 ms | 10–30 ms | Node-hours + storage | Global OLTP | | **Google Bigtable** | NoSQL Column | AP/CP | < 10 ms | < 10 ms | Node-hours | IoT, time-series | | **Azure Cosmos DB** | Multi-model | Tunable | < 10 ms | < 10 ms | RU/s provisioned | Globally distributed | | **MongoDB Atlas** | NoSQL Document | CP | 5–15 ms | 5–15 ms | Cluster size | Catalog, CMS | | **Redis Enterprise** | In-memory KV | AP | < 1 ms | < 1 ms | Per-GB | Cache, sessions | --- ## 3.5 Derivation 4: TrueTime Bounded Uncertainty (Spanner) ### Step 1: GPS and Atomic Clock Sources Google Spanner uses **GPS receivers** and **atomic clocks** in each datacenter. Let: - $\epsilon = $ worst-case clock skew between any two servers - Typical value: $\epsilon \leq 7 \text{ ms}$ ### Step 2: TrueTime API Spanner's API returns an interval, not a single timestamp: $$ TT.now() = [t_{\text{earliest}}, t_{\text{latest}}] $$ where $t_{\text{latest}} - t_{\text{earliest}} = 2\epsilon$. ### Step 3: Commit Wait To guarantee external consistency, Spanner waits: $$ t_{\text{commit wait}} = 2\epsilon \approx 14 \text{ ms} $$ This ensures the transaction is causally ordered across the globe. ### Step 4: Engineering Implication Despite spanning continents, Spanner achieves ACID with bounded uncertainty — **a NewSQL landmark**. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics > [!IMPORTANT] > The following **Mermaid diagrams** are compiled with strict adherence to the **Node Identifier Alpha Rule** and **Label Formatting Restriction** (no markdown tags inside node labels). --- ## 4.1 Diagram 1: Cloud Database Service Architecture (Block-Level Functional Flow) ```mermaid flowchart TB user1[CLIENT APPLICATION] api1[REST API GATEWAY] cp1[CONTROL PLANE
Provision, Scale, Backup] dp1[DATA PLANE
Query Execution] storage1[DISTRIBUTED STORAGE LAYER] shard1[SHARD 1] shard2[SHARD 2] shard3[SHARD 3] replica1[REPLICA A] replica2[REPLICA B] replica3[REPLICA C] monitor1[METRICS AND TELEMETRY] sla1[SLA MANAGER
Uptime, Latency] user1 --> api1 api1 --> dp1 api1 --> cp1 dp1 --> storage1 storage1 --> shard1 storage1 --> shard2 storage1 --> shard3 shard1 --> replica1 shard2 --> replica2 shard3 --> replica3 dp1 --> monitor1 cp1 --> sla1 monitor1 --> sla1 ``` **Interpretation**: The diagram depicts the canonical 3-tier architecture of a managed cloud database service. The **Control Plane** handles provisioning and lifecycle management, while the **Data Plane** executes read/write queries. Data is **sharded** and each shard is **replicated** for durability and fault tolerance. Telemetry feeds the SLA Manager, which enforces uptime guarantees. --- ## 4.2 Diagram 2: CAP Theorem Triangle and System Positioning ```mermaid flowchart LR capnode[CENTRAL NODE
CAP THEOREM] subgraph CAPNODES direction TB cnode[CONSISTENCY C] anode[AVAILABILITY A] pnode[PARTITION TOLERANCE P] end capnode --> cnode capnode --> anode capnode --> pnode subgraph APZONE direction TB dyn[DYNAMO DB
AP SYSTEM] cass[CASSANDRA
AP BY DEFAULT] end subgraph CPZONE direction TB bigtbl[BIG TABLE
CP SYSTEM] hbase[HBASE
CP SYSTEM] end subgraph CAZONE direction TB mysql[TRADITIONAL RDBMS
CA WHEN NO PARTITION] end cnode --> dyn anode --> dyn cnode --> bigtbl pnode --> bigtbl anode --> mysql cnode --> mysql ``` **Interpretation**: This visualization classifies production systems by their **CAP trade-off**. DynamoDB and Cassandra choose **AP** (available during partitions, eventually consistent). BigTable and HBase choose **CP** (consistent, may reject reads during partitions). Traditional RDBMS in single-datacenter deployments choose **CA** (no partition to tolerate). --- ## 4.3 Diagram 3: Polyglot Persistence in E-Commerce ```mermaid flowchart TB app1[E COMMERCE APPLICATION] subgraph DATABASES direction TB pg1[POSTGRESQL
User Profiles, Orders] mongo1[MONGODB
Product Catalog] redis1[REDIS
Cart, Session Cache] neo1[NEO 4J
Recommendation Graph] es1[ELASTIC SEARCH
Search Index] cass1[CASSANDRA
Clickstream, Analytics] end app1 --> pg1 app1 --> mongo1 app1 --> redis1 app1 --> neo1 app1 --> es1 app1 --> cass1 ``` **Interpretation**: This Polyglot Persistence pattern shows how a single application interacts with **6 specialized databases**, each chosen for its data model's fit. The application layer orchestrates cross-database transactions via **Saga Pattern** or **Eventual Consistency Compensations**. --- ## 4.4 Diagram 4: Database Migration Strategy Decision Flow ```mermaid flowchart TB start1[LEGACY ON PREMISE DATABASE] q1{NEEDS SCHEMA CHANGE?} p1[REHOST LIFT AND SHIFT] p2[REPLATFORM
e.g. MySQL to Aurora] p3[REFACTOR
Cloud Native DB] app1[BUSINESS CRITICAL OLTP?] app2[BIG DATA ANALYTICS?] sp1[GOOGLE SPANNER OR AURORA] sp2[GOOGLE BIGTABLE OR REDSHIFT] start1 --> q1 q1 -- NO --> p1 q1 -- MINOR --> p2 q1 -- MAJOR --> p3 p3 --> app1 app1 -- YES --> sp1 app1 -- NO --> app2 app2 --> sp2 ``` **Interpretation**: A decision tree guiding engineers through **6Rs migration framework** (Rehost, Replatform, Refactor, Repurchase, Retire, Retain). The final selection depends on workload type — OLTP vs Analytics. --- ## 4.5 Diagram 5: Replication and Quorum Mechanism ```mermaid flowchart TB client1[CLIENT] coord[COORDINATOR NODE] subgraph REPLICAS direction LR r1[REPLICA 1
Quorum Member] r2[REPLICA 2
Quorum Member] r3[REPLICA 3
Quorum Member] end client1 -- WRITE REQUEST W=2 --> coord coord --> r1 coord --> r2 coord --> r3 r1 -- ACK --> coord r2 -- ACK --> coord coord -- WRITE SUCCESS --> client1 r3 -. TIMEOUT .-> coord client1 -. READ REQUEST R=2 .-> coord coord -. READ .-> r1 coord -. READ .-> r2 r1 -. DATA .-> coord r2 -. DATA .-> coord coord -. READ RESPONSE .-> client1 ``` **Interpretation**: With **N=3, W=2, R=2**, the system tolerates **1 node failure** during writes or reads while maintaining **strong consistency** (R+W > N). # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 5.1 Part A — 3 Mark Questions ### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]** *Define Database as a Service (DBaaS). List any two advantages of using DBaaS in cloud computing.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Definition (2 marks):** Database as a Service (DBaaS) is a cloud computing service model that provides users with access to a fully managed database system without the need to install, configure, maintain, or operate the underlying hardware and software. The cloud provider handles provisioning, patching, backup, scaling, and high availability, while the user focuses on schema design and queries. **Two Advantages (1 mark):** 1. **Elastic Scalability** — Storage and compute resources can be scaled up or down on-demand based on workload patterns. 2. **Reduced Operational Overhead** — Automated backups, patching, and disaster recovery eliminate manual DBA intervention. --- ### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Sample Paper]** *Differentiate between SQL and NoSQL databases with respect to schema flexibility and consistency model.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Parameter | SQL (RDBMS) | NoSQL | |---|---|---| | **Schema Flexibility** | Rigid, predefined schema enforced at write time | Dynamic, schema-on-read, flexible | | **Consistency Model** | Strong ACID consistency | Eventual consistency (BASE) | --- ## 5.2 Part B — 14 Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice) > [!NOTE] > **As per KTU 2024 ESE Regulations**, students must answer ONE question of 14 marks from Module 4, choosing between two alternatives. Both alternatives are provided below. --- ### QUESTION A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 4 Internal Choice A]** **(a)** Explain the **CAP theorem** with a neat diagram. Discuss how the CAP theorem influences the design of distributed databases. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Compare and contrast **SQL, NoSQL, and NewSQL** databases with suitable examples. Discuss the concept of **Polyglot Persistence**. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution for Question A(a) — CAP Theorem (7 marks) **Step 1: Formal Statement (2 marks)** The CAP theorem, formulated by **Eric Brewer in 2000**, states that any distributed data store can simultaneously guarantee at most **two out of three** properties: - **Consistency (C)**: Every read receives the most recent write or an error - **Availability (A)**: Every request receives a (non-error) response, even if some nodes fail - **Partition Tolerance (P)**: The system continues to operate despite arbitrary network partitions between nodes **Formal inequality:** $$ C + A + P \leq 2 $$ **Step 2: Diagrammatic Representation (2 marks)** The CAP triangle has three vertices representing C, A, and P. Edges represent the trade-off pairs: - **CA edge**: Traditional single-datacenter RDBMS (no partition to tolerate) - **CP edge**: HBase, MongoDB (consistency preferred, availability sacrificed) - **AP edge**: DynamoDB, Cassandra (availability preferred, eventual consistency) **Step 3: Design Influence (3 marks)** 1. **Quorum Tuning** — Dynamo-style systems allow tunable $(R, W)$ parameters to shift between C and A. 2. **Replication Strategy** — Synchronous replication favors C; asynchronous favors A. 3. **Conflict Resolution** — AP systems use **vector clocks** or **last-write-wins** to handle conflicts. 4. **Geographic Distribution** — Multi-region deployments require explicit CAP decisions. --- #### Model Solution for Question A(b) — SQL vs NoSQL vs NewSQL (7 marks) **Step 1: SQL Databases (2 marks)** - **Examples**: MySQL, PostgreSQL, Oracle - **Properties**: ACID, rigid schema, vertical scaling, ANSI SQL - **Use case**: Banking, ERP, structured OLTP **Step 2: NoSQL Databases (2 marks)** - **Examples**: MongoDB (Document), Cassandra (Column), Redis (KV), Neo4j (Graph) - **Properties**: BASE, schema-less, horizontal scaling, API-based queries - **Use case**: IoT, social networks, big data **Step 3: NewSQL Databases (1.5 marks)** - **Examples**: Google Spanner, CockroachDB, Amazon Aurora - **Properties**: Distributed ACID with horizontal scale - **Use case**: Global OLTP requiring both consistency and scale **Step 4: Polyglot Persistence (1.5 marks)** Defined by **Martin Fowler (2011)**, Polyglot Persistence is the principle of using **multiple specialized databases** within a single application. For example, an e-commerce platform may use PostgreSQL for orders, MongoDB for catalog, Redis for cache, and Neo4j for recommendations. Each store is chosen for its optimal fit with the data model's structure. --- ### QUESTION B (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 4 Internal Choice B]** **(a)** Explain the **ACID properties** of relational databases and contrast them with **BASE properties** of NoSQL databases. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Describe the **Database-as-a-Service (DBaaS)** architecture with a block diagram. Explain **sharding and replication** strategies in cloud databases. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution for Question B(a) — ACID vs BASE (7 marks) **Step 1: ACID Properties (3.5 marks)** | Property | Definition | Example | |---|---|---| | **Atomicity** | A transaction is all-or-nothing | Bank debit + credit either both succeed or both fail | | **Consistency** | DB transitions between valid states | Foreign key constraints always satisfied | | **Isolation** | Concurrent transactions appear serialized | Two simultaneous balance checks don't interfere | | **Durability** | Committed data survives crashes | Power loss does not lose committed data | **Step 2: BASE Properties (2 marks)** | Property | Meaning | |---|---| | **Basically Available** | System remains responsive despite failures | | **Soft State** | State may evolve without input due to async replication | | **Eventual Consistency** | Replicas converge to same value given enough time | **Step 3: Comparative Analysis (1.5 marks)** - ACID favors **strong consistency** for mission-critical workloads. - BASE favors **high availability and partition tolerance** for web-scale workloads. - ACID is **single-node or small-cluster**; BASE is **distributed, geo-replicated**. --- #### Model Solution for Question B(b) — DBaaS Architecture with Sharding & Replication (7 marks) **Step 1: DBaaS Architecture (3 marks)** The DBaaS stack has 5 layers: 1. **Application Layer** — Customer-facing APIs (REST, gRPC) 2. **Control Plane** — Provisioning, scaling, patching 3. **Query Processing Layer** — SQL parser, optimizer, execution engine 4. **Storage Layer** — Distributed, replicated, sharded storage 5. **Physical Infrastructure** — Servers, NVMe SSDs, network fabric **Step 2: Sharding (2 marks)** - **Definition**: Horizontal partitioning of data across multiple nodes - **Types**: - **Range Sharding** — Partition by key ranges (e.g., users A-M, N-Z) - **Hash Sharding** — Hash function determines shard (e.g., MD5(user_id) mod 16) - **Directory Sharding** — Lookup table maps keys to shards - **Formula**: $S_{\text{per shard}} = D_{\text{total}} / N_{\text{shards}}$ **Step 3: Replication (2 marks)** - **Definition**: Maintaining multiple copies of data across nodes - **Types**: - **Synchronous** — Write waits for all replicas (strong consistency, higher latency) - **Asynchronous** — Write commits locally, replicates later (lower latency, eventual consistency) - **Quorum Constraint**: $R + W > N$ for strong consistency --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls — Module 4** > 1. **Confusing CAP with ACID** — CAP is about distributed *consistency under partition*; ACID is about transaction *integrity in a single DB*. Examiners deduct **2 marks** for this confusion. > 2. **Forgetting NewSQL** — When comparing SQL vs NoSQL, omitting NewSQL as a third class costs **1–2 marks**. > 3. **Missing the Diagram** — In CAP questions, failing to draw the triangle = **1–2 marks lost**. > 4. **No Examples** — Citing only MongoDB without specifying *type* (Document) loses marks. Always state: *type + example*. > 5. **Missing Quorum Formula** — In replication questions, writing $R + W > N$ without the **inequality derivation** loses **1 mark**. > 6. **Polyglot Persistence** — Students often *describe* it without giving a **concrete architecture example** = **1 mark lost**. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 4: Database Services** ### Core Definitions (MUST KNOW) - ✅ **DBaaS** = managed cloud database service with elasticity, multi-tenancy, and pay-per-use pricing - ✅ **CAP Theorem** = only 2 of {C, A, P} simultaneously; $C + A + P \leq 2$ - ✅ **ACID** = Atomicity, Consistency, Isolation, Durability — for SQL - ✅ **BASE** = Basically Available, Soft state, Eventual consistency — for NoSQL - ✅ **Polyglot Persistence** = multiple specialized DBs in one app (Fowler, 2011) ### Critical Formulas (MEMORIZE) - ✅ **Quorum**: $R + W > N$ for strong consistency - ✅ **Tolerance**: $t = N - W$ failures during write - ✅ **TrueTime**: $\epsilon \leq 7 \text{ ms}$, commit wait $= 2\epsilon \approx 14 \text{ ms}$ - ✅ **Per-shard capacity**: $D_{\text{shard}} = D_{\text{total}} / N_{\text{shards}}$ ### Service Mapping (REMEMBER) - ✅ **Amazon RDS / Aurora / DynamoDB** → AWS flagship DB services - ✅ **Google Cloud SQL / Spanner / Bigtable** → GCP flagship DB services - ✅ **Azure SQL DB / Cosmos DB** → Azure flagship DB services ### CAP Classifications (REMEMBER) - ✅ **DynamoDB** → AP (eventually consistent) - ✅ **BigTable / HBase** → CP - ✅ **Cassandra** → AP (tunable) - ✅ **Spanner** → CP with TrueTime - ✅ **MongoDB** → CP (single master) - ✅ **Traditional RDBMS (single DC)** → CA ### Migration Framework (REMEMBER) - ✅ **6Rs**: Rehost, Replatform, Refactor, Repurchase, Retire, Retain ### Frequent Pitfalls (AVOID) - ❌ Do not confuse CAP with ACID - ❌ Do not omit NewSQL in SQL vs NoSQL comparisons - ❌ Do not skip diagrams in CAP questions - ❌ Do not forget concrete examples for each database type

topics.title: SaaS Offerings: Gmail
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT525 - CLOUD COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** SaaS Offerings: Gmail

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned):**
> **Gmail** (Google Mail) is a flagship **Software-as-a-Service (SaaS)** email and communication platform delivered by Google over the internet. It is accessed primarily through a web browser or thin-client mobile applications, where the underlying infrastructure, application logic, storage, and security are entirely managed by the cloud provider. Gmail is a textbook example of public cloud SaaS, demonstrating multi-tenancy, elastic scalability, browser-based delivery, and zero local installation.

### 1.1 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

Imagine you want to send and receive letters. In the old world (on-premise model), you would have to **build your own post office** — buy land, construct the building, hire postal workers, buy trucks, maintain sorting machines, and set up a delivery network. Every time mail volume increases, you must expand your own building.

Now consider **renting a seat in a massive, professionally-run post office** (this is SaaS). You simply walk in, use the counter, and mail your letter. The post office already has:
- A giant building (data center)
- Sorting machines (storage & indexing systems)
- Postal workers (application servers)
- Security guards (authentication & anti-spam)
- Backup power and redundant routes (fault tolerance)

You pay nothing extra when more letters come in, and you don't fix the roof when it rains. **Gmail is exactly this** — Google owns the "post office," and you just use the email service through your browser or phone.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Gmail is the Gold-Standard SaaS Case Study for KTU:**
> 1. **Zero installation** — runs entirely on a browser
> 2. **Multi-tenancy** — one Gmail instance serves **1.8+ billion users** worldwide
> 3. **Elastic scaling** — automatic storage and compute scaling
> 4. **Pay-per-use (Freemium model)** — free for consumers, per-user/month for Google Workspace
> 5. **Continuous delivery** — feature updates pushed without user action
> 6. **High availability** — designed for **99.9%** uptime with no scheduled downtime

### 1.2 Key SaaS Metrics for Gmail

| Parameter | Value / Description |
|---|---|
| Free Storage (Consumer) | **15 GB** unified across Gmail, Drive, Photos |
| Paid Storage (Google One) | 100 GB to 30 TB plans |
| Daily Email Volume | **~100+ billion** emails processed per day |
| Active Users | **1.8+ billion** worldwide (as of 2023-2024) |
| Service Uptime SLA (Workspace) | **99.9%** monthly uptime guarantee |
| Delivery Model | Web browser, IMAP/POP3, Mobile apps |
| Underlying Cloud | Google Cloud Platform (GCP) |

### 1.3 SaaS Characteristics Demonstrated by Gmail

- **Network-based access:** Accessed over HTTP/HTTPS from any browser.
- **Centralized hosting:** All logic and data reside on Google's distributed data centers.
- **Multi-tenancy:** A single Gmail codebase serves billions of users with logical isolation.
- **Rapid elasticity:** Storage and compute scale transparently.
- **Measured service:** Free tier (ad-supported) and metered paid plans (Google Workspace).
- **Self-service provisioning:** A new account is created in seconds with no human interaction.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three-Layer SaaS Architecture of Gmail

Gmail's design can be broken down into three conceptual SaaS layers that align with how KTU 2024 expects students to classify cloud service models.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Layer 1 — Presentation Layer (Client Side)**
> - HTML, CSS, JavaScript served via HTTPS
> - Progressive Web App (PWA) capabilities
> - Lightweight mobile clients (Android/iOS) using REST APIs
> - **Zero client-side state** — all state is server-side

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Layer 2 — Application Layer (Gmail Frontend Servers)**
> - AJAX-driven dynamic UI
> - Conversation threading engine
> - Spam classifier (now powered by **Google Gemini / TensorFlow ML models**)
> - Search engine using proprietary indexing
> - Virus and malware scanning
> - HTML rendering engine (sanitizes user content)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Layer 3 — Backend / Storage Layer (Google's Distributed Infrastructure)**
> - **GFS / Colossus** distributed file system
> - **BigTable / Spanner** for structured metadata
> - **Borg / Kubernetes**-like cluster manager
> - **MapReduce** for batch analytics
> - Custom index servers for search

### 2.2 SaaS Delivery Models Supported by Gmail

| Delivery Model | Mechanism | KTU Concept Link |
|---|---|---|
| Web Application | Browser-based UI at `mail.google.com` | Pure SaaS |
| Mobile App | Native Android/iOS thin clients | Mobile SaaS |
| API-based Access | Gmail API (REST) for third-party integration | SaaS extensibility |
| Protocol-based Access | IMAP / SMTP / POP3 | Hybrid (SaaS + traditional protocol) |
| Enterprise Federation | Google Workspace domain integration | SaaS at organizational scale |

### 2.3 Gmail as a Multi-Tenant SaaS

**Multi-tenancy** means a single software instance serves multiple customer organizations (tenants). In Gmail:
- A **personal Gmail account** = one tenant (a single user)
- A **Google Workspace organization** = one tenant (a company with thousands of users)

All tenants share the same physical infrastructure, but data is **logically isolated** through access control lists, encryption keys (per-tenant keys in Workspace Enterprise), and namespace separation.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

Since SaaS is a conceptual topic, the "formulas" here are service-level performance equations and scalability models that examiners love to ask.

| Concept | Equation / Definition | Unit / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Total Cost of Ownership (SaaS) | $\text{TCO}_{\text{SaaS}} = C_{\text{subscription}} + C_{\text{internet}} + C_{\text{training}}$ | INR/month or USD/year |
| TCO (On-Premise Email) | $\text{TCO}_{\text{on-prem}} = C_{\text{server}} + C_{\text{license}} + C_{\text{admin}} + C_{\text{power}} + C_{\text{maintenance}}$ | Capital + Operational |
| Annual Savings | $S = \text{TCO}_{\text{on-prem}} - \text{TCO}_{\text{SaaS}}$ | Always positive in SaaS |
| Storage Allocation | $S_{\text{total}} = S_{\text{mailbox}} + S_{\text{spam}} + S_{\text{trash}}$ | GB |
| Service Availability | $A = \dfrac{\text{Uptime}}{\text{Uptime} + \text{Downtime}} \times 100\%$ | % — Gmail SLA: **99.9%** |
| Maximum Downtime per Month (at 99.9%) | $D_{\max} = 30 \times 24 \times 60 \times (1 - 0.999) = 43.2 \text{ minutes}$ | Calculated below |
| Concurrent User Capacity | $C = \dfrac{\text{Server capacity (req/s)}}{\text{Peak request rate per user}}$ | Users per server |
| Horizontal Scalability | $N_{\text{servers}} = \left\lceil \dfrac{L_{\text{total}}}{L_{\text{per\_server}}} \right\rceil$ | $L$ = load in req/s |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundary Condition Note:** For the SLA equation, Google Workspace provides **99.9%** uptime — this allows a maximum of **~43.2 minutes** of downtime per 30-day month. This is a frequently asked KTU numerical problem.

### 2.5 Engineering Utility of Gmail's SaaS Model

- **For Individuals:** Free, zero-maintenance communication with massive storage.
- **For Startups:** Avoids building an SMTP/IMAP stack — they can focus on product.
- **For Enterprises:** Centralized admin console, DLP (Data Loss Prevention), audit logs.
- **For Developers:** Gmail API enables programmatic mail, label, and thread management.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Maximum Allowed Downtime at 99.9% SLA

> This is a classic KTU numerical that appears almost every exam cycle. Let's derive it rigorously.

**Given:**
- SLA Availability $A = 99.9\%$
- Month duration $T = 30 \text{ days}$

**Step 1 — Convert the SLA into a downtime fraction.**

$$
A = 0.999 = \frac{\text{Uptime}}{\text{Total Time}}
$$

**Step 2 — Express downtime fraction.**

$$
1 - A = 0.001
$$

**Step 3 — Convert total time to minutes.**

$$
