# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### Question 1: Define Google Drive. List any three key features.

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition**: Google Drive is a cloud-based file storage and synchronization service developed by Google that allows users to store, share, and collaboratively edit files in real time across multiple devices.

**Three Key Features**:
1. **Cloud Storage** — Files stored on Google's servers (Colossus), accessible from any device with internet.
2. **Real-time Collaboration** — Multiple users can simultaneously edit Docs, Sheets, Slides using **Operational Transformation (OT)**.
3. **Cross-platform Synchronization** — Web, Desktop (Windows/Mac/Linux), iOS, Android with automatic sync.
4. *(Bonus)* Deep integration with Google Workspace (Gmail, Calendar, Meet).
5. *(Bonus)* Free 15 GB storage with paid tiers up to 30 TB.

---

### Question 2: List the five components of Microsoft Azure Platform Architecture.

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**Model Answer:**

The five components are:

1. **Windows Azure Compute** — Provides execution environments (Web Role, Worker Role, VM Role).
2. **Windows Azure Storage** — Scalable storage for blobs, tables, queues, and drives.
3. **Windows Azure AppFabric** — Middleware offering Service Bus and Access Control Service.
4. **Windows Azure Connect** — Secure IP-level connectivity between on-premise and cloud.
5. **Windows Azure CDN (Content Delivery Network)** — Distributed caching for static content at edge locations.

---

## PART B — Full-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the architecture of Google Drive in detail with a neat block diagram. Discuss how real-time collaboration is achieved. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain Windows Azure Compute services. Differentiate between Web Role, Worker Role, and VM Role. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — (a) Google Drive Architecture

**Google Drive is structured into 3 main layers**:

**1. Client Layer**:
- **Web App**: Browser-based UI built on JavaScript/AngularJS.
- **Desktop Client**: "Drive for Desktop" provides folder-level sync to local machine.
- **Mobile Apps**: iOS/Android apps with offline access and camera backup.

**2. Middleware / Application Layer**:
- **Authentication Server**: Implements **OAuth 2.0** for secure delegated access.
- **Frontend HTTP Servers**: Handle RESTful API calls, file uploads/downloads.
- **Collaboration Engine**: Implements **Operational Transformation (OT)** for concurrent edits.
- **Search Service**: Maintains a full-text inverted index for instant file search.

**3. Storage Layer**:
- **BigTable**: Stores metadata — file IDs, ownership, ACLs, version history.
- **Colossus (GFS successor)**: Stores actual file chunks (default **256 KB**).
- **Versioning Store**: Keeps previous versions for undo/restore.

**Real-Time Collaboration via OT Algorithm**:

When two users edit the same document:
- Each user's operation is sent to the server.
- The server serializes operations and detects conflicts.
- **OT transforms** incoming operations based on previously applied concurrent operations.
- All clients eventually converge to the **same final state** — guaranteeing **strong eventual consistency**.

Example:
- User A inserts "Hello" at position 0.
- User B inserts "World" at position 0 concurrently.
- After OT transformation → Final state: **"WorldHello"** on all clients.

This eliminates the "lost update" problem of naive last-write-wins approaches.

---

#### Model Solution — (b) Azure Compute Services

**Windows Azure Compute** provides three execution models:

| Feature | Web Role | Worker Role | VM Role |
|---------|----------|-------------|---------|
| **Purpose** | Front-end web apps | Background processing | Custom OS control |
| **Web Server** | IIS 7 pre-installed | No IIS | User-installed |
| **HTTP Endpoint** | Yes (auto load-balanced) | Optional (internal) | Optional |
| **Use Case** | ASP.NET websites, REST APIs | Batch jobs, queue consumers | Legacy apps needing specific OS |
| **Scalability** | Auto-scale via configuration | Scales independently | Manual |
| **Communication** | HTTP, Queue | Queue, Blob, internal endpoints | Any protocol |

**Web Role**: Best for hosting HTTP-based front-end applications. The Fabric Controller automatically load-balances incoming HTTP requests across all Web Role instances.

**Worker Role**: Best for non-HTTP, long-running tasks. Typically consumes messages from Queue Storage and writes results to Blob Storage.

**VM Role**: Provides full control over the OS image (uploaded as VHD). Used for migrating legacy on-premise applications that need specific software configurations.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Describe in detail the four types of Windows Azure Storage services with suitable examples. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain Microsoft Azure Platform Architecture with a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of the Fabric Controller. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — (a) Four Types of Azure Storage

**1. Blob Storage**:
- Stores **unstructured binary data** — images, videos, audio, backups, log files.
- Three blob types: **Block blobs** (general), **Page blobs** (random read/write, used for VHDs), **Append blobs** (log files).
- Example: A photo-sharing app stores user-uploaded images in Blob Storage and serves them via CDN.

**2. Table Storage**:
- **NoSQL key-attribute store** for semi-structured data.
- Tables have no fixed schema — each row (entity) can have different properties.
- Partition key + Row key for fast lookup.
- Example: Storing user profiles where some users have Twitter handles and others do not.

**3. Queue Storage**:
- Provides **asynchronous message passing** between application components.
- Messages can be up to 64 KB; queues can hold millions of messages.
- Example: Web Role places "process this order" messages in a queue; Worker Role consumes them.

**4. Drive Storage**:
- An **NTFS-formatted virtual hard drive** mounted as a network drive to a role instance.
- Allows legacy applications to use standard file I/O APIs (`CreateFile`, `ReadFile`, `WriteFile`).
- Example: Migrating a legacy on-premise Windows app that writes to local `C:\Data\` folder.

---

#### Model Solution — (b) Azure Platform Architecture & Fabric Controller

**Architecture Diagram** (refer to SECTION 4.3):

```
Region → Datacenter → Cluster → Rack → Blade (Server)
                  ↓
            Fabric Controller (Brain)
                  ↓
   ┌──────────┬──────────┬──────────┬──────────┐
   ↓          ↓          ↓          ↓          ↓
Compute    Storage   AppFabric   Connect     CDN
```

**Layers**:
1. **Fabric Layer**: Physical servers, racks, switches in datacenter.
2. **Fabric Controller Layer**: Distributed cluster orchestration software.
3. **Platform Services Layer**: Compute, Storage, AppFabric, Connect, CDN.
4. **Application Layer**: User-deployed Web Roles, Worker Roles, VMs.

**Role of the Fabric Controller**:

The **Fabric Controller** is the **centralized brain** of every Azure cluster. It is itself a distributed application running on dedicated nodes. Its responsibilities:

1. **Resource Provisioning**: Decides which physical server hosts a new role instance based on available CPU, RAM, and network capacity.

2. **Service Deployment**: Receives `.cspkg` (service package) and `.cscfg` (configuration) from developers and deploys them to selected servers.

3. **Health Monitoring**: Each role instance sends a **heartbeat every 30 seconds**. If heartbeat is missed, the instance is marked as failed.

4. **Automatic Healing**: On failure, the Fabric Controller restarts the instance on a healthy server without human intervention.

5. **Load Balancing**: Distributes incoming requests across all healthy instances of a role.

6. **OS & Patch Management**: Maintains the host OS image and applies updates across all servers in the cluster.

The Fabric Controller ensures **high availability, fault tolerance, and elastic scalability** — the three core promises of cloud computing.

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes students make — avoid these to score full marks:**
> 
> 1. **Confusing Fabric Controller with Load Balancer** — The Fabric Controller is a **cluster management application**, NOT just a load balancer. It handles provisioning, healing, and OS management.
> 
> 2. **Forgetting the fourth storage type (Drive)** — Many students only mention Blob, Table, Queue. Always include **Drive Storage** for full marks.
> 
> 3. **Saying "VM Role is the same as Azure VM"** — The original VM Role is **deprecated**. In modern Azure, this is replaced by **Azure Virtual Machines** (IaaS).
> 
> 4. **Not drawing a diagram** — KTU examiners deduct up to **2 marks** if the block diagram is missing or poorly labeled.
> 
> 5. **Confusing AppFabric Service Bus with Queue Storage** — Service Bus is for **cross-organization / firewall-traversal messaging**; Queue Storage is for **intra-Azure decoupling**.
> 
> 6. **Not mentioning Operational Transformation** in Google Drive — The OT algorithm is the **core differentiator** of Google Docs collaboration and fetches easy marks.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Google Drive** — Rapid-Fire Revision:
> - Cloud-based file storage + sync service by Google.
> - Built on **Colossus** (storage) + **BigTable** (metadata) + **GFS lineage**.
> - **15 GB free**; up to **5 TB per file**.
> - **OAuth 2.0** authentication.
> - **Operational Transformation (OT)** algorithm for real-time collaboration.
> - **Differential block-level sync** saves bandwidth.

> **Microsoft Azure Platform Architecture** — Rapid-Fire Revision:
> - **5 components**: Compute, Storage, AppFabric, Connect, CDN.
> - **Fabric Controller** = brain of every cluster (provisioning, healing, monitoring).
> - **3 execution roles**: Web Role (IIS), Worker Role (background), VM Role (custom OS — now Azure VMs).
> - **4 storage types**: Blob (unstructured), Table (NoSQL), Queue (async messaging), Drive (NTFS).
> - **AppFabric** = Service Bus + Access Control Service (claims-based auth).
> - **Azure Connect** = IP-level IPSec tunnel between on-premise and cloud.
> - **Azure CDN** = edge caching at 100+ cities for static content.
> - **Triple replication** locally; **11 nines (99.999999999%)** durability with Geo-Redundant Storage.

> **Cross-Comparison Anchor**:
> - Google Drive → **SaaS consumer product** (built on Google Cloud internal infrastructure).
> - Microsoft Azure → **PaaS/IaaS platform** (exposing underlying infrastructure to developers).
> - Both rely on **distributed file systems, metadata stores, and global datacenters**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hyper-V
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT525 - CLOUD COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Hyper-V

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Hyper-V** is Microsoft's native, hardware-assisted **Type-1 (bare-metal) hypervisor** that is tightly integrated into the Windows Server operating system and the Windows client platform (Windows 8/10/11 Pro, Education, and Enterprise editions). It enables the creation, deployment, and management of **virtual machines (VMs)** on a physical x86/x64 host, allowing multiple isolated operating system instances — each with its own virtual CPU, memory, storage, and networking — to run concurrently on a single physical server.

> [!NOTE]
> **Hypervisor** = A small, highly privileged software/firmware layer that creates, runs, and supervises virtual machines by arbitrating physical hardware resources among them. It is the *fundamental building block* of all cloud computing infrastructure (IaaS).

Formally, Hyper-V is delivered as a **Windows Server Role** and implements a **microkernelized hypervisor architecture** in which the virtualization control plane runs in a dedicated, hardware-isolated partition (the *root partition*), while guest operating systems run in isolated *child partitions*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Highlight — PECPT525 Module 4:**
> Hyper-V is covered under the broader umbrella of *Server Virtualization & Hypervisors*. Students must understand its position relative to **VMware ESXi, Citrix XenServer, and KVM**, and must be able to differentiate **Type-1 vs Type-2 virtualization**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition

Imagine a large **apartment building (the physical server)** sitting on a single plot of land.

- The **land + foundation + building skeleton + shared utilities (water, electricity, drainage)** represents the **physical hardware** (CPU, RAM, disk, NIC).
- The **building's main electrical room and security desk** represents the **Hyper-V hypervisor** — it decides who gets how much power, when, and enforces the rules.
- Each **independent flat/apartment** is a **Virtual Machine (VM)**. The flat has its own lock, its own meter, its own address, and its own tenant. Tenants in Flat-A cannot enter Flat-B — that is **isolation**.
- The **landlord's office** (where the building manager sits) is the **Root/Parent Partition** running Windows Server with the Hyper-V management tools.

So Hyper-V is essentially the "**building manager + security + utility distributor**" of a server, allowing many "tenants" (VMs) to share the same hardware fairly, securely, and efficiently.

> [!TIP]
> **Key Takeaway:** Hyper-V is **not** a program that runs *on top of* Windows like VMware Workstation. It is a *hypervisor that hosts* Windows. The relationship is inverted: **Windows runs ON Hyper-V**, not the other way around.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Boundary Values (Must Memorize)

| Parameter | Value | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Maximum logical CPUs per host** | **1024** (Windows Server 2022/2025) | Enterprise scalability |
| **Maximum virtual CPUs per VM** | **240** (Gen 2 VMs) | Per-VM vCPU cap |
| **Maximum RAM per VM** | **12 TB** (Gen 2) | Memory overcommitment boundary |
| **Maximum VMs per host** | **1024** (Windows Server 2022) | Practical density |
| **Maximum virtual disks (VHDX)** | **64 TB** | VHDX vs VHD (2 TB limit) |
| **Hyper-V Generation** | **Gen 1** (legacy BIOS) / **Gen 2** (UEFI, Secure Boot) | Architectural choice |
| **Hyper-V Version** | **v10.0** (Windows Server 2019/2022) | API & feature set |

## 1.4 Historical Context for KTU Examiners

| Year | Milestone |
|---|---|
| **2008** | Hyper-V 1.0 released with Windows Server 2008 |
| **2012** | Hyper-V 3.0 — Shared VHDX, SMB 3.0 support |
| **2016** | Hyper-V 5.0 — Nested Virtualization, Shielded VMs |
| **2019** | Hyper-V 6.0 — Default Security, Azure integration |
| **2022/2025** | Hyper-V 10.0 — Co-management, Azure Arc hybrid |


<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Hyper-V Architecture — The Microkernelized Model

Hyper-V uses a **microkernelized hypervisor architecture** (also called the *split-driver model*). The hypervisor itself is a thin layer (~1 MB) that contains only the *minimum* required to enforce isolation and arbitrate hardware access. All other "intelligence" (device drivers, management stack, VSPs) lives in the **root partition**.

### 2.1.1 Core Architectural Components

1. **Hyper-V Hypervisor (VTL 0 — Virtual Trust Level 0)**
   - Runs directly on hardware (Ring -1 in classic x86 protection rings).
   - Owns CPU scheduling, memory management, and interrupt control.
   - Has *no device drivers* — passes I/O to the root partition.

2. **Root Partition (Parent Partition)**
   - Runs the **host operating system** (Windows Server with Hyper-V role, or a small Hyper-V Server core).
   - Contains the **Virtual Machine Management Service (VMMS)**, **Virtualization Service Providers (VSPs)**, and the **WMI Provider**.
   - Acts as the *I/O proxy* for child partitions.

3. **Child Partitions (Guest VMs)**
   - Run **guest operating systems** (Windows, Linux, FreeBSD).
   - Do *not* have direct hardware access. They contain **Virtualization Service Clients (VSCs)** that communicate with VSPs in the root partition via the high-speed **VMBus**.

4. **VMBus** — A logical, in-memory, point-to-point communication channel (uses shared memory pages and *doorbell* mechanisms for inter-partition signaling).

5. **Virtualization Stack**
   - **VSP (Virtualization Service Provider)** — runs in the root partition; serves I/O requests.
   - **VSC (Virtualization Service Client)** — runs in the child partition; initiates I/O requests.
   - Communication follows the **VSP/VSC pair model** over VMBus.

6. **VM Worker Process (VMWP.exe)** — A per-VM user-mode process that hosts the VSP for that VM.

7. **Hyper-V Virtual Switch (vSwitch)**
   - A software-defined **Layer-2 Ethernet switch** implemented in the root partition.
   - Three switch types:
     * **External** — bound to a physical NIC (uplink to the physical network).
     * **Internal** — bound to the host OS only (host-to-VM communication).
     * **Private** — VM-to-VM only (isolated from host and physical network).

8. **WMI Provider (Hyper-V WMI)** — Programmatic management interface (`root\virtualization\v2` namespace). Used by PowerShell, SCVMM, and System Center.

9. **VMMS (Virtual Machine Management Service)** — Central daemon that orchestrates VM lifecycle (create, start, stop, snapshot, migrate).

### 2.1.2 Execution Flow of a VM I/O Request

1. Guest application issues a read/write (e.g., to virtual disk).
2. Guest OS storage miniport driver forwards the request to the **VSC (storvsc)**.
3. VSC places the request on the **VMBus** shared memory ring buffer.
4. VMBus signals the **VSP (storvsp)** in the root partition via a *doorbell* interrupt.
5. VSP executes the request against the **real physical driver** (e.g., Storport miniport → SATA/SAS/NVMe controller).
6. Result is returned to the VSC via VMBus.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why microkernelized?** It minimizes the hypervisor's attack surface, improves reliability (driver crashes are confined to the root partition), and enables the parent partition to be patched/rebooted (in some configurations) without disturbing running VMs.

## 2.2 Type-1 vs Type-2 Hypervisors — The Critical KTU Distinction

| Feature | Type-1 (Bare-Metal) | Type-2 (Hosted) |
|---|---|---|
| Runs on | Bare hardware | On top of a host OS |
| Performance | **High** (direct hardware access) | Lower (double-scheduling overhead) |
| Examples | **Hyper-V, VMware ESXi, Citrix XenServer, KVM** | VMware Workstation, Oracle VirtualBox, Parallels |
| Use case | Production servers, cloud data centers | Desktop testing, dev/lab environments |
| Hyper-V is… | **Type-1** (despite being bundled with Windows Server, the hypervisor *itself* boots before the Windows kernel in modern implementations) | N/A |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner's Trap:** Hyper-V is *not* a Type-2 hypervisor. It is a **Type-1 microkernelized hypervisor** that *co-exists* with a Windows Server management OS in the root partition. This is the most common KTU viva question.

## 2.3 Hyper-V Generation 1 vs Generation 2

| Feature | Generation 1 | Generation 2 |
|---|---|---|
| Firmware | Legacy BIOS | **UEFI** |
| Boot media | IDE only | **SCSI** (synthetic) |
| Secure Boot | ❌ No | ✅ Yes |
| Boot from VHDX (native) | ❌ | ✅ |
| Network boot (PXE) | Legacy | ✅ UEFI PXE |
| Memory limit | 1 TB (practical) | **12 TB** |
| Recommended for new VMs | Legacy only | ✅ **Always Gen 2** |

## 2.4 Key Virtualization Concepts in Hyper-V

### 2.4.1 Virtual Hard Disk (VHD / VHDX)

- **VHD** — Legacy format, max **2 TB**, fixed-size 512-byte logical sector.
- **VHDX** — Modern format, max **64 TB**, 4-KB logical sector, **resiliency to power failure** (logs metadata changes), trim/unmap support.

Three provisioning types:

| Type | Disk Usage | Creation Time | Best For |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Fixed** | Full size allocated upfront | Slow | Production, high-I/O performance |
| **Dynamically Expanding** | Grows as data is written | Fast | Dev/test, save storage |
| **Differencing** | Stores changes vs. parent VHDX | Fast | Short-lived test labs, checkpoint chains |

### 2.4.2 Checkpoints (formerly "Snapshots")

Hyper-V has two types:

- **Standard Checkpoints** — Capture VM state and data; full differencing disk chain. Useful for dev/test.
- **Production Checkpoints** — Use **Volume Shadow Copy Service (VSS)** inside the guest to create application-consistent backups. **Recommended for production.**

> [!WARNING]
> Production checkpoints require VSS-aware applications. Non-VSS apps (older Linux without `fsfreeze`) get *crash-consistent* checkpoints only.

### 2.4.3 Live Migration & Failover Clustering

- **Live Migration** — Move a running VM from one Hyper-V host to another **with zero perceived downtime** (down to sub-second pause) using shared storage (SMB 3.0, Cluster Shared Volumes) or **Shared-Nothing Live Migration** (uses direct-attached storage replication).
- **Quick Migration** — Saved-state migration (seconds-to-minutes downtime).
- **Storage Live Migration** — Move a VM's storage while running.

### 2.4.4 Hyper-V Replica

- **Asynchronous, VM-level replication** between two Hyper-V hosts/sites.
- RPO is typically **5 minutes** (configurable: 30 sec to 15 min).
- Survives site failure — DR cornerstone for SMB scenarios.

### 2.4.5 Nested Virtualization

- Running a Hyper-V VM *inside* another Hyper-V VM.
- Enabled per-VM via PowerShell:
  `Set-VMProcessor -VMName <name> -ExposeVirtualizationExtensions $true`
- Requires **VT-x / AMD-V** with **SLAT (Second Level Address Translation)** support.
- Uses: testing cloud stacks (e.g., Azure Stack HCI dev), security research, training labs.

### 2.4.6 Network Virtualization (Hyper-V Network Virtualization / HNV)

- Overlays virtual MAC + virtual IP addresses (CA — Customer Address) on top of provider addresses (PA) using **NVGRE** or **VXLAN** encapsulation.
- Foundation for **Software-Defined Networking (SDN)** in Windows Server.

## 2.5 KTU Formula / Metric Cheat Sheet

| Quantity | Formula / Value | Unit | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Total Host vCPUs | $\sum_{i=1}^{N} vCPU_i$ | vCPUs | Must not exceed logical CPUs |
| vCPU oversubscription ratio | $R_{over} = \dfrac{\sum vCPU_{VMs}}{vCPU_{host}}$ | dimensionless | Typical 2:1 to 4:1 |
| Memory overcommit ratio | $R_{mem} = \dfrac{\sum RAM_{VMs}}{RAM_{host}}$ | dimensionless | Hard limit $\leq 1.0$ (no memory overcommit by default) |
| VHDX max size | $64 \cdot 2^{40}$ bytes | **64 TB** | Logical sector **4096 B** |
| Live Migration bandwidth (typical) | $1 \text{ Gbps} \leq B \leq 10 \text{ Gbps}$ | Gbps | RDMA lowers CPU usage |
| Replication RPO (default) | $RPO = 5 \text{ min}$ | minutes | Configurable 30 s – 15 min |
| Disk IOPS consolidation | $IOPS_{VM} = IOPS_{backend} \cdot \alpha$ | IOPS | $\alpha$ = efficiency factor, $0 < \alpha \leq 1$ |
| CPU scheduling quantum | $\Delta t_{quantum} = 10 \text{ ms}$ (default) | ms | Windows hypervisor scheduler |
| vSwitch throughput | $\leq 10 \text{ Gbps}$ (per virtual NIC) | Gbps | Limited by physical NIC + RSS/vRSS |
| Maximum VMs per host | $N_{max} = 1024$ | VMs | Windows Server 2022+ |
| Maximum vCPUs per VM | $V_{max} = 240$ | vCPUs | Gen 2 only |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All numeric values above are the **authoritative limits documented by Microsoft for Windows Server 2022 / Hyper-V v10.0**. KTU exam answers should cite these explicitly.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Azure Public Cloud** — Every Azure VM is a Hyper-V VM on Azure hosts (Azure was built on Hyper-V before largely transitioning to custom KVM-based stack for newer regions, but Hyper-V remains foundational for **Azure Stack HCI**, **Azure Stack Hub**, and **Windows Server 2022 Datacenter**).
- **Private Cloud (IaaS)** — Enterprises deploy Hyper-V on Windows Server + System Center to deliver IaaS internally.
- **DevOps CI/CD** — Self-hosted agents run as Hyper-V Gen-2 VMs with **nested virtualization** for Docker/Kubernetes testing.
- **Disaster Recovery (DR)** — Hyper-V Replica between two sites gives SMBs near-enterprise DR at no extra licensing cost.
- **VDI (Virtual Desktop Infrastructure)** — Hyper-V hosts RDS session hosts or Windows 10/11 Enterprise pooled VDI desktops.

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: CPU Oversubscription Planning

A KTU exam may ask: *"Given a Hyper-V host with 2 physical sockets × 16 cores × 2 threads = 64 logical processors, and 256 GB RAM, design a VM consolidation plan."*

### 3.1.1 Given Data

$$
vCPU_{host} = 2 \cdot 16 \cdot 2 = 64 \text{ logical processors}
$$
$$
RAM_{host} = 256 \text{ GB}
$$

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Calculation

**Step 1 — Choose oversubscription ratio.**
For general-purpose workloads (web/app servers), industry best practice is $R_{over} = 2:1$ (or $R_{over} = 2$).

**Step 2 — Compute total allocatable vCPUs.**

$$
\sum vCPU_{VMs} = R_{over} \cdot vCPU_{host} = 2 \cdot 64 = 128 \text{ vCPUs}
$$

**Step 3 — Memory constraint (no overcommit).**

$$
\sum RAM_{VMs} \leq RAM_{host} = 256 \text{ GB}
$$

Reserving ~10% for the root partition:

$$
RAM_{alloc} = 0.9 \cdot 256 = 230.4 \text{ GB}
$$

**Step 4 — Choose VM SKU.**
Suppose 4-vCPU, 16 GB RAM VMs:

$$
N_{VMs}^{vCPU} = \left\lfloor \frac{128}{4} \right\rfloor = 32 \text{ VMs}
$$
$$
N_{VMs}^{RAM} = \left\lfloor \frac{230.4}{16} \right\rfloor = 14 \text{ VMs}
$$

**Step 5 — Bottleneck is the smaller value.**

$$
N_{VMs} = \min(N_{VMs}^{vCPU},\ N_{VMs}^{RAM}) = \min(32, 14) = \boxed{14 \text{ VMs}}
$$

**Step 6 — Verification.**

$$
\sum vCPU = 14 \cdot 4 = 56 \leq 64 \quad \text{(and } 56/64 = 0.875 \text{, well within 2:1 overcommit of } 128\text{)}
$$
$$
\sum RAM = 14 \cdot 16 = 224 \text{ GB} \leq 230.4 \text{ GB} \quad \checkmark
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory becomes the binding constraint**, which is the *most common real-world outcome*. CPU is usually over-provisioned; RAM is not.

## 3.2 Derivation: VHDX Size and Performance Trade-off

### 3.2.1 Provisioned Disk Space vs. Physical Space

Let $S_{prov}$ = provisioned (maximum) size, $S_{used}$ = actual data written.

For a **dynamically expanding VHDX**:

$$
S_{physical} = S_{metadata} + S_{used}
$$
$$
S_{metadata} \approx 2 \text{ MiB} \text{ (Hyper-V VHDX header + block allocation table)}
$$
$$
\text{Maximum savings} = S_{prov} - S_{used} - S_{metadata}
$$

**Example:** $S_{prov} = 1 \text{ TB}$, $S_{used} = 200 \text{ GB}$.

$$
S_{physical} \approx 0.0019 \text{ GB} + 200 \text{ GB} \approx 200.002 \text{ GB}
$$
$$
\text{Savings} \approx 824 \text{ GB}
$$

### 3.2.2 Trade-off Penalty

Dynamically expanding VHDXs incur an **expansion I/O** penalty on first write to a new block. Performance cost is approximately:

$$
T_{exp} = T_{block\_alloc} + T_{zero\_fill} + T_{metadata\_update}
$$

For Windows Server 2022, a typical $T_{exp}$ for a 2 MiB block is **~1–2 ms** on SSD (negligible) and **~10–50 ms** on HDD (noticeable).

## 3.3 Derivation: Hyper-V Replica Bandwidth & RPO

Let $D_{churn}$ = data churn rate per VM (GB/hour), $B$ = WAN bandwidth (Gbps), and $RPO$ = desired recovery point objective (seconds).

### 3.3.1 Theoretical Maximum RPO

$$
RPO_{min} = \frac{D_{churn}}{B} \cdot 8 \quad \text{(seconds, with } D_{churn} \text{ in Gbits)}
$$

Or in GB units:

$$
RPO_{min} = \frac{D_{churn} \cdot 8}{B_{Gbps}} \text{ seconds, or } \frac{D_{churn} \cdot 60}{B_{Gbps}/8} \text{ minutes}
$$

**Example:** $D_{churn} = 5 \text{ GB/hour}$, $B = 100 \text{ Mbps}$.

Convert:

$$
D_{churn} = \frac{5 \text{ GB}}{3600 \text{ s}} \approx 1.389 \text{ MB/s} = 11.11 \text{ Mbps}
$$
$$
RPO_{min} = \frac{11.11 \text{ Mbps}}{100 \text{ Mbps}} \cdot 60 \text{ s} = 6.67 \text{ seconds}
$$

### 3.3.2 Replication Frequency

Hyper-V Replica default sends deltas every **5 minutes** (300 s). If $RPO_{min} \leq 300$ s, the configured 5-min cadence is feasible; otherwise, increase bandwidth or compress via WAN optimization.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Hyper-V vs Other Hypervisors

| Feature | **Microsoft Hyper-V** | VMware ESXi | Citrix XenServer | KVM (Linux) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Hypervisor type | Type-1 microkernelized | Type-1 monolithic | Type-1 microkernel | Type-1 (Linux kernel module) |
| Host OS | Windows Server (in root) | Bare-metal Linux derivative | Bare-metal Linux | Linux kernel itself |
| Live Migration | ✅ (Shared/SMB/Shared-Nothing) | ✅ vMotion | ✅ XenMotion | ✅ virsh migrate |
| HA Clustering | ✅ Failover Clustering | ✅ vSphere HA | ✅ HA | ✅ Pacemaker/Corosync |
| Replica/DR | ✅ Hyper-V Replica | ✅ vSphere Replication | ❌ | ❌ (built-in) |
| Nested Virtualization | ✅ (Win Server 2016+) | ✅ | ❌ | ✅ |
| Memory overcommit | ❌ (strict) | ✅ (TPS, ballooning) | ✅ | ✅ (KSM) |
| Open-source | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ (Citrix Hypervisor 8.2 is open) | ✅ |
| License cost | Bundled in Windows Server | $$$$ | Free (open) | Free |
| Cloud integration | Azure-native | VMware Cloud on AWS | Citrix Cloud | OpenStack, AWS Nitro (loosely) |

## 4.4 Live Migration Sequence Diagram

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Admin as Hyper V Manager
    participant SrcHost as Source Hyper V Host
    participant VMM as VMMS
    participant DstHost as Destination Hyper V Host
    participant Shared as SMB 3.0 Shared Storage
    participant Net as Network RDMA

    Admin->>SrcHost: Initiate Live Migration of VM01
    SrcHost->>DstHost: TCP handshake port 6600
    DstHost->>Shared: Reserve target storage handle
    DstHost-->>SrcHost: Ready
    SrcHost->>VMM: Pre copy iteration 1
    loop Iterative Pre Copy Dirty Pages
        SrcHost->>DstHost: Transfer dirty memory pages
        DstHost-->>SrcHost: Ack
    end
    SrcHost->>DstHost: Quiesce and final copy
    SrcHost->>VMM: Suspend VM
    SrcHost->>DstHost: Transfer CPU and device state
    DstHost->>VMM: Resume VM
    VMM-->>Admin: Migration complete downtime less than 1 second
```

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]
**Differentiate between Type-1 and Type-2 hypervisors. State with justification whether Microsoft Hyper-V is a Type-1 or Type-2 hypervisor.**

**Model Answer:**

A **Type-1 hypervisor** (bare-metal) runs directly on the host hardware without an intervening host operating system. Examples: **Hyper-V, VMware ESXi, Citrix XenServer, KVM**.

A **Type-2 hypervisor** (hosted) runs as an application on top of a conventional host OS. Examples: **VMware Workstation, Oracle VirtualBox**.

Microsoft Hyper-V is a **Type-1 microkernelized hypervisor**. Although it is delivered as a role of Windows Server, the hypervisor itself loads *before* the Windows kernel, executes in a higher privilege level (Ring -1, VTL 0), and has direct hardware access. Windows Server runs *inside* the root partition as the management OS, not underneath the hypervisor.

> [!NOTE]
> *Self-Assessment Rubric (3 marks):* Definition Type-1 — 1 mark; Definition Type-2 — 1 mark; Correct classification of Hyper-V with justification — 1 mark.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]
**Explain the concept of Generation 1 and Generation 2 virtual machines in Hyper-V. Which is recommended for new deployments and why?**

**Model Answer:**

Hyper-V supports two VM firmware architectures:

- **Generation 1** uses **legacy BIOS** firmware, IDE virtual controllers for boot, and emulated devices. Maximum practical RAM is ~1 TB. It supports older guest operating systems that lack UEFI drivers.

- **Generation 2** uses **UEFI** firmware, SCSI synthetic controllers, and supports **Secure Boot**, **PXE boot using UEFI**, and boot from a **native VHDX**. Memory limit is **12 TB** per VM, and vCPUs up to **240**.

**Recommendation:** *Generation 2* is recommended for all new deployments because it offers superior performance (synthetic vs. emulated I/O), stronger security posture (Secure Boot), and greater scalability (RAM, vCPU, VHDX). Generation 1 should be reserved only for legacy OSes (e.g., Windows XP, Windows Server 2003) that cannot boot from UEFI.

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — 2024 Scheme ESE Model]

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **architecture of Microsoft Hyper-V**, clearly identifying the root partition, child partitions, hypervisor, VMBus, VSP, and VSC. Discuss how I/O requests are serviced using the VSP/VSC pair model. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare and contrast **Hyper-V with VMware ESXi and KVM** across the dimensions of hypervisor type, live migration, memory overcommit, and cloud integration. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a)

The Hyper-V architecture is a **microkernelized hypervisor model** with the following components:

1. **Hyper-V Hypervisor (VTL 0)** — A thin (~1 MB) layer running directly on hardware. Responsible for CPU scheduling, memory partitioning, and interrupt virtualization. Contains no device drivers (hence *microkernelized*).

2. **Root Partition** — Hosts the management operating system (Windows Server). Contains the **Virtual Machine Management Service (VMMS)**, all physical device drivers, **Virtualization Service Providers (VSPs)**, and the **Hyper-V WMI Provider**.

3. **Child Partitions** — Each child partition hosts one guest VM with its own OS. They contain **Virtualization Service Clients (VSCs)** that issue I/O requests.

4. **VMBus** — A high-speed, shared-memory logical channel for inter-partition communication. Uses *doorbell* mechanisms (MSR-based) for low-latency signaling.

**I/O Request Flow (VSP/VSC pair model):**

- Step 1: Guest application triggers an I/O operation (e.g., disk read).
- Step 2: Guest OS storage driver invokes the **VSC (storvsc)** inside the child partition.
- Step 3: VSC formats the request as a VMBus packet and places it into a shared **ring buffer** mapped in both partitions.
- Step 4: VSC signals the root partition by writing to a *doorbell* MSR.
- Step 5: **VSP (storvsp)** in the root partition picks up the packet, translates it, and issues the request to the **physical storage driver**.
- Step 6: Physical driver performs DMA to/from physical storage.
- Step 7: Completion is delivered back via VMBus to the VSC.
- Step 8: VSC completes the I/O in the guest OS, signaling the application.

*Diagrammatic reference: See Section 4.1 (Hyper-V High-Level Architecture).*

#### Model Solution for (b)

| Dimension | **Microsoft Hyper-V** | **VMware ESXi** | **KVM (Linux)** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Hypervisor type | Type-1 microkernelized | Type-1 monolithic | Type-1 (Linux kernel module) |
| Memory overcommit | **No** (strict 1:1; dynamic memory adds ballooning-style reclamation) | **Yes** (Transparent Page Sharing, memory compression, ballooning) | **Yes** (KSM page merging, swap) |
| Live migration | ✅ Hyper-V Live Migration (Kerberos/Cert auth, SMB 3.0, Shared-Nothing) | ✅ vMotion (vSAN, VMFS, NFS) | ✅ `virsh migrate` / `virt-manager` (libvirt + tuned KVM) |
| Cloud integration | **Native Azure** (Azure Stack HCI, Azure Arc hybrid) | VMware Cloud on AWS, VMware Cloud Foundation | OpenStack, AWS Nitro Systems (loosely), GCE |
| Open-source | ❌ | ❌ | ✅ |
| License | Bundled with Windows Server | $$$$ | Free (GPL) |
| Maximum vCPUs per VM | 240 (Gen 2) | 768 (vSphere 8) | 710+ (depends on host) |
| Management | Hyper-V Manager, SCVMM, PowerShell, WMI | vCenter, ESXi Web Client, PowerCLI | virt-manager, Cockpit, OpenStack Horizon |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfall:**
> 1. Students often **confuse hypervisor type**: Do not write that Hyper-V is Type-2. It is Type-1 microkernelized.
> 2. Students often **omit VMBus** in the architecture diagram. VMBus is the *defining* inter-partition channel of Hyper-V and earns you 1 mark by itself.
> 3. In comparison tables, students compare only 2 hypervisors. KTU requires **at least 3** for full 7 marks.
> 4. Do not write vague statements like "Hyper-V is better than VMware" — provide objective feature-based comparison.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a)** Explain in detail the **three types of Hyper-V Virtual Switches** (External, Internal, Private) with use cases. Describe **Hyper-V Replica** as a disaster-recovery mechanism, including RPO and replica modes. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A company wants to deploy a Hyper-V host with **2 physical CPUs × 16 cores × 2 threads** and **256 GB RAM** to host 4-vCPU / 16-GB Windows Server VMs. Assuming a 2:1 vCPU oversubscription ratio and 10% RAM reservation for the host, calculate the maximum number of VMs that can be hosted. Identify the binding constraint. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a)

**Hyper-V Virtual Switch Types:**

1. **External Virtual Switch**
   - Bound to a **physical NIC** of the host.
   - Provides VMs with connectivity to the **external physical LAN**.
   - Supports VLAN tagging (802.1Q), bandwidth management, and extensions (port mirroring, DHCP guard, router guard).
   - **Use case:** Production VMs requiring internet/LAN access.

2. **Internal Virtual Switch**
   - Bound to a **virtual NIC on the host OS** (no physical uplink).
   - Allows communication **only between VMs and the host**.
   - **Use case:** Isolated management network, Hyper-V Replica traffic, or backend cluster heartbeats.

3. **Private Virtual Switch**
   - **No host connectivity**, no physical uplink.
   - Communication strictly **VM-to-VM** within the switch.
   - **Use case:** Test labs, isolated multi-tier application testing, honeypots.

**Hyper-V Replica — DR Mechanism:**

Hyper-V Replica asynchronously replicates VM virtual hard disks from a **primary Hyper-V host** to a **secondary (replica) Hyper-V host** over the network. The replica VM is *powered off* at the secondary site and brought online only during failover (planned, unplanned, or test).

- **RPO (Recovery Point Objective):** Default **5 minutes** (configurable from **30 seconds to 15 minutes**).
- **Replication Modes:**
  - **Primary site ↔ Replica site** (one-way, default) — Suitable for asymmetric DR.
  - **Primary site ↔ Replica site (bidirectional / extended)** — Suitable for multi-site scenarios.
- **Authentication:** Kerberos (same domain/trusted) or **certificate-based** (across domains/workgroups).
- **Compression:** Built-in for WAN efficiency.
- **Failover Triggers:** Manual, scripted (PowerShell), or automatic via Azure Site Recovery.

#### Model Solution for (b) — Numerical

**Given:**

$$
vCPU_{host} = 2 \cdot 16 \cdot 2 = 64 \text{ logical processors}
$$
$$
RAM_{host} = 256 \text{ GB}, \quad VM_{spec} = 4 \text{ vCPU}, 16 \text{ GB}
$$
$$
R_{over} = 2:1, \quad RAM_{reserve} = 10\%
$$

**Step 1 — Total allocatable vCPUs:**

$$
\sum vCPU_{alloc} = R_{over} \cdot vCPU_{host} = 2 \cdot 64 = 128 \text{ vCPUs}
$$

**Step 2 — vCPU-limited VM count:**

$$
N_{VMs}^{vCPU} = \left\lfloor \frac{128}{4} \right\rfloor = 32 \text{ VMs}
$$

**Step 3 — Effective RAM for VMs (after 10% host reserve):**

$$
RAM_{alloc} = (1 - 0.10) \cdot 256 = 230.4 \text{ GB}
$$

**Step 4 — RAM-limited VM count:**

$$
N_{VMs}^{RAM} = \left\lfloor \frac{230.4}{16} \right\rfloor = 14 \text{ VMs}
$$

**Step 5 — Binding constraint:**

$$
N_{VMs} = \min(32, 14) = \boxed{14 \text{ VMs}}
$$

**Binding constraint = RAM** (memory becomes the limiting factor; CPU is over-provisioned by 2:1 and can support up to 32 such VMs).

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Calculation Mistakes:**
> - Forgetting to subtract host RAM reservation (10%) — loses 1 mark.
> - Using *physical cores* instead of *logical processors* (forgetting Hyper-Threading factor of 2) — loses 1 mark.
> - Failing to state *which resource* is the binding constraint — loses 1 mark.
> - Writing "32 VMs" without showing the min() operation — loses 2 marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU 2024 Revision — Memorize These Bullets:**

- **Hyper-V = Type-1 microkernelized hypervisor** from Microsoft (NOT Type-2).
- **Architecture has 3 layers:** Hypervisor (VTL 0) → Root Partition (Windows Server) → Child Partitions (Guest VMs).
- **VMBus** is the inter-partition communication channel; uses shared memory + doorbell MSRs.
- **VSP (root) ↔ VSC (child)** pair model handles all I/O; root partition owns all physical drivers.
- **Gen 2 VMs** are mandatory for new deployments: UEFI, Secure Boot, SCSI boot, 12 TB RAM, 240 vCPUs.
- **VHDX max = 64 TB** (VHD legacy max = 2 TB); logical sector 4 KB; power-failure-resilient.
- **VHDX types:** Fixed, Dynamically Expanding, Differencing. Fixed = best I/O performance.
- **Checkpoints:** Standard (dev/test) vs. **Production (VSS-based, app-consistent)**.
- **Live Migration** uses SMB 3.0 / shared storage; downtime < 1 second; RDMA supported.
- **Shared-Nothing Live Migration** = migration without shared storage (uses direct-attached storage).
- **Hyper-V Replica** = async VM-level DR; default RPO = 5 min; 30s–15 min configurable.
- **3 Virtual Switch types:** External (uplink to physical), Internal (host↔VM), Private (VM↔VM).
- **Nested Virtualization** = Hyper-V inside Hyper-V; needs `Set-VMProcessor -ExposeVirtualizationExtensions $true`.
- **Hyper-V limits (Server 2022):** 1024 VMs/host, 240 vCPUs/VM, 12 TB RAM/VM, 64 TB VHDX.
- **Azure is built on Hyper-V** foundation (Azure Stack HCI, Azure Stack Hub).
- **PowerShell mgmt:** `Get-VM`, `New-VM`, `Start-VM`, `Move-VM` (live migration), `Enable-VMReplication`.
- **WMI namespace:** `root\virtualization\v2`.
- **CPU oversubscription ratio** $R_{over} = \frac{\sum vCPU_{VMs}}{vCPU_{host}}$, typically 2:1 to 4:1.
- **Memory overcommit** is **NOT supported** natively (unlike VMware). Use **Dynamic Memory** for soft reclamation.
- **KVM** is the open-source Linux-kernel Type-1 hypervisor; Hyper-V's closest free rival for private clouds.
- **Fabric decision:** Use Hyper-V when the enterprise is Microsoft-centric (Active Directory, SCCM, Azure); use VMware for heterogeneous multi-cloud; use KVM for cost-sensitive / OpenStack deployments.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Azure Virtual Machine
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT525 - CLOUD COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Azure Virtual Machine

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

An **Azure Virtual Machine (Azure VM)** is an Infrastructure-as-a-Service (IaaS) compute offering provided by Microsoft Azure that delivers on-demand, scalable computing resources over the public cloud with the security and control of a physical server. According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for Cloud Computing, an Azure VM is defined as a software-emulated physical computer that runs an operating system, executes applications, hosts middleware, and persists data on virtualized hardware provisioned from Microsoft's global datacenter fabric.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (PECPT525 - Module 4):** Azure VM is the foundational IaaS primitive of Microsoft Azure. Every higher-level PaaS/SaaS service in Azure ultimately runs on top of the same Hyper-V based virtualized compute fabric. Understanding VM internals is the gateway to mastering Azure architecture.

Formally, an Azure VM is characterized by the 4-tuple:

$$
VM_{azure} = (vCPU, RAM, Storage, NetworkInterface)
$$

where each parameter is provisioned from a shared physical pool through the **Azure Compute Resource Provider (CRP)** running on the **Azure Fabric Controller (AFC)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Terminology Mapping (KTU Board Standard):**
> - **Tenant** → A dedicated, isolated instance of Azure Active Directory (now Entra ID) representing an organization.
> - **Subscription** → A billing and administrative boundary inside a tenant.
> - **Resource Group** → A logical container holding related VMs, disks, NICs, and NSGs.
> - **Region** → A specific geographical area containing one or more datacenters (e.g., Central India, East US).
> - **Availability Zone** → Physically separated datacenters within a region, each with independent power, cooling, and networking.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of an Azure VM as renting a **fully furnished, ready-to-use office cabin inside a massive skyscraper owned by Microsoft**.

- The **skyscraper** is the Azure datacenter (a physical building with thousands of servers, cooling, power backup, and ultra-fast fiber).
- **Your cabin** (the VM) feels like a private, isolated computer with its own OS, hard disk, RAM, and IP address, but it is actually sharing the skyscraper's resources (electricity, water, security) with thousands of other tenants.
- The **landlord's front desk** (Azure Portal / REST API / CLI) is where you submit your requirements: "I need 4 vCPUs, 16 GB RAM, 500 GB SSD, and Windows Server 2022." The front desk (Fabric Controller) checks availability and hands you the keys within minutes.
- **Flexible lease** (Pay-As-You-Go) means you only pay for the hours you keep the cabin occupied. Walk out, and the keys are returned. Want to upgrade? Just ask for a bigger cabin.
- **Floor plan** (Resource Group) is the cluster of cabins, elevators, and storage rooms you have booked together for one project.
- **Building wing** (Availability Zone) ensures that if a fire breaks out in your wing, your office has a twin in another wing running the same workload.

This analogy is critical for KTU short-answer questions: examiners love testing whether students can map "cloud service characteristics" (elasticity, measured service, resource pooling) onto real scenarios.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following metrics govern Azure VM provisioning and are used in KTU numerical problems:

| Metric | Symbol | Standard Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Hypervisor | - | **Hyper-V (Windows)** or **KVM (Linux hosts)** |
| Default vCPU:RAM ratio | - | **1:2, 1:4, 1:8** (series dependent) |
| Maximum vCPUs per VM | - | **128 vCPUs** (M-series) |
| Maximum RAM per VM | - | **3,904 GiB** (M-series) |
| Standard Temp Disk | - | **D: drive** (SSD, non-persistent) |
| OS Disk Type | - | **Premium SSD, Standard SSD, Ultra Disk** |
| Default SLA for single VM | - | **99.9% uptime** (with premium SSD) |
| SLA for Availability Set (2+ VMs) | - | **99.95% uptime** |
| SLA for Availability Zones | - | **99.99% uptime** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **SLA Tiers to Memorize for KTU Board Exams:**
> - Single VM (Premium SSD): **99.9%** → ≤ 43.2 minutes downtime/month
> - Availability Set: **99.95%** → ≤ 21.6 minutes downtime/month
> - Availability Zones (2 VMs across zones): **99.99%** → ≤ 4.32 minutes downtime/month

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Architecture of an Azure VM

Provisioning an Azure VM follows a deterministic orchestration pipeline. The KTU board frequently asks students to **list the steps of VM creation** (a guaranteed 3-mark question). The logic is structured below:

1. **Authentication & Authorization**: The request originates from Azure Portal, PowerShell, CLI, or REST API. Azure Entra ID (formerly Azure AD) validates the security principal (user/SPN) and checks Role-Based Access Control (RBAC) permissions on the target subscription.
2. **Resource Group Resolution**: The Fabric Controller (FC) locates the destination Resource Group (RG), which is the unit of deployment, lifecycle management, and RBAC scoping.
3. **Region & Quota Check**: The Azure Compute Resource Provider (CRP) validates that the requested region has available compute quota for the chosen VM SKU (Stock Keeping Unit).
4. **Hardware Allocation via Hypervisor**: The Azure Host Agent on a physical server interacts with **Hyper-V** to carve out a virtual partition. The hypervisor isolates CPU, memory, and I/O using:
   - **VT-x / AMD-V** hardware virtualization extensions
   - **Second Level Address Translation (SLAT)** for memory isolation
   - **SR-IOV (Single Root I/O Virtualization)** for direct NIC access
5. **Storage Staging**: A virtual hard disk (VHD) is mounted from Azure Storage (Page Blob for OS disk, Premium/Standard SSD for data disks).
6. **Network Interface Card (NIC) Attachment**: A virtual NIC is bound to a Subnet inside a Virtual Network (VNet). A private IP (and optionally a public IP) is assigned via DHCP from the VNet's address space.
7. **Boot Sequence**: The VM boots from the OS disk, cloud-init (Linux) or Azure VM Agent (Windows) installs, and the guest agent reports health back to the FC every **30 seconds** via the Azure Wire Server (host IP **168.63.129.16**).

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical IP for KTU Exam:** The static IP `168.63.129.16` is the Azure Wire Server address injected into every VM. It is used by Azure services (Key Vault, Managed Identity, KMS activation) to communicate with the platform. Block this IP = your VM loses access to Azure metadata.

### 2.2 VM Components (Mandatory KTU Diagram)

Every Azure VM is composed of **8 mandatory components** that examiners test in 14-mark questions:

| # | Component | Description |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **vCPUs** | Virtual CPU cores backed by physical cores on the host |
| 2 | **Memory (RAM)** | Allocated from the host NUMA nodes |
| 3 | **Temporary Disk (D:)** | Non-persistent SSD, lost on deallocation |
| 4 | **OS Disk** | Contains the boot VHD (max 2,048 GiB for Gen 1, 4,095 GiB for Gen 2) |
| 5 | **Data Disks** | Optional persistent storage (up to **64 data disks**) |
| 6 | **Virtual NIC (vNIC)** | Connects VM to a subnet in a VNet |
| 7 | **NSG (Network Security Group)** | Stateful firewall filtering inbound/outbound traffic |
| 8 | **Public IP (Optional)** | Inbound internet connectivity (static or dynamic) |

### 2.3 VM Series (KTU High-Yield Classification)

KTU Module 4 requires familiarity with VM sizing. The KTU board typically asks: *"Differentiate between B-series and D-series VMs"* or *"When would you choose M-series?"*

| Series | Workload | vCPU Range | RAM Ratio | vCPU:RAM | Key Feature |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **B-series** (Burstable) | Dev/Test, low-traffic web | 1–20 | 1:2, 1:4, 1:8 | Burstable CPU credits | Cost-optimized |
| **D-series** (General Purpose) | Apps, mid-tier DBs | 2–96 | 1:4 | Balanced | Default recommendation |
| **E-series** (Memory Optimized) | In-memory DBs (SAP HANA, Redis) | 2–104 | 1:8 | High memory | Large RAM footprint |
| **F-series** (Compute Optimized) | Batch processing, gaming | 2–72 | 1:2 | High vCPU density | Fastest vCPUs |
| **M-series** (Memory Mega) | Massive in-memory workloads | 8–128 | 1:8 to 1:16 | Ultra-high RAM (up to 3,904 GiB) | SAP HANA certified |
| **N-series** (GPU) | AI/ML, deep learning, rendering | 6–72 | 1:2 to 1:4 | NVIDIA Tesla/AMD Radeon | GPU acceleration |
| **L-series** (Storage Optimized) | Big Data, NoSQL, Data Warehousing | 4–80 | 1:8 | High disk throughput (IOPS) | Low latency NVMe |
| **H-series** (High Performance Compute) | HPC, molecular dynamics, CFD | 8–44 | 1:4 to 1:8 | InfiniBand interconnect | RDMA support |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Mnemonic for Series:** "**B**asic dev, **D**aily apps, **E**normous RAM, **F**ast CPU, **M**assive memory, **N**eural (GPU), **L**arge I/O, **H**PC."

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (Pricing & Cost Calculations)

The KTU board frequently includes a 7-mark numerical problem on **TCO (Total Cost of Ownership)** comparison between on-premise and Azure VM, or **pay-as-you-go (PAYG) vs Reserved Instance (RI)** calculations.

| Formula | Equation | Description |
|---|---|---|
| **Hourly Cost** | $C_h = C_{VM} + C_{OSDisk} + C_{DataDisk} + C_{IP} + C_{BW}$ | Sum of compute, storage, network charges |
| **Monthly Cost (PAYG)** | $C_m = C_h \times 730$ | 730 hours = 1 month of continuous usage |
| **Reserved Instance Savings** | $Savings_{RI} = C_{PAYG} - C_{RI}$ | RI offers **up to 72% discount** for 1-year, **up to 80%** for 3-year commitment |
| **Spot Discount** | $C_{spot} = C_{PAYG} \times (1 - 0.90)$ | Up to **90% off**, but eviction possible |
| **SLA Availability %** | $A = \left(1 - \frac{DT_{month}}{T_{month}}\right) \times 100$ | DT = Downtime, T = Total time |
| **Combined SLA** | $SLA_{combined} = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n}(1 - SLA_i)$ | For multi-component systems |

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** The constant **730 hours** is derived from 365.25 days × 24 hours ÷ 12 months ≈ 730.5 hours. Azure rounds down to 730 for monthly billing calculations.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Azure VMs underpin **mission-critical workloads** across engineering domains:

- **Mechanical/CFD Engineers**: Run ANSYS Fluent on **H-series** VMs with InfiniBand for 100+ Gbps inter-node communication.
- **Data Scientists**: Train deep learning models on **NDv2-series** VMs with 8 × V100 NVIDIA GPUs.
- **Civil Engineers**: Render BIM models (Revit) on **N-series** with NVIDIA GRID.
- **DevOps Teams**: Use **B-series** for CI/CD build agents, paying only during active build windows.
- **Academic Research (KTU Project Domain)**: Spin up a **D2s v3** VM (2 vCPU, 8 GB RAM) for OpenStack or Hadoop labs in under **3 minutes**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: TCO Comparison (On-Premise vs Azure VM)

This is the **highest-weightage numerical problem** in KTU Module 4, typically carrying **7 marks** as a sub-part of a 14-mark question.

**Problem Statement (KTU Style):**
> *"A startup requires 10 servers of configuration 8 vCPUs, 32 GB RAM, 1 TB SSD each, running 24/7 for 2 years. Calculate the total cost of ownership (a) on-premise and (b) on Azure VM. Comment on the savings."*

**Given Constants (Standard KTU Assumed Values):**
- On-premise server cost: ₹5,00,000 per server
- On-premise annual power + cooling: ₹40,000 per server per year
- On-premise annual maintenance: ₹15,000 per server per year
- Azure VM (D8s v3 equivalent) hourly cost: ₹15/hour
- 1 TB Premium SSD monthly cost: ₹2,000 per disk per month

**Part (a): On-Premise TCO Calculation**

$$
C_{hardware} = 10 \text{ servers} \times 5{,}00{,}000 = 50{,}00{,}000 \text{ INR}
$$

$$
C_{power,2yr} = 40{,}000 \times 10 \times 2 = 8{,}00{,}000 \text{ INR}
$$

$$
C_{maint,2yr} = 15{,}000 \times 10 \times 2 = 3{,}00{,}000 \text{ INR}
$$

$$
TCO_{onprem} = C_{hardware} + C_{power,2yr} + C_{maint,2yr}
$$

$$
TCO_{onprem} = 50{,}00{,}000 + 8{,}00{,}000 + 3{,}00{,}000 = 61{,}00{,}000 \text{ INR}
$$

**Part (b): Azure VM TCO Calculation**

$$
C_{compute,hr} = 10 \text{ VMs} \times 15 \text{ INR/hr} = 150 \text{ INR/hr}
$$

$$
C_{compute,2yr} = 150 \times 730 \times 24 \times 2 = 150 \times 35{,}040 = 52{,}56{,}000 \text{ INR}
$$

> **Logic Note:** 2 years = 2 × 365 × 24 = 17,520 hours, but Azure bills monthly. Using 730 hours/month × 24 months = 17,520 hours ✓

$$
C_{storage,2yr} = 10 \text{ disks} \times 2{,}000 \times 24 = 4{,}80{,}000 \text{ INR}
$$

$$
TCO_{azure} = 52{,}56{,}000 + 4{,}80{,}000 = 57{,}36{,}000 \text{ INR}
$$

**Savings Analysis:**

$$
Savings = TCO_{onprem} - TCO_{azure} = 61{,}00{,}000 - 57{,}36{,}000 = 3{,}64{,}000 \text{ INR}
$$

$$
\% Savings = \left(\frac{3{,}64{,}000}{61{,}00{,}000}\right) \times 100 = 5.97\%
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Verdict:** For **24/7 sustained workloads**, savings are marginal in PAYG. Azure becomes **dramatically cheaper** (up to 72% savings) when **Reserved Instances (RIs)** are committed, or when the workload is **spiky** (using **Auto-Scale** so VMs are deallocated during off-peak hours).

### 3.2 Derivation: Reserved Instance Savings

**Problem Statement:**
> *"A company runs a D8s v3 VM 24/7. PAYG cost is ₹15/hr. Calculate annual savings if they buy a 1-year Reserved Instance at 40% discount. Compare with 3-year RI at 60% discount."*

**PAYG Annual Cost:**

$$
C_{PAYG,yr} = 15 \times 730 \times 12 = 15 \times 8760 = 1{,}31{,}400 \text{ INR}
$$

**1-Year RI Cost (40% discount):**

$$
Discount = 0.40 \times 1{,}31{,}400 = 52{,}560 \text{ INR}
$$

$$
C_{RI,1yr} = 1{,}31{,}400 - 52{,}560 = 78{,}840 \text{ INR}
$$

**3-Year RI Cost (60% discount, averaged annually):**

$$
Discount_{3yr} = 0.60 \times 1{,}31{,}400 = 78{,}840 \text{ INR/yr}
$$

$$
C_{RI,3yr,annual} = 1{,}31{,}400 - 78{,}840 = 52{,}560 \text{ INR/yr}
$$

**Comparative Table:**

| Plan | Annual Cost (INR) | Savings vs PAYG | % Savings |
|---|---|---|---|
| PAYG | 1,31,400 | 0 | 0% |
| 1-Year RI | 78,840 | 52,560 | 40% |
| 3-Year RI | 52,560 | 78,840 | 60% |

> [!NOTE]
> **3-Year RI = ₹78,840 saved per year per VM.** For 10 VMs, this is **₹7,88,400 per year**, validating the KTU's emphasis on financial cloud planning.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: VM Sizing Decision Tree

| Workload Characteristic | Recommended Series | KTU Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Bursty dev/test environment | **B-series** | CPU credits accumulate when idle, spent on spikes |
| Web server with moderate load | **D-series** | Balanced vCPU:RAM ratio (1:4) |
| SAP HANA in-memory database | **M-series** | 1:16 vCPU:RAM, certified by SAP |
| Deep learning model training | **N-series (ND/NDv2)** | Multi-GPU with NVLink interconnect |
| SQL Server with high IOPS | **L-series** | NVMe-backed low-latency storage |
| Molecular dynamics simulation | **H-series** | InfiniBand RDMA for parallel MPI jobs |
| Big data Hadoop cluster | **E-series** | Memory-optimized for caching HDFS blocks |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 End-to-End Azure VM Provisioning Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    userA[User / DevOps Engineer] --> portalA[Azure Portal / CLI / PowerShell / REST API]
    portalA --> entraA[Azure Entra ID Authentication]
    entraA --> rbacA[RBAC Permission Check]
    rbacA --> fcA[Azure Fabric Controller]
    fcA --> quotaA[Region and Quota Validation]
    quotaA --> crpA[Compute Resource Provider CRP]
    crpA --> hyperA[Hyper V Hypervisor on Physical Host]
    hyperA --> vhdA[Mount OS Disk from Azure Storage]
    hyperA --> vnicA[Attach Virtual NIC to VNet Subnet]
    hyperA --> agentA[Install Azure VM Agent]
    agentA --> bootA[VM Ready State Reported via 168.63.129.16]
    bootA --> monitorA[Azure Monitor and Log Analytics]
    monitorA --> scaleA[Auto Scale and Update Domain Orchestration]
    scaleA --> userA
```

### 4.2 Azure VM High Availability & Disaster Recovery Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    regionA[Azure Region Example: Central India] --> zoneA[Availability Zone 1 Datacenter]
    regionA --> zoneB[Availability Zone 2 Datacenter]
    regionA --> zoneC[Availability Zone 3 Datacenter]
    zoneA --> vA[VM in Zone 1]
    zoneB --> vB[VM in Zone 2]
    zoneC --> vC[VM in Zone 3]
    vA --> lbA[Azure Load Balancer Frontend]
    vB --> lbA
    vC --> lbA
    lbA --> slaA[Combined SLA 99.99 Percent]
    regionA --> setA[Fault Domain Strategy]
    setA --> fd1[Update Domain 1]
    setA --> fd2[Update Domain 2]
    setA --> fd3[Update Domain 3]
    setA --> fd4[Update Domain 4 Update Domain 20]
    fd1 --> asA[Availability Set]
    fd2 --> asA
    fd3 --> asA
    fd4 --> asA
    asA --> slaB[Availability Set SLA 99.95 Percent]
```

### 4.3 Azure VM Storage & Networking Component Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    vmA[Azure Virtual Machine] --> osDiskA[OS Disk Premium SSD]
    vmA --> tempDiskA[Temp Disk D Drive Non Persistent]
    vmA --> dataA[Data Disk 1]
    vmA --> dataB[Data Disk 2]
    vmA --> dataC[Data Disk N up to 64]
    osDiskA --> storageA[Azure Storage Page Blob]
    dataA --> storageA
    dataB --> storageA
    dataC --> storageA
    storageA --> redundancyA[LRS ZRS GRS GZRS]
    vmA --> vnicA[Virtual NIC]
    vnicA --> subnetA[Subnet in VNet]
    subnetA --> nsgA[Network Security Group Firewall Rules]
    vnicA --> pipA[Public IP Address Optional]
    pipA --> internetA[Internet Inbound Outbound]
    subnetA --> vnetPeerA[VNet Peering to Other VNet]
```

### 4.4 VM Pricing Model Comparison (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    pricingRoot[Azure VM Pricing Strategy] --> payg[Pay As You Go]
    pricingRoot --> ri1[1 Year Reserved Instance]
    pricingRoot --> ri3[3 Year Reserved Instance]
    pricingRoot --> spot[Spot Instance]
    pricingRoot --> hybrid[Azure Hybrid Benefit]
    payg --> paygDesc[Highest Flexibility No Commitment 100 percent PAYG Rate]
    ri1 --> ri1Desc[Up to 40 percent Savings Moderate Commitment]
    ri3 --> ri3Desc[Up to 60 to 72 percent Savings Maximum Discount]
    spot --> spotDesc[Up to 90 percent Discount Eviction Possible for Batch Workloads]
    hybrid --> hybridDesc[Bring Existing On Premise Windows Server License Saves up to 80 percent on Compute]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Note:** The `node1`, `nodeA` style identifiers are used to avoid Mermaid reserved keyword conflicts. All labels use plain uppercase text for maximum rendering compatibility across devices.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
*Define an Azure Virtual Machine. List any four VM series with their primary workload.*

**Model Answer:**
An Azure Virtual Machine is an IaaS compute resource that emulates a physical server, providing on-demand vCPUs, memory, storage, and networking from Microsoft's global datacenter fabric.

Four VM series:
- **B-series** (Burstable): Dev/Test environments with variable CPU usage.
- **D-series** (General Purpose): Web servers, application tiers, mid-size databases.
- **E-series** (Memory Optimized): In-memory analytics, Redis, SAP HANA.
- **N-series** (GPU): AI/ML training, deep learning, 3D rendering.

*(3 marks: 1 for definition + 0.5 × 4 for series)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
*What is the role of the Azure Fabric Controller in VM provisioning?*

**Model Answer:**
The Azure Fabric Controller (FC) is the centralized orchestration layer responsible for:
1. Allocating physical host resources (CPU, RAM, storage) to VMs via the Hyper-V hypervisor.
2. Managing VM lifecycle (create, start, stop, deallocate, delete).
3. Monitoring VM health through the Azure Wire Server (`168.63.129.16`).
4. Enforcing fault domain updates and SLA guarantees.

*(3 marks: 1 for definition + 2 for role enumeration)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Question A (14 Marks) - Module Internal Choice

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified for 2024 Scheme]**

**Q. A (a)** Explain the architecture of an Azure Virtual Machine with a neat block diagram. List the mandatory components and explain the role of the Azure VM Agent. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

An Azure VM is composed of 8 mandatory components: vCPUs, Memory, Temporary Disk (D:), OS Disk, Data Disks, Virtual NIC, NSG, and an optional Public IP. The block diagram is shown below:

```
Azure VM Architecture:
+----------------------------------------+
|         Virtual Machine (Guest OS)      |
|  +-----------+  +-------------------+  |
|  |  vCPUs    |  |  Memory (RAM)     |  |
|  +-----------+  +-------------------+  |
|  +-----------+  +-------------------+  |
|  | D: Temp   |  | OS Disk (VHD)     |  |
|  +-----------+  +-------------------+  |
|  +-----------+  +-------------------+  |
|  | Data Disks|  | Virtual NIC       |  |
|  +-----------+  +-------------------+  |
|              Azure VM Agent             |
+----------------------------------------+
            |                |
            v                v
   Azure Wire Server    Azure Storage
   (168.63.129.16)      (Page Blobs)
```

**Role of Azure VM Agent:**
- It is a lightweight process (walinuxagent for Linux, WindowsAzureGuestAgent for Windows) that runs inside the guest OS.
- It reports VM health every **30 seconds** to the Fabric Controller.
- It enables **Azure Extensions** (Custom Script, Backup, Desired State Configuration, Diagnostic).
- It handles **sysprep / generalizing** for image creation.
- It manages **SSH key injection** and **password reset** operations. *(7 marks: 3 for diagram + 4 for agent role)*

---

**(b)** Compare the different Azure VM series (B, D, E, F, M, N) with respect to workload, vCPU range, and vCPU-to-RAM ratio. Recommend a series for (i) a CUDA-based deep learning lab and (ii) a SAP HANA in-memory deployment. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Series | Workload | vCPU Range | vCPU:RAM |
|---|---|---|---|
| B | Burstable dev/test | 1–20 | 1:2 / 1:4 / 1:8 |
| D | General purpose | 2–96 | 1:4 |
| E | Memory optimized | 2–104 | 1:8 |
| F | Compute optimized | 2–72 | 1:2 |
| M | Mega memory | 8–128 | 1:8 to 1:16 |
| N | GPU accelerated | 6–72 | 1:2 to 1:4 |

**Recommendations:**
- **(i) CUDA-based deep learning lab:** **N-series** (specifically **NDv2** or **NC A100 v4**) because it includes NVIDIA GPUs with CUDA cores, NVLink interconnect, and InfiniBand for multi-GPU training.
- **(ii) SAP HANA in-memory deployment:** **M-series** (e.g., **M208s v2**) because SAP HANA requires **1:8 to 1:16 vCPU-to-RAM ratio** with certification for terabytes of in-memory data.

*(7 marks: 3 for table + 2 + 2 for recommendations)*

---

### 5.3 Part B Question B (14 Marks) - Alternative Internal Choice

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper Style]**

**Q. B (a)** Describe the step-by-step procedure to create an Azure Virtual Machine through the Azure Portal. List the parameters you would configure in the "Basics" and "Disks" tabs. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Step-by-step procedure:**
1. Sign in to **portal.azure.com** using Azure Entra ID credentials.
2. Click **Create a resource** → **Compute** → **Virtual Machine**.
3. **Basics Tab**: Select Subscription, Resource Group, VM Name, Region, Availability Options, Security Type, Image (e.g., Windows Server 2022, Ubuntu 22.04), Size, Authentication (SSH key or password).
4. **Disks Tab**: Choose OS Disk type (Premium SSD/Standard SSD/Ultra Disk), enable/disable ephemeral OS disk, configure data disks (attach existing or create new).
5. **Networking Tab**: Configure Virtual Network, Subnet, Public IP, NIC NSG, Accelerated Networking.
6. **Management Tab**: Enable Boot Diagnostics, Identity (System-assigned Managed Identity), Auto-shutdown, Backup.
7. **Advanced Tab**: Custom data, cloud-init scripts, VM extensions.
8. **Tags Tab**: Apply metadata (Project, Environment, Owner).
9. **Review + Create**: Validate → **Create**. Deployment takes **3–5 minutes**.

**Key Parameters:**

| Tab | Parameters |
|---|---|
| Basics | Subscription, RG, Region, VM Name, Image, Size, Admin Username, Auth |
| Disks | OS Disk Type (Premium/Standard/Ultra), Disk Size, Data Disks (count + size) |

*(7 marks: 4 for steps + 3 for parameters)*

---

**(b)** A startup deploys 5 VMs of size D4s v3 (4 vCPUs, 16 GB RAM) running 24/7 for one year. Calculate the total Azure cost if (i) Pay-As-You-Go at ₹25/hour, (ii) 1-Year Reserved Instance at 35% discount, (iii) Spot Instance at 80% discount. Comment on the trade-offs. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Given:** 5 VMs × 24/7 × 365 days × ₹25/hr (PAYG baseline)

**Hours per year per VM:**

$$
H_{yr} = 24 \times 365 = 8{,}760 \text{ hours}
$$

**Total hours for 5 VMs:**

$$
H_{total} = 5 \times 8{,}760 = 43{,}800 \text{ hours}
$$

---

**(i) Pay-As-You-Go Cost:**

$$
C_{PAYG} = 43{,}800 \times 25 = 10{,}95{,}000 \text{ INR}
$$

---

**(ii) 1-Year Reserved Instance (35% discount):**

$$
C_{RI} = 10{,}95{,}000 \times (1 - 0.35) = 10{,}95{,}000 \times 0.65 = 7{,}11{,}750 \text{ INR}
$$

**Savings = ₹3,83,250**

---

**(iii) Spot Instance (80% discount):**

$$
C_{spot} = 10{,}95{,}000 \times (1 - 0.80) = 10{,}95{,}000 \times 0.20 = 2{,}19{,}000 \text{ INR}
$$

**Savings = ₹8,76,000**

---

**Comparison Table:**

| Plan | Annual Cost (INR) | Savings (INR) | Trade-off |
|---|---|---|---|
| PAYG | 10,95,000 | 0 | Maximum flexibility, no commitment |
| 1-Year RI | 7,11,750 | 3,83,250 | 1-year lock-in, 35% savings |
| Spot | 2,19,000 | 8,76,000 | **Risk of eviction**, only for fault-tolerant batch jobs |

**Trade-off Commentary:**
- **PAYG** suits unpredictable workloads (e.g., dev environments).
- **Reserved Instance** suits **steady-state production** workloads.
- **Spot Instance** suits **batch processing, CI/CD, ML training** that can tolerate eviction. **For 24/7 production, Spot is unsafe** — VMs can be deallocated with only **30 seconds notice** when Azure needs capacity back.

*(7 marks: 1 for hours + 2 + 2 + 2 for trade-off analysis)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing Stop vs Deallocate:** "Stop" keeps the VM allocated (you still pay for compute). Only "Stop (Deallocate)" releases the hardware and stops billing. Examiners test this with trap questions.
> 2. **Forgetting the Temp Disk:** Many students mention only OS and Data disks. The **D: drive Temp Disk** is non-persistent and is wiped on every reboot. It is ideal for **swap space** and **temp files**, but **never store database files here**.
> 3. **SLA Calculation Errors:** A single VM with premium SSD has **99.9% SLA**, not 99.99%. To get 99.99%, you need **2+ VMs in different Availability Zones** behind a Load Balancer.
> 4. **Wrong Discount Percentage:** Spot discount is up to **90%**, not 80%. RI 1-year is up to **40%**, 3-year is up to **72%**. Memorize these constants.
> 5. **Mixing Hyper-V with KVM:** Azure uses **Hyper-V** as the primary hypervisor (not VMware ESXi). Using the wrong hypervisor name in a 14-mark answer costs **2 marks**.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [ ] Azure VM is an **IaaS** offering from Microsoft Azure, running on **Hyper-V** hypervisor.
- [ ] Mandatory components: **vCPU, RAM, Temp Disk, OS Disk, Data Disks, vNIC, NSG, Public IP**.
- [ ] **Azure Wire Server IP = 168.63.129.16** — used for metadata, KMS activation, Managed Identity.
- [ ] VM SKU categories: **B (burstable), D (general), E (memory), F (compute), M (mega), N (GPU), L (storage), H (HPC)**.
- [ ] **B-series** uses CPU credit model — ideal for spiky workloads.
- [ ] **D-series** is the default recommendation for most production workloads.
- [ ] **M-series** is required for SAP HANA certification.
- [ ] **N-series** has NVIDIA GPUs — used for deep learning, CUDA, and rendering.
- [ ] SLA tiers: **Single VM (99.9%), Availability Set (99.95%), Availability Zones (99.99%)**.
- [ ] Availability Set has **Update Domains** (rolling updates) and **Fault Domains** (hardware failure isolation), up to **3 fault domains and 20 update domains**.
- [ ] Pricing models: **PAYG (no discount)**, **1-Year RI (≤40% off)**, **3-Year RI (≤72% off)**, **Spot (≤90% off, eviction-prone)**.
- [ ] **Stop (Deallocate)** releases hardware and stops billing. **Stop** alone does not.
- [ ] **D: drive (Temp Disk)** is **non-persistent** — data is lost on reboot/deallocation.
- [ ] **Premium SSD** provides sub-millisecond latency; **Standard HDD** has higher latency.
- [ ] **Accelerated Networking** uses SR-IOV for low-latency, high-throughput packet processing.
- [ ] **Managed Disks** are recommended over unmanaged (VHD in storage account) for better durability and scalability.
- [ ] **Azure Hybrid Benefit** allows reuse of existing on-premise Windows Server and SQL Server licenses for up to **80%** compute savings.
- [ ] **Virtual Machine Scale Sets (VMSS)** enable auto-scaling of identical VMs based on CPU, memory, or custom metrics.
- [ ] Azure CLI command to create a VM: `az vm create --resource-group <rg> --name <vm> --image <image> --size <size> --admin-username <user> --generate-ssh-keys`.
- [ ] VM **boot diagnostics** capture screenshots and serial console output for troubleshooting.
- [ ] Maximum **128 vCPUs** and **3,904 GiB RAM** are supported in M-series.
- [ ] **Proximity Placement Groups (PPG)** ensure low-latency communication by placing VMs in the same physical datacenter.
- [ ] **Azure Bastion** provides secure RDP/SSH access to VMs **without exposing a Public IP**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Compute Services
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT525 - CLOUD COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Compute Services

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

**Compute Services** in cloud computing refer to the on-demand, scalable, and metered provisioning of **processing power, memory, storage, and networking resources** delivered to consumers over the internet as a managed service. These services abstract away the underlying physical hardware, allowing users to deploy and run applications, workloads, and services without owning, managing, or maintaining the physical servers themselves.

According to the **NIST SP 500-292 Reference Architecture**, compute services fall under the *Resource Abstraction and Control Layer* and are delivered through three principal service models:

1. **Infrastructure-as-a-Service (IaaS)** – Virtual Machines, Bare-Metal Servers
2. **Platform-as-a-Service (PaaS)** – Managed runtimes, containers, orchestration
3. **Software-as-a-Service (SaaS)** – Ready-to-use applications

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight**: Module 4 explicitly emphasizes the differentiation between **Virtual Machines, Containers, and Serverless Computing** as the three primary compute abstraction paradigms, along with their corresponding hyperscaler offerings (AWS EC2, Azure Virtual Machines, Google Compute Engine, AWS Lambda, Azure Functions, Google Cloud Run).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Three Tiers of Restaurant Dining**

Imagine you want to serve a meal to your guests. You have three options:

| Abstraction Level | Restaurant Analogy | Cloud Equivalent |
|---|---|---|
| **High Control (IaaS)** | Rent a full kitchen — you buy groceries, cook, plate, and serve | Virtual Machines (EC2, Azure VM) |
| **Medium Control (PaaS)** | Rent a kitchen with a chef — you decide the menu, chef cooks | Containers (ECS, AKS, GKE) |
| **Zero Control (Serverless)** | Order from a restaurant menu — they cook, deliver, and clean up | Functions (Lambda, Azure Functions) |

The trade-off is simple: **more control = more responsibility**. You choose based on how much management overhead you want to retain versus delegate to the cloud provider.

### 1.3 Core Taxonomy of Compute Services

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Three Pillars of Cloud Compute Abstraction**

- **Virtualization-Based (VMs):** Hardware-level virtualization using hypervisors. Each VM runs its own OS kernel. Strong isolation, slow boot (seconds to minutes).
- **Container-Based:** OS-level virtualization sharing the host kernel. Lightweight, fast boot (milliseconds to seconds). Package-based deployment.
- **Serverless / Function-as-a-Service (FaaS):** Event-driven, stateless execution of small code units. Zero infrastructure management, sub-second billing granularity.

### 1.4 Standard Metrics & Constants

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry-Standard Compute Metrics (Bolded for Emphasis)**

- **vCPU** = **1 vCPU ≈ 1 physical CPU thread** (typically backed by a single hyperthread on x86_64 architecture)
- **ECU (EC2 Compute Unit)** = Equivalent CPU capacity of a **1.0–1.2 GHz 2007 AMD Opteron or 2007 Intel Xeon** processor (legacy AWS metric)
- **Memory-to-vCPU ratio:** Standard is **4 GiB RAM per vCPU** (1:4 ratio), with memory-optimized instances reaching **8:1 or 30:1**
- **Cold start latency (Lambda):** typically **100 ms – 5 seconds** depending on runtime and package size
- **Container density:** up to **10–100× higher** than VMs on equivalent hardware
- **Hypervisor Type-1 (Bare-Metal):** VMware ESXi, Microsoft Hyper-V, KVM
- **Hypervisor Type-2 (Hosted):** VirtualBox, VMware Workstation

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Virtual Machines — Operational Breakdown

A **Virtual Machine (VM)** is an emulation of a physical computer system that executes programs like a real machine. VMs rely on a **hypervisor** (Virtual Machine Monitor) to multiplex one physical host into multiple isolated virtual environments.

**Operational Logic Steps:**

1. **Resource Pooling:** Physical hardware (CPU, RAM, Disk, NIC) is aggregated into a resource pool.
2. **Hypervisor Insertion:** A Type-1 (bare-metal) or Type-2 (hosted) hypervisor sits between hardware and VMs.
3. **VM Instantiation:** Each VM receives virtualized resources (vCPU, vRAM, vDisk).
4. **Guest OS Boot:** Each VM independently boots its own OS kernel.
5. **Workload Execution:** Applications run inside the guest OS, unaware of virtualization.
6. **Hardware-Assisted Virtualization:** Modern CPUs use **Intel VT-x** or **AMD-V** to accelerate VM execution via ring de-privileging.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why VMs Exist in Cloud:** VMs provide **strong isolation boundaries** (separate kernels, separate memory spaces), making them ideal for multi-tenant cloud environments where customers must not interfere with each other.

### 2.2 Containers — Operational Breakdown

Containers package an application together with its **dependencies, libraries, and configuration files** into a single lightweight, portable unit. They share the host OS kernel but maintain isolated user-space via **Linux namespaces** and **cgroups**.

**Operational Logic Steps:**

1. **Image Build:** Application code + dependencies are packaged into a container image (Dockerfile → Docker Image).
2. **Layered Filesystem:** Images use a **Union Filesystem (OverlayFS)** with read-only layers and a writable top layer.
3. **Namespace Isolation:** Each container gets its own PID, Network, Mount, UTS, IPC, and User namespaces.
4. **cgroup Resource Limits:** CPU shares, memory caps, and I/O bandwidth are enforced per container.
5. **Container Runtime:** **containerd** or **CRI-O** executes containers per the OCI specification.
6. **Orchestration:** **Kubernetes** schedules containers across a cluster of nodes.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Containers Exist:** Containers solve the **"works on my machine"** problem by packaging the entire runtime environment, enabling consistent deployment from dev → staging → production.

### 2.3 Serverless Computing — Operational Breakdown

**Serverless** does not mean "no servers" — it means **servers are managed entirely by the cloud provider**. Developers write functions triggered by events; the provider handles provisioning, scaling, patching, and capacity planning.

**Operational Logic Steps:**

1. **Function Authoring:** Developer writes a stateless function (e.g., Python, Node.js, Java).
2. **Event Source Configuration:** Trigger is bound (HTTP request, S3 upload, queue message, schedule).
3. **Cold Start Path:** Provider provisions a sandbox, loads runtime, initializes function code.
4. **Warm Execution:** Subsequent invocations reuse the existing sandbox (sub-100 ms).
5. **Auto-Scaling:** Provider scales concurrent executions based on incoming load.
6. **Billing:** Charged per **GB-second** of compute and **number of invocations**.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Compute Metrics Cheat Sheet (KTU Board Exam Ready)**

| Metric | Formula / Definition | Unit | Boundary Condition |
