|---|---|---|---|
| **vCPU Allocation** | $vCPU = \left\lceil \frac{\text{Required MIPS}}{1 \text{ vCPU MIPS}} \right\rceil$ | count | Always round up |
| **EC2 Compute Unit (ECU)** | $ECU = \frac{\text{Reference vCPU cycles}}{2007 \text{ Opteron baseline}}$ | ECU | Legacy metric |
| **Memory-to-vCPU Ratio** | $R = \frac{\text{RAM (GiB)}}{\text{vCPU}}$ | ratio | $R \in [0.5, 30]$ |
| **Cold Start Latency** | $T_{cold} = T_{sandbox} + T_{runtime} + T_{init}$ | ms | $T_{cold} \geq 100$ ms |
| **Container Density Gain** | $D = \frac{N_{containers\_host}}{N_{VMs\_host}}$ | ratio | Typically $D \in [10, 100]$ |
| **Serverless Cost** | $C = (N_{invocations} \times P_{inv}) + (T_{exec\_sec} \times RAM_{GB} \times P_{GBs})$ | USD | $P_{GBs} \approx \$0.0000166667$ (Lambda) |
| **Auto-Scaling Threshold** | $N_{instances} = \left\lceil \frac{\text{Current Load}}{\text{Instance Capacity}} \right\rceil$ | count | Triggered at 60–70% CPU |
| **Hypervisor Overhead** | $OH = 1 - \frac{\text{VM Throughput}}{\text{Bare-Metal Throughput}}$ | % | Typically $5\% - 15\%$ |
| **Load Balancer Distribution** | $L_{i} = \frac{W_{i}}{\sum_{j=1}^{n} W_{j}} \times 100\%$ | % | $\sum L_{i} = 100\%$ |
| **High Availability SLA** | $A = \frac{T_{uptime}}{T_{total}} \times 100\%$ | % | AWS EC2 SLA $\geq 99.99\%$ |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall Alert:** Never use the vertical pipe `\vert` symbol inside markdown tables for this sheet — all delimiters are structural. For absolute values in equations, always use $\vert x \vert$ LaTeX syntax outside tables.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Compute Paradigm | Production Use Case | Why Chosen |
|---|---|---|
| VMs | Legacy ERP systems, monolithic databases | OS-level control, mature ecosystem |
| Containers | Microservices, CI/CD pipelines, ML inference | Portability, fast scaling |
| Serverless | Image processing, IoT backends, chatbots | Zero ops, event-driven bursts |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Serverless Total Cost of Ownership

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Worked Numerical Example — Lambda Cost Calculation**

A developer deploys a Lambda function that processes **5 million invocations per month**, with each invocation averaging **200 ms execution time** and using **512 MB RAM**.

**Given Parameters:**

- $N = 5{,}000{,}000$ invocations/month
- $T_{exec} = 200 \text{ ms} = 0.2 \text{ seconds}$
- $RAM_{GB} = \frac{512}{1024} = 0.5 \text{ GB}$
- $P_{inv} = \$0.20 \text{ per 1 million invocations}$
- $P_{GBs} = \$0.0000166667 \text{ per GB-second}$

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

$$
C_{total} = (N \times P_{inv}) + (T_{exec\_sec} \times RAM_{GB} \times P_{GBs} \times N)
$$

$$
C_{invocations} = \frac{5{,}000{,}000}{1{,}000{,}000} \times \$0.20 = 5 \times \$0.20 = \$1.00
$$

$$
C_{compute} = 0.2 \times 0.5 \times \$0.0000166667 \times 5{,}000{,}000
$$

$$
C_{compute} = 0.1 \times \$0.0000166667 \times 5{,}000{,}000
$$

$$
C_{compute} = \$0.00000166667 \times 5{,}000{,}000 = \$8.3335
$$

$$
C_{total} = \$1.00 + \$8.3335 = \$9.3335 \text{ per month}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The compute cost dominates invocations by ~8× in this scenario. Optimizing function duration or memory allocation yields greater savings than reducing invocation count.

### 3.2 Derivation: Auto-Scaling Decision Logic

Consider an application with the following scaling parameters:

- **Current CPU Utilization:** $U_c = 75\%$
- **Scale-Out Threshold:** $T_{out} = 70\%$
- **Scale-In Threshold:** $T_{in} = 30\%$
- **Current Instance Count:** $N_c = 4$
- **Min Instances:** $N_{min} = 2$, **Max Instances:** $N_{max} = 10$

$$
\text{Decision} = \begin{cases}
\text{Scale Out}, & U_c \geq T_{out} \\
\text{Scale In}, & U_c \leq T_{in} \\
\text{Hold}, & T_{in} < U_c < T_{out}
\end{cases}
$$

Since $U_c = 75\% \geq T_{out} = 70\%$, the system **scales out**. With a default scale-out step of $+2$ instances (AWS ASG default):

$$
N_{new} = \min(N_c + \Delta_{out}, N_{max}) = \min(4 + 2, 10) = 6
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Cooldown Periods Matter:** Without cooldown, scale-out oscillations would cause thrashing. AWS uses a default cooldown of **300 seconds** to stabilize the cluster.

### 3.3 Derivation: Container vs VM Density Ratio

Given:
- Host with $M = 64$ GB RAM
- VM footprint: $R_{VM} = 4$ GB RAM per VM (including OS overhead)
- Container footprint: $R_{ct} = 256$ MB RAM per container

$$
N_{VM} = \left\lfloor \frac{M}{R_{VM}} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor \frac{64}{4} \right\rfloor = 16 \text{ VMs}
$$

$$
N_{ct} = \left\lfloor \frac{M}{R_{ct}} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor \frac{64}{0.25} \right\rfloor = 256 \text{ containers}
$$

$$
D = \frac{N_{ct}}{N_{VM}} = \frac{256}{16} = 16
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Density Insight:** Containers achieve a **16× density improvement** in this scenario. In practice, with proper cgroup tuning, ratios of **50–100×** are achievable.

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master Comparison Table — VMs vs Containers vs Serverless (Board Exam Favorite)**

| Parameter | Virtual Machines | Containers | Serverless |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Isolation Level** | Process + Kernel | Process only | Process + sandbox |
| **Boot Time** | 30 s – 5 min | 100 ms – 5 s | 1 ms – 5 s (warm) |
| **OS Dependency** | Independent Guest OS | Shared host kernel | Managed runtime |
| **Resource Overhead** | High (GB per VM) | Low (MB per container) | None (auto) |
| **Scaling Granularity** | Instance-level | Container-level | Request-level |
| **Persistence** | Stateful (preferred) | Ephemeral (preferred) | Stateless only |
| **Hypervisor Required** | Yes | No | No |
| **Billing Unit** | Hourly | Per second | Per 100 ms |
| **Max Execution Time** | Unlimited | Unlimited | 15 min (Lambda) |
| **Cold Start** | None (pre-allocated) | None (running) | Yes (100 ms – 5 s) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Cloud Compute Service Stack (High-Level Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph ClientLayer["CLIENT ACCESS LAYER"]
        C1[Web Browser]
        C2[Mobile App]
        C3[CLI Tool]
        C4[SDK API Call]
    end

    subgraph EdgeLayer["EDGE AND DELIVERY LAYER"]
        E1[CDN CloudFront]
        E2[Load Balancer ALB]
        E3[API Gateway]
    end

    subgraph ComputeLayer["COMPUTE ABSTRACTION LAYER"]
        direction LR
        subgraph IAAS["IaaS Virtual Machines"]
            VM1[EC2 Instance]
            VM2[Azure VM]
            VM3[GCE Instance]
        end
        subgraph PAAS["PaaS Containers"]
            CT1[ECS Fargate]
            CT2[AKS Pod]
            CT3[GKE Deployment]
        end
        subgraph SAAS["Serverless FaaS"]
            FN1[Lambda Function]
            FN2[Azure Function]
            FN3[Cloud Function]
        end
    end

    subgraph OrchestrationLayer["ORCHESTRATION AND MANAGEMENT"]
        O1[Auto Scaling Group]
        O2[Kubernetes Controller]
        O3[EventBridge Scheduler]
    end

    subgraph InfraLayer["PHYSICAL INFRASTRUCTURE"]
        P1[Bare Metal Servers]
        P2[Hypervisor KVM]
        P3[Host Linux Kernel]
        P4[Networking Fabric]
    end

    C1 --> E1
    C2 --> E2
    C3 --> E3
    C4 --> E3
    E1 --> E2
    E2 --> IAAS
    E2 --> PAAS
    E3 --> SAAS
    IAAS --> O1
    PAAS --> O2
    SAAS --> O3
    O1 --> P1
    O2 --> P2
    O3 --> P3
    P1 --> P4
    P2 --> P4
    P3 --> P4
```

### 4.2 Virtual Machine Internal Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph AppLayer["APPLICATION LAYER"]
        A1[App Process A]
        A2[App Process B]
        A3[System Libraries]
    end

    subgraph GuestOS["GUEST OPERATING SYSTEM"]
        G1[Guest Kernel]
        G2[Guest Device Drivers]
        G3[Guest File System]
    end

    subgraph Hypervisor["HYPERVISOR VIRTUAL MACHINE MONITOR"]
        H1[VM Scheduler]
        H2[Memory Manager]
        H3[Emulated Hardware NIC Disk]
        H4[Hardware Assisted Intel VT-x]
    end

    subgraph HostHW["HOST PHYSICAL HARDWARE"]
        HW1[CPU Cores]
        HW2[DDR Memory]
        HW3[SSD Storage]
        HW4[10GbE NIC]
    end

    A1 --> G1
    A2 --> G1
    A3 --> G1
    G1 --> H1
    G2 --> H3
    G3 --> H3
    H1 --> HW1
    H2 --> HW2
    H3 --> HW3
    H4 --> HW4
    H1 --> H4
```

### 4.3 Container Architecture with Namespace Isolation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph HostKernel["HOST LINUX KERNEL SHARED"]
        K1[PID Namespace]
        K2[Network Namespace]
        K3[Mount Namespace]
        K4[cgroup Resource Limits]
    end

    subgraph C1["CONTAINER 1 NODE JS APP"]
        C1A[App Code]
        C1B[Node Runtime]
        C1C[App Files]
    end

    subgraph C2["CONTAINER 2 PYTHON APP"]
        C2A[App Code]
        C2B[Python Runtime]
        C2C[App Files]
    end

    subgraph C3["CONTAINER 3 REDIS CACHE"]
        C3A[Redis Binary]
        C3B[Config Files]
        C3C[Data Volume Mount]
    end

    subgraph Runtime["CONTAINER RUNTIME LAYER"]
        R1[containerd]
        R2[OCI Image Registry]
    end

    C1 --> R1
    C2 --> R1
    C3 --> R1
    R1 --> K1
    R1 --> K2
    R1 --> K3
    R1 --> K4
    R2 --> R1
```

### 4.4 Serverless Event-Driven Execution Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Event Source S3 Upload]) --> Trigger[EventBridge Rule Match]
    Trigger --> Invoke[Lambda Invoke API]
    Invoke --> Cold{Cold Start Check}
    Cold -->|Yes Cold| Provision[Provision Sandbox]
    Cold -->|No Warm| Execute[Execute Handler]
    Provision --> Load[Load Runtime Layer]
    Load --> Init[Run Init Code]
    Init --> Execute
    Execute --> Logic[Business Logic Execution]
    Logic --> Log[Write CloudWatch Logs]
    Logic --> SideEffect[Optional Side Effects S3 DynamoDB]
    SideEffect --> Bill[Bill GB Seconds]
    Bill --> End([Response Returned])
    Log --> End
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Question 1.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
> **Define Compute Services in cloud computing. List any three characteristics of cloud compute services.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition:** Compute services refer to the on-demand delivery of computing resources — including processing power, memory, storage, and networking — over the internet on a pay-per-use basis, abstracting the underlying physical infrastructure.

**Three Characteristics:**

1. **Elasticity:** Resources can be scaled up or down automatically based on workload demand.
2. **Metered Billing:** Users pay only for the compute capacity consumed (per second, per hour, or per invocation).
3. **Self-Service Provisioning:** Resources can be provisioned via APIs or consoles without human intervention from the provider.
4. **Resource Pooling:** Multiple tenants share the same physical infrastructure with logical isolation.

*(Any three well-explained — full 3 marks)*

---

> **Question 2.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
> **Differentiate between Virtualization and Containerization. Give one example of each.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Virtualization | Containerization |
|---|---|---|
| Abstraction | Hardware-level | OS-level |
| OS | Each VM has its own guest OS | Shares host OS kernel |
| Boot Time | Seconds to minutes | Milliseconds |
| Size | Gigabytes | Megabytes |
| Hypervisor | Required (VMware, KVM) | Not required |
| Example | AWS EC2, Azure VM | Docker, Kubernetes Pods |

**Examples:**
- **Virtualization:** AWS EC2 running Ubuntu Server 22.04 LTS on a t3.medium instance.
- **Containerization:** Docker container running an Nginx web server from the official `nginx:alpine` image.

---

### 📕 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

> **Question A (14 Marks).** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
> **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the three service models of cloud computing (IaaS, PaaS, SaaS) with suitable examples and responsibility distribution between provider and consumer.
> **(b) [7 Marks]** With a neat diagram, explain the architecture and working of a Virtual Machine including the role of hypervisors. Discuss Type-1 vs Type-2 hypervisors.

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]

**Cloud Service Models — Responsibility Distribution:**

| Layer | IaaS | PaaS | SaaS |
|---|---|---|---|
| Application | User | User | Provider |
| Data | User | User | Provider |
| Runtime | User | Provider | Provider |
| Middleware | User | Provider | Provider |
| OS | User | Provider | Provider |
| Virtualization | Provider | Provider | Provider |
| Server/Storage/Networking | Provider | Provider | Provider |

**IaaS (Infrastructure-as-a-Service):** Provides raw compute, storage, and networking. The user installs OS, middleware, and applications. *Examples: AWS EC2, Azure Virtual Machines, Google Compute Engine, DigitalOcean Droplets.*

**PaaS (Platform-as-a-Service):** Provides a managed runtime environment. The user only develops and uploads application code. *Examples: AWS Elastic Beanstalk, Azure App Service, Google App Engine, Heroku.*

**SaaS (Software-as-a-Service):** Provides ready-to-use applications accessed via browser. The user simply consumes. *Examples: Gmail, Microsoft 365, Salesforce, Slack, Dropbox.*

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]

**Virtual Machine Architecture:**

```
+--------------------------------+
|      Application Layer         |
+--------------------------------+
|     Guest Operating System     |   <-- VM
+--------------------------------+
|        Hypervisor (VMM)        |   <-- Virtualization Layer
+--------------------------------+
|    Host Operating System       |   <-- (Type-2 only)
+--------------------------------+
|      Physical Hardware         |
+--------------------------------+
```

**Working of a VM:**

1. The **hypervisor** creates virtual versions of CPU, memory, storage, and network (vCPU, vRAM, vDisk, vNIC).
2. The guest OS and applications run inside this virtualized environment, completely unaware of the underlying physical host.
3. The hypervisor schedules physical resources among multiple VMs.

**Type-1 vs Type-2 Hypervisors:**

| Aspect | Type-1 (Bare-Metal) | Type-2 (Hosted) |
|---|---|---|
| Installation | Directly on hardware | On top of host OS |
| Performance | High (production-grade) | Lower (development-grade) |
| Examples | VMware ESXi, Microsoft Hyper-V, Xen, KVM | VirtualBox, VMware Workstation, Parallels |
| Use Case | Enterprise data centers | Desktop testing, learning |

**Examples in Cloud:** AWS EC2 uses a customized Xen/KVM-based Type-1 hypervisor for production workloads.

---

> **Question B (14 Marks).** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
> **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain Container technology in detail. Discuss Docker architecture and the role of Kubernetes in container orchestration.
> **(b) [7 Marks]** With a neat diagram, explain Serverless Computing architecture. Discuss AWS Lambda as a representative FaaS platform with its pricing model.

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]

**Container Technology Overview:**

A **container** is a lightweight, portable, self-contained execution unit that packages application code together with its dependencies, libraries, and configuration. Containers share the host OS kernel but run in isolated user spaces using **Linux namespaces** (process, network, mount isolation) and **cgroups** (resource limits).

**Docker Architecture:**

```
+----------------------------------+
|       Docker Client (CLI)        |
+----------------+-----------------+
                 | (REST API)
                 v
+----------------------------------+
|      Docker Daemon (dockerd)     |
+----------------+-----------------+
                 |
   +-------------+-------------+
   |             |             |
   v             v             v
+-------+   +--------+   +---------+
|Images |   |Containers|  |Volumes  |
|       |   |         |   |Networks |
+-------+   +--------+   +---------+
                 |
                 v
+----------------------------------+
|  Container Runtime (containerd)  |
+----------------------------------+
|       Host Linux Kernel          |
+----------------------------------+
```

**Docker Components:**

- **Dockerfile:** Declarative recipe to build an image.
- **Docker Image:** Read-only template packaged in layers (UnionFS/OverlayFS).
- **Docker Container:** Running instance of an image.
- **Docker Hub / Registry:** Repository to store and distribute images.

**Kubernetes (K8s) Orchestration:**

Kubernetes is an open-source container orchestration platform that automates deployment, scaling, and management of containerized applications.

**Key K8s Components:**

| Component | Role |
|---|---|
| **Pod** | Smallest deployable unit (1+ containers) |
| **Deployment** | Manages ReplicaSets and rolling updates |
| **Service** | Stable network endpoint for Pods |
| **Ingress** | HTTP routing into the cluster |
| **Node** | Worker machine running Pods |
| **Control Plane** | API Server, Scheduler, etcd, Controller Manager |
| **kubectl** | CLI to interact with the cluster |

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]

**Serverless Computing Architecture:**

```
Event Source  --->  Trigger Service  --->  Function Platform
(S3 / API GW)        (EventBridge)         (AWS Lambda)
                                                  |
                                                  v
                                          +-------+-------+
                                          |   Sandbox     |
                                          | + Init Code   |
                                          | + Handler     |
                                          +---------------+
                                                  |
                                                  v
                                          CloudWatch Logs
                                          + Side Effects
                                          (DynamoDB/S3)
```

**Working of Serverless:**

1. Developer authors a stateless function and uploads it to the platform.
2. An **event source** (HTTP request, file upload, queue message, schedule) triggers the function.
3. The platform **provisions a sandbox**, loads the runtime, runs initialization code (**cold start**), then executes the handler.
4. Subsequent invocations may reuse the warm sandbox, reducing latency.
5. The platform **auto-scales** horizontally up to the account's concurrency limit.
6. Billing is computed per **invocation** + **GB-seconds of compute**.

**AWS Lambda — Representative FaaS:**

- **Supported Runtimes:** Node.js, Python, Java, Go, Ruby, .NET, custom runtimes.
- **Memory Range:** 128 MB – 10,240 MB (CPU scales linearly with memory).
- **Max Execution Time:** 15 minutes per invocation.
- **Deployment Package Limit:** 50 MB (zipped), 250 MB (unzipped) for direct upload; 10 GB via container images.

**Lambda Pricing Model:**

$$
C_{total} = (N_{inv} \times \$0.20 / 1M) + (T_{sec} \times RAM_{GB} \times \$0.0000166667)
$$

- **Free Tier:** 1 million requests + 400,000 GB-seconds per month (always free).
- **Concurrency Limit:** Default 1,000 concurrent executions per region (soft limit, can be raised).

> [!WARNING]
> **🛑 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Compute Services Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Confusing FaaS with SaaS:** FaaS = backend code execution; SaaS = end-user applications. Do NOT interchange these in long answers.
> 2. **Hypervisor Naming Errors:** Type-1 is **bare-metal** (installed on hardware), Type-2 is **hosted** (installed on an OS). Reversing this is an instant 1-mark deduction.
> 3. **Container ≠ Lightweight VM:** Containers share the host kernel; they are **NOT** full VMs. Writing "container is a small VM" loses 2 marks.
> 4. **Cold Start Confusion:** Cold start applies to **serverless**, NOT to VMs or containers. Misattributing cold start to EC2 = wrong concept.
> 5. **Missing Diagrams:** KTU board values diagrams heavily. A 14-mark question without a labeled architecture diagram typically caps at 8–9 marks. Always draw even a hand-sketched block diagram.
> 6. **Cost Calculation Units:** Lambda memory is billed in **GB-seconds**, NOT MB-seconds. Wrong unit conversion = -1 mark.

---

### 🧠 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Compute Services**

- ✅ **Compute Services** = on-demand, metered, elastic provisioning of CPU + RAM + Storage + Network over the internet.
- ✅ **Three Abstraction Paradigms:** Virtual Machines (IaaS), Containers (PaaS), Serverless (FaaS).
- ✅ **Hypervisor:** Type-1 (bare-metal, e.g., ESXi, KVM, Xen) vs Type-2 (hosted, e.g., VirtualBox). Cloud providers use **Type-1 exclusively** for production.
- ✅ **Containers** use **Linux namespaces + cgroups** for isolation; share the host kernel.
- ✅ **Docker** = image (read-only template) + container (running instance) + registry (storage).
- ✅ **Kubernetes** orchestrates containers via Pods, Deployments, Services, and a Control Plane.
- ✅ **Serverless** = event-driven, stateless, auto-scaled, billed per **invocation + GB-second**.
- ✅ **AWS Lambda limits:** max 15-min execution, 128 MB – 10,240 MB memory, default 1,000 concurrency.
- ✅ **VM density** vs **Container density**: containers achieve **10–100×** higher density on identical hardware.
- ✅ **Auto-Scaling** uses threshold-based rules with cooldown periods (default **300 s** in AWS).
- ✅ **Load Balancer** distributes traffic using algorithms like round-robin, least-connections, or weighted.
- ✅ **Hyperscaler Compute Services Map:** AWS = EC2 + Lambda; Azure = Virtual Machines + Functions; GCP = Compute Engine + Cloud Functions + Cloud Run.
- ✅ **Cold start latency:** typically **100 ms – 5 s** depending on runtime and package size.
- ✅ **High Availability:** Use **Multi-AZ deployments** with at least 2 instances across different zones to meet the **99.99% SLA**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Storage Services
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECPT525 - CLOUD COMPUTING > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Storage Services # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 What is Cloud Storage? **Cloud Storage** is a service model in which data is transmitted and stored on remote storage systems, maintained, managed, backed up, and made available to users over a network — typically the public Internet. It is a major component of the cloud computing stack, providing **scalability, durability, availability, and pay-per-use economics** that traditional on-premise storage cannot match. > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Definition (Syllabus-Aligned):** > "Cloud storage is a cloud computing model that stores data on the Internet through a cloud computing provider who manages and operates data storage as a service. It is delivered on demand with just-in-time capacity and costs, and eliminates buying and managing your own data storage infrastructure." ### Three Foundational Storage Paradigms Cloud storage is broadly classified into three primary data service abstractions. These are *not* competitors — they coexist, and the choice depends on the **use case, access pattern, and data structure**. | Abstraction | Granularity | Access Method | Typical Use Case | |---|---|---|---| | **Object Storage** | Whole object (blob) | RESTful HTTP API (`GET`, `PUT`, `DELETE`) | Media files, backups, Big Data, static website assets | | **Block Storage** | Fixed-size block (typically 16 KB – 256 KB) | Mounted as a raw disk via iSCSI/FC | Databases, OS boot volumes, low-latency transactional workloads | | **File Storage** | Hierarchical file/folder | NFS, SMB/CIFS mount | Shared home directories, lift-and-shift legacy apps, content management | --- ## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Smart Warehouse Network" > [!IMPORTANT] > **Conceptual Analogy: The Three Warehouses** Imagine you run a national logistics business. You have **three kinds of warehouses**, each optimized for a different cargo type: 1. **Object Warehouse (e.g., Amazon S3)** Think of a massive, automated warehouse where every item is placed in a **sealed box** with a unique barcode (the *key*). Workers cannot walk in and rearrange items — they only fetch the whole box by its barcode. **You cannot edit the contents; you replace the entire box.** *Best for:* Photos, videos, documents, backups — anything read or replaced as a whole. 2. **Block Warehouse (e.g., Amazon EBS)** Picture a private, dedicated storage room attached to a single office. The room is divided into **uniform numbered bins (blocks)**. The office worker can read/write to any bin anytime, very fast. Only that one office uses the room. *Best for:* Databases, operating system disks — workloads needing millisecond latency and random writes. 3. **File Warehouse (e.g., Amazon EFS / Azure Files)** Imagine a **shared corporate filing cabinet** accessible by many offices simultaneously. Files live in folders, folders live in folders, and everyone follows the same organizational rules (NFS/SMB). *Best for:* Shared application data, home directories, content repositories. This analogy maps directly onto the three real-world cloud services used by every KTU 2024 student. --- ## 1.3 Key Quality Metrics Every Cloud Storage System Guarantees > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Metrics You MUST Memorize (KTU Board-Exam Hot List):** - **Durability:** Probability that data will *not* be lost over a year. Example: Amazon S3 Standard offers **99.999999999 % (11 nines)** durability, i.e., if you store **10 million objects**, you can expect to lose **one object every 10,000 years**. - **Availability:** Percentage of time the service is operational and accessible. Example: S3 Standard = **99.99 %** availability (≈ 52.6 minutes downtime/year). - **Latency:** Time between a request and the first byte of response (ms or µs). - **Throughput:** Data transferred per unit time (MB/s, GB/s). - **IOPS:** Input/Output Operations Per Second — critical for block storage. - **Consistency Model:** Strong, eventual, or read-your-writes. > [!NOTE] > **SLA Convention:** 99.9 % = "three nines", 99.99 % = "four nines", 99.999 % = "five nines". A jump from three nines to four nines cuts allowed downtime by 90 %. --- # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Architectural Layers of a Cloud Storage System A production cloud storage system is built as a **layered stack**. Understanding the layers is essential for KTU Module-4 questions on architecture and design. ### Layer 1 — Frontend (Access Interface) - **REST/HTTP API** for object storage (S3, Azure Blob, GCS). - **POSIX-compliant mount** for block and file storage. - **SDKs** in Java, Python, Node.js, etc. ### Layer 2 — Control Plane (Metadata & Management) - Stores **metadata** (object name, size, content-type, version, ACL). - Manages **authentication & authorization** (IAM, OAuth, signature v4). - Enforces **quotas, replication policies, lifecycle rules**. ### Layer 3 — Data Plane (Storage Backbone) - The actual disks (HDDs, SSDs, NVMe). - Often uses **Distributed Hash Table (DHT)** to locate objects. - Implements **erasure coding** or **replication** for durability. ### Layer 4 — Backend Infrastructure (Physical) - Geographically distributed **data centers** organized into **Regions** and **Availability Zones (AZs)**. - Region = a physical geographic area (e.g., `ap-south-1` Mumbai). - AZ = one or more discrete data centers with redundant power/networking. --- ## 2.2 Consistency Models — A Board-Favorite Topic A consistency model defines **how recent / synchronized** the data returned by a read is, after a write. | Model | Guarantee | Example Service | |---|---|---| | **Strong Consistency** | After a write completes, *all* subsequent reads (anywhere) return the new value. | Amazon S3 (post-2020), Azure Cosmos DB (configurable) | | **Eventual Consistency** | If no new updates are made, *eventually* all reads will return the latest value. | Older S3, DynamoDB (default), DNS | | **Read-your-writes** | A process that wrote a value will always see that value in its own subsequent reads. | S3, DynamoDB | | **Causal Consistency** | Operations causally related are seen in the same order by all nodes. | Some NoSQL systems | --- ## 2.3 Durability and Availability — The Engineering Math Cloud providers achieve **11 nines of durability** by combining **redundancy** with **failure-domain isolation**. ### Replication Strategy - **Synchronous Replication:** Write is acknowledged only after being copied to *all* replicas. Slow but safe. - **Asynchronous Replication:** Write is acknowledged after being written to the primary; replicas catch up later. Fast but vulnerable to failure window. ### Erasure Coding (Reed-Solomon) Splits an object into $k$ data fragments and computes $m$ parity fragments. The original object can be reconstructed from **any $k$ of the $k+m$ fragments**. $$n = k + m$$ where: - $k$ = number of data fragments - $m$ = number of parity fragments - $n$ = total fragments stored **Example:** With $k = 10$ and $m = 4$, the system tolerates the simultaneous loss of any 4 fragments. ### The Durability Formula If each fragment has independent probability $p$ of surviving one year, durability $D$ is: $$D = \sum_{i=k}^{n} \binom{n}{i} p^{i} (1-p)^{n-i}$$ > [!NOTE] > For a 14-nine durability target with $k=10, m=4$, individual fragment survival $p$ must be ≥ 0.9999… (very high), justifying why hyperscalers use specialized hardware and AZ-isolated placement. --- ## 2.4 Storage Classes / Service Tiers (AWS S3 as Reference Model) | Tier | Availability | Use Case | Retrieval Cost | |---|---|---|---| | **S3 Standard** | 99.99 % | Frequently accessed data | None | | **S3 Intelligent-Tiering** | 99.9 % | Unknown/changing access | None | | **S3 Standard-IA** | 99.9 % | Infrequent access, rapid retrieval | Per-GB | | **S3 One Zone-IA** | 99.5 % | Re-creatable, infrequent | Per-GB | | **S3 Glacier Instant Retrieval** | 99.9 % | Archive, millisecond retrieval | Per-GB | | **S3 Glacier Flexible Retrieval** | 99.99 % | Archive, minutes-to-hours | Per-GB + request | | **S3 Glacier Deep Archive** | 99.99 % | Long-term archive, 12+ hours | Lowest \$ | > [!IMPORTANT] > Azure equivalent: Hot, Cool, Cold, Archive Blob tiers. > GCP equivalent: Standard, Nearline, Coldline, Archive. --- ## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Use | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Erasure Coding fragments | $n = k + m$ | Determine storage overhead | | 2 | Storage overhead ratio | $\text{OH} = \dfrac{n}{k} = 1 + \dfrac{m}{k}$ | Capacity planning | | 3 | Durability (simplified) | $D \approx 1 - (1-p)^n$ when $k=1$ | Quick estimate | | 4 | Availability → downtime | $\text{DT} = (1 - A) \times 525{,}600 \text{ min/yr}$ | SLA conversion | | 5 | Replication factor | $R$ = number of copies | Cost vs. durability | | 6 | Replication storage cost | $C_{\text{repl}} = R \times C_{\text{primary}}$ | Total storage cost | | 7 | Cross-region replication latency | $L_{\text{cr}} = 2 \times RTT + W$ | Async write time | | 8 | S3 standard durability | $D_{\text{S3}} = 0.99999999999$ | 11 nines | | 9 | Storage cost per GB-month | $C = \dfrac{\text{Monthly \$}}{\text{GB stored}}$ | TCO analysis | | 10 | CDN cache hit ratio | $H = \dfrac{\text{Hits}}{\text{Hits + Misses}}$ | Cache efficiency | > Real-world utility: erasure coding and replication math are used by **NetApp, Azure Blob (LRC), Facebook (HDFS-RAID), and Google Colossus** — every KTU viva must reference at least one real implementation. --- # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Worked Derivation 1 — Converting SLA to Allowed Annual Downtime The KTU board frequently asks: *"A cloud storage service offers 99.95 % availability. How many minutes of downtime are permitted per year?"* **Step 1 — Total minutes in a (non-leap) year.** $$\text{Total} = 365 \times 24 \times 60 = 525{,}600 \text{ min}$$ **Step 2 — Compute the allowed downtime fraction.** $$\text{DT} = (1 - A) \times \text{Total min}$$ **Step 3 — Substitute $A = 0.9995$.** $$\text{DT} = (1 - 0.9995) \times 525{,}600$$ **Step 4 — Simplify the bracket.** $$1 - 0.9995 = 0.0005$$ **Step 5 — Final multiplication.** $$\text{DT} = 0.0005 \times 525{,}600 = 262.8 \text{ min/year}$$ **Step 6 — Convert to hours for intuition.** $$262.8 \text{ min} \div 60 = 4.38 \text{ hours/year}$$ > **Engineering Insight:** A 99.95 % SLA sounds impressive but permits ~4.4 hours of annual downtime — acceptable for batch analytics, **NOT** for OLTP financial trading systems which require 99.999 % (≈ 5.26 min/year). --- ## 3.2 Worked Derivation 2 — Erasure Coding Storage Overhead **Problem:** A cloud object store uses Reed-Solomon erasure coding with $k = 6$ data fragments and $m = 3$ parity fragments. A user uploads a 300 GB file. Calculate: 1. Total fragments stored. 2. Size of each fragment. 3. Total physical storage used. 4. Storage overhead ratio. 5. Maximum simultaneous fragment losses tolerated. **Step 1 — Total fragments.** $$n = k + m = 6 + 3 = 9$$ **Step 2 — Size of each fragment.** $$\text{size per fragment} = \dfrac{300 \text{ GB}}{k} = \dfrac{300}{6} = 50 \text{ GB}$$ **Step 3 — Total physical storage.** $$\text{Total} = n \times \text{size per fragment} = 9 \times 50 = 450 \text{ GB}$$ **Step 4 — Storage overhead ratio.** $$\text{OH} = \dfrac{n}{k} = \dfrac{9}{6} = 1.5 \; (50\% \text{ overhead})$$ **Step 5 — Fault tolerance.** The system can lose any $m = 3$ fragments and still reconstruct the original 300 GB file. The loss of any 4th fragment makes reconstruction impossible. > **Comparison with 3-way replication (R = 3):** > Replication overhead = $\frac{3}{1} = 3.0$ (200 % overhead). For the same fault tolerance of 3 simultaneous failures, erasure coding uses **half the storage** — a critical engineering trade-off. --- ## 3.3 Worked Derivation 3 — S3 Durability Calculation **Problem:** A startup stores 1 billion objects in S3 Standard. Using the published durability of 99.999999999 % (11 nines), calculate the expected annual data loss. **Step 1 — Loss probability per object per year.** $$P_{\text{loss}} = 1 - D = 1 - 0.99999999999 = 1 \times 10^{-11}$$ **Step 2 — Expected losses.** $$E[\text{losses}] = N \times P_{\text{loss}}$$ **Step 3 — Substitute $N = 1{,}000{,}000{,}000$.** $$E[\text{losses}] = 10^{9} \times 10^{-11} = 10^{-2} = 0.01 \text{ objects/year}$$ **Step 4 — Interpretation.** A user storing 1 billion objects can statistically expect to lose **0.01 objects per year**, i.e., **one object every 100 years** — which is the engineering basis for S3's reputation as the gold standard of cloud storage. --- ## 3.4 Worked Derivation 4 — Cross-Region Replication (CRR) Write Latency **Problem:** An object is written to a primary region with synchronous local write latency $W_{\text{local}} = 10$ ms. It is asynchronously replicated to a backup region 3000 km away (round-trip network RTT ≈ 30 ms). The replication daemon polls every $T_{\text{cycle}} = 5$ s. Estimate the **average** time until the object exists in the backup region. **Step 1 — Local write acknowledgment time.** $$T_{\text{write}} = W_{\text{local}} = 10 \text{ ms}$$ **Step 2 — Worst-case lag before replication cycle picks the object.** On average, a new object waits half a polling cycle: $$T_{\text{wait}} = \dfrac{T_{\text{cycle}}}{2} = \dfrac{5000}{2} = 2500 \text{ ms}$$ **Step 3 — Network transfer time.** $$T_{\text{net}} = \text{RTT} = 30 \text{ ms}$$ **Step 4 — Total average replication lag.** $$T_{\text{repl}} = T_{\text{write}} + T_{\text{wait}} + T_{\text{net}}$$ **Step 5 — Substitute values.** $$T_{\text{repl}} = 10 + 2500 + 30 = 2540 \text{ ms} \approx 2.54 \text{ s}$$ > **Engineering Insight:** The CRR RPO (Recovery Point Objective) is therefore ≈ 2.5 s on average. A region failure could lose up to ~5 s of writes — acceptable for most apps, **unacceptable** for stock trading. --- ## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Block vs File vs Object | Dimension | Block Storage | File Storage | Object Storage | |---|---|---|---| | **Access Protocol** | iSCSI, FC, NVMe-oF | NFS, SMB | HTTP/REST, S3 API | | **Data Unit** | Block (16 KB – 256 KB) | File (KB – TB) | Object (KB – 5 TB) | | **Mutability** | Random read/write | Random read/write | Replace whole object | | **Metadata** | Limited (LBA, size) | POSIX (owner, perms) | Rich custom metadata | | **Search Capability** | None | Filename/path only | Key prefix, tags, SQL | | **Typical Latency** | sub-millisecond | 1–10 ms | 10–100 ms | | **Throughput** | Very high | High | Moderate–High | | **Scalability** | Limited by single VM | Limited by file system | Virtually unlimited (exabyte) | | **Cost / GB-month** | Highest | Medium | Lowest | | **Concurrent Clients** | Single attach (mostly) | Hundreds–thousands | Millions | | **Best Provider Example** | Amazon EBS, Azure Disk | Amazon EFS, Azure Files | Amazon S3, Azure Blob | | **Use Case** | Database, OS disk | Shared file shares, CMS | Media, backups, data lake | --- # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Cloud Storage Layered Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph CLIENT["CLIENT LAYER"] A1[Web Browser] A2[Mobile App] A3[EC2 VM] A4[Lambda Function] end subgraph ACCESS["ACCESS / API LAYER"] B1[HTTPS REST API] B2[POSIX Mount NFS] B3[Block Device Driver iSCSI] end subgraph CONTROL["CONTROL PLANE"] C1[Authentication IAM] C2[Metadata Service] C3[Lifecycle Policies] C4[Replication Manager] end subgraph DATA["DATA PLANE"] D1[Index Service DHT] D2[Storage Nodes Disk] D3[Erasure Coding Engine] end subgraph PHYSICAL["PHYSICAL INFRASTRUCTURE"] E1[Region ap-south-1] E2[Availability Zone 1a] E3[Availability Zone 1b] E4[Availability Zone 1c] end A1 --> B1 A2 --> B1 A3 --> B1 A3 --> B2 A3 --> B3 A4 --> B1 B1 --> C1 B2 --> C1 B3 --> C1 C1 --> C2 C1 --> C3 C1 --> C4 C2 --> D1 C3 --> D2 C4 --> D3 D1 --> D2 D3 --> D2 D2 --> E2 D2 --> E3 D2 --> E4 E2 --> E1 E3 --> E1 E4 --> E1 ``` ## 4.2 Storage Service Decision Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD Q1[Need to store data?] --> Q2{Access pattern?} Q2 -->|Random low-latency writes| R1[Block Storage
EBS Azure Disk] Q2 -->|Shared file system| R2[File Storage
EFS Azure Files] Q2 -->|Whole objects over HTTP| R3[Object Storage
S3 Azure Blob] Q2 -->|Archive cold data| R4[Glacier / Archive Blob] R1 --> X1[Database
OS Boot
Transaction Logs] R2 --> X2[CMS
Home Directories
Lift and Shift] R3 --> X3[Media Files
Static Website
Data Lake
Backups] R4 --> X4[Compliance Archives
Disaster Recovery] ``` ## 4.3 S3 Object Storage Internal Anatomy ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph OBJ["S3 OBJECT"] M1[Key: photos/2024/kerala.jpg] M2[Value: Binary Data Bytes] M3[Version ID: abc123] M4[Metadata: Content-Type size ACL] M5[Tags: env=prod] end subgraph BUCKET["S3 BUCKET my-kerala-bucket"] OBJ end subgraph REGL["REGION ap-south-1"] AZA[AZ a] AZB[AZ b] AZC[AZ c] BUCKET -.replicated.-> AZA BUCKET -.replicated.-> AZB BUCKET -.replicated.-> AZC end ``` ## 4.4 Content Delivery Network (CDN) Flow ```mermaid flowchart LR U[User in Kochi] -->|HTTPS GET image.jpg| EDGE[CloudFront Edge Location Mumbai] EDGE -->|Cache MISS| ORIGIN[S3 Bucket Origin ap-south-1] ORIGIN -->|Returns object| EDGE EDGE -->|Caches for TTL| EDGE EDGE -->|HTTPS 200 image.jpg| U U2[User in Delhi] --> EDGE U2 -->|Cache HIT served from edge| EDGE ``` ## 4.5 Lifecycle Policy State Machine ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> Standard Standard --> StandardIA: After 30 days no access StandardIA --> Glacier: After 90 days no access Glacier --> DeepArchive: After 365 days no access DeepArchive --> [*]: Expire delete after 7 years Standard --> [*]: Explicit delete StandardIA --> Standard: Access detected ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## Part A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions ### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model]` **Differentiate between Object Storage and Block Storage in cloud computing.** **Model Answer:** | Aspect | Object Storage | Block Storage | |---|---|---| | **Data Unit** | Whole object (blob) accessed via key | Fixed-size blocks addressed by LBA | | **Access Method** | RESTful HTTP API | Mounted as raw disk (iSCSI/FC) | | **Mutability** | Replace whole object | Random read/write to any block | | **Latency** | 10–100 ms | sub-millisecond | | **Scalability** | Virtually unlimited (exabytes) | Limited to single VM attachment | | **Use Case** | Media, backups, data lakes | Databases, OS boot disks | | **Example** | Amazon S3, Azure Blob | Amazon EBS, Azure Disk | Object storage is optimized for **throughput and scale**; block storage is optimized for **low-latency random I/O**. --- ### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Model]` **What is meant by "11 nines of durability" in cloud storage? Calculate the expected annual data loss for 100 million objects.** **Model Answer:** "11 nines of durability" means a **99.999999999 %** probability that an object will *not* be lost over a year. It is the durability guarantee offered by Amazon S3 Standard. **Calculation:** $$P_{\text{loss}} = 1 - 0.99999999999 = 10^{-11}$$ $$E[\text{loss}] = 10^{8} \times 10^{-11} = 10^{-3} = 0.001 \text{ objects/year}$$ Interpretation: statistically, **one object lost every 1000 years** for 100 million stored objects. --- ## Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **(a)** Explain the three primary cloud storage service models — Object, Block, and File storage — with suitable use cases and one real-world provider example for each. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Describe the architecture of a typical cloud object storage system. With a neat diagram, explain the roles of the access layer, control plane (metadata), and data plane (storage nodes). Discuss how **erasure coding** improves durability over simple replication. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution for Q.A(a) The three primary cloud storage service models are: **1. Object Storage** - Stores data as discrete units called *objects*, each containing the data, metadata, and a unique identifier (key). - Accessed via RESTful HTTP APIs (`GET`, `PUT`, `DELETE`). - Cannot be mounted as a filesystem; applications must use the API or SDKs. - **Use case:** Storing unstructured data such as images, videos, backups, log files, data lakes for analytics. - **Example:** Amazon S3, Azure Blob Storage, Google Cloud Storage. **2. Block Storage** - Divides data into equally sized blocks; each block is addressed by a Logical Block Address (LBA). - Attached to a single virtual machine as if it were a physical disk. - Supports random read/write with very low latency. - **Use case:** Boot volumes for VMs, relational databases (MySQL, Oracle), transactional workloads requiring high IOPS. - **Example:** Amazon EBS, Azure Managed Disks, Google Persistent Disk. **3. File Storage** - Provides a traditional hierarchical filesystem with folders and files. - Mounted by multiple compute instances simultaneously over NFS (Linux) or SMB (Windows). - POSIX-compliant, so legacy applications work without modification. - **Use case:** Shared home directories, content management systems (WordPress, Drupal), lift-and-shift of legacy applications. - **Example:** Amazon EFS, Azure Files, Google Filestore. > **Conclusion:** The choice depends on access pattern, latency, scalability, and cost — object for scale, block for performance, file for shared access. --- #### Model Solution for Q.A(b) A typical cloud object storage architecture has four logical layers: **1. Access Layer** - Provides the public interface — RESTful HTTP API over HTTPS, SDKs (Java/Python/Go), and CLI tools. - Authenticates requests using AWS Signature V4, OAuth 2.0, or Azure AD tokens. - Routes requests to the appropriate metadata and data services. **2. Control Plane (Metadata Service)** - Manages *where* an object lives — bucket policies, ACLs, versioning, object locks, lifecycle rules. - Stores metadata in a separate, highly consistent database (often NoSQL like DynamoDB or Cassandra). - Handles namespace management (bucket creation, listing). **3. Data Plane (Storage Backbone)** - The actual storage nodes containing the binary object data. - Uses a **Distributed Hash Table (DHT)** or consistent hashing to map object keys to storage nodes. - Stores data on commodity HDDs/SSDs, often grouped into **chunksets**. **4. Physical Infrastructure** - Geographically distributed across multiple **Availability Zones (AZs)** within a **Region**. - Each AZ has independent power, cooling, and networking — failure of one AZ does not affect others. **Erasure Coding vs. Replication:** | Scheme | Storage Overhead | Fault Tolerance | Best For | |---|---|---|---| | 3-way Replication (R=3) | 200 % | Lose any 2 of 3 copies | Hot data, small scale | | Erasure Coding (k=10, m=4) | 40 % | Lose any 4 of 14 fragments | Cold data, petabyte+ scale | Erasure coding splits an object into $k$ data fragments and $m$ parity fragments, distributed across AZs. The original is recoverable from **any $k$ fragments**. This achieves comparable durability to 3-way replication at **less than one-quarter the storage cost**, making it the preferred technique in modern systems (Azure LRC, Facebook HDFS-RAID, Google Colossus). --- ### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Model]` **(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the working of a **Content Delivery Network (CDN)** integrated with object storage. Discuss cache hit ratio, TTL, and origin shield. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Compare the various **storage tiers** offered by cloud providers (using AWS S3 as reference). Describe how lifecycle policies automate data movement across tiers. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution for Q.B(a) A **Content Delivery Network (CDN)** is a geographically distributed network of proxy servers (edge locations) that cache content close to end-users, reducing latency and origin load. **Working Mechanism:** 1. A user in Kochi requests `https://cdn.example.com/image.jpg`. 2. DNS routes the request to the nearest **edge location** (e.g., Mumbai). 3. The edge server checks its local cache: - **Cache HIT** → returns the object immediately (typically < 30 ms). - **Cache MISS** → fetches the object from the **origin** (S3 bucket in `ap-south-1`), serves it to the user, and caches it for future requests based on TTL. **Key Concepts:** - **Cache Hit Ratio (H):** $H = \dfrac{\text{Cache Hits}}{\text{Cache Hits + Cache Misses}}$. A high H (typically > 90 %) is essential for cost efficiency. Higher H → fewer origin fetches → lower origin cost. - **TTL (Time-To-Live):** Each cached object has a TTL set via response headers (`Cache-Control: max-age=…`). After TTL expiry, the next request re-validates with the origin. - **Origin Shield:** A dedicated intermediate caching layer between all edge locations and the origin. It collapses multiple simultaneous cache misses for the same object into a single origin request, dramatically reducing origin load during traffic spikes (e.g., a viral video). **Benefits:** reduced latency, offloaded origin traffic, DDoS mitigation, global reach. --- #### Model Solution for Q.B(b) Cloud storage **tiers** balance cost against access frequency and retrieval time. Using AWS S3 as reference: | Tier | Availability | Min Storage | Retrieval Time | Use Case | |---|---|---|---|---| | S3 Standard | 99.99 % | None | Instant | Frequently accessed | | Intelligent-Tiering | 99.9 % | None | Instant | Unknown/changing access | | Standard-IA | 99.9 % | 30 days | Instant | Infrequent, rapid retrieval | | One Zone-IA | 99.5 % | 30 days | Instant | Re-creatable, infrequent | | Glacier Instant | 99.9 % | 90 days | Milliseconds | Archive, fast access | | Glacier Flexible | 99.99 % | 90 days | Min–Hrs | Archive, low cost | | Glacier Deep Archive | 99.99 % | 180 days | 12+ Hrs | Long-term compliance | **Lifecycle Policy Automation:** A lifecycle rule is a JSON/XML configuration that automatically transitions objects between tiers or deletes them based on age or access patterns. **Example lifecycle policy (S3):** ```json { "Rules": [ { "ID": "ArchiveOldLogs", "Status": "Enabled", "Filter": { "Prefix": "logs/" }, "Transitions": [ { "Days": 30, "StorageClass": "STANDARD_IA" }, { "Days": 90, "StorageClass": "GLACIER" }, { "Days": 365, "StorageClass": "DEEP_ARCHIVE" } ], "Expiration": { "Days": 2555 } } ] } ``` **Result:** Logs are automatically moved from Standard → Standard-IA after 30 days → Glacier after 90 days → Deep Archive after 1 year, and permanently deleted after 7 years — **all without manual intervention**. This is the standard practice for compliance-driven workloads (HIPAA, GDPR, RBI archival). --- ## 5.1 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Deduction Traps (Direct from Valuation Experience):** > 1. **Confusing durability with availability** — Durability = survival of data; Availability = uptime of service. Most students mix them up; lose 1–2 marks per question. > 2. **Writing "EBS is shared storage"** — EBS is **single-attach** (except EBS Multi-Attach for limited use cases). Use EFS for shared file access. > 3. **Forgetting units in calculations** — Always write "minutes/year" or "GB" explicitly; bare numbers lose 0.5 marks. > 4. **Saying S3 has folders** — S3 has a **flat namespace**; the "folder" view is a UI illusion. Buckets contain keys with `/` in them. > 5. **Mixing up replication and erasure coding** — Replication = full copies; Erasure coding = mathematical fragments. Question 5–10 frequently tests this distinction. > 6. **Not drawing a diagram when asked** — A neat labeled diagram is worth **at least 1–2 marks** on its own in Part B. --- ## 5.2 Topic Recap — Rapid-Revision Checklist > [!IMPORTANT] > **Memorize these bullet points before walking into the exam hall:** - **Three storage types:** Object (HTTP), Block (mount), File (NFS/SMB) — know one provider, one protocol, one use case each. - **11 nines durability** = 99.999999999 % → S3 Standard benchmark. - **Region vs AZ** — Region is a geographic area; AZ is a data center within a region. - **Erasure coding formula** $n = k + m$; tolerate loss of any $m$ fragments. - **Replication storage overhead** = $R$ times (e.g., R=3 → 3× storage). - **Consistency models:** Strong (S3 post-2020), Eventual (older S3, DynamoDB default), Read-your-writes, Causal. - **Storage tiers** — Standard, IA, Glacier, Deep Archive (AWS) / Hot, Cool, Cold, Archive (Azure) — match to access frequency. - **Lifecycle policies** automate tier transitions based on age/access. - **CDN** = distributed edge cache; key metrics = cache hit ratio, TTL, origin shield. - **Cross-region replication** is **asynchronous** by default → expect ~seconds of RPO. - **S3 is not a filesystem** — flat namespace, no real folders, no random writes inside an object. - **Always quote units** in numerical answers: minutes/year, GB, ms, %. - **Draw a diagram for every 7-mark sub-question** that asks for architecture or workflow — guaranteed marks. ---
